title: The Adverbializer -no and Adverb Formation from Verbs
summary: The suffix -no deriving manner/degree adverbs from stative (property) verbs, set against related adverb-forming strategies.
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Adverbializer -no and Adverb Formation from Verbs
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: The suffix -no deriving manner/degree adverbs from stative (property) verbs, set against related adverb-forming strategies.

Form and domain

The form no converts a predicate into an adverbial constituent. At the
word level it appears as a suffix on a descriptive intransitive stem —
moyre-no 'slowly', tunas-no 'quickly' — and the
output is a manner or degree adverb. At the predicate level it follows a saturated finite
predicate phrase, especially a stative predicate or a negated action predicate, and links that
predicate as an adverbial circumstance to the following one. The two uses share a form; the
boundary between suffix and post-predicate particle is difficult to draw exactly. Nakagawa
finds a particle analysis cleaner for the finite-predicate cases but observes that it cannot
account for the frozen quantity adverb poronno 'many, much', whose
status as a single, semantically distinct word is well established nakagawa2024 340–341 ‹contested›.

Property meanings belong to the intransitive verb in Ainu. Forms such as
poro be big, become big and pirka be good, become good are
predicates, and a stative reading ordinarily carries an inchoative one alongside it nakagawa2024 124 ; shibatani1990 33–34 ; sato2008 2 . The adverbializer takes
such a predicate as its input. The position of adverbial material relative to valency and
person morphology is set out in verb-word-template ; the broader status of property
predicates is treated in no-adjective-class-property-verbs .

Manner and degree adverbs

Descriptive intransitive stems form manner and degree adverbs by suffixation with
-no . Ijäs gives the learner formulation: moyre be slow
yields moyre-no 'slowly' ijas2023 . Shibatani's example
illustrates the pattern in a full utterance: tunas-no , from
tunas be fast , modifies the property predicate pirka be good; become good , which in the imperative carries its inchoative reading shibatani1990 33 .

tunas-no pirka | be.fast-ADV be.good | Get well quickly! | Shibatani cites the form in his discussion of property predicates as verbs; tunas-no derives the manner modifier from the descriptive intransitive stem tunas, and pirka carries its inchoative 'become well' reading. | shibatani1990:33 | HK

moyre-no | be.slow-ADV | slowly | Ijäs's Lesson 3 teaching example, illustrating the regular descriptive-stem-to-adverb derivation. | ijas2023

input (descriptive vi)
adverbial form
value
evidence status

tunas 'be fast'
tunas-no
manner adverb 'quickly'
shibatani1990 33

moyre 'be slow'
moyre-no
manner adverb 'slowly'
ijas2023 (constructed)

poro 'be big'
poronno
quantity adverb 'many, much'
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 162

pon 'be small'
ponno
quantity adverb 'a little'
ijas2023

The lexicalized quantity forms poronno and
ponno are morphophonologically distinct from the transparent derivation.
Ijäs explicitly records poronno as the correct output and excludes the
regularized *porono ijas2023 ‹consensus›. The quantity
meaning of poronno brings it into contact with verbal number: subjects
modified by poronno may co-occur with either plural or singular
intransitive stems without a mechanical agreement rule nakagawa2024 162 ; sato2008 101 ‹contested›. That interaction is treated in suppletive-verbal-number .

Derived adverbs occupy no argument slot and leave the valence of the modified predicate
unchanged. Nakagawa's arity table assigns arity 0 to adverbial prefixes such as
ar- 'completely' and ru- 'slightly', which
occupy the same valence-neutral zone in the verb word nakagawa2024 137, 140 .
The suffix -no belongs with that side of the template. The
valence-changing morphology that contrasts with this neutral zone is surveyed in
transitivity-and-valence-classes .

Stative and negative-manner linkage

When no follows a saturated predicate phrase, it links that predicate as
an adverbial state or manner to the following predicate. Nakagawa places it in the
temporal-succession connective group alongside wa and
kor , but with a narrower aspectual profile: wa
links predicates whose first element delivers a result-state, kor marks
concurrent action, and no is conditioned on a stative first predicate or
a negated action predicate nakagawa2024 340–341 . Refsing's Shizunai data show
the same adverbializing value in that dialect, where the particle follows a prohibitive
predicate to supply the attendant negative state for the polite imperative refsing1986 241 .

The negative construction is the dominant use. The frame somo … ki no —
negating a predicate with the standard negative support verb ki 'do' and
then adverbializing the result with no — yields the 'without doing'
reading and is near-obligatory in that function; substituting wa or
kor is unexpected in negated contexts sato2008 45–46
‹consensus›. The support construction somo … ki itself is treated in
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

a=ona-ha somo mokor no kamuyoritak kor an | 4.POSS=father-POSS NEG sleep ADV pray CONJ exist | My father was praying to the gods without sleeping. | No adverbializes the negated intransitive predicate somo mokor, making it the attendant circumstance under which the progressive predicate kamuyoritak kor an holds. | nakagawa2024:340 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 614

ku=caranke ka somo ki no ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=complain even NEG do ADV 1SG.S=return | I went home without complaining. | The negative support frame ka somo ki, including the scalar particle ka, is adverbialised as a unit by no. The following predicate ku=hosipi retains its own first-person subject index. | sato2008:44 | SAR

iteke ukoyki no tup ne ukosaraye yan | PROH quarrel ADV two COP share POL | Divide it in two and share it without quarrelling! | Shizunai. No adverbialises the prohibitive predicate iteke ukoyki; the polite imperative yan closes the main predicate. | refsing1986:241 | SHI | Refsing ex. 418

Ijäs identifies the same two uses of no and presents a further
conditioning factor: she describes it as the choice when the linked clauses share a subject,
with wa favoured when they do not ijas2023 . The primary
grammars derive the no / wa alternation from
aspectual and polarity properties of the first predicate — stative or negated predicates take
no — and Refsing's Shizunai conjunctionalizer treatment is consistent
with that account nakagawa2024 340–341 ; sato2008 45–46 ; refsing1986 241 ‹contested›. Subject continuity is frequent in negated contexts because a manner condition is
naturally predicated of the same participant as the following event; the grammars do not extend
this tendency to a grammatical rule.

The connective inventory and the position of no in the wider
temporal-succession system are treated in clause-linkage-overview ; the syntax of
manner and degree adverbial subordination is in manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses .

title: Tripartite, Nominative-Accusative, and the Central Alignment Debate
summary: How the 1sg and indefinite cells show distinct A/S/O exponents (tripartite) against the accusative readings of other cells, and the system-wide synthesis — hierarchical/inverse vs active-stative vs tripartite — including ditransitive and clause-type alignment.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Tripartite, Nominative-Accusative, and the Central Alignment Debate
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: How the 1sg and indefinite cells show distinct A/S/O exponents (tripartite) against the accusative readings of other cells, and the system-wide synthesis — hierarchical/inverse vs active-stative vs tripartite — including ditransitive and clause-type alignment.

The alignment question

Hokkaido Ainu encodes the S/A/O distinction through person indexes that appear on the
finite verb — head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 . The
architecture of the four formal persons and the combination rules are set out in
person-marking-architecture ; the present chapter evaluates the typological
labels that have been applied to the paradigm and assesses how far hierarchical and
inverse analyses bear on the Hokkaido data. The split-intransitive question — whether
the S position subdivides by agentivity — belongs to
alignment-split-intransitive . Free-NP realization and the relationship
between indexing and nominal argument structure are taken up in
argument-realization-indexing .

Row-wise alignment

Reading the person-indexing paradigm cell by cell, the morphological alignment is
mixed. The standard Saru–Chitose forms, cited directly in Bugaeva, Nakagawa, Tamura,
and Satō, yield four distinct row patterns
bugaeva2012 471–472 ; nakagawa2024 165–169 ;
tamura1972 17–39 ; sato2008 31–32 .

person cell
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
row alignment

1SG
ku=
ku=
en=
S=A≠O; nominative–accusative

1PL.EXCL
ci=
=as
un=
S≠A≠O; tripartite

2SG
e=
e=
e=
S=A=O; neutral

2PL
eci=
eci=
eci=
S=A=O; neutral

3
Ø
Ø
Ø
S=A=O; zero or absent

4 / INDEF

=an
i=
S≠A≠O; tripartite

Bugaeva's characterisation follows from the table: 1SG is nominative–accusative
(S=A, distinct from O); second and third person are neutral (all three roles carry
the same form); the first plural exclusive and the indefinite/fourth-person rows
are tripartite, because each uses a distinct form for S, A, and O. She summarises
the language as "mixed (but basically tripartite)"
bugaeva2012 472, 498 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

The tripartite character of those two rows rests on a positional split: the 1PL.EXCL
A form ci= and the fourth-person A form
a= are prefixes on the transitive stem, while the S
counterparts =as and =an are suffixes
on the intransitive stem; the O forms un= and
i= are prefixal but formally distinct from both
nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 472 . The Tokachi
example places the 1PL.EXCL A and S exponents within a single clause chain ‹consensus›:

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | The exclusive group is marked as A by the prefix ci= on the transitive o 'board' and as S by the suffix =as on the intransitive plural arki 'come'; the object counterpart un= is not triggered in this chain. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

The fourth-person O prefix i= indexes the undergoer in oral
narrative — including the narrating protagonist — and together with a=
for A and =an for S makes the tripartite character of that row
verifiable in a single utterance nakagawa2024 169 :

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=lodge CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The fourth-person O prefix i= indexes the narrative protagonist three times; with a= for A and =an for S, all three roles are kept formally distinct within the same paradigm row. | nakagawa2024:169 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

Bugaeva's three-way gradation of the indexes by prosodic and separability tests —
word-like =an and =as , clitic-like
a= and eci= , full prefixes such as
ku= and en= — and Nakagawa's independent
identification of =an as separable from the verb by particles
reflect a cline of morphological tightness that is orthogonal to the alignment classification
bugaeva2012 472–473 ; nakagawa2024 49, 183 ;
sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85 ‹contested›. The ordering diagnostics
belong to personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Catalogue of analyses

The literature assigns different labels because it addresses different questions:
synchronic Hokkaido exponence, historical reconstruction, Sakhalin comparison, and
the referential structure of the fourth-person set. The table below collects the
principal positions; attributions via Dal Corso are second-hand for this grammar.

analysis
principal locus
domain
status for Hokkaido

mixed nominative–neutral–tripartite
Bugaeva (read directly); Okuda 2015 (reported via Dal Corso)
Hokkaido person-indexing morphology
best-supported synchronic label for the paradigm ‹consensus›

mixed nominative–neutral–inverse
Dal Corso 2025
‹SA› Sakhalin person indexing
contrastive; Dal Corso warns the diachrony and hierarchical analysis may not apply to Hokkaido ‹contested›

ergative stage → neutral → nominative

Dal Corso 2023/2025
chiefly ‹SA›, the fourth-person dimension

Satō 2014, reported by Dal Corso
Proto-Ainu diachrony
reported diachronic proposal ‹speculative›

Satō 2024, reported by Dal Corso
history of the 2SG/2PL rows
diachronic account of one row; synchronic neutrality is uncontested ‹contested›

Hokkaido valency and the two-affix cap

Dal Corso's mixed nominative–neutral–inverse account is formulated for ‹SA› Sakhalin.
He dismisses the tripartite component for Sakhalin by arguing that the S and A forms
sharing common origin belong to a nominative sub-system rather than tripartite cells,
while the fourth-person or participant-referentiality-mismatch dimension is analysed as
inverse dalcorso2025align 187, 207, 213 . He is explicit that this
diachrony may not extend to Ainu as a whole and that the hierarchical analysis
may not apply to Hokkaido dalcorso2025align 188, 200 ‹contested›.
The Hokkaido description accordingly retains Bugaeva's cell-based tripartite label
for the two rows in which S, A, and O carry distinct forms and distinct sides of
attachment.

The active attribution

An "active" or split-intransitive characterisation of Ainu person marking appears in
the secondary literature and is commonly attributed to Shibatani. The text accessible
for this grammar is the 1988 LSJ abstract, which treats noun incorporation and argues
that the incorporable nominal corresponds to the patient subject of an intransitive
clause or to a transitive object — explicitly invoking an unaccusative analysis
shibatani1988 210–211 . The abstract assigns no alignment label to the
person-indexing paradigm, and the 1990 work in which an active characterisation may
appear was not available to verify shibatani1988 210–211 ‹speculative›.

The person-indexing paradigm itself does not partition the intransitive subject by
agentivity. Agentive intransitives and patientive or change-of-state intransitives take
the same S-form set once the verb is intransitive; the conditioning factor is valency,
not semantic role nakagawa2024 126–127 . Satō's 2025 characterisation
places Ainu with head-marking languages in the sense of nichols1986
and develops the argument-indexing system without assigning an alignment label
sato2025a 361, 373–374 ‹speculative›. The split that the literature
pursues under the active or split-intransitive name surfaces in verb classes,
incorporation, and valency-changing morphology, and is treated in
alignment-split-intransitive .

Hierarchical effects in person combinations

Two aspects of the paradigm are better described in hierarchical or referential terms
than in a flat S/A/O frame: the speech-act-participant combination cells and the
referential range of the fourth-person set.

The second-plural prefix eci= serves as the portmanteau for
all combinations in which a first-person A acts on a second-person O —
1SG.A→2SG.O, 1SG.A→2PL.O, 1PL.EXCL.A→2SG.O, and 1PL.EXCL.A→2PL.O — using a
single prefix where a sequence of first- and second-person indexes might be expected
tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2008 38 ;
bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 .
The cell and its ambiguity with the plain 2PL marker are developed in
eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

nep ka eci=kasuy pe an ya
2SG.O=help NMLZ exist Q"
tr="Is there anything I can help you with?"
cite="nakagawa2024:173"
dial="SAR"
place="Nakagawa ex. 128, after Honda 2001: 44"
note="The single prefix eci= encodes 1SG.A acting on 2SG.O; no separate first- and second-person indexes co-occur. The form is homophonous with the plain 2PL index in A, S, and O."
/>

The 2→1 direction is irregular in a different way. Most dialectal combination tables
carry a second-person A index followed by the first-person object form, but Saru and
Chitose suppress the expected second-person subject prefix and use bare
en= for 2SG→1SG; Chitose extends the bare form to 2PL→1SG
as well nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 .

The fourth-person set adds a second hierarchical dimension through its referential
range. Five uses cluster in the same form-set: the inclusive first plural, the
honorific address to a respected second person, the indefinite or agent-defocused
transitive subject, the narrative first person in oral literature, and logophoric
reference inside a quotation nakagawa2024 174–179 ;
tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 .
The honorific use brings the hierarchy most plainly into view: one addressee is
indexed with the fourth-person suffix on a plural verb stem.

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | The addressee is a single person, yet the fourth-person S suffix =an selects the plural stem arki. The plural signals honorific elevation rather than number of referents. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140, woman addressing an adult man

The fourth-person A prefix defocuses the transitive agent while the patient remains
the grammatical object; Ainu has no subject-promoting passive. The same prefix
participates in the indefinite construction that Japanese or English translations
often render passively tamura1972 34 ; sato2008 203–205 ;
nakagawa2024 176–177 .

wen kamuy a=maketa wa a=rayke ru-we ne | bad god 4.A=defeat CONJ 4.A=kill track-POSS COP | The evil kamuy was defeated and killed. | The fourth-person prefix a= fills the A position and defocuses the agent; the lexical patient remains the object of both transitive verbs. The passive-flavoured English translation reflects agent-defocusing, not a voice alternation in the Ainu. | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 145

Bugaeva analyses the inclusive and quotation uses as unified by a logophoric function:
the set organises participant reference from a discourse perspective, with the singular
pronoun asinuma glossed by native speakers as 'oneself' rather
than 'I' bugaeva2008 40–43 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 .
Dal Corso's participant-referentiality-mismatch framing for ‹SA› Sakhalin treats the
same dimension as a referential hierarchy of speech-act participants, but explicitly
limits that analysis to Sakhalin data
dalcorso2025align 196–200 ‹contested›.

The Hokkaido evidence supports a synthesis: person indexing is row-wise mixed in
morphological alignment, while the fourth-person set and the eci=
portmanteau and 2→1 irregular cells display a referential-participant hierarchy
overlaid on the S/A/O frame. This overlay does not replace the tripartite exponence
of the 1PL.EXCL and fourth-person rows; both dimensions are operative in the same
paradigm ‹original-needs-review›. The basis is the factbase-verified five-use
analysis of the fourth person and the directly read combination data
( nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura1972 27–28, 34 ;
bugaeva2008 38, 40–43 ); the synthesis is offered as an integrative
label for an existing pattern, not as a new theoretical commitment.

Third person and the two-affix cap

The third-person row is formally uniform — Ø for A, S, and O — but the
interpretation is contested. Nakagawa treats third person as the absence of any affix:
a bare noun phrase satisfies the verb's valency position without triggering a marker
nakagawa2024 169 . Satō argues for a zero third-person affix, required
to account for the cap of two personal indexes per verb and the ungrammaticality of
three-index combinations in causative and ditransitive configurations
sato2023b 39 ; sato2025a 369 . Bugaeva's paradigm
table writes Ø in the third row, describing the surface form without adjudicating
between the two analyses bugaeva2012 471 ‹contested›.

Ijäs's learner grammar presents the same operational generalization for L2 learners:
a finite verb obligatorily indexes its non-third participants while third-person
arguments surface as bare noun phrases ijas2023 . Her presentation is
compatible with both sides of the zero-versus-absent debate.

seta cep nukar | dog fish see | The dog sees the fish. | Constructed following Ijäs's pedagogical model (L2–L3); both arguments are third person and no personal index appears on the verb. Nakagawa's analysis requires no third-person affix here; Satō's analysis posits a zero affix for each third-person role. | ijas2023 | HK

Ijäs's fixed-order statement for ditransitives — indirect object between subject and
object — is a teaching simplification. Nakagawa's three-place-verb examples show
that the two object phrases and the subject are not locked in a single linear order
nakagawa2024 363–365 ; ijas2023 .

The ditransitive evidence bears directly on alignment. Satō argues that the object
index in three-place verbs such as kore 'give' ordinarily
identifies the human recipient while the theme stays unindexed — a secundative
pattern in the indexing slot sato2008 147 ;
sato2023b 40, 47 . Nakagawa describes the same verbs through valency,
with two bare object phrases when both are third person
nakagawa2024 363–365 . Both accounts converge on the generalization
that a verb hosts at most one subject index and one object index; the difference is
whether the unfilled third-person position contains a zero marker ‹contested›.

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | The 1SG.A subject is indexed; both non-subject participants are third person and appear as bare noun phrases with no object index. Satō's analysis posits a zero third-person object for the recipient; Nakagawa's analysis requires no zero. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

The full object-indexing system and its relationship to
transitivity-and-valence-classes valency classes are treated in
argument-realization-indexing .

Synthesis

The most defensible synchronic label for Hokkaido Ainu person-indexing is
person-conditioned mixed morphological alignment. Row by row: 1SG is
nominative–accusative (S=A≠O), second and third persons are neutral (S=A=O), and
the 1PL.EXCL and fourth-person rows are tripartite (S≠A≠O, with side of attachment
realising the S/A distinction) ‹consensus›. A language-level label such as active,
ergative, or inverse characterises at most one row accurately: the active label is
unverified for the agreement paradigm
shibatani1988 210–211 ; the ergative analysis is a diachronic
reconstruction for Proto-Ainu; and the inverse label is Dal Corso's analysis of
‹SA› Sakhalin, explicitly limited by its author to that variety
dalcorso2025align 187–188, 213 .

Above the row-wise pattern, a hierarchical overlay is operative in the fourth-person
set and in the eci= portmanteau and 2→1 irregular cells. This
overlay encodes referential distinctions — inclusive versus exclusive, honorific versus
plain, logophoric versus direct report — that fall outside the binary S/A/O frame. The
overlay is grounded in the factbase-verified five-use analysis of the fourth person and
the directly read combination data, and is presented as an integrative account of the
competing labels rather than a new theoretical proposal ‹original-needs-review›.

Several questions remain open. Whether the positional contrast that grounds the
tripartite label (prefix for A, suffix for S) warrants a tripartite classification
or is better captured diachronically, as Dal Corso proposes for ‹SA›, is a live
disagreement dalcorso2025align 207, 213 ‹contested›. The origin of
second-person neutrality — whether it reflects an original non-distinction or a lost
S/A≠O pattern — is unresolved; Satō (2024, as reported by Dal Corso) argues for the
original account, while most earlier work assumes a later merger
dalcorso2025align 194–195 ‹contested›. The zero-versus-absent debate
for third person remains open between Nakagawa and Satō ‹contested›.

title: The Split-Intransitive (Active–Stative) Core
summary: The foundational alignment fact: intransitive subjects split between the agentive (A-set) and patientive (O-set) exponents, and the Split-S vs Fluid-S question.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Split-Intransitive (Active–Stative) Core
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The foundational alignment fact: intransitive subjects split between the agentive (A-set) and patientive (O-set) exponents, and the Split-S vs Fluid-S question.

What split-intransitive alignment predicts

A split-intransitive, or active–stative, alignment divides intransitive subjects into two
agreement classes: subjects that pattern with transitive A, and subjects that pattern with
transitive O. The conditioning factor is typically control, volition, or affectedness
stassen1997 23–28 ; foleyvanvalin1984 29–37 .
The Hokkaido Ainu finite person-index system makes no such division. A predicate's
transitivity class — zero-place, one-place, two-place, or three-place — fixes which indexes
appear on the verb; within the one-place class, no further agentivity distinction feeds the
morphology nakagawa2024 124–128 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 .

The morphological architecture, including the full S/A/O paradigm and the side-encoding
that makes the 1PL.EXCL and fourth-person rows tripartite, is described in
person-marking-architecture the personal-affix architecture chapter . This
chapter addresses the narrower diagnostic: whether the intransitive S column itself splits
by agentivity. It also explains where an active-looking division genuinely occurs — in
lexical classification, incorporation diagnostics, and valency-changing morphology — and
why that domain does not constitute an agreement-level split ‹consensus›.

domain
organising axis
Hokkaido pattern
treated in

finite person indexes
transitivity class and formal person
one S set per person row; no agentivity split within S
person-marking-architecture personal-affix architecture

person-row alignment
cell in the person paradigm
1SG accusative; 2/3 neutral; 1PL.EXCL and 4 tripartite
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate alignment debate

lexical classification
semantic role and event structure
patient-like S patterns with O for incorporation diagnostics
transitivity-and-valence-classes valence classes

valency-changing morphology
derivational template
transitivity changed first; ordinary S/A/O indexes assigned to the result
verb-word-template verb word template

All intransitive subjects use the S set

Nakagawa's valence chapter gives the decisive test. Hokkaido intransitive verbs with an
acting, controlling subject and intransitive verbs whose sole argument is a patient,
experiencer, or affected entity alike take the intransitive S forms. Agentive intransitives
and patientive ones are both one-place verbs; the personal-index system responds to the
valence count, not to the semantic role nakagawa2024 126–127 . His own
examples pair a zero-place ambient predicate — which bears no person index at all — with an
experiential predicate whose sole argument is an affected experiencer indexed by the
fourth-person S suffix =an , the same suffix an agentive subject of
the same person would carry.

mean cuk ne pe merayke=an ka somo ki no | be.cold autumn COP thing feel.cold=4.S FOC NEG do ADV | It was a cold autumn, but I did not feel cold. | The ambient predicate mean 'be cold (of weather)' is zero-place and indexes no participant. The experiential predicate merayke 'feel cold' is one-place; its affected experiencer carries the fourth-person S suffix =an, the same form an agentive fourth-person subject would carry. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15, after Kayano 1974a

A force-of-nature predicate provides the same diagnostic from a different angle. In
rittursere be rolled by waves , the wave element is incorporated into the
stem and the human undergoer is the sole grammatical subject. That undergoer is indexed by
=an ; the fourth-person object form i= does
not appear. A patient-like meaning does not move the index toward the O set
nakagawa2024 126–127 .

rir-tursere=an kor mom=an ru-we ene an hi ne hine | wave-roll=4.S while flow=4.S track-POSS thus exist NMLZ COP CONJ | rittursere=an kor mom=an ruwe ene an hi ne hine | I was rolled by the waves and drifted along, and so … | Surface rittursere is segmented as rir-tursere following Nakagawa's analysis of the incorporated wave element. Both patientive intransitives carry =an, not the object form i=. | nakagawa2024:490 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 1119

The first singular supplies a complementary diagnostic. The 1SG row is
nominative-accusative: ku= serves as both A and S, opposed to the
object en= bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 . A 1SG agentive intransitive and a 1SG experiential intransitive look identical
in their person index; the object form en= appears only when the
participant is the O of a transitive verb. The identity belongs to the 1SG row of the
paradigm and is not tracking control ‹consensus›.

Lexical classes, the unaccusative diagnostic, and incorporation

Within the intransitive class, a semantically motivated distinction is widely recognized.
Intransitive verbs whose subject is a semantic agent — labelled S a —
behave differently from those whose subject is a semantic patient or undergoer
( S o ) with respect to certain derivational and syntactic operations
foleyvanvalin1984 29–37 ; mithun1984 854–856 .
In Hokkaido Ainu the distinction operates at the lexical and derivational level; it does
not feed finite person-index assignment.

The clearest evidence for an S a /S o cut comes from noun
incorporation. The accessible Shibatani source is the 1988 conference abstract, which
states that the incorporable nominal is the patient subject of an intransitive clause and
the object of a transitive clause — an unaccusative framing stated in terms of semantic
role and grammatical relation shibatani1988 210–211 . The abstract
contains no alignment-type term. A broader active-alignment characterisation sometimes
associated with shibatani1990 cannot be attributed on the basis of the pages
read; that book could not be independently consulted for this grammar ‹speculative›. The
noun-incorporation domain, including the patient-subject diagnostic, is developed in
noun-incorporation-object noun incorporation .

The S a /S o distinction is therefore a valence and lexical-class
diagnostic, not an agreement-level split. A patientive intransitive subject is eligible
for certain derivational processes — notably the unaccusative-style incorporation
Shibatani describes — that agentive subjects are not. Once the predicate's transitivity
class is fixed, its sole argument takes the S set regardless of whether that argument is
an agent, experiencer, or undergoer.

Valency-changing morphology and the S set

Valency-changing morphology moves predicates across transitivity classes before person
indexing is assigned. The antipassive or indefinite-object prefix i- antipassive / something detransitivises a transitive verb whose object is
indefinite or backgrounded; the resulting predicate is intransitive, and its sole
argument — which is an agent — takes the S set, formally identical to that of any
intransitive experiencer tamura1972 35 ; nam2021 62–74 .
Reflexive yay- and reciprocal u- fill the
object position and reduce transitivity in the same way. Applicatives and causatives add
object positions, moving a predicate from intransitive to transitive class
nakagawa2024 196–199 ; sato2023b 40 .

The antipassive i- is the case most directly relevant to the
split-S question, because applying it to a semantically transitive stem produces an
intransitive whose subject is an agent yet whose S index is formally identical to that of
an intransitive experiencer. The prefix must also be distinguished from the fourth-person
object pronoun i= , which is an O-form of the indefinite/fourth
series alongside A-form a= and S-form =an ;
the two are kept apart by the transitivity class of their host, not by the meaning of
the argument nam2021 62–64 ; bugaeva2021antip 235–239 .
The antipassive and its origin debate are treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i the detransitive i- .

Zero-place predicates and the valence boundary

The boundary between zero-place and one-place predicates marks the outer limit of S-set
assignment. Ambient weather predicates such as sirsesek be hot (weather)
and mean be cold (weather) are zero-place: they index no participant
nakagawa2024 127–129 . Felt-temperature predicates such as
sesek feel hot and merayke feel cold are one-place and
carry a person index on their experiencer subject. The zero-place/one-place boundary
separates argument-free from argument-bearing predicates; it is a valence distinction,
not an agentivity one.

Ijäs treats weather expressions as avalent and places ambient forms in that category;
Nakagawa agrees on the category but leaves apto as 'rain falls'
analytically uncertain, since apto 'rain' is an autonomous noun
that can function as a subject in other constructions nakagawa2024 128–130 ;
ijas2023 ‹contested›. Property-concept predicates —
pirka be good , poro be big ,
hure be red / become red and related items — are one-place intransitives
in the baseline Hokkaido analysis. Ijäs notes that descriptive verbs also carry inchoative
readings, so a single lexeme spans a stative and its onset without changing the person
index. Both uses index the sole argument as S. Their predication syntax is treated in
property-concept-predication .

Ijäs's L2–L3 claims in this domain

Ijäs's learner grammar organises valence through the metaphor of verbal "pockets": the
verb carries participant information obligatorily, while external noun phrases are optional
cross-references. Analytically this is the head-marking architecture of
nichols1986 , used also by Satō sato2025a 361 . Her
Lesson 2 states that intransitive verbs take one argument and Lesson 3 that transitives
take two; these arity classes align with Nakagawa's zero/one/two/three-place distinctions
and Satō's valence discussion nakagawa2024 124–128 ;
sato2023b 37–47 ; ijas2023 .

Two Ijäs formulations that bear on the split-S question carry caveats. Her Lesson 9
account of i- as an antipassive filling the object position with a
generic 'thing/place' meaning aligns in substance with the Bugaeva line; Satō uses a
derivational-prefix label without the term 'antipassive' ijas2023 ;
sato2008 232–233 ; bugaeva2021antip 235–236 ‹contested›.
Her Lesson 3 formulation that the indirect object falls between subject and object in
ditransitives is a pedagogical word-order simplification; Nakagawa's ditransitive examples
show no fixed order for the two non-subject noun phrases nakagawa2024 363–365 ; ijas2023 ‹contested›.

The alignment label and the broader debate

For Hokkaido person indexing, the secure description is mixed morphological alignment by
person row, with no productive split-S agreement. Bugaeva's formulation remains the
standard account: neutral alignment in second and third person, nominative-accusative in
1SG, and tripartite in 1PL.EXCL and the indefinite/fourth person bugaeva2012 472, 498 ; bugaeva2008 32 . Nakagawa's valence chapter blocks an
agentivity-conditioned reading of the S column nakagawa2024 126–127 .
Satō frames the same system as head-marking and argues for a zero third-person affix on
the basis of the two-index cap and ditransitive restrictions; Nakagawa denies a
third-person exponent. That dispute affects the model of the zero row but produces no
active/stative split among the overt S forms nakagawa2024 169 ;
sato2023b 39, 47 ; sato2025a 369 ‹contested›.

Sakhalin work sharpens the typological contrast. Dal Corso rejects a tripartite component
for ‹SA› Sakhalin, proposing a mixed nominative-neutral-inverse analysis, and explicitly
cautions that his diachrony and hierarchical account may not apply to Hokkaido
dalcorso2025align 187–188, 200, 213 ‹contested›. The tripartite,
hierarchical, and inverse positions are weighed against one another in
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate ; the Sakhalin contrast leaves the
Hokkaido picture intact.

The productive-split question has one remaining open edge. The literature does not yet
provide a verb-by-verb inventory of whether any Hokkaido intransitive verb alternates
between A-like and O-like S marking according to control or discourse construal. Such an
inventory would require a raw-token study across the corpus; the normalised frequency layer
strips personal-affix clitics and cannot supply per-affix counts for individual S forms.
No factbase example shows an intransitive undergoer indexed by the object forms
en= , un= , or i= ;
the available evidence supports the uniform-S generalization as the correct synchronic
description ‹original-needs-review›.

title: The Antipassive / Detransitive i-
summary: The prefix i- 'something/someone' absorbing the object and detransitivizing, with the antipassive-vs-incorporation debate and its origin.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Antipassive / Detransitive i-
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The prefix i- 'something/someone' absorbing the object and detransitivizing, with the antipassive-vs-incorporation debate and its origin.

Form and function in the arity calculus

The prefix i- antipassive / detransitivizing prefix occupies the object
position of a transitive verb with a semantically generic 'thing, someone, people', reducing
the verb's arity by one and rendering it intransitive. In Nakagawa's arity calculus the
operation is a standard −1 step: a two-place transitive yields a one-place intransitive, and a
three-place ditransitive yields a two-place transitive nakagawa2024 190–192 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 . The resulting intransitive takes suffixal S
indexes — =as (1PL.EXCL.S) and =an (4.S) —
where the base transitive had taken prefixal A indexes such as ci= and
a= . This person-affix shift is the single operative diagnostic for the
detransitivization sato2008 96 ; nakagawa2024 137, 140 . The
place of the operation in the wider valency system is set out in
valency-voice-overview .

The prefix sits immediately before the verb root, inside the person-index layer. Under
Nakagawa's position-class template it occupies the same slot as the other valency-decreasing
prefixes yay- , si- , and u- , and it combines freely with applicatives outside it and causative suffixes after the stem:
ko-i-ruska (APPL-ANTIP-be.angry at), a re-transitivized applicative
of the antipassive i-ruska 'be angry', is well-formed, and so is the
further reflexivized si-ko-i-ruska
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 . The derivational chain
ruska (vt) → i-ruska (vi) → ko-i-ruska (vt) → si-ko-i-ruska (vi) → i-ruska-re (vt,
'make sb angry') → ko-i-ruska-re (vd) shows that
i- is not a terminal derivation but a mid-chain element and that the
arity bookkeeping remains coherent at every step bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 .
In the corpus, iruska 'be angry' appears 655 times (rank 264), making
it among the most frequent antipassive derivatives ‹corpus-confirmed›.

i-ri=an wa or-o wa sini=an na | ANTIP-skin=4.S CONJ place-POSS from rest=4.S FIN | I will skin the catch and then rest. | The intransitive suffix =an on i-ri confirms that the antipassive has intransitivized ri 'skin sth'; the Patient — the catch — is outside the argument frame. Both clause-final =an tokens carry fourth-person S reference. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.3 | SHI | Shizunai Board of Education 1991–1995, O0130054_6; Handbook ex. 24b

Semantic domains and lexicalization

The antipassive is productive across a restricted range of semantic classes. Bugaeva, working
from Nakagawa's corpus, identifies 48 verbal lexemes that form antipassive derivatives,
concentrated in Perception/Cognition, Ingesting, Interaction, Communication, Grooming, and
Traditional-Activity verbs bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 . Absent from the set
are Creation, Transformation, and Contact-by-Impact verbs — a distribution consistent with the
typological generalization that antipassives in non-ergative languages preferentially target
low-transitivity predicates ‹contested›.

Selected antipassive derivatives by semantic class (primary Saru/Chitose data; nakagawa2024 190–192 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 )

base (transitive)
antipassive form
approximate meaning
semantic class

nu 'hear sth'
i-nu
listen (generally)
Perception

nukar 'see/look at sth'
inkar (< i-nukar )
gaze, look
Perception (lexicalized)

ku 'drink sth'
i-ku
drink alcohol (euphemism)
Ingesting

e 'eat sth'
ipe (< i-e )
food; eat a meal
Ingesting (lexicalized)

kasuy 'help sb'
i-kasuy
help (do helping)
Interaction

huraye 'wash sth'
i-huraye
do the washing
Grooming

ri 'skin sth'
i-ri
skin the catch
Traditional Activity

omante 'send off sb'
i-omante
send off the bear-god's spirit
Traditional Activity (lexicalized)

carpa 'offer sth'
i-carpa
make ancestral offerings
Traditional Activity (lexicalized)

Many antipassive derivatives have lexicalized to a fixed, conventionalized object reading.
i-ku no longer simply means 'drink something' but specifically 'drink
alcohol' — a euphemistic narrowing noted by Nakagawa as 婉曲 (indirect
expression) — while i-omante and i-carpa are
restricted to ritual contexts nakagawa2024 191–192 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 . At the extreme of lexicalization the morphological boundary is no longer transparent:
inkar 'gaze' (< i-nukar ) and
ipe 'food; eat a meal' (< i-e ) have undergone
phonological fusion and are synchronically nominal or verbal roots in their own right nakagawa2024 192 . Bugaeva and Kobayashi further report that i- is fossilized
residually on the adverb i-mak 'behind' and the particle
i-y-os(no) 'afterward, later', where the original Patient-incorporating
reading is entirely opaque bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 .

ene wa poka a=reka hi ka isam menoko i-huraye kor an na | thus CONJ even 4.A=praise NMLZ even not.exist woman ANTIP-wash while exist FIN | A woman of incomparable beauty was doing the laundry. | The antipassive i-huraye 'do the washing' is intransitive; the specific object (laundry) is inferrable from context but suppressed from the argument frame. The embedding clause ene wa poka a=reka hi ka isam means literally 'there is no way to praise her' — the conventional formula for incomparable beauty. | nakagawa2024:191 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 170

The discourse motivation for the antipassive parallels what Bugaeva identifies typologically:
i- is preferred when discourse focus falls on the Agent or the activity
as a whole rather than on a specific, individuated Patient bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 . Bugaeva also notes that the prefix can antipassivize a Recipient or a direct Causee, as in
i-newsar-ka 'amuse people' and i-oripak-ka
'make people feel humble' — an antipassivization of the causee rare across languages
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 .

The label debate: antipassive or derivational prefix?

The functional description is uncontroversial; the grammatical label is not. The traditional
analysis, associated with Tamura and reported through Bugaeva and Kobayashi, treats
i- as a generalised object marker — a derivational prefix introducing
an unspecified object 'someone, thing' — without applying the voice category
antipassive bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 . Satō's textbook grammar
follows this line, describing the identical prefix as derivational i-
'人を' (people-as-object) and listing no antipassive in its voice inventory sato2008 232–233 . On this account i- is a lexical derivational element that selects a
generic reading rather than a voice morpheme that restructures argument relations.

Bugaeva's antipassive reanalysis argues from three converging criteria that
i- meets the typological definition of an antipassive: it
systematically demotes the Patient/Theme/Recipient from a core object position; its output
always carries an unspecified or generic object reading; and it is discourse-conditioned,
appearing precisely where the speaker defocuses the Patient to foreground the Agent or the
activity bugaeva2021antip 232–237 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 .
Nakagawa endorses this reanalysis, noting that the term 逆受動態 (antipassive) has become
standard in recent work following Bugaeva, and adopts it while acknowledging the derivational
tradition nakagawa2024 192 . Ijäs's learner grammar also uses the antipassive
label; her characterization — the prefix 'takes the place of the object and fills it with a
generic meaning of thing/place' — renders Bugaeva's substance accurately ijas2023
‹contested›.

The two positions share substantial common ground: both agree that the prefix occupies the
object position, that its semantic contribution is generic and unspecified, and that the verb
becomes intransitive by the person-affix test. The disagreement is whether this slot-filling
constitutes a grammaticalized voice alternation or a lexical derivation that happens to
intransitivize. The width of the prefix's semantic scope — extending to Recipient and Causee
demotion, not only Patient — and its discourse conditioning give the antipassive account
stronger typological grounding. This chapter adopts Bugaeva's label while registering Tamura's
and Satō's derivational reading as the earlier, still-maintained alternative ‹contested›.

Homophony: antipassive i- and the fourth-person object i=

Two surface strings i- must be kept distinct. The antipassive prefix
i- is a derivational element in the slot immediately before the verb
root; the indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm fourth-person object clitic
i= is a person index belonging to the transitive object paradigm. Both
are segmentally identical, and both can appear on the same verb surface, but their
distributional profiles diverge bugaeva2021antip ; nam2021 62–63 .

The antipassive prefix satisfies the object slot derivationally and renders the verb
intransitive: the derived verb takes S suffixes and cannot simultaneously host a separate
object clitic. The fourth-person clitic, by contrast, leaves the verb transitive and is
cross-referenced by a prefixal A index alongside the i= object clitic.
A minimal diagnostic: i-nu=an (ANTIP-hear=4.S) is intransitive, proven
by the suffixal =an ; while a=i=nu
(4.A=4.O=hear) is transitive and the i= there is the object clitic, not
the antipassive prefix nakagawa2024 190–191 . The arity computation — the same
instrument that distinguishes applicative e- from affiliative
e- and from the second-person clitic e= — is the
sole disambiguating tool in both cases nakagawa2024 192–193 .

Syntactic diagnostics and re-applicativization

The antipassive instantiates an S=A alternation: the Agent argument of the transitive base
surfaces as the sole argument of the antipassive intransitive, cross-referenced by the same
person indexes that mark the transitive A. The alternation is illustrated by Bugaeva and
Kobayashi's minimal pair: nea kamuy a=ri 'I skin that bear'
(transitive, specific Patient) versus i-ri=an '(I will) skin the catch'
(antipassive, Patient removed from the frame) bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 . In
the antipassive the prefixal a= is absent because there is no object
slot to fill; the suffixal =an carries logophoric first-person-singular
reference in the narrative context.

The 1PL.EXCL intransitive suffix =as cannot attach to a transitive
verb, so its presence on i-nu=as and
i-ku=as 'we drank (alcohol)' unambiguously proves the intransitive
status of these antipassive derivatives nam2021 62 . The latter form is
attested in a Saru oral narrative (Tamura 1993[1979]: 6, cited via Bugaeva 2021: 219 in Nam
2021: 62) and constitutes the core corpus evidence that the Person-Affix test extends to real
discourse contexts, not only constructed elicitation.

The antipassive can be re-applicativized: the applicative prefix e-
reintroduces a content or instrument object to the now-intransitive stem, raising arity by one
and yielding a transitive again. The result is that e- and
i- cancel each other's arity effects while changing the
type of object expressed:

tokaci wa ek pewre kur monrayke e-i-kasuy | Tokachi from come young man matter APPL-ANTIP-help | A young man who came from Tokachi helped with the work. | e-i-kasuy combines the content applicative e- (+1) with the antipassive i- (−1), netting zero arity change relative to the base kasuy 'help sb'. The applied object monrayke 'matter, work' specifies what the helping is directed at, while the human Patient-person remains suppressed and unindexed. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.3 | SAR | Sunazawa 1983: 160; Handbook ex. 25c

This pattern, where an applicative overlays an antipassive to shift the object type without
restoring the suppressed Patient, is productive and has a direct structural parallel in the
re-applicativization of other arity-decreasing prefixes. The derivational chain through
ruska documented in valency-voice-overview traces the same
logic: the arity decrements and increments compose arithmetically, and each step is attested in
the corpus.

Origin: the three-way reconstruction debate

Three accounts of the historical source of antipassive i- are in active
competition, and none is settled in the sources read directly for this grammar. The dispute
turns on the direction of the relationship between the antipassive prefix and the fourth-person
object clitic i= , and on what the ultimate etymon of both might be.

The traditional view, associated with Kindaichi, Tamura, and Shibatani and reported through
Nam, derives the antipassive from the bound fourth-person pronoun: the clitic was
grammaticalized into a derivational voice prefix as its object-demoting function became
conventionalized nam2021 64 ‹contested›.

Bugaeva reverses the direction. In her 2021 analysis the antipassive i-
arose from object-incorporation of a generic noun 'thing / place / time', in the same way that
the body-part prefixes he- 'head' and ho-
'bottom' arose from incorporated body-part nouns. Once the incorporated generic noun
grammaticalized into a Patient-demoting voice morpheme, the antipassive form — superficially
resembling the pronoun — extended to unspecified speech-act participants, and this extension
gave rise to the clitic i= . The antipassive thus preceded the pronoun
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.3 ; bugaeva2021antip 235–239
‹contested›.

Nam's 2021 reconstruction agrees with Bugaeva that the antipassive is older than the pronoun
but proposes a different etymon. Drawing on the parallel between the antipassive prefix, the
clause-final nominalizer -i (< * ihi ), and
the possessed suffix -hV / -VhV , he reconstructs
the source as Proto-Ainu * hi / * ihi 'thing,
place, time'. The initial consonant was lost through the accumulated phonological effect of a
long incorporation history: incorporated noun-stems that are word-initial rarely follow a
vowel, which would have preserved the h, and word-initial * h was
subsequently lost in that position. Cross-linguistic parallels include Winnebago
wa- and Bantawa kha , both generic-noun-origin
antipassive markers nam2021 65–74 ‹contested›.

A single fossilized form bears directly on all three positions. The transitive verb
ikka steal, take by stealth has been analyzed as
i-kV-ka (ANTIP-steal-CAUS), a frozen antipassive re-transitivized by
the direct causative suffix -ka , synchronically opaque but historically
preserving the derivational layer. This analysis is attributed to Bugaeva (2010) by nukopoli in
the aynu-corpora Discord community (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026-05-19) aynucorporadiscord ; bugaeva2010 . Since Bugaeva (2010) is not read directly for this grammar, and
since the competing segmentation ik-ka vs.
i-kV-ka cannot be decided from the surface form alone, the fossil
analysis is graded ‹contested›. Vovin's competing reconstruction of Proto-Ainu * ihka for the same root implies no antipassive layer (reported via the same Discord discussion).
If the Bugaeva/community segmentation is correct, ikka parallels the
lexicalized antipassives inkar and ipe at a
deeper historical stratum where the causative has since merged with the root ‹speculative›.

The width of the antipassive's semantic scope — extending to non-human objects and to ritual
'thing/place' readings ( i-omante , i-mak
'behind') — fits the generic-noun hypothesis better than the pronoun-first account, since a
grammaticalized human pronoun would be expected to bias the antipassive toward human Patients
rather than generating the observed range ‹original-needs-review›. This synthesis is tied to
the factbase evidence for the residual distribution and Nam's phonological argument and requires
editorial review; it goes beyond any single cited source.

Relation to the middle ci- and the A/P split in arity-decreasing voices

The middle prefix ci- , treated in full in
middle-anticausative-passive , likewise reduces arity by one but from the affected
side: it presents an event as experienced by the Patient rather than suppressing the Patient
from the frame. The paired minimal set in Nakagawa is:
a=cari 'I scatter (something)' (transitive, Patient as object, agent
indefinite) versus ci-cari 'it is scattered' (middle, affected noun as
sole subject, agent absent) nakagawa2024 190–191 . Both operators are arity −1,
but i- foregrounds the Agent as S while leaving the Patient unindexed,
while ci- foregrounds the Patient as S while leaving the Agent
unindexed. The two are therefore the A-perspective and P-perspective members of a single arity
layer, and the Handbook glosses the latter object-resultative ( RES )
where this chapter's analysis uses ANTIP
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 .

A community-proposed analysis identifies a productive series of middle-voice derivatives in
which ci- takes a transitive or causative base and produces an
intransitive middle/anticausative: cikarkarpe 'made thing',
copirasa 'spread (intr)', ceosmare 'rush forward'
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2023-12-08) aynucorporadiscord . This extends the
picture in bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 and is graded ‹contested› pending
systematic corpus verification. The ci- / i- split
documented here aligns with the reflexive split treated in reflexive-yay and
reflexive-possessive-si : multiple arity-decreasing operations target different
argument roles, and the arity calculus in valency-voice-overview provides the
unifying framework.

title: The e- Applicative
summary: The e- applicative promoting content/theme, instrument, reason and 'about/concerning' obliques to object.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The e- Applicative
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The e- applicative promoting content/theme, instrument, reason and 'about/concerning' obliques to object.

Valency effect and position

The prefix e- belongs to the applicative-system-overview applicative series together with applicative-ko ko- applicative ko- and
applicative-o o- applicative o- . An overview of all three
and their place in the valency architecture is given in valency-voice-overview .
Its function is to add one participant to the verb's argument set: an intransitive
becomes transitive and a transitive becomes a double-object verb, consistently raising
arity by one regardless of the semantic role of the added argument nakagawa2024 192–194 ; bugaeva2006 186–188 . The valency-class diagnostic of
transitivity-and-valence-classes confirms the change: after
e- attaches to an intransitive, the verb takes the prefixal agent
indexes ci= and a= and resists the
intransitive S suffixes nakagawa2024 137, 140 .

Positionally, e- occupies the slot between the outer person prefix
and the root: person — applicative — root — forming-suffix — causative-suffix —
suffixal-person nakagawa2024 210–212 . The prefix and root together
form a single accentual unit; Nakagawa analyses the applicative-plus-verb sequence as one
word for prosodic purposes, a pattern the community archive documents with forms such as
kotán → ekótanne (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord 2024) aynucorporadiscord ; nakagawa2024 192–194
‹consensus›. Among the three applicatives, ko- is the most
frequent, followed by e- and then o- ;
by Bugaeva's estimate, applicative-derived verbs account for roughly one-third of all
Ainu verbs and up to seven percent of the total lexicon bugaeva2006 188 .

The instrument sub-type (see §2 below) offers the most direct illustration of the +1
arity effect. The verb tuye cut is transitive in its base form and takes
one object (the thing cut); e-tuye raises it to a three-place verb
whose new object position is occupied by the cutting instrument.

sirkorkamuy emus e-tuye | standing.tree sword APPL-cut | He cut the standing tree with a sword. | The instrument emus 'sword' is the applied object licensed by e-, which raises tuye from a two-place to a three-place verb. Both objects — tree and sword — are core arguments marked by person affixes in a three-person frame. | nakagawa2024:194 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 173, after Monbetsu-chō 1969: 237

Sub-types and semantic roles of the applied object

Nakagawa distinguishes four sub-types of e- by the paraphrase
properties of the applied-object slot nakagawa2024 195 :

sub-type
adpositional paraphrase
semantic reading

(1) instrument
ani / ari 'by means of'
'〜で以て' (instrument, means)

(2) purpose
kusu 'for, in order to'
'〜するために' (purpose, goal)

(3) content / theme
— (no adpositional equivalent)
'〜について / 〜を / 〜が' (content, theme-patient)

(4) locative

ta / un /
peka 'at, to, along'

'〜〈場所〉に/で/を' (location, path)

Bugaeva's corpus study of the Chitose and Saru dialects supplies percentage figures across
all attested applied-object roles bugaeva2006 190 . The intr/tr column
records what proportion of each role class is derived from an intransitive versus a
transitive base.

role of the applied object
%
intr-base / tr-base

Content
39
91 / 9

Theme-Patient
17
96 / 4

Means-Instrument ( ani )
13
84 / 16

Cause
8
83 / 17

Location ( ta )
5
75 / 25

Range
4
72 / 28

Manner ( kor )
3
100 / 0

Purpose ( kusu )
2
100 / 0

Path ( peka )
2
100 / 0

Benefactive / Comitative / Malefactive
7
80 / 20

The instrument sub-type (type 1, 13%) has a clear ani -paraphrase.
A straightforward instance is a general capability statement using the motion verb
apkas walk :

a=kemaha a=e-apkas easkay | 4.POSS=foot 4.A=APPL-walk can | One can walk on one's own feet. | The fourth-person prefix a= marks both the possessed noun (a=kemaha 'one's foot/leg') and the agent of the derived transitive (a=e-apkas 'one walks by means of'). The foot is the instrument applied object, paraphrasable as kemaha ani apkas. | nakagawa2024:195 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 176

The purpose sub-type (type 2, 2%) takes a goal or activity noun that can be replaced by
a kusu -phrase:

turepta e-arpa | lily.bulb APPL-go | go to dig lily-bulbs | Verbal phrase. The applied object turepta encodes the purpose of the motion, paraphrasable as turepta kusu arpa 'go for the purpose of digging lily-bulbs'. Both Manner and Path roles (each 2%) derive exclusively from intransitive bases. | nakagawa2024:195 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 180

Locative applied objects (type 4, combining Location 5% and Path 2%) take a place or
postpositional noun, paraphrasable with ta ,
un , or peka :

to teksama e-an | lake edge APPL-be | to be at the lake's edge | The place noun to teksama 'lake edge' is the locative applied object; paraphrasable as to teksama ta an 'be at the lake's edge'. The base an 'be, exist' is intransitive; e- makes it transitive with the location as its sole object. | nakagawa2024:195 | CHI

Path applied objects carry a directional relation along a route rather than a static
location. The example below has a postpositional noun bearing the first-person object
prefix as the applied object:

i=corpokke e=e-hoyupu yak pirka na | 1SG.O=under 2SG.A=APPL-run COND good FIN | You had better run under me. | The postpositional noun corpokke 'under' carries the 1SG object prefix i=, yielding i=corpokke 'under me' as the path applied object. The outer second-person agent prefix e= precedes the applicative e- and the root hoyupu 'run' in the single verb word e=e-hoyupu. | nakagawa2024:197 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 184

Content and Theme-Patient: the dominant and non-oblique uses

Content (39%) and Theme-Patient (17%) together constitute more than half of all attested
e- forms and derive almost entirely from intransitive bases
(91% and 96% respectively). These roles correspond to Nakagawa's type (3): the applied
object has no adpositional paraphrase, and e- here functions as
direct transitivization rather than oblique promotion bugaeva2006 191 ;
nakagawa2024 195–196 . The verb simpay worry is
intransitive in its base form; the thing worried about enters as a Content applied object:

nep e-simpay | what APPL-worry | worry about something | The applied object nep 'what/something' is the content of the worry. No adpositional paraphrase exists; e- is the sole grammatical strategy for licensing this object. | nakagawa2024:196 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 181

The derivational chain ikka steal (intransitive base) →
e-ikka 'steal something' →
ko-e-ikka 'steal something from someone' illustrates successive
applicativization: each step adds one argument bugaeva2006 191 . The
e-ikka step adds the stolen item as a Theme-Patient applied object
with no adpositional equivalent.

Bugaeva draws a typological inference from these data: most Ainu e-
applicatives are the only grammatical means of expressing a given participant relation, so
they are obligatory in the sense that the cross-linguistic option of an adpositional
alternative simply does not exist bugaeva2006 187, 191–196 . The Peterson
typology of applicative constructions provides the comparative frame for this
characterization peterson2007 .

A periphrastic pattern complementary to the morphological applicative exists for the
instrumental reading: the construction ani X p ('the thing with
which one does X') expresses instrumentality as a nominalization rather than through the
e- prefix — for example, ani e=kanpinuye p
'a thing with which one writes' (= pencil) or ani humi a=aste p
'a thing with which one produces sound' (= musical instrument; nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord 2026) aynucorporadiscord ‹corpus-confirmed›. The two constructions
serve different functions: the morphological applicative frames the instrument inside
the verbal predicate; the ani ... p nominalization names the
instrument as a noun.

Lexicalized forms and the preverb question

Three homophonous prefixes occupy the same surface slot and require the arity computation
for disambiguation: the applicative e- (+1), the affiliative
e- 'head of' (0), which fills a body-part position while
retaining its own possessor, and the second-person agent clitic e=
(a person index, effective −1 on the available slots) nakagawa2024 192–193 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.3 . The minimal contrast between the
applicative and the affiliative — he-puni 'lift one's own head'
versus e-puni 'lift someone's head' — is set out in
applicative-system-overview .

Many verbs carry a lexicalized e- whose applicative analysis is
opaque in current usage. A community-proposed decomposition treats eykaun surpass, exceed as applicative e- applied to kaun
derived from the postpositional phrase ka un 'be on top of'
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024) aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›. The
etymology is semantically motivated but cannot be verified against the diachronic record
from the read sources; synchronically, eykaun functions as a
transitive base.

The analytical question of whether some e- forms are better
described as lexical preverbs (case-relation prefixes) than as applicatives in the
cross-linguistic sense is not engaged by the principal read sources. Bugaeva treats all
three markers as applicatives throughout and positions the older labels — 充当相
(Kindaichi and Chiri 1936) and case-prefix 格の関係を表わす接頭辞 (Chiri 1942) — as
historical precursors rather than competing analyses bugaeva2006 186 ;
nakagawa2024 192–193 . Baker's incorporation analysis, which treats
applicative formation as P-incorporation in the syntax, is the nearest theoretical
alternative present in the read literature baker1988 ; shibatani1990 65 ‹contested›. A dedicated assessment appears in applicative-system-overview .

Stacking, re-applicativization, and the feeding of noun incorporation

e- stacks freely with ko- in both orders,
ko-e- and e-ko- , with no documented
meaning difference between the two sequences on the same verb type. In
e-ko-isoytak tell stories to someone about something ,
e- contributes the Content applied object (the stories told) and
ko- the Addressee (the person addressed) bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 ; bugaeva2006 188 . The ordering constraints on stacked applicatives
and their interaction with reflexive and reciprocal morphology are developed in
applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding .

e- may apply to a verb that has already been
antipassive-detransitive-i antipassivized with i- antipassive , restoring transitivity while the antipassive's absorbed participant remains
unexpressed. The compound e-i-kasuy help people with something illustrates
this: i-kasuy 'do helping' is intransitive (the beneficiaries
absorbed by i- ), and e-
re-applicativizes it to take a Content object encoding the nature of the help.

tokaci wa ek pewre kur monrayke e-i-kasuy | Tokachi from come young man work APPL-ANTIP-help | A young man who came from Tokachi helped with the work. | The antipassive i- absorbs the unspecified beneficiaries of kasuy 'help'; e- then re-applicativizes the resulting intransitive, taking monrayke 'work' as the Content applied object. The relative clause tokaci wa ek pewre kur 'young man who came from Tokachi' modifies the subject. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.3 | SAR | Sunazawa 1983: 160; Handbook ex. 25c

The +1 arity effect of e- also opens the path to oblique noun
incorporation. Instruments, locations, and purpose arguments cannot incorporate directly,
because they are adjuncts, not core arguments; once e- promotes
them to object status, the derived object becomes incorporable. Shibatani formulates the
dependency as a general condition: oblique incorporation in Ainu is invariably mediated
by a prior applicative shibatani1990 62, 66 ; nakagawa2024 484 . The incorporated forms at-e-uk 'catch with a net' (instrument;
cf. at ani uk ) and turepta-e-arpa 'go to
dig lily-bulbs' (purpose) both rest on the same e-
applicativization illustrated above. Kaiser's analysis of the applicative-incorporation
interaction provides a formal account of the feeding relation kaiser1998 . The incorporation patterns that result are treated in
noun-incorporation-object and
noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate .

title: The ko- Applicative
summary: The ko- applicative promoting goal/recipient, comitative and adversative ('to/with/against') obliques to object.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The ko- Applicative
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The ko- applicative promoting goal/recipient, comitative and adversative ('to/with/against') obliques to object.

Overview

The prefix ko- applicative: toward / together-with adds a participant —
typically a goal, recipient, addressee, or comitative partner — to the argument frame of the
verb as a new core object, raising arity by one
nakagawa2024 197–198 ; bugaeva2006 186, 188 . On a one-place
intransitive base the derived verb is transitive; on a two-place transitive the result is a
three-place double-object verb. The applied object enters the object slot and may be
cross-referenced by the object-series person prefix — the operative arity diagnostic
described in valency-voice-overview the valency overview and grounded in
applicative-system-overview the applicative system .

Among the three applicative prefixes of Hokkaido Ainu,
ko- is the most frequent. Bugaeva's Saru and Chitose corpus
places the order ko- > e- >
o- by token frequency; taken together, applicative verbs account
for roughly one-third of all verbs and up to seven percent of the entire Ainu lexicon
bugaeva2006 188 . Nakagawa characterizes the applied object as 〜に向かって
(directed toward) or 〜とともに (together with), two senses that correspond to the
Goal / Addressee cluster and the Comitative cluster in Bugaeva's role classification
nakagawa2024 197 .

kanto kotor a=ko-hopuni | sky vicinity 4.A=APPL-fly.up | I flew up toward the sky. | Base hopuni 'fly up' is intransitive; ko-hopuni is transitive with the Goal kanto kotor 'sky-vicinity' as applied object. The fourth-person A prefix a= marks the transitive subject. | nakagawa2024:194 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 174

Semantic roles of the applied object

Bugaeva's systematic role-by-role classification of ko- tokens in
her Saru and Chitose sample yields the distribution in Table 1
bugaeva2006 190 . Each percentage is the share of
ko- tokens in which the applied object bears that role; the intr/tr
column records the proportion derived from an intransitive vs transitive base verb.

Table 1. Semantic roles of the ko- applied object (Bugaeva 2006: 190)

applied-object role
% of tokens
intr base / tr base (%)
adjunct paraphrase

Addressee
20
75 / 25
—

Goal
19
95 / 5
—

Comitative
18
79 / 21
tura

Benefactive
18
50 / 50
—

Malefactive
6
86 / 14
—

Recipient
5
34 / 66
—

Source
5
34 / 66
wa

Cause / Range / Purpose / Location
9
90 / 10
—

The four largest categories — Addressee, Goal, Comitative, and Benefactive — account for
roughly three-quarters of all uses. All four share a common structural denominator: the
applied object is an entity the action is directed toward or performed alongside. For the
Comitative role, where the postposition tura 'together with' offers
a paraphrase, the applicative construction promotes the companion to the object slot and
makes it indexable by the object person-prefix
bugaeva2006 191 . For most other roles no regular oblique paraphrase exists
and the applicative is the only means of encoding the relationship. The Recipient and Source
categories (5% each) stand out for their predominantly transitive base verbs (66%), a
profile consistent with transfer and extraction verbs rather than the one-place motion verbs
that feed the Goal uses (95% intransitive base)
bugaeva2006 190 .

Goal and addressee: relations directed toward a person

With motion verbs, ko- licenses the physical or spatial goal as the
new core object. hopuni fly up (intransitive) yields
ko-hopuni fly up toward (transitive) (example above) and
hosipi return home (intransitive) yields
ko-hosipi return to (a place or entity) (transitive) ; in both cases the
base intransitive takes S-series suffixes while the derived transitive takes A-series
prefixes.

a=kor casi a=ko-hosipi wa | 4.A=have castle 4.A=APPL-return CONJ | I returned to my castle. | The Goal applied object is a=kor casi 'my castle'; base hosipi 'return home' is intransitive, ko-hosipi transitive. | nakagawa2024:198 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 185

With speech and emotion verbs the applied object is an Addressee — the person to whom an
utterance or attitude is directed. The pair
ko-yayirayke be grateful to (someone) versus
e-yayirayke be grateful for (something) makes the semantic split explicit:
ko- takes the human target of the gratitude (Addressee),
e- takes the content — the thing one is thankful for — both with the
same base yayirayke give thanks, be grateful
bugaeva2006 190 ; nakagawa2024 197–198 . The Saru corpus
attests ko-yayirayke with a person-deity as applied object:

petkorkamuy kamuy katkemat a=ko-yay-irayke hi a=ye a | river.deity deity woman 4.A=APPL-REFL-be.grateful NMLZ 4.A=say PFV | saying that one gave thanks to the river goddess | The ko- applied object is petkorkamuy kamuy katkemat 'the river-deity divine woman'; yay-irayke 'show gratitude (reflexive of irayke)' is the base from which ko- derives the Addressee-licensed transitive. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 9; ILCAA materials aa-irc/009#289

Comitative and benefactive uses

When the applied object is a comitative participant — one who acts jointly with the subject
— ko- corresponds to the postpositional adjunct
tura 'together with'. The applicative construction promotes the
companion to an indexable object position, which the adjunct cannot do. This function
interacts productively with noun incorporation: when the comitative applied object is
incorporated, it merges with ko- inside the verb word, and the verb
returns to intransitive arity. Nakagawa gives a Chitose example in which two comitative
participants are incorporated in parallel constructions:

a=mun-ko-tata a=toy-ko-tata ayne | 4.A=garbage-APPL-chop 4.A=earth-APPL-chop then | I chopped it up together with garbage, then together with earth, and … | The comitative applied objects mun 'garbage' and toy 'earth' are incorporated between the person prefix and ko-; each incorporated form is transitive (4.A prefix a=), confirming the arity increase from the intransitive base tata. | nakagawa2024:198 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 190

Benefactive uses (18%) cover contexts in which the applied object's interests are served
by the action, with an approximately even split across intransitive and transitive base
verbs. At their boundary the Malefactive category (6%) shades into adversative uses: the
applied object is the entity against whose interest the action runs, most commonly derived
from intransitive bases (86%), consistent with the pattern of adding a new human object to
a one-place activity verb
bugaeva2006 190 .

The ko-/o- contrast

Both ko- and o- applicative: into / toward a place
can encode a directional Goal, and the primary distributional division concerns the
applied object's reference type: o- takes place nouns,
ko- takes persons, animals, and more abstract directional targets
nakagawa2024 198–199 . Nakagawa provides a near-minimal pair with the
same intransitive base ek come (singular) :

tumi o-ek | war APPL-come.SG | War came to my village. | o- applied object is a place noun (the village, contextually understood); o-ek is transitive. | nakagawa2024:199 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 192

tumi ko-ek | war APPL-come.SG | War came against my sister. | ko- applied object is a person (the sister, contextually understood); ko-ek is transitive. | nakagawa2024:199 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 193

The o- applicative is restricted to place-denoting applied objects;
ko- extends to persons and, as the opening
kohopuni example shows, to spatial targets that function as
directional goals without being named place nouns in the strict sense
( kanto kotor 'sky-vicinity'). The
o- applicative is considerably rarer across Hokkaido dialects and
is largely confined to verse register nakagawa2024 199 ; its distribution
and relationship to the spatial noun o(r)- are treated in
applicative-o .

ko- in the derivational chain and with e-

Because ko- raises arity by one, it feeds and is fed by other
valency operations in a regular derivational calculus. The chain built on
ruska be angry about something (transitive) illustrates eight consecutive
derivations: ruska (vt, +2) →
ko-ruska (vd, +3; adds a person-target of anger) →
yay-ko-ruska (vt, +2; reflexive fills the object slot) →
i-ruska (vi, +1; antipassive) →
ko-i-ruska (vt, +2;
ko- re-applicativizes the antipassivized verb) →
si-ko-i-ruska (vi, +1) →
i-ruska-re (vt) →
ko-i-ruska-re (vd)
sato2023b 40 (citing bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 ).
The step i-ruska → ko-i-ruska is
particularly revealing: ko- restores a goal participant that the
antipassive i- had absorbed, returning the verb to transitive; the
A-series prefix confirms the derived transitivity:

ku=ko-i-ruska | 1SG.A=APPL-ANTIP-be.angry | I am angry at (someone). | ko- applicativizes i-ruska 'be angry (intransitive after antipassive)', adding a person-goal as the new object; 1SG.A prefix ku= confirms transitivity. See also the antipassive chapter.

ko- also stacks with the applicative-e e- applicative .
When both prefixes appear on the same verb, e- adds its Content or
Instrument applied object and ko- adds its Addressee or Recipient
applied object; the orders ko-e- and
e-ko- are both attested with no reported semantic difference
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 ; bugaeva2006 188 . The speech
verb isoytak narrate, tell stories is the standard illustration:
e-ko-isoytak is a three-place frame in which
e- encodes the topic narrated and
ko- the listener:

ku=e-ko-isoytak | 1SG.A=APPL-APPL-narrate | I narrate to (someone) about (something). | e- adds the content/topic (first applied object), ko- adds the addressee/listener (second applied object); with the 1SG.A prefix ku= and both applied objects as free NPs, the verb is three-place. | bugaeva2006:188

A sequential applicativisation of the same type underlies
ko-e-ikka steal something from someone : base
ikka 'steal (vi)' → e-ikka 'steal
something (vt, e- adds the stolen item)' →
ko-e-ikka 'steal something from someone (vd,
ko- adds the Source)'
bugaeva2006 191 . Here the
ko- applied object bears the Source role (the person stolen from),
one of the Table 1 categories in which the transitive base (64%) predominates, as this
chain confirms.

The learner grammar of ijas2023 includes
ekoyayirayke among its ditransitive examples (Lesson 3). The form
parses as e-ko-yay-irayke : e- adds the
thing one is grateful for, ko- adds the person being thanked, and
yay- is the direct-reflexive prefix on the base
irayke 'thank'. Ijäs's VD label captures the three-place output
but does not expose the derivational layering; her example sentences are constructed and
the morphological decomposition is spelled out here following
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . For the
i- -containing members of the chain, see
antipassive-detransitive-i .

Lexicalization and less transparent ko- forms

Many verb stems containing ko- have undergone lexicalization, with
internal structure that ranges from fully transparent to opaque. The form
kosirepa arrive at, reach has been proposed as
ko- + sirepa , where
sirepa itself decomposes as sir-epa
(environment + e- applicative + root pa
'go'), giving an original sense of directed motion toward one's surroundings
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-14) ‹contested›.
The Chitose compound kotan-kosirepa=an 'one arrives at the village'
incorporates kotan 'village' as the
ko- applied object; the fourth-person S-series suffix
=an proves that the incorporation has reduced arity back to one
place. The phrasal counterpart kotan a=kosirepa keeps
kotan as a separate transitive object under the A-series prefix
a= , and together the two forms supply the person-affix word-hood
diagnostic aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023-12-14)
‹contested›.

A community-proposed etymology identifies
kopan dislike, find distasteful as
ko- + pan , with
pan a deverbal form of pa 'mouth'; the
semantic drift toward 'distasteful, taboo' is then tied to the later
ko-kopan and the related sir-pan
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024-11-11) ‹speculative›.
The synchronic meaning of kopan 'dislike' does not transparently
reflect the proposed compositional structure, and the analysis remains unverified against
the primary lexicographic literature.

A grammatically more consequential pattern involves ko- appearing
within a natural-force verb stem. The Chitose form
ku=sir-ko-peker 'I stayed up until dawn' has been parsed as an
