title: Clause Linkage in Ainu: Typology and Inventory
summary: Framing chapter mapping Ainu clause combining onto the coordination–cosubordination–subordination continuum and laying out the full inventory of clause-linking morphemes.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Clause Linkage in Ainu: Typology and Inventory
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: Framing chapter mapping Ainu clause combining onto the coordination–cosubordination–subordination continuum and laying out the full inventory of clause-linking morphemes.

Finite clauses joined by post-predicate particles

Hokkaido Ainu builds complex sentences by chaining fully finite clauses. Each linked predicate
keeps the ordinary verbal morphology of its own clause — person indexing, polarity, plural stem
choice, aspectual auxiliaries, and any clause-final evidential or nominalizer — and the link
between clauses is carried by a separate post-predicate particle such as
wa , hine , akusu ,
kor , kusu , or yakun . There
is no finite/non-finite split in the verb and no converb series: the Ainu verb does not oppose a
sentence-final form to a special linking form, so a Japanese rendering in
〜て gives no warrant for a converb analysis nakagawa2024 514 . The typological profile of the language states the same fact directly — Ainu has no
participles, converbs, or other standard non-finite forms bugaeva2022 . The
connectives are therefore particles that join finite clauses, glossed here as
conj and not as verbal suffixes.

a=yupi-hi i=resu hine oka=an pe ne hike | 4.POSS=elder.brother-POSS 4.O=raise CONJ exist.PL=4.S NMLZ COP CONJ | My elder brother raised me, and we lived on, and … | Each clause around hine is finite: i=resu carries its own fourth-person object index, oka=an its fourth-person S; pe ne closes the chain on a formal-noun predicate before the further connective hike. | nakagawa2024:346 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 634

The particle is looser than a verbal suffix in prosody as well as in structure. Nakagawa,
reporting Tamura, notes that a speaker may close the first clause with full sentence-final
intonation, pause, and only then produce the connective and the clause that follows it; on this
account the connective belongs to neither of the clauses it joins nakagawa2024 514 . Refsing records the same delayed continuation for Shizunai, where a pause may fall between
the verb expression and the conjunctionalizer refsing1986 238 . This autonomy
feeds a genuine disagreement over constituency: Nakagawa declines to posit a clause
( 節 ) unit or a main-versus-subordinate division for Ainu, holding that the
apparatus cannot be carried over from Japanese or English unchanged, while Refsing and the
Handbook describe the same surface domain with terms such as conjunctionalizer, adverbial-clause
marker, and subordinate clause nakagawa2024 510, 514 ; refsing1986 238 ; bugaeva2022 ‹contested›.

Two word-classes do the linking work, and keeping them apart resolves most of the descriptive
clutter. The post-predicate conjunctive particle ( 接続助詞 ) is bound to the
predicate it closes; a clause-initial connective adverb such as orowa(no)
‘and then’ or rapok(ke) ‘meanwhile’ is free and can stack after a
conjunctive particle. Refsing makes this the organizing cut of her chapter, between bound
conjunctionalizers and free conjunctions; Nakagawa keeps only the particles as a
助詞 class and folds the free connectives into the adverb phrase refsing1986 238 ; nakagawa2024 516 .

conjunctive particle ( 接続助詞 )
connective adverb ( 副詞句 )

position
bound, follows the predicate it closes
free, clause-initial

can begin a sentence
normally no
yes

examples
wa, hine, kor, no, akusu, kusu, yakun, korka, yakka, sekor
orowa(no), ora, rapok(ke)

relation to a host
requires the verb phrase it modifies
needs no host; may stack after a particle

Several of the particles are segmentally identical to material from other classes, and the
grammar resolves the clash by word-class and position rather than by form.
Wa is the sequential linker, the ablative case particle, and a
sentence-final emphatic; kor is a linker and the transitive verb ‘have’;
kusu is a causal/purpose linker, a free ‘therefore’, and part of the
intention auxiliary kusu ne ; no is a linker and
the adverb-forming suffix -no nakagawa2024 340, 353 ; ijas2023 .

Inventory and routing

Nakagawa organizes the Saru-based conjunctive particles into seven semantic groups — temporal
succession, unfolding event, condition, adversative, concession, cause/purpose, and
conjecture/manner — together with a parallel-listing use nakagawa2024 337–356 .
Refsing's Shizunai grammar reaches a near-identical seven-way taxonomy, with local realignments:
Shizunai uses kane for the simultaneity that Saru marks with
kor , and konno for an inevitable-consequence
conditional where Saru again uses kor refsing1986 238–250 .
The detailed treatment of each domain belongs to the chapters routed below; this chapter sets out
the system and the boundaries between them.

domain
main Hokkaido linkers
core function
detail chapter

sequential and integrated chaining
wa , ayne
sequence, manner, result-state, cause; protracted action reaching a result
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining

neutral chaining and discovery
hine , akusu , awa
ordered events; ‘did X and then found Y’
hine-akusu-switch-reference

simultaneity
kor , dialectal kane
overlapping events, the progressive, the habitual conditional
simultaneous-kor-clauses

condition, concession, adversative
yakun , yak , ciki ,
yakka , korka
hypothetical and factual premises, permission, counter-expectation
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses

cause and purpose
kusu , wa kusu ,
hi kusu
reason, cause, aim; future and intention in kusu ne
causal-and-purpose-clauses

manner, state, degree, inference
no , pekor , noyne ,
kotom
state, negated manner, simile, evidential inference, degree
manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses

reported speech and naming
sekor , dialectal ari / ani
direct quotation, sound and name frames; indirect complement markers
quotative-complementation-sekor

narrative cohesion
orowa , connective p / hike , tail-head repetition
recapitulation, new episode, discourse continuity
tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion

Whole-corpus word-form counts confirm that these items carry the descriptive load, with the
homonym caveat kept in view. In the aligned Hokkaido corpus wa (≈50,600),
kor (≈29,300), and kusu (≈16,000) are among the
commonest words, but those totals fold in the ablative and final-particle
wa , the verb kor ‘have’, and the future
kusu ne , and so overstate the connective. The cleaner items, where few
homonyms intrude, still show the shape of the system ‹corpus-suggested›:

particle
corpus count
primary function

hine 14,368 neutral clause-chaining

yakka 7,536 concession ‘even if’

sekor 6,991 quotative

yakun 4,516 hypothetical conditional

akusu 3,861 sequential discovery

korka 3,700 adversative

hike 3,159 loose link / adversative

awa 2,071 sequential discovery (verse)

The coordination–subordination continuum

The inventory spans a continuum rather than a clean coordinate/subordinate dichotomy. At one
edge, hine enumerates events in temporal order and takes no stance on how
they are related; at the other, the complementizing uses of sekor ,
kuni , kunak , yak , and
kusu place an entire clause under a verb of speech, thought, or command
nakagawa2024 338, 470–479, 507–513 . Most linkage falls between the poles: the
medial clause stays finite and keeps its own argument indexes while the chain is read as one
larger episode, the configuration that the coordination–subordination typology of foleyvanvalin1984 treats as cosubordination.

micipa ku=kar hine ohaw or k=omare | honewort 1SG.A=gather CONJ soup place 1SG.A=put.in | I gathered honewort and put it into the soup. | A plain hine sequence: two finite predicates, each with first-person A indexing, listed in order with no claim of dependence. | sato2008:44 | SAR

Wa binds more tightly. In Tamura's characterization, as transmitted by
Nakagawa, wa gathers two situations into one coherent event; Nakagawa
divides its readings into result-state, manner or method, and cause-and-effect nakagawa2024 339–342 . Satō gives the same three-way description and supplies the diagnostic that separates Ainu
wa from a same-subject converb: the two clauses it joins may have
different subjects, which a Japanese 〜て clause cannot sato2008 45–46 .

hekaci suma osura wa ontaro hokus | child stone throw CONJ barrel fall.over | The child threw a stone, and the barrel fell over. | A different-subject wa chain: the thrower is the subject of the first clause, the barrel of the second. | sato2008:45 | SAR

Akusu and awa sit at a third point: a realized
first event is followed by a discovered, witnessed, or newly unfolding second one, with the
causal connection left open. Nakagawa treats the two as semantically equivalent and conditioned
by register and dialect — Saru prose favours akusu , Saru verse and
several other varieties favour awa nakagawa2024 345 . Satō
describes the domain as factual condition and ties awa to oral literature
sato2008 47–48 .

kanpi ku=sanke ru-we ne akusu itasa kanpi ek ru-we ne | letter 1SG.A=send.out track-POSS COP CONJ in.return letter come track-POSS COP | I sent a letter, and a reply came. | Akusu attaches after a ruwe ne evidential tail: the first clause is already closed as an established fact before the discovery linker is added. | sato2008:47; sato2009:52 | CHI

Reference tracking without switch-reference morphology

Hokkaido Ainu tracks participants across a chain through the obligatory person indexes on each
finite verb and through discourse expectation; it has no same-subject/different-subject converb
morphology bugaeva2022 . The connectives carry only statistical tendencies. Refsing
finds that the predicates joined by wa share a subject in about ninety
percent of her Shizunai material, yet Satō shows the preference is not a constraint, since
wa tolerates different subjects refsing1986 240 ; sato2008 46 . The result is a same-subject-preferring chainer with no dedicated morphology.

The no / wa choice is derived from the first
predicate rather than from coreference. Nakagawa and Satō tie no to the
aspect and polarity of that predicate: stative predicates and negated action predicates take
no , with the high-frequency reading ‘without doing X’ under negation nakagawa2024 340–341 ; sato2008 45–47 . Ijäs offers the learner's heuristic that
no marks a same-subject negated clause and wa a
different-subject one; that framing is hers, and the primary grammars condition the choice on
polarity and aspect, not on subject identity ‹contested› ijas2023 .

ku=caranke ka somo ki no ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=complain even NEG do CONJ 1SG.S=return | I went home without complaining. | No follows a negated action predicate; both clauses share the first-person subject, so the choice of no over wa here tracks polarity, not a change of subject. | sato2008:44 | SAR

The nearest approach to a switch-reference device is discourse-structural. The Handbook's
descriptive grammar reports that the sequence of a perfective a auxiliary
and a connective such as korka , wa , or connective
p tends to fall where the scene, topic, or actor of a narrative changes,
the perfective marking a situation as completed and cut off from the present bugaeva2022 ‹speculative›. The exact author and page of that passage have not been confirmed against the
print edition, and the device is a clause-chaining boundary marker, not grammaticalized
switch-reference. Whether the sequential connectives hine and
akusu encode subject continuity in a switch-reference-like opposition or
instead encode discourse (un)expectedness is the central open question of the domain ‹contested›;
it is adjudicated against a corpus count of subject continuity in hine-akusu-switch-reference , and the reference-tracking toolkit as a whole is drawn together in reference-tracking .

Condition, concession, cause, and the mood of the matrix clause

The conditional system is organized by realis status and, distinctively, by the sentence type of
the matrix clause. Satō's dedicated study finds that Ainu conditionals are mood-constrained but
do not privilege the epistemic mood the way Japanese conditionals do: the language has no
conditional restricted to truth-judgement, yet it has conditionals that require an imperative or
interrogative matrix sato2009 53–54 . Yakun is the broadest
member, attested with declarative, interrogative, imperative, and volitional or conjectural
matrices, and so it bears most of the translator's load across Japanese
レバ , タラ , and ナラ sato2009 51–53 ; nakagawa2024 347 . Yak attaches to law-like or warning
premises and clusters with an evaluative pirka ‘good’ or
wen ‘bad’; ciki and conditional
kor are the mood-bound members, ciki introducing a
premise for a command or question and kor the general-causation
conditional that elsewhere requires a wh-interrogative matrix sato2009 50–51 ;
nakagawa2024 349–351 .

e=ype rusuy haw-e ne yakun tan kaboca e | 2SG.S=eat.a.meal DES voice-POSS COP CONJ this pumpkin eat | If you are saying that you are hungry, eat this pumpkin. | The protasis closes on the reportative tail hawe ne before yakun; the matrix is an imperative, one of the several moods yakun freely admits. | sato2009:52 | CHI | Satō ex. 28

Concession and counter-expectation sit alongside the conditionals. Yakka
‘even if, even though’ states that the matrix holds whatever the event of the marked clause, and
in the frames yakka pirka / yakka wen it grants or
withholds permission; korka ‘but’, transparently kor when
plus ka even , marks counter-expectation nakagawa2024 351–352 ;
sato2008 53 . Ijäs banks the same set for the learner, and her listing
construction illustrates the concessive cleanly:

apto as yakka upas as yakka wakka poro yakka sonno k=ek kusne wa | rain fall CONJ snow fall CONJ water be.big CONJ truly 1SG.S=come intend FIN | Even if it rains, even if it snows, even if there is a flood, I will truly come. | A teaching example built on a triple yakka listing; constructed, not corpus-attested. The future kusu ne appears here in its reduced one-word form kusne. | ijas2023

Cause and purpose are centred on kusu . Plain
kusu gives the purpose of the following event, especially with motion
verbs, or its reason; wa kusu and hi kusu are
reason-only forms that present the first event as actual nakagawa2024 352–353 ;
sato2008 53–54 ; refsing1986 251–252 . The same segmental material
enters the intention auxiliary kusu ne , the copula taking no person index,
so the detail chapter separates clause-linking kusu from future and
intention syntax causal-and-purpose-clauses .

sisam itak ani ku=respa p ne kusu aynu itak sinep ka erampewtek pa | Japanese language INS 1SG.A=raise NMLZ COP CONJ Ainu language one even not.understand PL | Because I raised them in Japanese, they do not understand even one word of Ainu. | Kusu marks the preceding formal-noun predicate p ne as the cause of the following clause; the matrix verb erampewtek takes the plural pa. | nakagawa2024:353 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 672

Interfaces with evidentiality, complementation, and reported speech

Conjunctive particles attach outside the clause-final evidential frame. A clause may close on a
nominalizer-plus-copula evidential such as ruwe ne or
hawe ne and then take a linker, giving attested orders
ruwe ne akusu , hawe ne yakun , and
hawe ne kusu ; the evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but below
the connective, so kusu ne ruwe ne is well formed while
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not nakagawa2024 393 . The general
order for a linked clause is thus predicate – evidential – connective. The inferential value is
treated in ruwe-ne-inferential and the system as a whole in evidential-schema-overview . Satō notes one cross-linguistically marked interaction at this seam:
akusu can follow a ruwe ne tail, where the Japanese
factual と / たら rejects a preceding
のだ sato2009 52–53 .

Several connectives are formal nouns pressed into linking service, and the same set runs a
grammaticalization cline. A formal noun can be a relative-clause head that fills an argument gap,
a complement marker that nominalizes a saturated clause, or a connective that is neither subject
nor object of the following clause; Nakagawa distinguishes the three by the argument structure
around the marker, and places connective p , pe ,
and hike ‘but, and’ at the connective end of the cline nakagawa2024 507–516 . This links clause combining to nominalization, treated in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization and nominalization-p-pe .

Sekor is the bridge into reported speech. In Saru and Chitose it frames a
direct quote with a verb of speaking or thinking, names entities in the pattern
X sekor a=ye ‘call it X’, and frames cries and mimetic sound, its history
transparent in se make a sound, say ‘say’ plus kor while ‘while’
nakagawa2024 470 ; ijas2023 . It is also the one connective that
triggers person reassignment: a third party's quoted first person is expressed by fourth-person
forms while the narrator's own quoted speech keeps the first person, the mechanism behind the
fourth-person voice of oral narrative, which Bugaeva reanalyses as logophoricity nakagawa2024 470–471 ; bugaeva2008 40–44 . The interaction belongs with person-marking-architecture and is developed in quotative-complementation-sekor .

tap a=ye katu Piratur sekor a=ye haw-e ne | now 4.A=say way Piratur QUOT 4.A=say voice-POSS COP | The way it is said now is to call it Piratur. | Sekor frames the quoted name; the fourth-person a=ye and the reportative tail hawe ne close the naming frame. | nakagawa2024:392 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 778, after Kawamura 1985: 30

Dialect and comparative edges

The inventory is dialectally uneven, with Saru and Chitose the best-described baseline in
Nakagawa and Satō and Shizunai a full reference grammar in Refsing. Shizunai realigns two cells:
kane marks the simultaneity that Saru assigns to
kor , and konno the conditional/consequential
function refsing1986 248–250 ; nakagawa2024 342, 347–348 .

a=kor katkemat utar an=oskur kane kesto an konno oka=an | 4.A=have lady PL 4.A=cherish CONJ every.day exist CONJ exist.PL=4.S | Cherishing our ladies, we lived on day after day. | Shizunai kane marks the simultaneity Saru would mark with kor, and konno the recurrent-time conditional; both differ from the Saru forms. | nakagawa2024:342 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 617; Shizunai, after Okuda

Tek is the clearest micro-debate. It is not a connective in Saru or
Chitose, where it is an auxiliary, yet in most other dialects it links clauses with an
‘immediately upon X’ sense. Nakagawa's synthesis is geographical: western dialects near Saru
carry the short-duration restriction, while eastern descriptions dispute whether short duration
belongs to the meaning at all, and one analysis makes its core function parallel listing nakagawa2024 343–344 ‹contested›. A related open question is whether the bare verb-plus-verb sequences of Tokachi,
Ishikari, and Shiranuka — where Saru requires an intervening connective — constitute a serial
verb construction nakagawa2024 518–521 ‹contested›. Sakhalin supplies a contrast
at the edge of the system, where sa kusu covers
the continuation and result-state range that Hokkaido splits between
kor and wa , rather than marking cause or purpose
nakagawa2024 344 .

Open points

The foundational descriptive source for the whole domain, Tamura's Saru study of the connective
particles, could be reached only through Nakagawa and Refsing; the classic characterization of
wa as fusing two events into one, and the formulation that the connective
belongs to neither clause, both rest on that intermediated witness ‹speculative›. The Handbook's
claims on negation asymmetry and on the perfective a scene-switch device
await verification against the print edition bugaeva2022 ‹speculative›. The
quantitative base is also thin: the available corpus figures are homonym-mixed word-form counts,
and the per-function rates that would settle how often connective wa
differs from the ablative, or how far hine and
akusu correlate with subject continuity, belong to hine-akusu-switch-reference and tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion ‹corpus-suggested›.

title: Conditional, Temporal, and Concessive Clauses

part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Conditional, Temporal, and Concessive Clauses
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The yak(un)/ciki conditionals, their temporal 'when' uses and realis/irrealis split, and the morphologically related concessives yakka 'even if' and korka 'although'.

The conditional, concessive, and adversative subsystem

Hokkaido Ainu marks hypothetical, temporal, and concessive relationships between clauses
through post-predicate particles attached after the full finite predicate of the antecedent
clause. Each linked predicate is fully finite — it carries its own person indexing, polarity,
aspect, and any clause-final evidential — and the particle follows, belonging to neither of
the two clauses it joins nakagawa2024 514 ; refsing1986 238 .
The principal members of the conditional–concessive subsystem are:
yakun (hypothetical conditional),
yak if, when (habitual or evaluative) and
yakne (evaluative and oral-literary conditionals),
ciki / cik (mood-restricted conditional),
kor in its conditional use (habitual or temporal),
yakka (concessive and permissive), and
korka (adversative).

Two dimensions organize the system: the realis status of the antecedent clause, and the
sentence type the particle requires or permits in the matrix clause. Satō's dedicated study of
Chitose conditional expressions finds that Ainu conditionals impose no restriction tied to
epistemic-judgement mood, yet they do impose restrictions linked to directive and interrogative
illocution — a distributional pattern not predicted by hierarchies of conditionality derived
from Japanese sato2009 53–54 .

These particles are conjunctive particles ( 接続助詞 ) in Nakagawa's
classification: post-predicate, bound forms distinct from the free connective adverbs such as
orowa 'and then' and rapokke 'meanwhile',
which can stack after them nakagawa2024 337, 347–352, 516 . The architecture
of the whole clause-linking inventory is set out in clause-linkage-overview ; cause
and purpose particles are treated in causal-and-purpose-clauses , and the
complementizer uses of kuni , yak , and
kusu in indirect speech belong to
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements .

yakun — the hypothetical conditional

Yakun is the broadest and most frequent of the conditional particles,
attested in Saru and Chitose with declarative, imperative, interrogative, and conjectural
matrix clauses alike; Satō's study finds no mood restriction on it
sato2009 51–53 ; nakagawa2024 347 . Its semantic scope covers
the hypothetical one-off conditional: the protasis states a premise and the matrix presents
its consequence. Japanese distributes comparable functions across three conditional forms
(レバ, タラ, ナラ); Ainu lays them largely on yakun , so a Japanese
rendering must disambiguate by context sato2009 53 .

The etymology is transparent: conditional yak if, when plus the
allative/attributive postposition un to, toward (allative) , a segmentation
reported for Shizunai by Refsing and supported by morpheme decomposition
refsing1986 256 . The particle can also head a sentence independently, with
the anaphoric reading 'in that case, then', a free-standing use that marks its partial
grammaticalization beyond the strictly bound conditional
nakagawa2024 347 ; ijas2023 .

Refsing notes a 'when'-nuance in Shizunai realis contexts, where
yakun attaches after an event already in progress or completed,
yielding something closer to 'when X happened, Y followed'
refsing1986 256 ‹corpus-suggested›.

sirkunne yakun suy ruyanpe an nankor | get.dark CONJ again storm exist probably | If it gets dark, there will probably be another storm. | yakun with a conjectural main clause (nankor 'probably'); Satō uses this Chitose example to illustrate the absence of mood restrictions on yakun. The particle is compatible with nankor here, with an imperative elsewhere (Satō 2009: 52 ex. 28), and with a declarative in general statements. | sato2008:51; sato2009:51 | CHI

The protasis of a yakun conditional may carry its own evidential tail
before the particle, since the verified ordering is [VP] – evidential – connective. The
sequence hawe ne yakun 'if (it is said that) X' presents the protasis
as reported hearsay before conditionalizing it; ruwe ne yakun presents
it as established fact nakagawa2024 393 . The interface with the evidential
system is discussed in the final section of this chapter.

yak and yakne — habitual and evaluative conditionals

Where yakun handles one-off hypotheticals,
yak marks a habitual or law-like premise whose consequence the
speaker evaluates. Its hallmark is a strong tendency to be followed by the evaluative
predicates pirka be good or wen be bad , or by a directive
expression; it carries a warning nuance when the evaluation is negative
nakagawa2024 349 ; refsing1986 256–257 ;
sato2008 50–51 .

The idiomatic frame yak pirka 'it would be good if one did X'
functions as mild advice or prescription ('one should X'); its negative counterpart
yak wen 'it would be bad if one did X' conveys prohibition or warning
('one must not X') nakagawa2024 349 . Both frames can be reinforced by
anak / anakne ,
easir 'only then', or tasi
refsing1986 256–257 . These obligation frames differ from the permissive
frames yakka pirka / yakka wen built on
the concessive particle, treated below.

a=e=siknure yak isitoma=an | 4.A=2SG.O=let.live CONJ be.frightening=4.S | Keeping you alive is frightening. | Nakagawa ex. 651. yak with an evaluative-stative matrix (isitoma 'be frightening / dangerous'); the indefinite 4th-person agent a= covers an unspecified subject ('one'), and =an marks 4th-person S on the matrix predicate. The Japanese rendering 'お前を生かしておくと恐ろしい' confirms the warning-evaluation reading. | nakagawa2024:349 | SAR

In the Ishikari dialect the sequence yak easir (conditional
yak plus easir 'only then') extends the
evaluative conditional into an explicit obligation construction — 'it is only fitting if
one does X' — moving from evaluation toward necessity ‹contested›; the analysis is proposed
in community discussion and awaits corroboration from a primary grammar of Ishikari
(nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord 2026).

Yakne functions similarly to yak but is
associated with prayer and oral-literary register;
yakne pirka is extremely rare and the form appears mainly in the
fixed phrase wa ne yakne (sequential
wa plus copula ne plus conditional
yakne ), concentrated in kamuy yukar and prayer texts
nakagawa2024 349 .

ciki/cik — the mood-restricted conditional

Ciki (Shizunai, Samani, and Tokachi cik )
presents a premise for which the speaker issues a command or asks a question; the particle
consequently requires an imperative or interrogative matrix clause and is ill-formed with a
declarative sato2009 50–51 ; nakagawa2024 350 ;
refsing1986 255 . This places ciki at the opposite
end of the mood-freedom scale from yakun . The stock formula opening
instructions and demonstrations illustrates the imperative use:

itak=an ciki e=nu katu ene an hi | speak=4.S CONJ 2SG.A=hear manner thus exist NMLZ | As I speak, listen in the following manner. | Nakagawa ex. 655. ciki closes the premise clause (itak=an 'one speaks / as I speak'), and the matrix is a nominalized directive — the standard formula for opening a speech, recipe, or demonstration. The imperative force is embedded in the nominalized ene an hi 'the manner in which (you are to) listen'. | nakagawa2024:350 | SAR

Refsing characterizes Shizunai cik as carrying an assumption: the
speaker takes the protasis to be true or imminent and issues a directive or question on
that basis, distinguishing it from the purely hypothetical
yakun refsing1986 255 . In the Horobetsu and
Chitose dialects, ciki overlaps in use with the
sequential-discovery particle akusu — it can mark factual discovery
('did X, and then found Y') — a temporal reading absent from the mood-restricted Saru form
nakagawa2024 350–351 ‹contested›.

The etymology of ciki is not settled by the primary grammars. A
community-proposed segmentation reads the particle as ci (a
continuative or plural auxiliary attested in most dialects outside Saru and Chitose) plus
ki 'do', making the conditional a lexicalized construction; the
primary sources treat the form as an unanalyzed particle ‹contested›
(nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord 2024). A diachronic connection between the
conditional ciki and the homophonous adverb
ciki 'now' — pointing to a grammaticalization path from 'now (if
…)' toward the conditional–admonitive — is advanced in community discussion but likewise
awaits support from primary sources ‹speculative›
(antitwilight, aynucorporadiscord 2024).

Conditional kor — temporal and habitual

The particle kor while; when; have carries two distinct conjunctive uses —
simultaneity ('〜しながら') and a habitual or general-causation conditional ('〜するたびに /
〜すると') — that are sometimes indistinguishable in context
nakagawa2024 340, 347 ; ijas2023 . The simultaneity use is
treated in simultaneous-kor-clauses ; the present section concerns the
conditional reading.

In the conditional use, kor encodes general causation or a
recurrent event: 'whenever X, Y'. This differs from yakun (one-off
hypothetical) in that the protasis describes something that holds repeatedly or as a
general law. Satō finds that conditional kor takes a declarative
matrix in general-causation statements; for a one-off individual event the particle is
restricted to wh-interrogative matrices sato2009 51 . The Shizunai
equivalents are konno / koonno , and those of
Horobetsu, Shiranuka, and Kushiro are ko , all serving the
conditional function that Saru assigns to kor
nakagawa2024 348 ; refsing1986 248–249 .

sironuman kor cikap utar iwak | get.dark CONJ bird ASS go.home | When(ever) it gets dark, the birds go home. | Satō's illustration of the habitual-conditional kor: darkness is a recurrent condition and the birds' return a recurrent response. The associative plural utar after cikap collects the birds as a group. A formal-noun tail pe ne frequently closes the matrix of such clauses to foreground the generalization. | sato2008:49 | SAR

A formal-noun tail pe ne / p ne frequently
closes the matrix of a kor conditional, yielding the reading 'it
is the sort of thing that happens when X' and underlining the habitual or general-truth
character of the pattern nakagawa2024 347 . This is an instance of the
nominalizer p functioning as a connective after a saturated clause;
the formal-noun continuum is treated in clause-linkage-overview .

The realis and mood-constraint split: a comparative table

Satō's study, based on Chitose data with consultant Shirasawa Nabe, produces the following
typological finding: Ainu conditionals are mood-constrained but not in the way Japanese
conditionals are. Japanese conditionals tend to be organized around epistemic-judgement mood;
Ainu conditionals impose no such restriction on yakun , yet they
restrict ciki to imperative or interrogative matrices and restrict
conditional kor to declarative (for general truths) or
wh-interrogative (for individual events) sato2009 53–54 . The table below
summarizes the five particles Satō compares; the factual conditionals
akusu and awa are included for
completeness and are treated in detail in hine-akusu-switch-reference .

particle
type
matrix mood
Japanese ≈
key source

yakun
hypothetical (one-off)
unrestricted — declarative, imperative, interrogative, conjectural

sato2009 51–53

タラ

レバ
sato2009 50–51 ; nakagawa2024 350

kor (conditional)

sato2009 51 ; nakagawa2024 347

sato2009 52 ; nakagawa2024 345

Satō frames the finding against Japanese typology: Japanese has no conditional restricted
to imperative or interrogative matrices, whereas Ainu has two
( ciki and conditional kor ); Japanese has
conditionals restricted to epistemic-judgement mood, while Ainu has none. Whether this
constitutes evidence against a universal conceptual hierarchy of conditionality is an open
question ‹contested› sato2009 53–54 .

yakka — concessive and permissive

Yakka 'even if, even though' is the concessive particle. Corpus
counts place it at roughly 7,500 tokens in the aligned Hokkaido corpus, the highest among
the conditional–concessive particles and a signal of its frequency in narrative and daily
speech alike nakagawa2024 352 . The etymology is transparent: conditional
yak if, when plus scalar ka even, also , literally 'even if',
the same analysis given for Shizunai by Refsing refsing1986 254 . Dialect
variants yakkay (Tokachi, Shiranuka) and
yakkayki (Kushiro verse) carry an emphatic or rhythmic suffix found
also on korkay nakagawa2024 352 .

Nakagawa distinguishes two readings by the temporal orientation of the protasis. With a
future or hypothetical protasis, yakka yields a suppositional
concessive: 'whatever happens with X, Y holds'. With a past protasis the reading shifts
toward adversative: 'though X occurred, Y is/was so'
nakagawa2024 352 . The following example shows both
yakun and yakka in a single utterance:

kironnu=an yakun a=i=rayke yakun a=i=rayke yakka pirka | be.satiated=4.S CONJ 4.A=4.O=kill CONJ 4.A=4.O=kill CONJ be.good | If I am full, even if they kill me — let them; it is fine. | Nakagawa ex. 641. The first two clauses deploy yakun for successive hypothetical premises; the third uses yakka with the evaluative pirka in the concessive-permissive reading. The indefinite 4th-person agent a= covers an unspecified actor; i= marks the 4th-person object. | nakagawa2024:347 | SAR

The idiomatic frames yakka pirka and
yakka wen are the primary permissive and non-permissive
constructions of Hokkaido Ainu. Yakka pirka licenses an action for
the addressee ('you may X'); yakka wen withholds that license
('you may not X') ijas2023 ; refsing1986 254 . These differ
structurally and semantically from the obligation frames yak pirka
/ yak wen , which prescribe or prohibit an action as a habitual norm
rather than granting or denying a right. The permissive and deontic dimensions of the
conditional system connect to the broader modal apparatus in
epistemic-irrealis-modality and to the imperative constructions of
imperative-and-prohibitive .

e=e yakka pirka | 2SG.A=eat CONJ be.good | You may eat it. | Teaching illustration of the permissive yakka pirka frame, constructed after Ijäs L8 ex. 11-5. The Shizunai parallel e=kor yakka pirka 'you may keep it' (Refsing 1986: 254, ex. 448) confirms the construction across dialects. | ijas2023

A distinct use builds a parallel-listing construction from the pattern
[NP] ne yakka [NP] ne yakka … , surveying nominal alternatives under
the concessive and asserting that the main clause holds regardless of which alternative
obtains ('whether X or Y, both X and Y')
nakagawa2024 355–356 .

korka and hikeka — adversative

Korka 'but, however' marks the matrix clause as counter to the
expectation raised by the preceding clause. Corpus frequency of roughly 3,700 tokens
confirms its steady use across oral literature and modern prose
nakagawa2024 351 . The etymology is the same construction-type as
yakka : conditional kor when; while plus scalar
ka even, also — literally 'even when' — pointing to an original conditional
reading that has shifted entirely toward counter-expectation
nakagawa2024 351 ; ijas2023 . The dialect variant
korkay (Samani, Tokachi, Shiranuka, Kushiro) carries the same
emphatic suffix found in yakkay
nakagawa2024 351 .

nay poro p ne korka nay nutap ka hutne uske ne wa an | river big NMLZ COP CONJ river bend even narrow place COP CONJ exist | The river was big, but even the bends had become narrow. | Nakagawa ex. 662. The first clause closes on a formal-noun predicate p ne before korka; the particle spans the expectation set by the size of the river and the counter-result of narrow meanders. The resultative wa an at the end of the matrix presents a state that has come into being. | nakagawa2024:351 | SAR

Hikeka (Ishikari ikeka ; Shiranuka and
Kushiro ikekay ) occupies overlapping semantic space. Some analysts
classify it under concession (譲歩) rather than adversative (逆接), but its distribution in
attested texts is largely interchangeable with korka
nakagawa2024 351–352 ; sato2008 52 ‹contested›.
Etymologically it is the loose-link formal noun
hike thing, fact (linking noun) plus
ka even, also , a formation parallel to the
kor + ka source of
korka .

Interface with the evidential system

The conditional and concessive particles of this chapter attach outside the clause-final
evidential frame. The ordering [finite VP] – evidential nominalizer – connective is
verified: hawe ne yakun 'if (it is reported that) X',
ruwe ne akusu 'and (as established fact) Y was then discovered',
and ruwe ne korka 'though X is the case' are all attested
nakagawa2024 393 . The evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but
below the connective: kusu ne ruwe ne is well-formed while
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not, confirming that the connective scopes over
the evidential nakagawa2024 393 . The evidential paradigm is treated in
ruwe-ne-inferential .

A cross-linguistically notable asymmetry at this seam was identified by Satō: the factual
conditional akusu can follow a ruwe ne
tail — the functional equivalent of Japanese のだ — whereas the Japanese factual conditional
と / たら rejects a preceding
のだ . Ainu thus allows [clause — established fact —
discovery-link], while Japanese does not; this difference argues that the interaction of
evidentiality with clause-linking is organized differently in the two languages
sato2009 52–53 .

The deontic constructions built on the conditional system —
yak pirka / yak wen (obligation and
prohibition) and yakka pirka / yakka wen
(permission and non-permission) — intersect with the modal and epistemic system of
epistemic-irrealis-modality and with the imperative and prohibitive constructions
of imperative-and-prohibitive .

title: Denominal and Deverbal Verb Derivation
summary: Non-valency processes that build verbs from nouns and from other verbs, including verb-verb compounding and noun/verb conversion.
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Denominal and Deverbal Verb Derivation
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: Non-valency processes that build verbs from nouns and from other verbs, including verb-verb compounding and noun/verb conversion.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu derives verbal stems from nominal and verbal sources through mechanisms that must
be distinguished from the valency and voice operations treated in the chapters routed from
valency-voice-overview . Valency operations — applicatives, causatives, antipassive,
reflexive, and reciprocal — alter the verb's external argument count in a principled and largely
productive way. The processes documented below either place a base into an argument class without
changing that count, or build a new stem whose argument class is determined by the source. They
include the combination of bound nominal roots with verbal heads, the copula
ne be, become and the transitive verb kar make, do as routes for
nominal predication, adverbial prefixes, and the deverbal stem modifications that alter lexical
aspect or number without altering argument structure.

The architecture is head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 : a derived stem
must enter one of the four argument-count classes — zero-place ambient predicates, one-place
intransitives, two-place transitives, or three-place verbs with two bare objects — and the
person-index set then diagnoses the class of the result. Nakagawa assigns every formative a
numerical arity value so that the derived form's external argument capacity is computable from
the visible morphology nakagawa2024 136–139 . Satō accepts the same
head-marking premise but separates three simultaneously active valency dimensions: an invariant
argument-slot count, macrorole transitivity, and the morphological class of the derived form;
the copula ne and three-place verbs motivate the split sato2023b 42–61 .
The two accounts are presented together where they produce the same classification and
distinguished where they diverge ‹contested›.

formative or pattern
arity value (Nakagawa)
verb-forming role
chapter route

−1

§ below; noun-incorporation-object for productive object incorporation

−1

0
add body-part or positional material without changing the slot count

§ below; copula-ne-predicate-nominals

+2 (lexical transitive)

§ below; light-and-pro-verbs

0

+1

applicative-system-overview

causative-morphological-re-e-te

suppletive-verbal-number

Denominal ambient predicates

The most clearly documented denominal verbs in the Hokkaido evidence are ambient zero-place
predicates built from bound nominal roots. Nakagawa classifies
sirpirka be fine weather (< sir surroundings, weather +
pirka be good ), mean be cold (weather) (< me-
'coldness root' + an exist ), hawas it is said (<
haw voice + as stand ), sirkunne get dark , and
sitcuk become autumn as zero-place verbs: they take neither a subject nor an
object, and therefore no person index appears on them nakagawa2024 128–130 .
Shibatani describes the same ambient and weather predicates as a form of subject incorporation,
with the nominal element supplying the domain that an ordinary intransitive would predicate of
an external subject shibatani1990 33 . The fuller treatment of the incorporative
analysis is in noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding .

The autonomy of the source noun determines whether the string constitutes a single word or a
two-word sequence. The roots sir- and me- cannot
stand as ordinary nouns in the relevant predicates — *sir anak pirka and
*me ka an are ungrammatical — so sirpirka and
mean are single zero-place verbs. The noun apto rain ,
by contrast, is free, and Nakagawa leaves apto as 'it rains' undecided
between a zero-place compound and a two-word noun-plus-verb clause
nakagawa2024 129–130 ‹contested›. The same bound-vs-free diagnostic applies
to other weather-like expressions.

A set of temperature terms shows the zero-place and one-place contrast side by side. The
zero-place member describes the ambient climate and carries no person index; the one-place member
describes a felt bodily state and carries the intransitive subject index.

semantic domain
zero-place climate predicate
one-place felt-state predicate

cold
me-an
me-rayke

cool
sir-me-man
me-man

warm
sir-popke
popke

hot
sir-sesek
sesek

me-an cuk ne pe me-rayke=an ka somo ki no | coldness-exist autumn COP NMLZ coldness-kill=4.S even NEG do CONJ | It was a cold autumn, but I did not feel cold. | The zero-place climate predicate me-an carries no person index. The one-place felt-state predicate me-rayke carries the fourth-person S suffix =an. Both contain the coldness root me-, but they belong to different argument classes. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15, after Kayano 1974a

Property predicates — forms such as poro be big; become big and
pirka be good; become good — are intransitive verbs with no separate adjective
class; a stative reading ordinarily carries an inchoative one as well nakagawa2024 124 ; shibatani1990 33–34 . Their clausal behaviour is treated as a subclass in
no-adjective-class-property-verbs and property-concept-predication .
The denominal ambient predicates above are their zero-place counterpart: the nominal root takes
the same finite-verb template but licenses no external argument.

The copula ne as a denominal predicate head

The copula ne be, become is the primary path from a nominal expression to a
finite predicate. A noun or noun phrase precedes ne as a predicate
nominal, and the result is a two-place construction with a subject and a predicate nominal
argument. Nakagawa classifies ne as a two-place verb, yet it admits
only the subject person index: the object-indexed form *en=ne is
excluded in his diagnostic example, while the subject takes the transitive-subject prefix set
nakagawa2024 132 ; shibatani1990 49–50 .

okkayo ku=ne | man 1SG.A=COP | I am a man. | The subject carries the prefixal transitive-subject form ku=; no object index appears. Nakagawa contrasts this with the ungrammatical *en=ne in the same passage. | nakagawa2024:132 | HK | Nakagawa ex. 23

Satō uses this pattern to motivate the separation of three valency dimensions. The
argument-slot count of ne is two, since the construction predicates a
class or identity; its macrorole transitivity is one, since the predicate nominal is not a
referential undergoer and therefore licenses no object affix; its morphological valency is
transitive, which explains the transitive-subject-prefix pattern. Lexicalized denominal
compounds such as nispa a=ne 'I am a master' and
inne 'be numerous' (< ir-ne ) follow the
same transitive-prefix pattern sato2023b 52 ‹contested›. Nakagawa's surface
observation that ne never takes an object index and Satō's principled
account of why it does not are two descriptions of the same morphological fact.

Because Ainu property verbs carry an inchoative reading alongside their stative one, a noun plus
ne can similarly express both 'be X' and, in the appropriate aspectual
context, 'become X'. A productive denominal suffix -ne detached from
the copular construction has no independent support in the available evidence; lexicalized forms
such as inne are best analysed as the copula incorporated into a compound
stem rather than as the output of an autonomous verbalizing suffix ‹corpus-suggested›. The
syntax and semantics of predicate-nominal constructions are treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals .

kar and the word–phrase boundary

The transitive verb kar make, do supplies a second denominal route. A noun or
nominalized expression may function as the object of kar , and frequent
collocations may lexicalize as single verb stems. The synchronic default is that
kar is an independent lexical transitive verb. Nakagawa's example
tanpe kar pe en=ne , cited to show the copula's blocked object index,
shows kar functioning as the verb of a relative clause 'the one who
made this'; it takes ordinary transitive morphology and is clearly separate from
ne nakagawa2024 132 . Horobetsu
kar-ici 'make, do' — with the eastern plural suffix
ci inside the person index — is a verbal-number fact about the stem
itself, not evidence for a productive denominal verbalizer nakagawa2024 154–155 .

A transparent N kar sequence is therefore a two-word clause with
kar as its verb. A single derived verb stem arises only where
morphological diagnostics confirm it: fixed wordhood, phonological fusion, or person and
number morphology showing that the nominal element has been incorporated into the verb word
rather than occupying an external object slot. Nakagawa applies the same bound-vs-free
diagnostic to the weather predicates: bound roots such as me- and
sir- support a single-word analysis, while free nouns leave the
boundary unsettled nakagawa2024 129–130 . Without those independent tests,
the default analysis of N kar is a two-word clause ‹corpus-suggested›.
The light-verb and pro-verb uses of kar , including its behaviour with
incorporated objects, are treated in light-and-pro-verbs and
noun-incorporation-object .

Deverbal derivation without valency change

Several deverbal processes change the shape or the lexical-aspect profile of a verb stem without
altering its argument frame. Nakagawa assigns arity 0 to adverbial prefixes such as
ar- 'completely' and ru- 'slightly': they modify
the degree or thoroughness of the denoted event and leave the external argument count unchanged
nakagawa2024 137 . Their position in the verb template and their interaction
with person indexing are set out in verb-word-template .

Reduplication adds iterativity, continuity, or intensity without altering the argument count.
Nakagawa distinguishes root reduplication, partial-stem reduplication, and full-stem
reduplication: turi stretch (sth) yields tutturi
(root-reduplicated, marking repeated action), turiri
(partial, marking result-state persistence), and turituri (full, marking
sustained repetition). He treats reduplication as encoding lexical aspect rather than verbal
number, though the two interact at the surface nakagawa2024 151–153 ;
nakagawa2022 §1 .

The Type-C vowel-replacement alternation creates plural stems from singular ones without
changing argument count: tuye cut (sg) / tuypa cut (pl) ,
yasa tear (sg) / yaspa tear (pl) . Nakagawa assigns arity 0 to
the suffix -pa in this pattern, so the plural stem enters the same
argument class as the singular source nakagawa2024 137, 149–150 . The full
verbal-number system — Types A (suppletive), B ( -n/-p ), and C
( -V/-pa ), the auxiliary pa , and the relationship
between subject vs object plurality and stem choice — is treated in
suppletive-verbal-number .

Derived intransitives formed by prefixes such as si- ,
he- , and ho- on transitive bases inherit the
verbal-number type of their source. Nakagawa lists si-turi /
si-turpa 'stretch (intr)', ho-puni /
ho-punpa 'get up, fly up', and ho-yupu /
ho-yuppa 'run' as derived intransitives that still undergo the
Type-C alternation nakagawa2024 150–151 . Number agreement in these forms
follows the subject — the single argument of the intransitive — because the prefix has
converted the transitive base to an intransitive. The argument class of the output, not
the source, determines the number-agreement target nakagawa2024 145–146 .
The prefixes themselves, insofar as they create anticausative or middle-like readings, are
treated in the voice chapters via valency-voice-overview .

Transitivity pairs

Hokkaido Ainu has almost no productive lability: a single stem serving as both intransitive and
transitive is extremely rare. Satō reports only roski stand (pl) / stand sth up (pl)
as the standard case of a form that fills both slots, and he presents it as an exception rather
than a productive pattern sato2008 97 . The near-absence of lability means that
most intransitive–transitive correspondences are lexical pairs, partial derivations, or
suppletive-looking forms that require separate learning sato2008 97 ;
nakagawa2024 131–132 .

Three formal correspondence types occur. First, identical surface forms: only
roski . Second, partial formal similarity, as in
nunpa strain, wring (vt) and inunpa strain liquor for ritual offering
(vi), where the intransitive member is analyzable as the antipassive prefix
i- attached to the transitive base, though the pair has lexicalized to
distinct meanings sato2008 97 . Third, no formal similarity at all — suppletive-looking
pairs of basic vocabulary where the relationship between the two stems is not synchronically
derivational nakagawa2024 131 . Selected pairs of the third type are listed below.

meaning area
intransitive (1-place)
transitive (2-place)

look / see sth
inkar
nukar

hear / hear sth
inu
nu

steal (as activity) / steal sth
ikka
eikka

laugh / laugh at sth
mina
emina

eat a meal / eat sth specific
ipe
e

speak / say sth
itak
ye

think (intr) / think of sth
yaynu
ramu

The class membership of a form is diagnosed formally, not semantically. A vi takes the
suffixal fourth-person subject marker ( =an ) and the suffixal
first-person-plural exclusive marker ( =as ); a vt takes the
corresponding prefixal forms ( a= , ci= ).
The contrast between inkar and nukar —
glossed by the same Japanese verb 見る — appears within a single sentence:

inkar=an akus e=an ru-we a=nukar | look=4.S then 2SG.S=exist track-POSS 4.A=see | When I looked, I saw that you were there. | The vi stem inkar carries the fourth-person S suffix =an. The vt stem nukar carries the fourth-person A prefix a=. Both index the same narrative first person, but their different morphological position reveals the grammatical transitivity distinction. | nakagawa2024:131 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 18

Bugaeva and Kobayashi identify a related pattern of uncoded role alternation. Certain one-place
verbs may take either an agent-like or a theme-like subject without any change in form:
as stand can express both 'stand there (agent subject)' and 'stand upright
(theme subject)'. The alternation becomes visible under causativization — an agent subject
yields the indirect 'make sb stand' formation, a theme subject the direct 'stand sth up'
formation bugaevakobayashi2022 §5 . That distinction intersects with the
transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs transitivity-pair suffix system , where the
inventory of -V , -ke , and
-ka formations and their productivity are treated in detail.

Valency-changing derivation routed outward

Productive valency-changing operations form a separate system whose paradigms are documented in
the voice and valency chapters. Applicatives e- ,
ko- , and o- add or promote an object and raise
the verb's arity by one; the productive causatives -re ,
-e , and -te add a causer argument; antipassive
i- absorbs the object and yields an intransitive; reflexives
yay- / si- and reciprocal
u- fill the object slot internally so that a subject and an object of
the same person are not both indexed by person affixes bugaevakobayashi2022 §4 ;
nakagawa2024 137–139, 183 . Their paradigms are in
applicative-system-overview , causative-morphological-re-e-te ,
antipassive-detransitive-i , reflexive-yay ,
reflexive-possessive-si , and reciprocal-u .

The homophony of the prefix e- shows why holding these categories apart
matters. In one derived verb it is the applicative with arity +1 (adding an object); in another
it is the affiliative nominal prefix with arity 0 (contributing a body-part relation); in another
it is a second-person object index with arity −1. Nakagawa's worked examples
a=ureepunpa 'I kicked it up with my foot' (applicative, arity +1) and
a=koinawepunpa 'we offered inau' (affiliative, arity 0) are
distinguished only by the arity computation, since the resulting argument count differs
nakagawa2024 138–139 . The affiliative nominal prefix is defined in
concept-form-affiliative-form .

Locative-object verbs form a small class whose distinguishing property is that their object is
a bare place expression. Nakagawa lists oma be in / enter a place ,
osma plunge into a place , and kus pass through a place as
two-place verbs of this type. The single base-form three-place verb in his inventory is
o put X into a place ; derived three-place locative-object verbs such as
omare put into , osmare plunge into , and
kuste pass through are formed by applicative or causative processes from
non-locative bases nakagawa2024 212–213 .

upas tum a=oma wa karkarse=an | snow midst 4.A=enter CONJ roll=4.S | I sank into the snow and rolled. | The locative-object verb oma takes the bare place expression upas tum as its object; no case marker intervenes. The conjunctive particle wa links the clause to the following intransitive karkarse, which carries the fourth-person S suffix =an. | nakagawa2024:212 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 221

The derivational chain that Satō documents on the base ruska be angry at
illustrates the regularity of the full valency system: applicative ko-
raises the base to a three-place verb ( ko-ruska ); reflexive
yay- reduces it ( yay-ko-ruska ); antipassive
i- yields a one-place intransitive ( i-ruska );
applicative ko- re-applies ( ko-i-ruska ); and
causative -re raises that in turn ( i-ruska-re ,
ko-i-ruska-re , and further) sato2023b 40 ‹consensus›.
Each step alters the verb's argument class by a calculable amount; the individual formatives
are treated in the chapters named above.

Ijäs's L2–L3 framework

Ijäs's Basic Lessons 2 and 3 supply a pedagogical framing of the same system. Her zero-place,
one-place, two-place, and three-place classes correspond to Nakagawa's N-place inventory, Satō's
argument-slot count, and the Handbook valency chapter ijas2023 ;
nakagawa2024 125–139 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §§2–3 . Her
"pockets" image — person markers fill positions on the verb itself before and more obligatorily
than free noun phrases — is a learner-facing rendering of head-marking, and is especially close
to Satō's argument-slot language sato2023b 42–51 . Her statements that bare
verb forms carry no tense and that nouns are number-neutral agree with all primary sources
nakagawa2024 124–125 ; shibatani1990 33 .

Her Lesson 2 zero-place weather verbs match Nakagawa's zero-place category and correctly include
the inchoative reading of descriptive predicates such as hure be red; become red .
One qualification on her treatment: she presents apto as alongside the
bound-root zero-place forms, but apto rain is a free noun and Nakagawa leaves
the word-boundary status of the expression unresolved nakagawa2024 129–130
‹contested›.

numan hekaci cep koyki | yesterday boy fish catch | The boy caught fish yesterday. | Ijäs Lesson 3. A constructed two-place transitive frame; subject and object are third-person and carry no segmental person marker. The example is pedagogical and is not corpus-attested. | ijas2023

Two Ijäs simplifications require a caveat from the primary sources. Third person is segmentally
zero in all attested forms, and her explicit Ø- notation is descriptively
harmless; the theoretical status of that zero is nonetheless disputed, with Nakagawa treating it
as the absence of an affix and Satō positing a zero third-person affix to preserve the two-index
cap nakagawa2024 137 ; sato2023b 39 ; sato2025b 23–24 ‹contested›. Her presentation of the indirect object of a three-place verb in a fixed
position between subject and direct object reflects a teaching default; Nakagawa's examples
with epakasnu teach show that the two objects and the subject are freely
ordered by discourse conditions nakagawa2024 363–365 ‹consensus›.

Her forward reference to verbs with distinct singular and plural stems belongs with
suppletive-verbal-number ; her adverbial -no examples with
adverbializer-no . Neither stem alternation nor the adverbializer alters the argument
structure of the base.

title: The eci= Portmanteau (1A→2O) and Second-Person Plural Syncretism
summary: The cumulative eci= exponent encoding a 1sg subject acting on a 2nd-person object, and its syncretism with the 2pl subject/object marker eci=.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The eci= Portmanteau (1A→2O) and Second-Person Plural Syncretism
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The cumulative eci= exponent encoding a 1sg subject acting on a 2nd-person object, and its syncretism with the 2pl subject/object marker eci=.

The two functions of eci=

The prefix eci= occupies two cells in the Hokkaido Ainu
personal-index system. As the second-person plural index it marks the agent, subject,
or object of a predicate whose addressee participant is plural, in parallel with the
second-person singular e= . As the portmanteau for first-to-second
combinations it simultaneously indexes the subject and the object when a first-person
agent — singular or exclusive plural — acts on a second-person object, singular or plural.
The two functions share a single surface form, producing a syncretism that surrounding
context consistently resolves nakagawa2024 166–173 ; tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2012 471–474 .

The full S/A/O paradigm and its alignment patterns are set out in person-marking-architecture . The present chapter develops the two functions of eci= , the
syncretism between them, and the interaction with Hokkaido plural marking. Adjacent paradigm
material belongs to first-second-person-singular-affixes for e= and en= , to object-indexing-monotransitive for the
two-index cap and ditransitive object choice, and to person-number-interactions
for plural agreement.

structural environment
function of eci=
e= counterpart

intransitive predicate
2PL.S
e= 2SG.S

transitive predicate, O = 3rd person
2PL.A
e= 2SG.A

transitive predicate, A = 3rd person, O = 2nd person plural
2PL.O
e= 2SG.O

transitive predicate, A = 1st person (any number), O = 2nd person (any number)
1→2 portmanteau: indexes A and O simultaneously
eci= (no separate 2SG-only form exists for this cell)

Second-person neutrality: e= and eci=

Both e= and eci= carry no subject–object
distinction: the same form appears whether the addressee is the agent of a transitive verb,
the sole argument of an intransitive verb, or the object of a transitive verb with a
third-person agent bugaeva2012 471–472 ; sato2008 31–32 ;
tamura1972 27 . Tamura's formulation is explicit: the second-person affix
marks the subject and the object without distinction tamura1972 27 . The
S=A=O patterning places second person in the neutral column of the mixed alignment system,
alongside the zero third person, and against the nominative–accusative 1SG and the
tripartite 1PL.EXCL and fourth person ‹consensus›.

role
2SG
2PL (Saru–Chitose baseline)
2PL (Ishikari, Tokachi)

S: intransitive subject
e=
eci=
es=

A: transitive subject, O = 3rd person
e=
eci=
es=

O: object, A = 3rd person
e=
eci=
es=

sinen ne e=oman wa e=ray yakun | one COP 2SG.S=go CONJ 2SG.S=die COND | If you go alone and die, ... | Both intransitive verbs oman and ray carry e= because the addressee is singular; the role is the same (2SG.S) in both clauses. | nakagawa2024:165 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 106

eci=paye tek nean cise oro eci=epa cik anakne | 2PL.S=go.PL CONJ that house LOC 2PL.A=reach COND TOP | When you (plural) go and reach that house, ... | eci= marks 2PL.S on the intransitive paye in the first clause and 2PL.A on the transitive epa in the second: one form across two distinct syntactic roles within one sentence. | nakagawa2024:167 | TOK | Nakagawa ex. 116, after Sawai 1996: 69

Nakagawa's combination tables record eci= as the 2PL index in
Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, and Shizunai, while Ishikari and Tokachi also show
es= in the second-plural row nakagawa2024 166–172 .
Where a dialect supplies es= , it fills the same neutral paradigm
cell with the same functional range.

The 1A→2O portmanteau

When a first-person agent acts on a second-person object, eci=
indexes the complete local-person relation. The four subject/object number combinations —
1SG acting on 2SG, 1SG acting on 2PL, 1PL.EXCL acting on 2SG, and 1PL.EXCL acting on
2PL — all receive the same prefix. The stacked sequences
*ku=e= , *ku=eci= ,
*ci=e= , and *ci=eci= are unattested and
ungrammatical. Bugaeva enumerates all four covered combinations explicitly; Nakagawa's Saru
combination table places eci= in every 1→2 cell; Tamura's earlier
Saru analysis already treats the form as the second-person affix used in the
first-to-second-person relation bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; tamura1972 27–28 ‹consensus›.

A: subject
O: object
index
excluded analytic sequence

1SG
2SG
eci=
*ku=e=

1SG
2PL
eci=
*ku=eci=

1PL.EXCL
2SG
eci=
*ci=e=

1PL.EXCL
2PL
eci=
*ci=eci=

nep ka eci=kasuy pe an ya
2SG.O=help thing exist Q"
tr="Is there anything I can help you with?"
cite="nakagawa2024:173"
dial="SAR"
place="Nakagawa ex. 128, after Honda 2001: 44"
note="Nakagawa identifies eci= as the 1SG.A→2SG.O portmanteau. No separate ku= or e= appears; the single prefix satisfies both the agent and object indexing requirements of the transitive predicate kasuy."
/>

The portmanteau character of the 1→2 form is confirmed by the contrast with the 1→4
combination. Nakagawa's Saru combination table records eci=i=
when the agent is first person and the object is fourth person: the agent index
eci= and the fourth-person object i= appear
as separate prefixes nakagawa2024 170 . The 1→2 cells merge the two slots
into one, in keeping with the two-index cap: a verb carries at most one agent index and
one object index, and the portmanteau satisfies both positions simultaneously. The cap and
its consequences for three-argument verbs are treated in object-indexing-monotransitive .

Resolving the syncretism

A verb beginning with eci= is formally ambiguous among 2PL.S,
2PL.A, 2PL.O, and the 1→2 portmanteau. Several cues resolve the ambiguity. On an
intransitive predicate, which has no object slot, the form can only be 2PL.S. On a
transitive predicate with an overt third-person subject noun phrase, the 2PL.O reading
is forced; with an overt third-person object, the 2PL.A reading holds. The portmanteau
reading arises on a transitive predicate whose agent is a locally tracked first-person
participant. Ainu discourse tracks participants across turns through the bound indexes and
does not regularly deploy independent pronouns for reference maintenance; the free forms
eani (2SG) and ecioka (2PL) add emphasis
or mark contrast rather than supplying a referent from scratch
nakagawa2024 163–164, 250–251 ; tamura1971 3–5 .

The 2→1 configuration presents an asymmetry in Saru and Chitose. Where other Hokkaido
dialects form 2SG→1SG by combining a second-person agent index with the first-person
object en= , Saru uses bare en= alone,
suppressing the expected *e=en= ; Chitose extends this to 2PL→1SG,
making *eci=en= ungrammatical in that variety as well
nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 . The full 2→1
paradigm and its dialect variation are developed in first-second-person-singular-affixes .

Plural marking and eci=

The 2PL value carried by eci= is participant-number indexing and is
independent of verbal number. Hokkaido Ainu expresses verbal plurality through three further
devices: suppletive stem alternations such as arpa /
paye 'go', a root-attaching suffix -pa , and
— in Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari — an outer plural auxiliary that follows the fully
person-inflected verb nakagawa2024 140–159 ; bugaeva2012 471, 481 ; sakaguchi2024 12 . Each device occupies a distinct structural position
and can co-occur with eci= .

device
domain of plurality
position
co-occurrence with eci=

suppletive plural stem ( paye , arki , …)
S plurality in intransitives; O or event plurality in transitives
lexical stem selection
yes; eci=paye = 2PL.S + go.PL

stem suffix -pa
verbal / event plurality
inside person suffixes and causatives, attached to root
independent of the personal index

outer plural pa (Saru, Chitose, Ishikari)
agent / participant plurality
outside the fully person-inflected form
follows eci= verb when additional plurality is marked

Nakagawa's minimal pair illustrates the ordering diagnostic for the outer auxiliary versus
the stem suffix: iki=an pa has the outer pa
following the fourth-person suffix, while iki-pa=an has the stem
suffix -pa inside the person marker nakagawa2024 158 .
Sakaguchi formalises the criterion — a marker that attaches to the fully person-inflected
form is a clitic rather than an affix — for Sakhalin Ainu; the most directly relevant
Hokkaido evidence is the Saru/Chitose/Ishikari outer pa
sakaguchi2024 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. Person–number agreement and the
selection of plural stems are treated in person-number-interactions .

Ijäs's learner grammar and the reference paradigm

The online learner grammar by Ijäs introduces e= in Lesson 2 as
the second-person singular index on intransitive verbs; the plural counterpart is deferred
ijas2023 . Lesson 5 then presents eci= as the marker
for a first-person singular agent acting on a second-person singular object, and
e= as the second-person singular object form with a third-person
agent ijas2023 . Her pedagogical scope covers one number combination of the
portmanteau; the reference sources extend eci= to all four
first-to-second-person subject/object number combinations and additionally treat it as the
full 2PL index across A, S, and O ‹consensus›.

e=arpa | 2SG.S=go | You go. | Constructed from Ijäs Lesson 2. Her pedagogical hyphen notation (e-arpa) is normalized here to the personal-index boundary =. | ijas2023

tanpe eci=kore na
2SG.O=give FIN"
tr="I give this to you."
cite="ijas2023"
constructed
note="Constructed from Ijäs Lesson 5, presenting a 1SG-agent-plus-2SG-object construction. The reference paradigm generalises eci= to all four 1→2 number combinations. As a ditransitive, kore indexes the recipient (2nd person) in the object slot; the thing given (tanpe) is a bare noun phrase."
/>

Ijäs notes that second-person neutrality creates an interpretive ambiguity: a predicate
carrying e= alongside a third-person participant can be read with
the addressee as either agent or object unless additional context fixes the role
ijas2023 . The observation matches the structural picture: predicate valence and
the state of participant tracking supply what the person index alone leaves open.

Notation

This grammar writes the personal indexes with = , including
e= and eci= . The symbol is a house
convention for the personal-index boundary and makes no claim about morphological status.
Nakagawa writes all person markers with = for convenience,
explicitly noting that the symbol is unrelated to phonological or syllabic structure
nakagawa2024 49 . Bugaeva assigns graduated status on prosodic and
separability grounds: eci= is clitic-like — inseparable from the
stem and not shifting the accent kernel — while ku= ,
ci= , e= , en= ,
un= , and i= are full prefixes that do shift
it bugaeva2012 472–473 ‹contested›. Ijäs writes e-
and eci- with a hyphen and notes that equal signs are also in
common use ijas2023 ; the forms in this chapter are normalized accordingly.
The morphological boundary status of the Hokkaido personal indexes is treated in
personal-affix-template-and-ordering .
