title: First-Person Plural and the Clusivity Question (ci=/=as, un=)
summary: The exclusive 1pl set — subjective ci=/=as and objective un= — and the contested inclusive/exclusive opposition with the indefinite person.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: First-Person Plural and the Clusivity Question (ci=/=as, un=)
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The exclusive 1pl set — subjective ci=/=as and objective un= — and the contested inclusive/exclusive opposition with the indefinite person.

The exclusive first plural paradigm

The first-person plural of Hokkaido Ainu is exclusively exclusive: ci=
as the transitive subject (A), =as as the intransitive subject (S),
and un= as the object (O) always refer to a group from which the
addressee is absent. Nakagawa, Tamura, Satō, and Bugaeva converge on this characterisation
without qualification nakagawa2024 163–164 ; tamura1972 18–19 ;
sato2008 31–32 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 .

The free pronoun for this row is Saru coka , with dialectal counterparts
Tokachi ciokay and ciutari . Both the Saru and
Tokachi forms are built on the transitive-subject prefix ci= followed
by the plural existential oka(y) 'there are', nominalized — so
coka(y) is, in origin, 'those who exist as
ci= -ers' tamura1971 3–5 ; nakagawa2024 253 . The three-cell paradigm, set within the full person architecture, is introduced in
person-marking-architecture .

The table below places the exclusive row beside the fourth-person/indefinite row, whose first
function is the inclusive 'we'. The contrast between the two rows is the structural basis of
clusivity in Hokkaido Ainu.

row
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
referential value

1PL.EXCL — pronoun Saru coka , Tokachi
ciokay / ciutari

ci= (prefix)
=as (suffix)
un= (prefix)
we, excluding the addressee

4 / indefinite — pronoun Saru aoka , Tokachi
anokay / anutari

a= / dial. an= (prefix)

=an (suffix)
i= (prefix)
inclusive we; honorific you; impersonal one; logophoric narrator

Before a vowel-initial stem, ci= reduces to c= ,
parallel to the reduction of first-singular ku= to
k= in the same phonological context nakagawa2024 49, 166 ; bugaeva2012 467–468 .

Tripartite patterning: S, A, and O are distinct

The exclusive first plural is one of two tripartite rows in the Hokkaido paradigm: all three
role exponents differ in form and position. ci= precedes a transitive
stem as A; =as follows an intransitive stem as S; and
un= again precedes a transitive stem as O. The S/A side-switch —
prefix for A, suffix for S — is the morphological basis on which Bugaeva assigns this row a
tripartite classification bugaeva2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 ;
nakagawa2024 166–167 .

The Tokachi example below shows both subject functions within a single clause chain: the
exclusive group is the A of the transitive verb o 'board' and, in
the same utterance, the S of the intransitive plural motion verb arki
'come'.

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | ci= indexes the exclusive group as A of the transitive o; =as indexes the same group as S of the intransitive plural arki. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

The object prefix un= occupies the normal O-prefix slot of the
transitive verb alongside a distinct subject index. In the Saru example below, the transitive
agent is an indefinite/impersonal fourth person indexed by a= , while
the exclusive group stands as object, indexed by un= .

sanbyakugojuen a=un=kore hike ka | 350.yen 4.A=1PL.EXCL.O=give although even | although we were given 350 yen, … | The prefix sequence a=un= stacks a fourth-person (indefinite/impersonal) A and a first-plural-exclusive O on a single transitive stem. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2, AA Institute Ainu materials [aa-irc/004#109]

un= is formally separate from the fourth-person object
i= : the two prefixes belong to different paradigm rows and index
different participants. Their historical relationship — including whether Hokkaido
un= and the Sakhalin counterpart in= are
cognates of the exclusive O or of the indefinite O — is part of the broader alignment debate
and is pursued in alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate ‹contested›.

Clusivity: the exclusive row and the fourth-person inclusive

The semantic domain 'we' is distributed across two formal rows. The exclusive forms
ci= / =as / un= index a
speaker-only group; addressee-inclusive 'we' falls to the fourth-person row
a= / =an / i= . Nakagawa
identifies inclusive 'we' as the first of five referential values of the fourth person; Tamura
treats the same morphology as an inclusive first plural in her eleven-person Saru analysis; and
Bugaeva places the inclusive value at the centre of the indefinite paradigm nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 ; tamura1972 18–19, 39 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 .

eani hem paye=an ro | 2SG also go.PL=4.S HORT | Let you and me go too! | The hortative construction includes the addressee; the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural stem paye, not the exclusive =as. | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

Tamura notes that coka is the genuine plural of
kani 'I', while aoka would more accurately be
described as a speaker-plus-addressee category — though she retains the label 'inclusive first
plural' for it tamura1972 19 . The exclusive row carries no inclusive value in
any Hokkaido description consulted. The complete morphology and the non-inclusive uses of
a= / =an / i= belong to
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm and fourth-person-honorific .

Terminological disagreement over the fourth-person row does not affect the clusivity fact.
Nakagawa's 'fourth person' is a formal class label; Tamura distinguishes several persons whose
morphology is neutralised; Bugaeva's 'indefinite' groups inclusive, honorific, logophoric, and
impersonal functions; and Takahashi, on Tokachi, adopts 'inclusive person' as a unifying
functional term, arguing that the participant structure of the inclusive gives a principled
account of all the disparate uses nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; takahashi2015 205–206, 210
‹contested›. Across these analyses, addressee-inclusive 'we' takes the fourth-person forms.

Tokachi evidence and dialect scope

Takahashi's Tokachi description provides the most explicit formulation of the
exclusive/inclusive opposition for a single Hokkaido dialect. The exclusive side has
ci- , -as , and un- , with
free pronouns ciokay and ciutari ; the inclusive
side has a- / an- , -an ,
and i- , with anokay and
anutari takahashi2015 206–207 . Takahashi's hyphens for
personal markers are a source-internal convention; they correspond to the
= -notation for personal indexes used here.

Takahashi also notes that the inclusive row selects plural verb morphology even when the
referent is a single additional person: a sole addressee triggers plural stem agreement on an
inclusive-marked verb takahashi2015 209 . Nakagawa documents the same pattern
for the fourth-person honorific and hortative uses — one esteemed individual addressed with a
plural stem — and for the narrator in most dialects outside Saru and Chitose nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–176 . The person-number agreement consequences and the conditioning of plural stems are developed
in person-number-interactions and suppletive-verbal-number .

In oral-narrative texts, the exclusive series serves a genre-specific narrator function. In
kamuy yukar, the divine protagonist uses ci= / =as / un= as self-reference, distinguishing the kamuy narrator's
speech from the human first-person voice of uwepeker, which uses the fourth person nakagawa2024 602–604 . Whether this pattern derives from an original first-person plural strategy or a
register-internal specialisation is debated sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ‹contested›. These narrative and logophoric dimensions are treated in fourth-person-honorific and logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Sakhalin Ainu presents a different configuration. Sakaguchi reports that the
ci= -series functions as a grammatically plural encoding of the first
singular, while the fourth-person an= -series covers both first singular
and first plural without an exclusive/inclusive split of the Hokkaido type sakaguchi2024 26–27, 162–163 ‹SA›.

Morphological status of the indexes and notation

The = boundary on personal indexes and the -
boundary on derivational and number morphology are descriptive conventions, not a uniform
claim about morphological status. Bugaeva distinguishes three grades for the Hokkaido indexes,
using prosodic autonomy and separability as diagnostics: =as and
=an are word-like (they may be separated from the stem by intervening
material and have autonomous prosodic weight); a= and
eci= are clitic-like; and ku= ,
ci= , en= , and un= are
fuller prefixes that shift the accent kernel bugaeva2012 472–473 . Sakaguchi's
explicit diagnostics, formulated for Sakhalin material, classify -as
as root-attaching (affixal) and fourth-person =an as autonomous
(clitic-like) — reversing Bugaeva's ranking for =as — while both
agree that =an is the most autonomous of the subject indexes sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. Nakagawa writes all person indexes with = as a
convenience notation, explicitly disavowing any clitic claim nakagawa2024 49 .
The ordering diagnostics and the clitic/affix boundary question are pursued in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Ijäs's learner grammar writes the exclusive markers as ci- ,
-as , and un- , with hyphens used as a teaching
convention rather than a phonological boundary symbol ijas2023 . The constructed
example below shows the exclusive intransitive-subject marker in a simple locative clause.

cise or ta okay=as | house place LOC exist.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | We are at the house. | Constructed after the pattern of Ijäs's learner materials. The exclusive S suffix =as is written -as in that source; okay is the plural existential stem. | ijas2023 | HK

title: First- and Second-Person Singular Affixes (ku=/en=, e=/e=)
summary: The SAP-singular core: 1sg subjective ku= vs objective en=, and 2sg e= (syncretic across A/S/O), with their morphophonology and distribution.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: First- and Second-Person Singular Affixes (ku=/en=, e=/e=)
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The SAP-singular core: 1sg subjective ku= vs objective en=, and 2sg e= (syncretic across A/S/O), with their morphophonology and distribution.

Forms and alignment

In the Hokkaido Ainu person paradigm, the first- and second-person singular cells are
distinguished by whether the language maintains or collapses the S/A/O contrast within the row.
First singular keeps two distinct forms: ku= for the single argument of
an intransitive verb (S) and for the transitive subject (A), and en=
for the object (O). Second singular has one form, e= , in all three
roles. In alignment terms, the 1SG row is nominative–accusative (S=A≠O) and the 2SG row is
neutral (S=A=O)
nakagawa2024 165–169 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 ; sato2008 31–32 ; tamura1972 27 ; bugaeva2008 32 . The architecture of the
complete paradigm — including the side-encoding of the first plural exclusive and the fourth
person, the two-index cap on the verb, and the boundary-notation convention — is set out in
person-marking-architecture . The present chapter treats the 1SG and 2SG rows in
detail.

formal person
free pronoun (Saru)
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
alignment

1SG
kani
ku= ; Saru/Chitose/Mukawa k= before
vowel-initial stems (a, u, e, o)
ku= ; same allomorphy
en=
S=A≠O; nominative–accusative

2SG
eani
e=
e=
e= ; 1→2 uses portmanteau eci= instead
S=A=O; neutral

The free pronouns kani and eani are optional
emphatic or contrastive expressions; ordinary finite clauses carry the person information on
the verb alone. Their etymology — kani from the 1SG prefix plus an
existential base; eani invariant across the surveyed Hokkaido dialects
— and their distribution relative to the verbal index are treated in
independent-personal-pronouns
tamura1971 3–5 ; nakagawa2024 250–253 .

First-person singular subject: ku= and k=

The prefix ku= indexes 1SG in both the intransitive-subject and the
transitive-subject roles; the two uses share a single form. This identity of S and A, set
against the distinct object en= , is the formal basis for describing the
1SG row as nominative–accusative
nakagawa2024 166–169 ; bugaeva2012 472 .

orwano ku=cise or ta ku=an kor | then 1SG.POSS=house place LOC 1SG.S=exist while | Then, while I was in my house, … | The intransitive existential ku=an indexes 1SG.S. The nominal ku=cise carries the homophonous first-person prefix in the possessive domain (glossed POSS here); nominal possession belongs to the chapter on the possessed form. | nakagawa2024:164 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 104, after Sunazawa Kura 1983: 75

In Saru, Chitose, and Mukawa, ku= reduces to k=
before stems beginning in a , u ,
e , or o :
ek 'come' yields k=ek , and
eramuan 'understand' and eunkeray 'receive'
appear as k=eramuan and k=eunkeray . Nakagawa
writes the Hidaka-western and Chitose convention as k(u)= , recording
the same alternation; the notation reflects phonological context, not two morphologically
separate forms
nakagawa2024 49, 166 ; bugaeva2012 467–468 .

aynu itak k=eramuan wa kusu icen k=eunkeray wa pirka pirka | Ainu language 1SG.A=understand CONJ because money 1SG.A=receive CONJ good good | Because I had learnt Ainu, I was able to receive money, which was very good. | Both transitive verbs show k=, the reduced allomorph of 1SG.A ku= before the vowel-initial stems eramuan and eunkeray. | nakagawa2024:167 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 111

Bugaeva classifies ku= as a full prefix: it is inseparable from the
stem and shifts the accent kernel, properties that set it apart from the more autonomous
intransitive-subject suffixes =an and =as , which
can be separated from the verb by intervening words and form their own prosodic units
bugaeva2012 472–473 . Nakagawa writes all personal indexes with
= as a practical orthographic convention, noting the symbol carries no
claim about clitic status nakagawa2024 49 . Satō and Tamura write these forms
with a hyphen; the learner grammar of Ijäs does likewise, stating the hyphen is a teaching
convention rather than a pronunciation symbol
ijas2023 . Sakaguchi's explicit clitic/affix criteria — developed primarily for
Sakhalin material, and applied to Hokkaido only by extension — place root-bound personal
indexes in the prefixal class and rank =an / =as
as the most autonomous subject markers; on these diagnostics, ku=
would belong to the fully prefixal end
sakaguchi2024 ix, 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. The ordering diagnostics are
developed in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Ijäs introduces ku= early as the obligatory subject marker on both
transitive and intransitive verbs, presenting it before the optional independent pronoun and
treating the pronoun as emphatic or clarifying rather than grammatically required
ijas2023 . This is consistent with the head-marking account of the Ainu verb given
by Satō sato2025a 361 and with the typological framework of
nichols1986 . An idiosyncratic note in Ijäs's phonetic guidance specifies
that initial i retains the fuller form and that careful Saru speech may
realise the u vowel before other vowels; these are L2-oriented
observations about pronunciation and do not affect the morphological alternation
ijas2023 .

ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=return | I go back. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 2, which uses this verb type to introduce 1SG.S on an intransitive. Ijäs writes ku-hosipi. | ijas2023 | SAR

First-person singular object: en=

The object counterpart of 1SG is en= . It indexes a first-person
singular participant in the O role: the patient of a monotransitive verb or, with three-place
verbs such as kore 'give' and epakasnu
'teach', the human recipient rather than the theme
nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 47 ;
sato2025a 368–371 . The secundative object-indexing pattern — recipient indexed,
theme left as a bare noun phrase — is developed in object-indexing-monotransitive .

en=epunkine wa en=kore yan | 1SG.O=protect CONJ 1SG.O=give IMP | Please protect me. | The imperative force supplies a second-person subject; both instances of en= mark the 1SG participant as the object/recipient. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, menokoyukar 'Kamuy yukar 4' [aa-irc/011#5]

nep kameaske ene ne yakkay en=kasuy kun pe an cik pirka hike | what monster thus COP even 1SG.O=help should NMLZ exist if good although | It would be good if any monster were to help me. | The prefix en= indexes the speaker as the object of kasuy 'help'; the agent is left to context. | nakagawa2024:169 | TOK | Nakagawa ex. 122, after Takahashi 2007: 63

A syncretism affects en= in the Saru and Chitose combination tables.
Across most Hokkaido dialects, a second-person subject acting on a 1SG object surfaces as a
second-person A exponent followed by en= — e=en= for 2SG→1SG or eci=en= for 2PL→1SG. In Saru and Chitose, the
expected second-person A exponent is absent, and bare en= covers the
2SG→1SG cell; Chitose extends the same bare form to 2PL→1SG. The bare
en= form is therefore shared between the 3→1SG and 2→1SG cells in
these dialects, and the subject is recovered from discourse or from an overt pronoun
nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 ‹contested›. The
full dialect-by-dialect combination table, and the parallel 1→2 portmanteau
eci= , are treated in eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

Ijäs presents the bare- en= situation to learners as a predictable rule:
when the 1SG participant is in O position and the subject is second or third person, the verb
shows only en= , with the subject inferred from context
ijas2023 . The constructed example below illustrates the Saru/Chitose 2SG→1SG
reading.

topenpe en=kore | sweets 1SG.O=give | You gave me sweets. | Constructed following Ijäs. The translation supplies a 2SG subject from context; in Saru/Chitose the verb shows only en=, which covers both 3→1SG and 2→1SG readings. The phenomenon is attested in Nakagawa 2024: 170, 173 and Satō 2008: 146–147. | ijas2023 | SAR

In the literary and oral-narrative register, the 2→1SG configuration maps to
i= , the fourth-person object index, with the subject again unexpressed
sato2008 261 . That use belongs to the functional range of the fourth person
and is treated in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Second-person singular: e=

The 2SG index e= is neutral across argument roles: one form marks
2SG.S, 2SG.A, and 2SG.O alike. This pattern is shared by the 2PL form
eci= , so neither the singular nor the plural second-person index
distinguishes subject from object
tamura1972 27 ; sato2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 .
On an intransitive verb, e= marks 2SG.S; on a transitive verb with a
third-person counterpart, e= may mark either 2SG.A or 2SG.O, and the
role is supplied by the co-occurring indexes and by discourse.

sinen ne e=oman wa e=ray yakun | alone COP 2SG.S=go CONJ 2SG.S=die if | If you go alone and die, … | Both intransitive verbs carry e= as 2SG.S; no object is present. | nakagawa2024:165 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 106

kunneywa wakka e=ku | morning water 2SG.A=drink | You drink water in the morning. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 3. The prefix e= indexes a 2SG transitive subject; the third-person object wakka receives no index. | ijas2023 | SAR

The role ambiguity of e= on a transitive verb is inherent to the
neutral row. In a clause such as kanpisos e=kore , the prefix may signal
either 2SG.A (the addressee gives the book to someone) or 2SG.O (someone gives the book to
the addressee), depending on which participant is third person; the gloss assigned in
interlinear analysis follows what the discourse establishes. Ijäs flags this ambiguity
explicitly in her fifth lesson as a diagnostic for learners
ijas2023 .

When a 1SG or 1PL.EXCL subject acts on a second-person object, the portmanteau
eci= replaces any sequence of separate first- and second-person
indexes. All four 1→2 combinations — 1SG→2SG, 1SG→2PL, 1PL.EXCL→2SG, 1PL.EXCL→2PL — surface
as eci= , so that forms such as *ku=e= and
*ci=e= do not occur
tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 . The portmanteau's double role — 1→2 combination
exponent and 2PL index for S, A, and O — is treated in
eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

nep ka eci=kasuy pe an ya
2SG.O=help NMLZ exist Q"
tr="Is there anything I can help you with?"
cite="nakagawa2024:173"
dial="SAR"
place="Nakagawa ex. 128, after Honda 2001: 44"
note="The single exponent eci= encodes the 1SG.A→2SG.O combination; no separate ku= or e= appears."
/>

Second-person neutrality is the consensus descriptive position for Hokkaido Ainu
tamura1972 27 ; sato2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 .
Its diachronic status is more open: most treatments assume an earlier S/A≠O distinction was
lost, while Satō's reported 2024 position treats neutrality as original — a strategy of
empathy or deferred agency never preceded by a subject/object split
dalcorso2025align 194–195 ‹contested›. The alignment-level debate and the
competing system-wide accounts are taken up in
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

title: The Formal / Defective Noun Set (ruwe, hawe, siri, humi, hi, pe, kur)
summary: The closed set of bound 'formal' nouns that head nominalized and evidential constructions, defined here as a morphosyntactic subclass with shared diagnostics.

title: Honorific and Logophoric Uses of the Fourth Person
summary: The indefinite/fourth person used to refer respectfully to esteemed humans and kamuy, and as the self-reference device of the first-person narrator in oral literature (the hero's 'I'); the affix-paradigm facts of these uses, with full discourse deployment in Part XIX.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Honorific and Logophoric Uses of the Fourth Person
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The indefinite/fourth person used to refer respectfully to esteemed humans and kamuy, and as the self-reference device of the first-person narrator in oral literature (the hero's 'I'); the affix-paradigm facts of these uses, with full discourse deployment in Part XIX.

The honorific and logophoric functions in the fourth-person system

The honorific and logophoric values of the fourth person belong to the same formal row as its
inclusive and indefinite uses: the transitive-subject prefix
a= (dialectally an= ), the
intransitive-subject suffix =an , and the object prefix
i= . The S/A/O architecture of this tripartite row and its
agent-defocusing function are set out in person-marking-architecture and
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm . The present chapter treats two discourse-anchored
functions: respectful address to a socially elevated second person, and self-reference by a
narrated protagonist or by a speaker whose words are embedded inside a quotation
nakagawa2024 175–179 ; tamura1972 19–20, 28 ;
bugaeva2012 475–476 .

role
form
honorific value
narrative / logophoric value

A: transitive subject
a= , dialectally an=
honored addressee as actor on a transitive predicate
narrator or reported speaker as actor on a transitive predicate

S: intransitive subject
=an
honored addressee as intransitive subject
narrator or reported speaker as intransitive subject

O: object
i=
honored addressee as patient or recipient
narrator or reported speaker as patient or recipient

Nakagawa subsumes all these values under a single fourth-person label; Tamura's Saru analysis
separates the honorific second person and the quotative first person as distinct categories;
Bugaeva's cover term is indefinite, with the quotation use reanalysed as logophoric; Satō
discusses the literary and quotative functions under the literary-person problem without positing
a fourth-person label; Takahashi, working on Tokachi, uses "inclusive person" for the same
form range
nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura1972 19–20, 28, 39 ;
bugaeva2008 37–44 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ;
sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ;
takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›.

Honorific address to an exalted second person

The fourth-person row indexes a second person of elevated social standing. Nakagawa cites two
primary contexts: a woman addressing an adult man, and any speaker addressing a kamuy in
prayer; in both, the verb takes fourth-person morphology and a plural stem even when the
addressee is singular
nakagawa2024 175–176 ; bugaeva2012 475 . Tamura's Saru account
places the same frame alongside contexts of esteem toward an adult
tamura1972 28 .

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | The addressee is a single person; the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural motion stem arki rather than the singular ek. The evidential frame ru-we an closes the question. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140; a woman addressing an adult man

The plural-stem requirement in this single-referent frame is grammatically obligatory for the
honorific use. The same constraint — one referent triggering plural verbal morphology — operates
across the honorific, inclusive, and narrative fourth-person functions
nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–176 . Person–number interactions in the verbal system
are treated in person-number-interactions . The syntax of prayer address to a kamuy
and the attendant ritual formulas are developed in honorific-ritual-and-taboo-registers .

Ijäs's online pedagogical grammar documents several present-day address strategies for L2
learners, including plural-stem imperatives with the final particle
yan final particle (request) as a routine polite request, and the use of
nispa gentleman; master and katkemat respected woman as address
terms for non-relatives ijas2023 . The following constructed example illustrates
the plural-stem polite imperative, a strategy structurally distinct from the fourth-person S
suffix =an used in the honorific paradigm:

eteun arki yan | here come.PL FIN | Please come here. | Ijäs pedagogical example. The plural stem arki and the final particle yan form a polite request without deploying fourth-person morphology; it is a parallel addressee-politeness resource, not an instance of the honorific paradigm described above. Ijäs further notes that attaching nispa and katkemat to personal names as titles is not uniformly attested in traditional usage; such address conventions belong to contact and revitalization contexts rather than to the inherited verbal honorification system. | ijas2023 | HK

The narrator's fourth person in oral literature

In Hokkaido oral narrative, the protagonist's self-reference is expressed by the fourth-person
row. The verb takes fourth-person morphology, and Japanese translations of the same passages
render the protagonist with first-person pronouns, because the referent is the narrated self
nakagawa2024 177–179, 562 . Satō discusses this under the literary-person
problem; Bugaeva places it alongside the logophoric use
sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ;
bugaeva2008 37–44 . All three grammatical roles are attested. The following Saru
example shows the intransitive-subject suffix and the possessive prefix together:

ratcitara hosipi=an yakka a=uni un tunasno hosipi=an pe ne kusu | slowly return=4.S though 4.POSS=home to quickly return=4.S NMLZ COP because | Although I returned slowly, because I was able to return home quickly, … | The intransitive stem hosipi takes the fourth-person S suffix =an; a= functions as the narrator's possessive prefix on uni 'home'. Saru and Chitose allow a singular stem with a singular fourth-person narrator. | nakagawa2024:165 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 109; singular Saru narrator

Saru and Chitose permit a singular narrator to appear with a singular verb stem, as in the
example above. Dialect variation is substantial: in Horobetsu, the verb may carry a singular
fourth-person index while the only available free pronoun is the plural
aokay ; in Shizunai, the free pronouns distinguish singular
anani from plural anoka , but the verb is
consistently plural nakagawa2024 174–176, 254–256 ‹contested›. These
opposite mismatches between pronoun number and verbal number across the dialect area indicate
that the number dimension of the narrator form diverged from a common source.

The object prefix i= indexes the narrator as patient or recipient
alongside a= and =an . A single predicate can
carry both the agent-defocusing a= and the narrator-object
i= :

a=rehe Ikuresuye sekor a=i=reko | 4.POSS=name Ikuresuye QUOT 4.A=4.O=name | I was named Ikuresuye. | a= on rehe is the narrator's possessive prefix; on reko it is an indefinite-agent prefix (someone named); i= indexes the narrator as the named object. The co-occurrence of fourth-person A as agent-defocuser and fourth-person O as narrator-object on one predicate yields a passive-like Japanese reading (私はイクレスイェと名付けられた). | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 7 [aa-irc/005#1]

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=shelter CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The object prefix i= recurs across three transitive predicates, indexing the narrator as the entity pitied, sheltered, and given to. The homophonous detransitivising prefix i- of the antipassive construction is morphologically and functionally distinct. | nakagawa2024:169 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

The distinction between the fourth-person object prefix i= and the
antipassive detransitiviser i- 'something' requires attention to
syntactic context. The antipassive is treated in antipassive-detransitive-i .

The debate over the literary person

Earlier analyses distinguished a "literary" person (雅語の人称) from an "everyday" person
(日常語の人称), treating the narrative fourth-person as a register marker. Nakagawa rejects
this bifurcation: prose tales (uwepeker) use fourth-person marking for the protagonist even
though their register is conversational, while prayers (inonno itak), though formally elevated,
use first-person ku= for the praying speaker. The controlling factor
is discourse role: a character's story-internal self-representation takes the fourth person;
a speaker's real-world first-person address takes ku=
nakagawa2024 600–602 .

Satō advances three objections to reducing the literary person to the indefinite
sato2004 179–184 . First, Ainu speakers and translators consistently interpret
the narrative form with first-person reference. Second, the indefinite in its agent-defocusing
function is referent-non-specific and incompatible with an overt free pronoun; yet narrators
use asinuma alongside fourth-person verbal morphology, indicating
referential specificity. Third, kamuy-yukar texts from Horobetsu use the first-plural
exclusive row ci=/=as/un= as the divine narrator's person — a fact
that resists derivation from an indefinite base nakagawa2024 602–604 .
Nakagawa counters that most texts mix the first-plural exclusive and the fourth person, and
that the uniformly first-plural presentation of Chiri Yukie's 1923 神謡集 is likely an
editorial regularisation, since Kanari Matsu's tellings of the same material show mixed usage.
The divine narrator pattern and its genre context are examined in
heroic-and-divine-verse .

Bugaeva's approach sidesteps the origin question by relocating the quotation-internal use to
logophoricity and documenting it in non-folklore Asahikawa reported-speech data
bugaeva2008 41–44 ; bugaeva2012 475–476 . Since the form
appears wherever a discourse source refers to itself — in oral literature and in everyday
reported speech alike — a register-based account cannot be primary. The three analyses agree
on the empirical core while diverging on grammatical category and diachronic origin ‹contested›.

Logophoric reference in reported speech

When a third party's speech is embedded after the quotative complementizer
sekor quotative particle , that speaker's self-reference appears as fourth-person
morphology in place of ordinary first-person ku= or
ci= . Tamura had already treated this as a "quotative first person" in
Saru; Bugaeva reanalyses it as logophoricity in the typological sense, where a dedicated form
tracks the self of the source of a reported utterance
tamura1972 19–20, 22–23 ; bugaeva2008 37–41 ;
bugaeva2012 475–476 .

a=ye wa anun katkemat a=nure sekor a=ye acapo hawean kor | 4.A=say CONJ other lady 4.A=tell QUOT 4.A=say uncle say while | "I will tell it and let another lady hear it," the uncle said. | Both predicates of the embedded quotation (a=ye, a=nure) carry fourth-person a= as the reported uncle's self-reference; Nakagawa notes that the actual first-person utterance would have used ku=ye and ku=nure. The narrator's own framing verb a=ye is fourth-person for the separate reason that it is the narrator's story-world first person. | nakagawa2024:179 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 153, after Tamura 1984: 22

The logophoric analysis receives support from non-folklore contexts. Bugaeva's Asahikawa data
show the same pattern in everyday reported speech, severing the connection to elevated register
bugaeva2008 41–44 . Nikitina and Bugaeva develop the syntactic argument
further, proposing that logophoric reported speech in Ainu is a structurally distinct category,
neither indirect speech nor ordinary direct speech nikitina2021 . The dialectal
scope remains uneven: Nakagawa confirms the phenomenon for Saru and Chitose, while its
extension to other dialects is less well documented nakagawa2024 179 ‹contested›.

The free pronoun asinuma (< a= +
sinuma 'oneself') is the fourth-person singular self-referential
pronoun. Bugaeva notes that the consultant Ito Oda consistently glossed
asinuma as Japanese 自分 'oneself', never as
私 'I', which is consistent with a logophoric interpretation: the
pronoun tracks the perspective of the contextually salient discourse source rather than fixing
the speaker of the utterance bugaeva2008 41–43 .

The morphology of the logophoric use is identical to that of the honorific and narrative uses:
a= , =an , and i= serve
all three. Disambiguation depends on discourse frame — quotative embedding, story narration,
or respectful address — together with the number of the verb stem and the presence of the
free pronoun asinuma or its plural counterpart
aoka . The clusivity contrast between the exclusive
ci=/=as/un= and the fourth person is developed in
first-person-plural-and-clusivity . The sekor construction and the syntax of
quotative complementation are treated in quotative-complementation-sekor . The
extended analysis of logophoric narration and reported discourse is in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

title: The Genetic Position of Ainu: Isolate Status and Macro-Comparison Controversies
summary: Ainu as a language isolate, and a methodologically explicit survey of the contested macro-relationship and deep-contact proposals.
part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Genetic Position of Ainu: Isolate Status and Macro-Comparison Controversies
Part: Part I — The Language and Its Setting

title: Headless, Internally-Headed, and Noun-Modifying Clause Analyses
summary: The analytic question of whether Ainu adnominal clauses are externally-headed RCs, headless/internally-headed relatives, or a unified 'general noun-modifying clause' type.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Headless, Internally-Headed, and Noun-Modifying Clause Analyses
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The analytic question of whether Ainu adnominal clauses are externally-headed RCs, headless/internally-headed relatives, or a unified 'general noun-modifying clause' type.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu builds noun-modifying clauses prenominally: a finite verb phrase precedes a
head noun with no relative pronoun and no change to the verb's morphology; the relativized
argument's role is recovered from a gap in argument structure and from any postpositions
retained inside the clause nakagawa2024 503 ; bugaeva2015 47 ;
shibatani1990 39 . When no lexical noun is available or intended as the head,
a member of the closed class of formal-defective-nouns formal nouns fills
that role, supplying a generic semantic type — 'person', 'thing', 'people', 'place', 'time'
— while the clause itself names the referent. In continuous Hokkaido Ainu text these
formal-noun heads appear more often than common-noun heads in relativization contexts
bugaeva2015 47 .

Two further analytical questions organize the later sections. The first is whether any
Hokkaido Ainu constructions approach the internally-headed pattern of noun modification —
in which the notional head is expressed inside the modifying clause rather than outside it
— and whether that pattern involves a form of
noun-incorporation-object noun incorporation . Dal Corso raises this for
southern Hokkaido Ainu indirect evidential constructions and proposes a
classificatory-incorporation analysis dalcorso2020 47, 51–52 . The second
is whether the perception-complement constructions built with possessed sensory formal nouns
are biclausal — an external formal noun heading a relative clause — or whether they have
grammaticalized toward monoclausal complex predicates. Both questions remain contested in
the literature ‹contested›.

The p thing (nominalizer) ~ pe thing (nominalizer) formal
noun in its RC-head and complementizer uses is treated in nominalization-p-pe ;
the gap strategy, the accessibility hierarchy, and postposition stranding in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses ; lexicalized forms that crystallized from
headless relative clauses in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization ; and the
evidential clause-final uses of the sensory formal nouns in
evidential-schema-overview and in ruwe-ne-inferential .

The formal-noun inventory for headless nominalizations

The formal nouns that serve as generic heads form a compact, closed set. Nakagawa lists
them as a distinct word class defined by weak lexical autonomy — they follow another element
to build an NP — and shows that they carry arity −1, on a par with common nouns in their
citation form nakagawa2024 257–258, 261–262 . This arity value allows a
formal noun to fill the open argument slot of a clause with total arity ≥ +1 and produce a
headless relative-clause nominal nakagawa2024 261–262, 504 .

formal noun
semantic type of head
example

p ~ pe
thing; person (generic)
a=e p 'what one eats'

kur (< kuru person )
person (polite)
ku=kor-kur 'the one I have; husband'

utar
people (plural)
arki utar 'the people who came'

hi ~ i
thing, time, place (abstract)
oro ta cise kar hi 'the place where (X) built a house'

usi ~ uske
place, time
a=nu usi 'a place to ask about'

hike
the one, side (≈ 'one that')
ki hike 'the one that does so'

Among person-type heads, p ~ pe is maximally generic and extends to
human referents with a pejorative or collective edge in those contexts;
kur (< kuru person ) selects specifically for a
personal referent and carries a polite register. Shibatani describes
-kur as a phrasal nominalizing suffix in Classical/yukar Ainu,
productive alongside -p(e)
shibatani1990 33–34 :

ku=kor-kur | 1SG.A=have-NMLZ | the one I have; my husband | Classical Ainu (Itadori/Ishikari data in Shibatani 1990). The person-type suffix -kur ( utar head provides person semantics with plurality. Bugaeva's
accessibility-hierarchy study records it as a headless head for clauses with a plural human
referent:

cise soy pakno arki utar | house doorway up.to come.PL people | the men who came up to the doorway of the house | arki is the suppletive plural of ek 'come'; 3rd-person subjects have zero person-marking, so the verb appears without a prefix. utar 'people' is the formal-noun head of the headless relative clause. | bugaeva2015:47 | SAR

The p ~ pe formal noun extends to human agents when context provides
that reading. Bugaeva attests a pair of parallel headless nominals whose open argument is
the subject of a motion verb:

kem sawot pe tumi sawot pe | famine flee NMLZ war flee NMLZ | those who flee famine, those who flee war | pe heads two parallel clauses whose argument gap is the S/agent of sawot 'flee'. The thing-type nominalizer pe reads as 'person' by contextual inference; no person prefix appears because the referent is generic/3rd-person. | bugaeva2015:48 | SAR

Headless nominalizations across the accessibility hierarchy

The argument positions available to headless nominalizations are the same as those available
in externally-headed relative clauses. Under the noun-phrase accessibility hierarchy of
keenancomrie1977 , Hokkaido Ainu relativizes subjects, agents, objects,
and a range of obliques through the gap strategy; the alienable possessor and the standard
of comparison resist this because of island constraints and the absence of an accessible
comparee position respectively bugaeva2015 47–49 . These exclusions carry
through to the headless type: there is no headless relativization of an alienable possessor
or a comparison standard in Bugaeva's data.

For oblique participants, the governing postposition stays inside the relative clause when
the head is a formal noun, as it would with a common-noun head. The place-type formal noun
usi ~ uske heads clauses with a locative, dative, or directional
gap:

eun a=nu usi ka isam | to 4.A=hear place even NEG.exist | there was no place to ask about | The dative/directional postposition eun 'to, about' remains inside the relative clause; usi fills the gap as the headless place-head. The fourth-person prefix a= marks a generic participant. The construction follows the same postposition-stranding pattern as an externally-headed oblique relative. | bugaeva2015:49 | SAR

Nakagawa's arity accounting governs the boundary between a headless relative-clause nominal
and the complementizer use of the same formal noun. When the clause preceding
p ~ pe or hi is already saturated (arity 0),
the formal noun nominalizes the event or fact as a whole rather than filling a participant
gap nakagawa2024 261–262, 507 . The complementizer use is treated in
nominalized-complements-control-raising and for
p ~ pe specifically in nominalization-p-pe .

The appositive construction and the GNMCC question

Shibatani identifies a distinct pattern in Classical/yukar Ainu that he calls the
appositive construction : a clause followed by a bare sensory noun in its
concept form — without the possessive suffix — where the noun names the perceptual
character of the event described by the clause
shibatani1990 41 :

aynu ek hum | person come sound | the sound of a man's coming | Classical Ainu (Itadori/Ishikari data). The bare concept form hum 'sound' — not the possessed hum-i — takes the preceding clause as its content. Shibatani reads this as a construction intermediate between a relative clause and an evidential nominalization. | shibatani1990:41 | HK

okkayo cis sir | man cry appearance | a sight of a man's crying | Again the bare concept form sir 'appearance', not the possessed siri. Compare the evidential siri ne, where the possessed form sir-i heads the clause-final nominalization and is closed by the copula ne. | shibatani1990:41 | HK

In the standard description of the Saru/Chitose perception-complement construction, the
sensory noun carries the possessive suffix — haw-e 'the voice
of—', sir-i 'the appearance of—', hum-i
'the sensation of—' — and the suffix cross-references the preceding finite clause as the
implicit possessor sato2008 175–178 ; nakagawa2024 508 .
On that analysis the construction is an externally-headed formal-noun relative clause: the
head carries arity −1, the possessed suffix makes the semantic link between head and clause
overt, and the possessed formal noun is then an argument of the matrix perception verb or
the copula.

Bugaeva's relativization study and Dal Corso's subsequent work both observe a distributional
difference when the sensory noun appears in its bare (non-possessed) form alongside an
existential or posture verb: hum as 'there is a sensation of',
siri an 'there is an appearance of'
bugaeva2015 100 ; dalcorso2020 52 . In this variant the
possessed link that sustains the external-RC analysis is absent. Bugaeva reads the
distribution as showing characteristics of a general noun-modifying clause construction
(GNMCC) — a type in which a nominal element classifies the clause referent from inside
rather than standing as an external head:

pirka aynu a=ne hum as | good person 4.A=COP sound exist | (someone) felt like a good person | The bare hum 'sound/sensation' (not the possessed hum-i) combined with the existential as 'stand/exist'. Bugaeva reads this as an emerging GNMCC pattern in which hum classifies the type of sensation rather than heading a gapped external relative clause. Dal Corso 2020: 52 cites the same distributional contrast in his structural analysis. | bugaeva2015:100 | SAR

The possessed and non-possessed variants have different distributional profiles: the
possessed head functions as an argument of a matrix perception verb
(e.g. nukar see , nu hear ), while the bare-noun variant
appears as a predication in its own right, typically clause-final. Whether the distributional
difference warrants a distinct structural analysis — GNMCC versus external RC — or reflects
a stage of bleaching along the same grammaticalization cline is not settled by the sources
accessible here ‹corpus-suggested›.

Dal Corso's internally-headed RC and classificatory-incorporation proposal

Dal Corso develops the GNMCC observation into a formal structural claim specific to the
indirect evidential constructions of southern Hokkaido Ainu. He argues that
siri ki , siri an ,
hawe as , and humi as are instances of
internally-headed relative clauses (IHRCs) in which the sensory noun is incorporated
into the event clause as a classifier rather than serving as an external head
dalcorso2020 47, 51–52 . The proposal comprises two components: (i) an
IHRC claim — the notional head is expressed inside the modifying clause — and (ii) a
classificatory noun incorporation (CNI) claim — the mechanism is a type of noun
incorporation in which the incorporated noun classifies the event described by the clause
dalcorso2020 52 .

The argument turns on the contrast between direct and indirect evidential pairs. The
direct forms ( siri ne , hawe ne ,
humi ne ) carry the possessive suffix and close with the copula
ne ; the possessive suffix cross-references the preceding clause as
the possessor, sustaining an external-RC analysis. The indirect forms replace
ne with existential or posture verbs ( an ,
as , ki ), and the sensory noun appears
without possessive morphology; Dal Corso reads the absence of possessive marking as a
diagnostic that the sensory noun is incorporated into the clause as a classifier rather
than standing as an external head outside it dalcorso2020 51–52 .

The analysis has not been adopted by Bugaeva, Satō, or Nakagawa in their accounts of
Hokkaido Ainu ‹contested›. Bugaeva's relativization study treats the same perception
complements as noun-complement structures where the possessed nominal head cross-references
the clause as a whole, and characterizes the non-possessive variant as an emerging GNMCC
pattern rather than a settled IHRC structure
bugaeva2015 79, 92, 100 . Nakagawa and Satō describe the sensory formal
nouns as complementizers of the perception domain without positing an IHRC subcase
nakagawa2024 508 ; sato2008 175–178 . The CNI claim further
intersects with the broader question of what constitutes noun incorporation in a language
where the nominalization-to-lexicalization cline already accounts for many apparent
incorporation effects; the incorporation dimension is taken up in
noun-incorporation-object .

The wider biclausal-versus-monoclausal question — whether [clause]
ruwe ne , [clause] hawe ne , and their kin are biclausal (an
external formal noun heading the preceding clause, the copula predicating the result:
tamura2000 227 ; sato2008 175 ) or are grammaticalizing
toward monoclausal complex predicates ( bugaeva2016 306–315 ;
dalcorso2018 24–25 ) — is addressed with its evidence in
nominalization-p-pe and ruwe-ne-inferential . No constituency
proof in the sources available here settles the question ‹contested›.

The nominalization-to-lexicalization cline

Headless relative clauses sit at one end of a cline whose other end is a lexical noun.
Bugaeva documents a set of Hokkaido Ainu nouns that crystallized historically from
[VP]–p or [VP]–i nominalizations
bugaeva2016 240–251 :

lexicalized noun
historical source (morphemes)
meaning

aep food (< a=e-p )
4.A=eat-NMLZ
'food' (lit. 'thing one eats')

ci=ronnu-p
1PL.A=kill-NMLZ
'fox' (lit. 'things we kill')

ape-o-i
fire-enter-NMLZ
'hearth' (lit. 'place where fire enters')

These forms illustrate Bugaeva's Stage II of Hokkaido Ainu nominalization development: a
nominalizing word follows a full finite clause and the result gradually freezes into a noun,
the clause eventually compressing into a stem bugaeva2016 243, 385–397 .
The productive synchronic counterpart is the headless RC with
p thing (nominalizer) ~ pe thing (nominalizer) ; the line
between productive construction and lexicalized noun is determined by morphological
productivity shibatani1990 33–34 .

Nakagawa's diagnostic distinguishes an active relative-clause nominalization from a compound
noun: the nominalization retains the postposition or applicative that marks the head's
semantic role inside the clause, while a compound leaves the relation between its members
lexically implicit. The contrast with instruments is sharpest:
a=e-suke-p 'kitchen utensil' retains the applicative
e- that licenses the instrument, whereas the plain compound
sukemakiri 'kitchen knife' does not
nakagawa2024 506–507 . The lexicalized inventory is treated in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

Ijäs observes that place nouns such as ipeusi restaurant derive historically
from headless relative clauses with usi place as the head, and that
lexicalized usi -type nouns need no accompanying relational noun when
used synchronically, unlike the productive headless-RC construction
ijas2023 ; nakagawa2024 258 . The observation fits Bugaeva's
Stage-II account and the productive/fossilized distinction Nakagawa draws
bugaeva2016 245–251 .

Dal Corso's CNI proposal connects the cline back to the IHRC discussion. If the bare
sensory noun in the indirect evidential constructions is an incorporated classifier, it
occupies an inside-clause position analogous to the nominalizing element in the
lexicalization cline, rather than the external-head position of the possessed formal noun.
The proposal would extend the incorporation-nominalization interface already visible in the
lexicalized nouns above to a productive grammatical construction, making classificatory
incorporation a third strategy alongside externally-headed and headless relative clauses.
Whether this extension is warranted by the southern Hokkaido data, or whether the indirect
evidential constructions are better handled as partial grammaticalization of the
external-RC pattern, is among the questions Dal Corso raises without fully settling
dalcorso2020 52 ‹contested›.

title: The hine/akusu Contrast and Switch-Reference-Like Linkage
summary: The sequential connectives hine and akusu and the long-running debate over whether they encode same-/different-subject (switch-reference) or discourse (un)expectedness.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The hine/akusu Contrast and Switch-Reference-Like Linkage
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The sequential connectives hine and akusu and the long-running debate over whether they encode same-/different-subject (switch-reference) or discourse (un)expectedness.

The two particles and the question they raise

Two conjunctive particles anchor this chapter. hine and then (neutral chainer)
closes a finite clause and places it before the next in temporal order, without committing to
any causal, manner, or integrative relation between the two events.
akusu and then (discovery) closes a realized first event and opens the clause
in which a consequence is encountered or witnessed. Both are bound, post-predicate particles —
not verbal suffixes, not converbs — and both join fully finite clauses that carry their own
person indexing, polarity, aspect, and any evidential tail, in the pattern established for the
whole inventory in clause-linkage-overview . The typological question they raise —
whether their distribution tracks same-subject versus different-subject reference in the manner
of switch-reference morphology foleyvanvalin1984 — runs through the
descriptive literature on Ainu clause-linking. The evidence supports a discourse-semantic
account rather than a grammaticalized subject-tracking one: neither particle carries a
same-subject or different-subject feature, and the observed correlations with subject continuity
follow from the particles' discourse-semantic roles rather than from an independent
reference-tracking function.

hine: neutral temporal enumeration

hine places two events in temporal sequence and takes no stance on how they are
related. Nakagawa's characterization is precise: hine "lists events in
time order" without any commitment to causal, manner, or purpose connection
nakagawa2024 338 . The bond is therefore looser than
wa and (integrative chainer) , which fuses two acts into one coherent event —
presenting the first as the method, result-state, or cause of the second — and looser than
akusu , which signals a discovered or witnessed consequence
nakagawa2024 338–339 ; sato2008 44 ; refsing1986 242 .

In Saru and Chitose prose, hine is the dominant chainer for the stock
opening that establishes the narrator's household before the story begins. The formula
… an hine oka=an — one or more existence clauses strung together and
closed by the plural existential 'we lived on' — recurs with minimal variation across the
genre. The pattern is so regular that Refsing identifies hine as more
frequent in folk tales than in colloquial speech refsing1986 242 :

a=onaha an a=unuhu an hine oka=an | 4.POSS=father exist 4.POSS=mother exist CONJ exist.PL=4.S | My father was there, my mother was there, and we lived on. | Nakagawa ex. 601; a stock prose-tale opening. The three items — father, mother, living — are listed in order with hine as a pure temporal enumerator, no causal claim made. | nakagawa2024:338 | SAR

Corpus texts show the same frame pivoting into the episode proper via the formula
oka=an pe ne hike 'and we lived on, and …', where the formal-noun
connective hike and (loose link) opens the narrative event:

rikun kanto ta a=saha i=resu hine oka=an pe ne hike | above sky LOC 4.POSS=elder.sister 4.O=raise CONJ exist.PL=4.S NMLZ COP CONJ | My elder sister in the sky raised me, and we lived on, and … | The hine chain closes on oka=an pe ne hike, the standard narrative-pivot formula. The formal-noun pe and the loose-link hike then open the episode proper. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子「uwepeker 民話15」, AA研アイヌ語資料, aa-irc/022#0

Etymology, phonology, and dialect distribution

The particle derives from the nominalizer hi the fact that; the thing that
plus the copula ne be, is : hi + ne >
hine (Tamura 1972b: 158, via refsing1986 243 ;
Kindaichi 1960: 193, via refsing1986 243 ). The source material is reached
here through intermediaries; the etymology is reported, not independently verified. It aligns
with a wider pattern in which nominalization-plus-copula material grammaticalizes into
clause-linkers, visible also in the evidential system (see ruwe-ne-inferential ).

Phonologically, word-initial h is regularly dropped in normal-speed
speech, and the resulting vowel i fuses with a preceding final
consonant: ek hine 'came-and' → [ekine];
a=ne hine → [aneine]. Ishikari, where initial
h is generally absent, writes the form
ine . In rapid speech the vowel itself may elide to bare [ne]
nakagawa2024 338 ; refsing1986 243 .

Dialect distribution is uneven. Tokachi uses hinne (e.g. Honbetsu)
and hine in alternation; Shiranuka and Kushiro write
ine ; Honbetsu lacks both forms entirely, the chaining function
falling to other resources. In Shiranuka, frequency is lower than
wa and tek combined
nakagawa2024 338 . The whole-corpus count of approximately 14,368 tokens —
the highest-frequency temporal-sequence particle with few competing homonyms — is a usable
signal of the particle's prominence, with the usual caveat that Hokkaido and Sakhalin
varieties are not separated in the aggregated count ‹corpus-suggested›.

akusu and awa: sequential discovery

akusu and awa belong to Nakagawa's group-② 'unfolding event' (事態の展開)
connectives, alongside hike and
ayne and finally (after a protracted action)
nakagawa2024 345–347 . Their core function is to present a completed first
event followed by a second event that comes to the participant's attention — "the anterior
event actually happened, and then coincidentally (偶発的に) the posterior event occurred"
sato2009 52 . Satō classes the construction as a factual condition
(事実的条件): the protasis is realized rather than hypothetical, and the apodosis reports the
encountered consequence sato2008 47 ; sato2009 52 .

The discovery flavor of akusu — English 'whereupon', 'and then (I
found/saw that)' — is not a causal claim. Nakagawa makes this explicit: the causal connection
between E1 and E2 is irrelevant to the particle; what it marks is that E2 comes to the
attention of a participant after E1 is complete, whatever the causal structure may be
nakagawa2024 345 :

nea pon yuk a=rayke akusu | that small deer 4.A=kill CONJ | After killing that small deer, … | The fourth-person agent a= completes the killing; akusu opens the clause in which the narrator discovers or experiences what follows. The continuation is not preserved in the banked fragment. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | 川上まつ子「isoytak 独話1」, AA研アイヌ語資料, aa-irc/003#73

Akusu attaches after a clause-final evidential tail — confirming the
ordering [VP]–evidential–connective established for the system as a whole. When the first
clause closes on ruwe ne , the anterior event is presented as an
established fact before the discovery linker opens the next clause:

kanpi ku=sanke ru-we ne akusu itasa kanpi ek ru-we ne | letter 1SG.A=send.out track-POSS COP CONJ in.return letter come track-POSS COP | I had sent a letter, and in return a letter came. | Satō ex. 16 / Satō 2009 ex. 32. The discovery linker attaches outside the ruwe ne evidential tail. The cross-linguistic asymmetry with Japanese — factual と/たら cannot follow preceding のだ, but Ainu akusu can follow ruwe ne — is discussed in §5. | sato2008:47; sato2009:52 | CHI

The akusu/awa register and dialect split

Akusu and awa are semantically equivalent;
no descriptive source establishes any difference in meaning. Their distribution is
conditioned by register and dialect: in Saru, prose texts favour
akusu while verse texts heavily favour
awa ; Horobetsu and Ishikari use awa
regardless of register; Shizunai and Tokachi use akusu
nakagawa2024 345 . Satō's characterization of awa
as the oral-literature (口承文芸) variant aligns with the verse skew in Saru
sato2008 47–48 ‹consensus›:

particle
Saru prose
Saru verse
Horobetsu / Ishikari
Shizunai / Tokachi
etymology

akusu / akus
primary
rare
rare
primary
a (PFV) + kusu (consequential)

awa
rare
primary
primary
rare
a (PFV) + ?

The etymology of akusu as a (perfective
auxiliary) + kusu (consequential) is supported by Refsing and is
consistent with the particle's semantics — a completed event ( a :
'situation done and closed') from which a consequence follows
( kusu ) refsing1986 248 . The second morpheme of
awa has not been identified with confidence
refsing1986 248 . Shizunai uses the phonologically reduced form
akus (word-final vowel devoicing and deletion)
refsing1986 248 .

Whole-corpus word-form counts register approximately 3,861 tokens for
akusu and 2,071 for awa . Both are among the
cleaner particle counts with few homonyms, and the ratio is consistent with a mixed-register
corpus containing proportionally more prose than verse ‹corpus-suggested›. Per-function
counts that would separate Hokkaido from Sakhalin are not available from the current search
tools.

The switch-reference question

Switch-reference — dedicated morphological marking on clause-linkers that co-indexes subject
continuity between clauses foleyvanvalin1984 — is absent from Hokkaido
Ainu. The language has no converbs, no non-finite verb forms, and no SS/DS feature on any
conjunctive particle bugaeva2022 ; nakagawa2024 514 . What can be
asked is whether the choice among particles statistically or semantically correlates with
subject continuity, and whether any such correlation is grammaticalized or follows from the
particles' discourse-semantic content.

Statistical tendencies and their source

The one quantified figure in the literature is Refsing's Shizunai count: approximately
ninety per cent of the predicate pairs joined by wa share a subject
refsing1986 240 . No comparable count exists for
hine or akusu in any Hokkaido variety; the
quantitative base is a single informant's Shizunai material (factbase §6, gap 6). The
ninety-per-cent figure is consistent with a preference but demonstrably not with a
constraint: Satō shows that wa tolerates different-subject junctions
without any change in form, as in the child-throws-stone / barrel-falls example where the
thrower and the barrel are different participants — a junction Japanese
〜て cannot make sato2008 45–46 . No primary grammar
states a same-subject requirement for hine , and its neutral-listing
semantics entails no such restriction nakagawa2024 338 ;
refsing1986 242 .

The simultaneity particle kor while (simultaneous) provides a direct test:
it takes different-subject complements without restriction, as in Saru
ku=horipi kor en=nukar 'she looked at me while I danced'
ijas2023 , corroborated by an oral-literature example in Satō
sato2008 49–50 . Shizunai kane "generally" takes
same-subject complements but exceptions are attested refsing1986 250 . Across
the particle inventory, any same-subject preference is a graded tendency, not a binary SS/DS
grammatical feature.

Ijäs offers the pedagogical heuristic that negated clauses take
no when the two predicates share a subject and
wa when they do not ijas2023 . Nakagawa and Satō derive
the no / wa choice from the aspect and polarity
of the first predicate — stative and negated action predicates take
no — rather than from subject identity
nakagawa2024 340–341 ; sato2008 45–47 . The Ijäs framing is
noted because it has entered pedagogical circulation ‹contested›; the primary descriptive
sources do not support it.

Expectedness and discourse stance

Nakagawa's and Satō's analyses locate the hine /
akusu contrast in event structure and narrative stance, not in
subject continuity. The neutral temporal enumerator hine naturally
chains a single protagonist's sequential acts — producing a predominance of same-subject
pairs without encoding subject identity. The discovery connective
akusu marks that the narrator encounters or witnesses the second
event after the first is complete, a framing that often foregrounds a new or unexpected
participant and thereby produces an apparent different-subject tendency. The observed SS/DS
distributions are statistical consequences of discourse function, not evidence for a
grammaticalized opposition nakagawa2024 338, 345 ;
sato2008 44, 47 ‹consensus for the basic semantic descriptions›.

The narrative boundary device

The nearest structural analogue to a switch-reference signal is discourse-organizational
rather than morphological. The Handbook's descriptive grammar notes a tendency for the
sequence of the perfective auxiliary a perfective (situation complete)
followed by a connective such as korka but, although ,
wa , or the formal-noun connective p thing (formal noun as connective)
to fall at transitions of scene, topic, or acting participant in narrative
bugaeva2022 ‹speculative›. The perfective marks the anterior situation as
completed and cut off from the present; an adverb of time typically follows to open the new
episode. The printed location of this observation has not been confirmed against the
Handbook print edition (factbase §6, gap 5), so the claim is treated as speculative.

The device is a clause-chaining boundary convention. No morpheme on the connective particle
encodes subject identity; the signal comes from the co-occurrence of the perfective auxiliary
with a particular linker at a discourse transition. Subject identity across a chain is
tracked by the obligatory person affixes on each finite predicate, the mechanism drawn
together in reference-tracking .

Graded positions

The competing positions, arranged by evidence grade:

position
source
grade

No morphological switch-reference; conjunctive particles carry no SS/DS feature;
person affixes track argument continuity
bugaeva2022 ; nakagawa2024 514
consensus

wa tends same-subject (≈90% in Shizunai) but imposes no
grammatical constraint
refsing1986 240 ; sato2008 45–46
corpus-suggested (one dialect, one informant; no Saru count for hine)

hine / akusu encode discourse stance
(neutral ordering vs discovery), not subject continuity
nakagawa2024 338, 345 ; sato2008 44, 47
consensus

no / wa conditioned by SS vs DS
ijas2023
contested — primary grammars condition on polarity/aspect, not subject identity

Perfective a + korka /
p tends to fall at scene and actor transitions
bugaeva2022 (Handbook Part II, page unverified)
speculative

The SS tendency of hine and the apparent DS lean of
akusu both follow from discourse semantics; no separate
reference-tracking feature is warranted; corpus annotation of subject continuity at
these junctions would provide the empirical test
synthesis from the above; no single source states this directly
original-needs-review

The ‹original-needs-review› synthesis draws on what each source establishes independently.
Hine neutrally chains events along a single narrative strand — which
by default is the protagonist's sequential acts — hence the same-subject predominance
without a grammatical constraint. Akusu frames the second event as
encountered or discovered after the first is complete, a perspective that frequently involves
a new or surprising participant and thereby shows an apparent different-subject lean. Neither
particle requires a particular subject configuration; the SS/DS distributions reflect the
default narrative configuration of each connective's domain. A corpus annotation of subject
continuity across hine and akusu junctions
in Saru text would supply the empirical test ‹original-needs-review›.

The evidential–connective interface

Conjunctive particles attach outside the clause-final evidential frame. The ordering
[VP]–evidential–connective, established for the system as a whole in
clause-linkage-overview , means that akusu can follow a
ruwe ne tail, yielding … ruwe ne akusu … . The
evidential sits above clause-internal aspect but below the clause-linker, so
kusu ne ruwe ne is grammatical and
*ruwe ne kusu ne is not nakagawa2024 393 .

Satō identifies a typologically notable property of this interface. Japanese factual conditional
と / たら cannot follow a preceding
のだ , yet Ainu places no such constraint on the
ruwe ne akusu sequence: the first event is presented as
knowledge-established fact and the discovery linker then opens the encountered consequence
sato2009 52–53 . The construction presents the two layers as separately
stackable because they occupy distinct positions in the clausal hierarchy.

A member of the aynu-corpora Discord community corroborates both the frequency and the
discourse function of this sequence. nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) observes that
ruwe ne is associated with scene transition and summary framing in
prose narrative, and that ruwe ne akusu functions as a compound of
that discourse anchoring with the discovery connective
aynucorporadiscord ‹asserted; corroborated by sato2009 52 ›. The
observation is consistent with the evidential+linker ordering described by Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 393 and with the ruwe ne system set out
in ruwe-ne-inferential .

Dialect edges

The particles described above rest primarily on Saru and Chitose attestation. Several
dialect-specific details bear on the comparison. Shizunai uses
akus (the word-final vowel devoiced and deleted) for the same discovery
function refsing1986 248 . Horobetsu and Ishikari favour
awa regardless of register, while prose-oriented Shizunai and Tokachi
use akusu as the default nakagawa2024 345 . The
category is stable across the dialect continuum; phonological shape and genre weighting shift,
not the underlying semantic function.

Tokachi uses hinne alongside hine for neutral
chaining; Honbetsu Tokachi shows neither form. These micro-variations caution against
projecting Saru frequency data onto the dialect cluster as a whole
nakagawa2024 338 . The full topology of the conjunctive-particle system across
Hokkaido dialects is surveyed in clause-linkage-overview ; the conditional and
concessive members of the system are treated in
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses .

Open questions

The foundational Saru description of the conjunctive particles, Tamura's 1972 study of the
〜して constructions (Tamura 1972b), is the primary source for nearly
every claim about hine in the grammars consulted here, but it was not
read directly — all attributed page numbers reach it through
nakagawa2024 and refsing1986 ‹speculative for those page numbers›.
The quantitative base is thin: Refsing's ninety-per-cent same-subject count for
wa in Shizunai is the only published figure; no per-function counts
for hine or akusu in any Hokkaido variety
exist in the accessible literature. The whole-corpus totals — approximately 14,368 for
hine , 3,861 for akusu , 2,071 for
awa — are homonym-mixed, Hokkaido-and-Sakhalin combined, and carry no
subject-continuity annotation. Whether akusu junctions show measurably
lower same-subject rates than hine junctions — the empirical test of
the ‹original-needs-review› synthesis — remains open and requires bespoke corpus annotation
over a tagged Saru text. The reference-tracking apparatus as a whole is drawn together in
reference-tracking ; narrative cohesion and tail-head linkage are treated in
tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion .
