title: The Indefinite/Fourth Person: Forms, Reference, and Agent-Defocusing
summary: The morphology of the indefinite person (a=/an=/=an, i=) and its referential range — generic 'one', inclusive 'we', definite fourth-person reference — and its agent-defocusing 'passive'/impersonal use with the does-Ainu-have-a-passive debate.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Indefinite/Fourth Person: Forms, Reference, and Agent-Defocusing
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The morphology of the indefinite person (a=/an=/=an, i=) and its referential range — generic 'one', inclusive 'we', definite fourth-person reference — and its agent-defocusing 'passive'/impersonal use with the does-Ainu-have-a-passive debate.

The formal paradigm

Three verbal indexes make up the Hokkaido Ainu form-set called the fourth person by
Nakagawa, the indefinite by Bugaeva, and the inclusive person by Takahashi:
a= or dialectally an= as the subject of a
transitive verb (A role), =an as the subject of an intransitive verb
(S), and i= as the object (O). The full S/A/O architecture of all four
persons is laid out in person-marking-architecture ; this chapter covers the
internal paradigm and the referential values licensed by the row.

role
Hokkaido form (Saru/Chitose baseline)
position
principal values

A: subject of a transitive verb
a= , dialectally an=
prefix, before the transitive stem

inclusive or hortative agent; generic/impersonal human agent; honorific addressee;
narrative protagonist; possessor in fourth-person nominal forms

S: subject of an intransitive verb
=an
suffix, after the intransitive stem

inclusive or hortative subject; honorific addressee; narrative or logophoric subject

O: object of a transitive verb
i=
prefix, before the transitive stem

fourth-person patient, recipient, or undergoer; narrator's 'me/us'; honored or inclusive
object

The row is parallel in structure to the first plural exclusive
ci= / =as / un= : both
keep distinct forms for all three roles. The S/A distinction is expressed by the side of
attachment — a transitivity-conditioned position switch absent from the first singular and the
second person. That asymmetry is the morphological basis on which Bugaeva calls these two rows
tripartite bugaeva2012 471–472 ; bugaeva2008 32 ; nakagawa2024 165–169, 174 .

eani hem paye=an ro | you also go.PL=4.S HORT | Let you and me go too! | The addressee-inclusive hortative uses the plural stem paye with =an following as S. | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

Side-encoding and allomorphy

The subject index selects its position by transitivity. With an intransitive predicate, the
fourth-person subject appears as the suffix =an ; with a transitive
predicate, the same referent is indexed by the prefix a= . The
following sentence contains both on adjacent predicates of different transitivity nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 471–472 .

rus patek a=mi wa hotke=an pa | fur only 4.A=wear CONJ sleep=4.S PL | We wore only fur and slept. | The transitive mi 'wear' takes the prefix a=; the intransitive hotke 'sleep' takes the suffix =an. The plural auxiliary pa follows outside the person index. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 7 [aa-irc/005#157]

In Saru and the dialects to the west, a= is the regular
transitive-subject form. Elsewhere the longer allomorph an= appears in
varying distributions: Tokachi uses an= before vowel-initial stems
without first-syllable accent and a= elsewhere; Ishikari and Samani
favour an= ; Shiranuka mostly has an= with
a= chiefly before ye say ; Shizunai shows no such
split. Even Saru, Chitose, and Horobetsu preserve an= in verse
formulae, and Nakagawa regards an= as historically prior nakagawa2024 167–168 ‹speculative›. The Hokkaido-wide quantitative distribution of the two allomorphs is
incompletely documented ‹corpus-suggested›.

The clitic-or-affix status of =an and a= is
contested: Bugaeva treats =an as word-like and
a= as clitic-like on the basis of prosodic independence and
separability; Nakagawa writes all person indexes with = as a
convenient notation and separately documents the high autonomy of =an ;
Sakaguchi's formal clitic criterion — developed on Sakhalin evidence — converges on the same
autonomy ranking for the fourth-person S form bugaeva2012 472–473 ; nakagawa2024 49, 183 ; sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85, 163 ‹contested›. Ordering and wordhood
diagnostics are treated in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

The plural auxiliary pa of Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari positions
itself outside the person index (stem =an pa ), while the stem-forming
plural suffix -pa sits inside (stem -pa=an ).
Nakagawa's minimal pair from Saru makes the contrast explicit nakagawa2024 158 ; sakaguchi2024 12 :

nenpoka iki=an pa wa siknu=an wa | somehow do=4.S PL CONJ survive=4.S CONJ | Somehow we managed and survived, and … | The plural auxiliary pa follows iki=an, external to the fourth-person S suffix =an. | nakagawa2024:158 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 87

irwak sinne sorekusu iki-pa=an | sibling like because do-PL=4.S | because we were like siblings … | The stem-forming suffix -pa precedes =an; the ordering is reversed relative to the auxiliary pa in the preceding example. | nakagawa2024:158 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 88

Referential range: five uses

Nakagawa lists five referential values for the same formal row — addressee-inclusive first
plural, honorific second person, indefinite or impersonal human agent, the narrator or
protagonist of oral narrative, and the quoted speaker inside reported speech — stressing that
no single meaning covers all five nakagawa2024 174–179 . Tamura distributes
the same Saru forms across several distinct persons in her eleven-person system; Bugaeva uses
the cover label "indefinite" and reanalyses the quotation use as logophoric; Satō treats the
forms as polysemous; Takahashi's Tokachi account adopts "inclusive person", arguing that the
inclusive value is primary and the other uses follow from it tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34, 39 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 ; sato2008 203–208, 259–262 ; takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›.

function
typical reference
number behaviour

① inclusive
speaker plus addressee, 'we including you'
plural verb stem; common in cohortative frames

② honorific addressee
esteemed second person; human addressing adult men or kamuy
plural stem even for a single addressee

③ indefinite / impersonal
generic human agent, 'people, one, someone'
plural stem; agent may appear in an or wa phrase

④ narrative self-reference
protagonist or narrator's "I" in oral narrative
singular stems possible in Saru/Chitose; plural elsewhere

⑤ logophoric reported speaker
the "I/we" of a quoted or reported speaker
dialectal scope; best established for Saru

Inclusive and impersonal reference

The inclusive value fills the gap left by the first plural exclusive. The set
ci= / =as / un=
excludes the addressee; addressee-inclusive 'we' therefore requires the fourth-person row
nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 ; tamura1972 18–19 ; bugaeva2012 472, 475 . Tokachi preserves the same clusivity split, and Takahashi observes that the
inclusive/fourth-person series triggers plural verb forms even where the referent would
otherwise be singular takahashi2015 206–209 .

In its impersonal or generic value, a= indexes an unspecified human
agent on a transitive predicate. The patient remains in the O role; the verb retains
transitive valence; an explicit agent-like participant may appear in a source phrase headed by
or wa 'from'. Japanese translations routinely render the construction
with a passive, but Ainu has no subject-promoting passive morphology: the agent does not
receive subject marking, and the patient is not promoted to a nominative argument tamura1972 34 ; sato2008 203–205 ; nakagawa2024 176–177 .

wen kamuy a=maketa wa a=rayke ru-we ne | bad god 4.A=defeat CONJ 4.A=kill track-POSS COP | The evil kamuy was defeated and killed. | The patient wen kamuy stays in the object role across two transitive predicates; a= supplies an unspecified human agent. | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 145

kamuy or wa a=epunkine wa kamuy or wa a=kasuy wa | god place from 4.A=protect CONJ god place from 4.A=help CONJ | being protected by the gods and helped by the gods, … | The source phrase kamuy or wa names the agent-like participant; the verbs carry fourth-person A and the patient is not promoted to grammatical subject. | nakagawa2024:177 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 146

Ijäs's learner grammar illustrates the same generic-human value, glossing
a=nu as 'people hear (it)' in a lesson on perceptible sounds ijas2023 . Her notation uses a hyphen where this chapter writes = ; that
reflects a pedagogical convention carrying no claim about clitic or affix status.

maw a=nu | breeze 4.A=hear | One hears the breeze. | Constructed from Ijäs Lesson 7, where a=nu illustrates the generic-human A value; maw 'breeze' fills the object slot. | ijas2023 | HK

The generic-agent prefix combines with the nominalizer
p ~ pe to yield participant nominalizations of
the type a=e-p 'food' (literally 'what people eat'). The following
example shows a=e p in a headless relative construction:

tapan kina a=e p ne toan kina somo a=e p ne | this plant 4.A=eat NMLZ COP that plant NEG 4.A=eat NMLZ COP | This plant is one that people eat; that plant is one that people do not eat. | The head p is the thing eaten; a= supplies the unspecified human eater. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 14 [aa-irc/020#27]

The morphology and arity diagnostics of p ~
pe are developed in nominalization-p-pe .

The fourth-person object i= and the detransitive prefix i-

The object index i= belongs to the same paradigmatic row as
a= and =an . In narrative and address it indexes
the fourth-person argument as patient, recipient, or undergoer — the narrator's 'me' or the
honored person as object nakagawa2024 169 .

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=lodge CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The repeated i= is fourth-person O, indexing the narrator/protagonist as object and recipient across three transitive predicates. | nakagawa2024:169 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

a=rehe Ikuresuye sekor a=i=reko | 4.POSS=name Ikuresuye QUOT 4.A=4.O=name | I was named Ikuresuye. | The naming formula combines fourth-person possessive a= on rehe 'name', fourth-person A on the predicate, and fourth-person O i= for the narrator/protagonist. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker folktale 7 [aa-irc/005#1]

The prefix i= is phonologically identical to the detransitivising
prefix i- 'something/someone', which incorporates a generic object and
reduces the verb's transitivity. The two are notated differently — =
for the person index, - for the derivational prefix — and they differ
syntactically: the person index occupies the O slot of an already-transitive stem, while the
antipassive derives an intransitive stem from a transitive base. Tamura already keeps the two
apart on functional grounds; their diachronic relationship is disputed. Older accounts derive
the detransitive prefix from the fourth-person object; Bugaeva argues in the opposite
direction, connecting the antipassive to O-incorporation of a generic noun; Nam proposes a
generic-noun source *hi/*ihi 'thing, place' for the detransitive
prefix directly tamura1972 35 ; bugaeva2021antip 235–239 ; nam2021 62–74 ‹contested›. The detransitive prefix and its morphology are treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i .

Honorific, narrative, and logophoric uses

Honorific use applies the fourth-person row to an esteemed addressee. Nakagawa describes
women addressing adult men and humans addressing kamuy in prayer; Tamura documents the same
politeness value for Saru; Satō groups it with the literary-person system of oral performance
nakagawa2024 175–176 ; tamura1972 28 ; sato2008 259–262 . The verb takes a plural stem even for a single referent, a number agreement conditioned
by register rather than by the count of persons.

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | A single addressee is indexed by =an on the plural motion stem arki; the plural stem reflects honorific register. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140, woman addressing an adult man

In oral narrative the protagonist's first person is expressed by the same row. In Saru and
Chitose a singular narrator may appear with singular verb stems and the pronoun
asinuma ; Horobetsu and Shizunai display opposite mismatches between
verb number and the available pronouns nakagawa2024 174–179, 254–256 . Nakagawa
treats the narrative use as the indefinite person applied to an unanchored referent; Satō
argues the literary self-reference derives from a first-person strategy and cannot be
collapsed into the indefinite; Bugaeva, finding comparable uses in non-folklore Asahikawa
texts, removes the dependence on an elegant register entirely sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ; bugaeva2008 37–44 ‹contested›.

ratcitara hosipi=an yakka a=uni un tunasno hosipi=an pe ne kusu | slowly return=4.S although 4.POSS=home to quickly return=4.S NMLZ COP because | Although I returned slowly, because I was able to return quickly to my home, … | A singular narrator uses =an on both intransitive predicates and the possessive prefix a= on uni 'home'; the singular stem hosipi confirms singular-narrator morphology in Saru. | nakagawa2024:165 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 109

The fifth use — the quoted speaker's self-reference inside a reported-speech clause — is
analysed by Bugaeva as logophoric: the fourth-person row tracks the discourse-source of the
embedded proposition, functioning as a participant-tracking device independent of literary
genre bugaeva2008 37–44 ; tamura1972 19–20, 22–23 ; nakagawa2024 179 . The social and discourse dimensions of the honorific and logophoric values are developed
in fourth-person-honorific and logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse .

Terminology and comparative note

The competing labels encode different analytical choices. Nakagawa's "fourth person" positions
the row as a formal residual class within a four-person paradigm, gathering everything that
persons one to three cannot express nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 . Tamura's Saru
analysis distributes the same forms across the inclusive, two quotative persons, the honorific
second, and the indefinite, treating the labels as functionally distinct values rather than a
single form-class tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34, 39 . Bugaeva's "indefinite (IND)"
foregrounds the generic and inclusive core; the quotation use is relabelled logophoric, a
move supported by its occurrence outside the narrative register bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 . Takahashi's "inclusive person" gives the Tokachi
system a unified participant label, arguing that the inclusive function is primary takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›.

Dal Corso applies the label "fourth person" to the cognate Sakhalin AN-series by word form,
while his diachronic reanalysis — which rejects the tripartite component for Sakhalin and
treats the cognate system as mixed nominative–neutral–inverse — is explicitly cautioned as
inapplicable to Hokkaido dalcorso2025align 187–188, 200, 213 ; sakaguchi2024 26–27, 162–163 ‹contested›. For Hokkaido, the tripartite characterization of
a= / =an / i= —
formalized in Bugaeva's mixed-alignment account and adopted in the architecture chapter —
remains the standard analysis bugaeva2012 472 .

title: The kuni Complementizer and Purpose Complements
summary: The irrealis complementizer/nominalizer kuni and the purposive kus(u) / kuni ne, marking future-oriented complements of manipulation, desire, and purpose.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The kuni Complementizer and Purpose Complements
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The irrealis complementizer/nominalizer kuni and the purposive kus(u) / kuni ne, marking future-oriented complements of manipulation, desire, and purpose.

kuni in the complementizer system

The particle kuni nominalizes a finite VP into a nominal whose content
is irrealis, prospective, or deontic — what the reporting subject expects, wishes, or presents
as obligatory. It is a member of the small set of complementizers that take a saturated VP and
make it the argument of a transitive matrix predicate, forming what Nakagawa calls the
indirect-speech ( 間接話法 ) system
nakagawa2024 475–476 . The complementizer yak marks
the retrospective or ongoing counterpart; hi and
p ~ pe nominalize without a temporal restriction
nakagawa2024 475–478 ; sato2008 176–177 . The closed class of
formal-defective-nouns formal nouns that includes
p ~ pe is defined there; the arity arithmetic
that separates complementizer from relative-clause use is developed in
nominalization-p-pe .

Three construction types exploit kuni . Before
ye say or ramu think , it nominalizes a VP expressing what the
reporting subject says will happen, believes should happen, or directs someone to do. Before
the formal noun p thing; (pejorative) person , it yields a purposive or deontic
nominal ( kuni p ). And before the copula
ne be directly, Classical Ainu data show an expectative predication meaning
'it must be that' or 'in order that' ( kuni ne )
shibatani1990 83–84 . These three are treated in the sections below.

Corpus search returns 6,113 tokens of kuni (rank 36), a figure that
conflates the complementizer with any phonologically identical material and gives an upper
bound on the construction's frequency.

The irrealis complement of ye and ramu

The matrix verbs that select kuni are
ye say and ramu think . Each member of the indirect-speech
complementizer set marks the temporal or epistemic stance the reporting subject takes toward
the reported content. kuni occupies the prospective end of the
system: the embedded VP describes what will, should, or is wished to come about
nakagawa2024 476 .

complementizer
temporal / epistemic value
matrix verbs

kuni
future / inferred / wished / directive
ye , ramu

kunak
future / inferred
ye , ramu

yak
already-happened / ongoing
ye , ramu , takar

kusu
directive (combined with a causative context)
ye

Two syntactic properties hold across all four members. Final particles of the main-clause type
( na , ya , etc.) cannot appear inside the
embedded clause; they are anchored to the speaker's own utterance, not to the reported one.
And the referential perspective inside the embedded clause shifts from the speaker's to the
reporting subject's: a first-person speaker becomes third or fourth person as the locus of
predication nakagawa2024 475 . These properties confirm that each
complementizer heads a nominal object: even yak , originally a
conditional conjunctive, cannot be followed directly by an intransitive verb
(* yak hawean 'that someone spoke' is ungrammatical as a standalone),
because as a nominal it must be the transitive verb's object
nakagawa2024 475–476 .

hosipi=an kuni a=ye | return=4.S PURP 4.A=say | I said I would return. | Nakagawa's own example. The fourth-person intransitive suffix =an marks the reported subject (the speaker's self-reference shifted to 4th person inside the embedded clause); kuni nominalizes the prospective VP as the object of ye. | nakagawa2024:476 | HK

ahun kuni a=ye akusu toykooripak | enter PURP 4.A=say CONJ be.very.abashed | When I told them to enter, they were very abashed. | kuni nominalizes the directive ahun 'enter' as the content of ye 'say'; akusu links the result clause temporally. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8 (part 2) [aa-irc/007#213]

ramu 'think' selects the same kuni
complement when the thinker's belief is prospective or deontic. The following illustration
conforms to the pattern Nakagawa establishes nakagawa2024 476 :

pirka kuni ku=ramu | be.good PURP 1SG.A=think | I think it would be good. | Constructed to illustrate the kuni complement of ramu; the pattern is established by Nakagawa 2024: 476, which lists both ye and ramu as matrix verbs for kuni. | HK

The distinction between kuni and kunak within
the prospective subsystem is the most discussed. Nakagawa assigns the directive reading
exclusively to kuni : a command or instruction reported under
ye takes kuni ; the two forms are not freely
interchangeable even in purely inferred contexts
nakagawa2024 476–477 ‹corpus-suggested›. The
kuni / yak contrast is cleaner: the pair
divides prospective from retrospective content, mirroring a tense–modality divide in the
reported proposition.

kuni p: purposive and deontic nominals

When kuni is followed by the formal noun
p (allomorph pe after a consonant-final stem),
the compound nominalizes the preceding VP and yields two related readings depending on how
the head is construed: a purposive nominal 'thing to V / what is to be done', and a deontic
or propositional nominal 'what will / must happen'. Both are well attested in the Saru corpus.

The purposive reading is clearest when kuni p denotes objects or
activities defined by their intended function. Two parallel purposive nominals appear in a
single clause:

míre kuni p ére kuni p nepki ne ki wa | clothe PURP NMLZ feed PURP NMLZ task COP do CONJ | making things to clothe (the grandchild) and things to feed (the grandchild) her task, | Two parallel kuni p nominals ('thing to clothe', 'thing to feed') are predicated as the grandmother's task via nepki ne ki wa; the construction names purpose-defined objects. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#355]

The deontic or propositional reading appears when kuni p is itself
predicated. Combination with the copula and the inferential formal noun
ruwe yields the frame
[VP] kuni p ne ruwe ne , which presents the VP-content as inevitable
or obligatory and asserts it as established fact (see ruwe-ne-inferential ):

a=e=túskote kuni p ne ru-we ne na | 4.A=2SG.O=tie.up PURP NMLZ COP track-POSS COP FIN | You will surely be tied up. | The fourth-person agent a= with the second-person object e= yields a passive-like reading; kuni p forms the deontic nominal, ne is the copula predicated over it, and ruwe ne frames the whole as recognized fact. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2 [aa-irc/004#130]

nen poka iki wa yayetuytak kuni p ne ru-we ne na | who even do CONJ make.a.living PURP NMLZ COP track-POSS COP FIN | Somehow, (the old man) will surely manage to make a living. | The embedded clause nen poka iki wa yayetuytak expresses the desired future outcome; kuni p ne ruwe ne presents its coming-to-pass as deontic and epistemically certain. nen poka 'somehow / by whatever means' is an indefinite expression built on the interrogative nen 'who'. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 10 [aa-irc/010#139]

The four main patterns built on kuni , ranging from indirect speech
through purposive nominal to the full epistemic deontic frame, are summarized in the
following table nakagawa2024 476 ; bugaeva2016 294 :

pattern
structural description
reading

[VP] kuni [ye / ramu]
kuni nominalizes VP as argument of matrix predicate
indirect speech / thought with prospective content

[VP] kuni p
kuni + formal noun p
purposive nominal 'thing to V', 'what is to be done'

[VP] kuni p ne
deontic nominal + copula
deontic predication 'is a thing that must / will happen'

[VP] kuni p ne ruwe ne
deontic nominal + copula + inferential formal noun
epistemic deontic 'it is surely a thing that must / will happen'

The p of kuni p is the same formal noun
treated in nominalization-p-pe . Its arity (−1) closes a purposive clause
with an open participant slot (purposive nominal reading) or nominalizes a saturated
irrealis clause as a whole (deontic reading). The complement-taking predicates that select
these nominals are discussed in nominalized-complements-control-raising ; the
overlap with the deontic modal domain in deontic-necessity .

kuni ne: expectative predication

Shibatani groups kuni followed directly by the copula
ne alongside the other formal-noun-plus-copula modal expressions
( ruwe ne , siri ne ,
hawe ne , humi ne ) under the label "expectative
mood". The form yields two readings in his Classical Ainu data: an epistemic 'it must be
that [VP]' and a purposive 'in order that [VP]'
shibatani1990 83–84 .

On this account, kuni itself functions as a formal-noun-like head
nominalizing the preceding VP; the copula predicates that nominalization. The two readings
correspond directly to the deontic and purposive readings of kuni p ne ,
where the additional formal noun p is present between
kuni and the copula. Whether bare
kuni ne and kuni p ne are diachronically
related variants or a single construction with optionally present
p is not settled by the sources read here ‹speculative›.

Shibatani's data are from Classical epic Ainu (yukar traditions of the Itadori and Ishikari
areas). No read source provides modern Saru or Chitose corpus examples of bare
kuni ne without the intervening p ; the banked
Saru corpus examples above all show the kuni p ne form. Nakagawa's
grammar, based on modern Saru and Chitose, records
kuni p ne ruwe ne as the productive frame
nakagawa2024 476 .

The formal-noun-plus-copula paradigm underlying both constructions connects
kuni to the evidential system: Nakagawa observes that clause-final
evidential phrases ( ruwe ne , siri ne , etc.)
constitute one type of complementizer applied to a main clause, and
kuni participates in the same nominal architecture
nakagawa2024 508 . The full paradigm is set out in
evidential-schema-overview . Purpose clauses built on
kuni in adverbial position are treated in
causal-and-purpose-clauses .

Morphological status and open questions

Two analyses of kuni appear in the literature and make different
predictions about its internal structure. Nakagawa treats it as a conjunctive particle
( 接続助詞 ) with formal-noun-like behavior
( 形式名詞的な用法 ) — a free form that nominalizes the preceding VP and
feeds it to a transitive matrix predicate, on the same footing as
yak and kunak
nakagawa2024 476 . On this analysis, kuni p is a
sequence of two free forms: the particle kuni followed by the formal
noun p .

Bugaeva's morphological analysis decomposes kuni-p as
kun-i-p — an obligation-related root
kun- (semantics uncertain), a possessive suffix
-i , and the nominalizer -p — yielding a deontic
head meaning 'the thing that must be done', without necessarily treating
kuni and p as syntactically separate
bugaeva2016 294 ‹contested›. The lexical source of the root
kun- remains unverified.

The broader constituency question — whether kuni p ne ruwe ne is
biclausal (a nominalizing particle and formal noun heading the preceding clause, closed by
the copula) or is grammaticalizing toward a monoclausal complex predicate — runs parallel to
the same debate for the evidential frame ruwe ne , discussed in
nominalization-p-pe and ruwe-ne-inferential . No read source provides
a constituency test that decides between them for the kuni
construction specifically ‹contested›
nakagawa2024 476 ; bugaeva2016 294 .

Shibatani's Classical data add a historical dimension: his "expectative mood" label groups
kuni ne with the possessed sensory-noun heads, suggesting the
formal-noun-like behavior Nakagawa observes reflects a partly grammaticalized noun origin —
the same diachronic cline by which ruwe ,
siri , and hawe moved from sensory nouns to
nominalization heads shibatani1990 83–84 ‹speculative›.

title: Lexical-Head and Formal-Noun Nominalization (kur, uske, ruwe/hawe/siri/humi)
summary: Nominalization headed by lexical nouns (kur 'person', uske/usi 'place', ike) and by the grammaticalized formal-noun set (ruwe, hawe, siri, humi, hi, pe) acting as clause-nominalizing heads.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Lexical-Head and Formal-Noun Nominalization (kur, uske, ruwe/hawe/siri/humi)
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: Nominalization headed by lexical nouns (kur 'person', uske/usi 'place', ike) and by the grammaticalized formal-noun set (ruwe, hawe, siri, humi, hi, pe) acting as clause-nominalizing heads.

Overview

Hokkaido Ainu forms nominalized clauses by placing a
formal-defective-nouns formal noun after a finite predicate. The verb retains its
ordinary person marking; the formal noun supplies the nominal head and converts the preceding
material into an NP. The class is closed and its members differ in the semantic profile they
impose — 'thing', 'person', 'place', 'appearance', 'voice', and so on — and in whether they
function as relative-clause heads, complementizers, or both
nakagawa2024 257–262, 503–513 ; sato2008 174–180 ;
bugaeva2016 226–228 .

The allomorphs p thing, person (nominalizer) ~
pe thing, person (nominalizer) , their participant-nominalization and
complementizer functions, and the arity diagnostic that distinguishes the two readings are
treated in nominalization-p-pe . The gap strategy, accessibility hierarchy, and
postposition stranding that characterize all relative clauses are covered in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses . The clause-final evidential uses of
ruwe the trace of , siri the appearance of ,
hawe the voice of , and humi the sound/feeling of are treated in
evidential-schema-overview . The present chapter addresses the morphological contrast
between the concept form and the affiliative (possessed) form of these nouns,
the semantic profiles that govern head-selection across the formal-noun inventory,
hi thing, place, time in its several uses, the sensory-noun formal nouns as
non-evidential perception-complement heads, and the cline from clausal nominalization to
lexicalized noun.

Concept form and affiliative (possessed) form

Each formal noun has two shapes described in concept-form-affiliative-form . The
bare concept form (概念形) is the citation form of the noun, autonomous in a sentence.
The affiliative form , or possessed form (所属形), marks the noun as belonging
to an antecedent; it is the shape in which the sensory-noun formal nouns appear in their
evidential and perception-complement uses. For the four sensory nouns the two forms are
distinguished by a possessive suffix nakagawa2024 258, 表66 :

concept form
possessed form
POSS suffix
source meaning

ru
ruwe
-(w)e
'track, path'

sir
siri
-i
'appearance, state'

haw
hawe
-e
'voice'

hum
humi
-i
'sound, sensation'

Satō observes that a noun in its possessed form presupposes an antecedent — something it
belongs to — so that placing a sensory noun in this form after a finite clause grammatically
encodes that the described situation is already held as given by the speaker
sato2008 135 . This presupposition distinguishes evidential
ruwe ('the trace of —', implicating a recognized antecedent event)
from lexical ru ('a track'). Dialect evidence reinforces the contrast:
southern Hokkaido (Saru, Chitose) uses the possessed forms in the clause-final evidential
frame, while Tokachi and Ishikari close the clause with the bare concept forms
ru , sir , haw ,
hum
nakagawa2024 110, 390 ; takahashi2013 129–131 .

Principal formal nouns as nominal heads

All formal nouns have arity −1, which allows them to terminate a relative clause (closing an
arity ≥+1 gap) or a saturated complement clause (arity 0) alike
nakagawa2024 261–262 . The table below covers those relevant to nominalization;
the full catalogue with arity derivations appears in formal-defective-nouns .

form
gloss
as RC head
as complementizer
notes

p ~ pe
'thing; (pej.) person'
✓
✓
allomorphy by stem-final segment; see nominalization-p-pe

hi (~ i after C)
'thing, time, place'
✓
✓
maximally abstract; see below

hike
'the one/side'
✓
borderline
grammaticalizes toward adversative conjunction hikeka

katu
'manner'
—
✓
< kat 'form/shape' + POSS; assertive use

usi(ke)
'place, time'
✓
✓
pan-Hokkaido; uske specific to Saru/Chitose/Shizunai

kur
'person'
✓

< kuru 'person'; honorific/polite register

'people, companions'
✓

✓

The pair usi / uske are dialectally distributed:
uske is attested in Saru, Chitose, and Shizunai; the forms
usi and usike are more widely spread across
Hokkaido nakagawa2024 258 . Both head relative clauses without requiring the
relational noun or that movement-case postpositions need before
locative complements bugaeva2015 47 .
Shibatani lists the nominalizers -p(e) , -i ,
-ike , and -kur as productive phrasal and clausal
nominalizating suffixes in classical/yukar Ainu
shibatani1990 33–34 , corresponding to the word-class of formal nouns in
Nakagawa's synchronic description nakagawa2024 257–258 .

Division of labour across the inventory

The formal-noun head selected for a nominalized clause is determined primarily by the semantic
category of the reference being expressed. p ~ pe
and hi cover the widest range but along different dimensions:
p ~ pe profiles a concrete entity — a thing
or person that can be encountered or handled — while hi is maximally
abstract, covering fact, time, and place readings alike
nakagawa2024 257 ; sato2008 174 . Where the referent is
specifically a person and an honorific register is required,
kur (from kuru person ) is used;
utar extends this person head to a group. The place nouns
usi(ke) and uske head nominalizations that
refer to a location or time without the concrete/abstract ambiguity of
hi .

Satō's diagnostic sharpens the contrast between the relative-clause use and the complementizer
use for any formal noun. A formal noun functioning as a relative-clause head heads a
clause from which a participant has been gapped; the original sentence can be reconstructed by
reinserting that participant. A formal noun functioning as a complementizer
nominalizes the whole clause as a proposition, and no such reconstruction is possible
sato2008 174–175 . Both p ~
pe and hi fill either role depending on
the arity of the clause they follow; the sensory-noun heads serve both functions as well,
though their complement use is semantically restricted to perception contexts.

Person formal noun kur produces nominalizations whose referent is a
specific individual. In classical and epic texts these regularly lexicalize:
kotan kor-kur 'chief' (lit. 'person who has a village'),
ray-kur 'the dead' (lit. 'person(s) who die')
shibatani1990 34 . Compound nominalizations of this type remain analytically
transparent even after lexicalization, illustrating the continuity between productive RC
nominalization and the lexicalized stratum treated in the final section.

hi — semantic range and the place/time split

hi thing, place, time is the most abstract formal noun in the inventory; its
gloss covers 'thing, matter, fact', 'time when', and 'place where'. After a consonant it
surfaces as -i — as in the lexicalized
ape-o-i 'hearth', treated in the next section
nakagawa2024 257 ; sato2008 169 .

In the 'thing/fact' and 'time' readings, hi nominalizes a saturated
clause (arity 0) and functions as a complementizer. The nominalized clause may serve as the
grammatical subject of a matrix predicate:

ku=kemeyki kor ku=itak hi iyotta pirka | 1SG.A=sew CONJ 1SG.A=talk NMLZ most good | it is best to talk while sewing | my talking-while-sewing is most good | After Tamura 2000: 126. hi takes two person-marked, fully specified clauses and nominalizes the whole as the grammatical subject of iyotta pirka 'is most good'. | bugaeva2016:261 | SAR

Or as the object of a complement-taking predicate. In the following example,
hi appears twice: as the object-head of
a=ye 'say' (arity +1, 'what to say') and of
a=kar 'do' (arity +1, 'what to do'). In each case the clause before
hi has an open slot, so hi fills the head
rather than the complementizer role:

a=ye hi ka a=kar hi ka a=eramiskari | 4.A=say NMLZ even 4.A=do NMLZ even 4.A=not.know | not knowing what to say or what to do | ninjal2003 | CHI | Oda Ito, NINJAL corpus of Ainu oral literature (Chitose) [ninjal-folklore/026#61]

The 'place' reading calls for a different structure: the governing postposition is retained
inside the relative clause, marking hi as the locative head (arity +1
because the location slot is gapped):

oro ta cise kar hi | place LOC house build NMLZ | the place where someone built a house | ta (LOC) retained inside the RC signals the locative role of hi; compare the time reading below, where no postposition is present. | nakagawa2024:511 | HK

With the time and abstract-fact readings, the clause is saturated and
hi nominalizes it whole. The postposition that follows
hi is not part of the RC but a postposition governing the entire
nominalized clause:

pon=an hi wano | be.small=4.S NMLZ since | since I was small | hi nominalizes the intransitive clause pon=an (arity 0); wano 'since/from' governs the nominalized clause as a temporal adverbial. | nakagawa2024:510 | HK

Nakagawa frames the place/non-place split as a tendency. The 'place' reading is more concrete
and closer to an ordinary noun, so the grammatical relation requires overt expression; the
'time/fact' reading is abstract and freely nominalizes the whole clause. Okuda (1989), quoted
and refined by nakagawa2024 510–511 , first systematized this contrast. An
attested exception — seta utar osoma hi 'the dogs' privy', from
知里幸恵's kamuy yukar collection (Horobetsu), which uses a 'place' reading without a retained
postposition — shows the constraint does not hold categorically
nakagawa2024 511 ‹corpus-suggested›.

The locative formal nouns usi and uske offer an
alternative to hi for the 'place' reading. Because they are inherently
locative, they require no retained movement-case postposition. The directional
postposition eun 'toward' is instead retained for the oblique head:

eun a=nu usi ka isam | toward 4.A=ask place even not.exist | there was no place to ask about it | Bugaeva ex. 16. nu 'hear, ask about'; eun 'toward' retained inside the RC signals the directional role; usi 'place' is the formal-noun head. The NP is the argument of isam 'not exist'. | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Ijäs observes that usi -headed compound nouns — such as
ipeusi 'restaurant' (lit. 'eating-place') — are historical RC
nominalizations, and that usi -words require no relational noun
or before movement-case postpositions
ijas2023 ‹consensus›. A locative relative clause with a plain common noun head
does require the relational noun:

oro ta k=an kotan | place LOC 1SG.S=exist village | the village I live in | Ijäs Lesson 11. oro 'place/vicinity' (relational noun) fronts before ta (LOC). With a usi-type head no oro would be needed. | ijas2023 | HK

Sensory-noun formal nouns as perception-complement heads

Apart from their clause-final evidential function (treated in evidential-schema-overview
and ruwe-ne-inferential the inferential ruwe ne ), the
possessed-form sensory nouns ruwe , siri ,
hawe , and humi function as nominal heads of
complement clauses whose matrix predicate is a perception or cognition verb. In this
intra-sentence use, the matrix verb follows the formal noun rather than closing the clause
sato2008 175–178 .

Each formal noun is selected by a specific subset of perception predicates:
nukar 'see' takes siri (the visual scene) or
hi (an abstract perceived content);
nu 'hear, ask about' takes hawe (verbal
content), humi (a non-verbal sensation), or
hi ; cognition predicates eramiskari
'not know' and eraman 'understand' take hi
or p ~ pe
sato2008 175–178 . The complement-taking predicates and the structural
properties of these complements are treated in nominalized-complements-control-raising .

Within the visual domain, Satō distinguishes the two available heads:
siri applies to an action or situation in progress at reference time
— a scene being witnessed — while ruwe applies to an already-existing
state or non-progressive action asserted as recognized fact
sato2008 178 . In the following example the speaker uses
siri to frame the visible absence of large trees as the scene that
prompted anger:

poro cikuni oar isam sir-i ku=nukar wa sonno ku=iruska | big tree at.all not.exist appearance-POSS 1SG.A=see CONJ truly 1SG.A=be.angry | I saw that there were no big trees at all, and was truly angry. | sir-i = sir 'appearance/state' + possessed suffix -i. siri heads the complement of ku=nukar 'I saw'; ku=iruska follows wa (CONJ) as a second, main-clause predicate. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | Kanzaki Masayoshi, Ainu Times (ainu-times/015/3#7)

The shift from this intra-sentence perception complement (matrix verb follows
siri ) to the clause-final evidential frame (copula
ne follows) instantiates Bugaeva's Stage II grammaticalization path:
the nominal head that was an argument of a perception predicate becomes the terminal element
of an evidential construction bugaeva2016 229–304 . The two uses remain
synchronically productive and distinct; the evidential use is the more grammaticalized
endpoint ‹consensus›.

The constituency of the whole construction — whether it is biclausal (a possessed noun heading
the preceding finite clause, closed by the copula) or is grammaticalizing toward a monoclausal
complex predicate — is unsettled. The biclausal analysis is held by Tamura, Okuda, and Satō,
and is endorsed by Nakagawa under the label 補文標識 'complementizer'
nakagawa2024 508 ; sato2008 175 . Bugaeva argues that the
erstwhile nouns are considerably grammaticalized and the construction is moving toward a
monoclausal complex predicate, supported by the fact that the formal noun no longer heads a
full NP ( *pirka ruwe ne kewtum is ungrammatical — no determiner or
attributive modifier can attach)
bugaeva2016 306–315 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 ‹contested›.

Lexicalized formal-noun nominalizations and the RC–compound boundary

A relative-clause NP of the form [(NP…) V nmz] can crystallize into a
single lexical noun when the constituent parts are reproduced as a unit with a conventional,
fixed meaning. Bugaeva traces a grammaticalization cline from productive RC nominalization to
lexicalized noun in the Hokkaido data, placing Hokkaido at Stage II of a broader areal
typology (Stage I = zero-nominalization; Stage II = nominalizing word; Stage III–IV in
Sakhalin = possessed verbal forms)
bugaeva2016 240–251, 385–397 . The lexicalized products are morphologically
transparent but synchronically function as ordinary nouns.

Two items illustrate the pattern with allomorphs of different formal nouns:

ci=ronnu-p | 1PL.A=kill-NMLZ | fox | thing(s) that we kill | ci= (1PL.A) + ronnu 'kill' + -p (NMLZ = formal noun p/pe, 'thing'). The generic patient 'things we kill' has been conventionalized as the species name for fox. | bugaeva2016:245 | HK

ape-o-i | fire-enter-NMLZ | hearth | the place where fire enters | -i is the allomorph of formal noun hi after a consonant, giving the 'place' reading. ape 'fire' is incorporated; o 'enter'. Contrast aep (4.A=eat-NMLZ) 'food', where -p gives the concrete 'thing' reading of p/pe. | bugaeva2016:245 | HK

The allomorph split across these two items reflects the same head-selection principle visible
in productive clausal nominalizations: -p ~ -pe
yields a concrete entity ('thing killed', 'thing cooked'), while
-i (allomorph of hi ) yields a location
('place where fire enters'). The semantic specialization survives lexicalization.

A relative-clause NP and a compound noun are superficially similar — both consist of a
predicate-like element preceding a noun — but the arity test separates them
nakagawa2024 506–507 . The compound
tureptani 'uba-lily digging-stick' juxtaposes
turep 'uba-lily' and tani 'digging-stick'
without marking the semantic relation between them; the RC reading 'the stick with which that
woman dug uba-lilies' requires the instrumental postposition
ani 'with' to be retained inside the clause, making the instrument
role explicit:

nea menoko ani turepta ni | that woman INS dig.uba.lily stick | the stick with which that woman dug uba-lilies | ani (INS) is retained inside the RC because ni 'stick/wood' fills the instrument role. The compound tureptani suppresses the grammatical relation; the RC must express it. | nakagawa2024:506 | HK

The diagnostic generalizes: in an RC nominalization the semantic relation between the
formal-noun head and the predicate is grammatically specified — via a retained postposition,
the verb's argument structure, or an applicative prefix. In a compound noun, that relation
is implicit. Lexicalized RC nominalizations such as ci=ronnu-p and
ape-o-i were once transparent RC structures; the grammatical marking
has become inert but remains recoverable from the morphology.

Ijäs's observation that usi -words such as
ipeusi 'restaurant' ('eating-place') descend historically from RC
structures aligns with Bugaeva's Stage II account and with Shibatani's finding that
-p(e) , -i , -ike , and
-kur function as both phrasal and lexical nominalizers
ijas2023 ; shibatani1990 33–34 ; bugaeva2016 240–251
‹consensus›. The productive and lexical levels are not sharply bounded; what counts as a new
RC formation versus a lexicalized item depends on the frequency and semantic
conventionalization of the combination.

title: Light Verbs, Pro-Verbs, and the Auxiliary-Verb Construction
summary: The 'do' pro-verbs ki/iki, the structural support uses of ne and an, and the general auxiliary-verb (complex-predicate) construction.
part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Light Verbs, Pro-Verbs, and the Auxiliary-Verb Construction
Part: Part IX — The Verb: Structure, Transitivity, and Verbal Number
Summary: The 'do' pro-verbs ki/iki, the structural support uses of ne and an, and the general auxiliary-verb (complex-predicate) construction.

The support-verb inventory

Hokkaido Ainu has a small set of high-frequency verbs whose lexical meanings are bleached in
particular constructions while their ordinary verbal morphology remains visible. The central
member is ki do , a transitive verb that supplies a verbal host for a preceding
event expression; the same support pattern appears in clausal negation, clause linkage under
negation, and some pro-verbal uses where the lexical content is recovered from context. The
copula ne be, become and the existential an exist also serve as
supports, especially in predicate-nominal, evidential, existential, and aspectual frames nakagawa2024 124–129, 132, 374 ; bugaeva2012 492–496 . The shared condition is structural: the support verb
supplies the finite predicate position, so person marking, negation, and clause-linking material
attach to a verb word of the kind described in verb-word-template .

support verb
ordinary valence
support use
diagnostic behaviour
main detail chapter

ki / iki 'do'
transitive; takes transitive-type subject marking
formal verb with an event or predicate complement; support for somo ki
in V (ka) somo ki , the lexical predicate keeps its person marking while ki is unindexed
negation-somo-standard-clausal

ne 'be, become'
two-place copular verb; the predicate complement is not indexed as an object
predicate-nominal support; closes formal-noun evidentials and nominalized clauses
subject affix appears, object affix is excluded
copula-ne-predicate-nominals

an 'exist'
intransitive existential; plural counterpart oka
existential predicate, component of zero-place weather verbs, and aspectual/evidential support
indexes its subject as S when a subject is present; zero-place compounds built on an index none
existential-locational-an-oka

The inventory rests on the same valence diagnostics as ordinary verbs. Intransitives take S
markers, transitives take A/O architecture, and the side-switching first-plural and fourth-person
forms provide the clearest test nakagawa2024 125–128, 165–169 ; sato2008 94–98 . Nakagawa's arity calculus counts person indexes, derivational prefixes, noun incorporation,
and base verbs in a single computation; Satō separates argument slots, macrorole transitivity,
and morphological valence, using ne as a central case where two argument
slots coexist with only one indexable macrorole nakagawa2024 136–139 ; sato2023b 42–52 ‹contested›. The support verbs preserve ordinary head-marking morphology even where their
lexical semantics is weak.

Ki as the formal verb of somo negation

The clearest grammaticalized use of ki is the periphrastic negative frame
V (ka) somo ki . The notional predicate precedes the negative adverb
somo , optionally followed by the focus/additive particle
ka ; ki supplies the finite verbal head that
somo negates. In Bugaeva's formulation, the preceding notional verb
functions as an object-like complement of ki ; Nakagawa describes the same
configuration as a use of the formal verb ki that verb-phrasifies the
preceding material bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 374, 407 . The
person index belongs to the notional predicate; ki itself is unindexed in
the periphrasis. The direct frame somo V places the negator before the
whole person-marked verb and does not split a person prefix from the stem sato2008 124 ; ijas2023 . Nakagawa finds no clear semantic difference between the two
arrangements, and Satō notes that ordinary verbal negation very often uses the periphrastic
pattern nakagawa2024 374 ; sato2008 22, 124 .

nep a=nukar ka somo ki korka | something 4.A=see even NEG do although | Although I did not see anything, | Nakagawa ex. 833. The lexical verb nukar bears the fourth-person A prefix; ki carries no person index. The indefinite nep receives its negative reading inside the scope of the negator. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

eci=yayeymontasa somo ki yakka | 2PL.S=take.revenge NEG do even.if | Even if you do not take revenge, | Nakagawa ex. 834. The same frame without ka; the intransitive predicate keeps its 2PL.S prefix while ki remains unindexed. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

The internal constituency of somo ki is disputed. Bugaeva and Nakagawa
keep the compositional analysis in which ki is the formal verb and the
preceding predicate is its complement bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 374 . Satō, in the Chitose description reported by Takahashi, treats
somo ki as a single auxiliary; Takahashi leaves the historical path open
sato2008 86 ; takahashi2016 74 ‹contested›. The disagreement
bears on constituency while both analyses place the construction in the same post-predicate slot.
The distribution and scope of the negator are developed in
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

Ki as event support and pro-verb

Outside negation, ki verbalizes event-denoting material and can stand
where English translations reach for 'do'. The complement may be a predicate stem, an event
nominal, or a nominalized clause, and its transitive subject is marked by the A-set, as expected
for a transitive verb. This use provides the bridge between ordinary lexical
ki and the negative support frame: both build a finite predicate out of
material whose own syntax is already present.

kira=an ka apkas=an ka eaykap no cis patek a=ki kor | flee=4.S even walk=4.S even cannot ADV cry only 4.A=do while | Unable to flee or to walk, doing nothing but cry, | The light predicate cis patek a=ki 'do only crying' shows ki with an overt event complement; the fourth-person A prefix appears on ki. | nakagawa2024:315 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 492

e=itak haw-e ku=nu hi renkayne itasaytak ku=ki kusu ne na | 2SG.S=speak voice-POSS 1SG.A=hear NMLZ following reply 1SG.A=do PURP COP FIN | I will listen to what you say and answer accordingly. | The event nominal itasaytak 'reply' is made predicative by ku=ki and combines with the future-intentional kusu ne; haw-e is glossed as a possessed noun. | nakagawa2024:435 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 966

The positive frame V ka ki also belongs here. Nakagawa cites
ipe ka a=ki and rok=an ka ki in comparing
post-predicate and preverbal negative patterns, and treats the event before
ka ki as the complement verbalized by ki nakagawa2024 374 . These examples license a restricted pro-verb analysis: ki can carry
the finite morphology for an event already specified by an adjacent complement ‹consensus›. They
do not demonstrate an unrestricted substitute for a missing verb phrase; broader VP-substitution
uses would require a separate corpus study ‹corpus-suggested›.

The variant iki is clearest in template diagnostics. The plural suffix
-pa and the outer plural clitic pa form a minimal
ordering pair with this verb: iki-pa=an has root-bound
-pa inside the fourth-person subject suffix, while
iki=an pa has the clitic after the person-inflected word nakagawa2024 156–159 . That ordering pair shows why support verbs still count as verbs: they take the ordinary
person and number morphology when they function as the predicate of a clause, even where their
lexical content is bleached.

Ki, somo ki, and clause linkage

A negated action predicate normally links to a following clause with no ,
yielding the common 'without doing X' reading. Nakagawa and Satō state the rule that stative
predicates and negated action predicates take no , with
wa excluded from this environment nakagawa2024 340–341 ;
sato2008 44–47 . The result is a three-level stack:
ki supplies the predicate of the negated clause,
somo negates it, and no links the whole to the
following clause.

ku=caranke ka somo ki no ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=complain even NEG do ADV 1SG.S=return | I went home without complaining. | The negated action predicate is closed by ki before no links it to the following clause. The clause-linkage system is treated in detail in the clause-linkage overview. | sato2008:44 | SAR

The same architecture appears below formal-noun nominalization and evidential predicates. A
negated verb phrase can stand inside the proposition headed by the possessed formal noun
ruwe ; ne then predicates the nominalized
proposition. The negation is internal to the nominalized clause, while the copular evidential
frame scopes over it nakagawa2024 393 .

sinep ka a=isamka ka somo ki ru-we ne kusu | one even 4.A=lose even NEG do track-POSS COP because | Since I did not lose even a single one, | Nakagawa ex. 779. The negated support frame a=isamka ka somo ki is embedded below ru-we ne; negation falls inside the evidential while the copular frame scopes over the whole proposition. The particle kusu links the evidential frame as a reason clause. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR

This ordering is a useful boundary test. In … somo ki ruwe ne the
proposition is negated and the evidential remains outside the scope of negation. When
somo falls on the copula of the formal-noun predicate, as in
ruwe somo ne , the result is a high-scope rhetorical or convictional
frame, a reading outside ordinary propositional negation nakagawa2024 393 . The
details of that outer frame are routed to ruwe-ne-inferential and
negation-somo-standard-clausal . For the linking inventory see
clause-linkage-overview .

Ne as copular support

The copula ne is formally transitive in valence classification, yet only
its subject is indexed. Nakagawa gives okkayo ku=ne 'I am a man' with a
transitive-type subject prefix and rejects object indexing on the predicate complement nakagawa2024 132 ; Shibatani likewise describes the copula as taking transitive subject morphology shibatani1990 50 . Satō's three-valency analysis accounts for the pattern by separating the two-place predicate
relation from indexable macroroles: the predicate nominal is a complement of the copular
relation, while only the subject is available for person indexing sato2023b 52
‹contested›.

frame
shape
support function of ne
routing

copula-ne-predicate-nominals

nominalization-p-pe

okkayo ku=ne | man 1SG.A=COP | I am a man. | Nakagawa ex. 23. The first-person prefix is the transitive-subject form; the predicate nominal okkayo is not indexed as an object. | nakagawa2024:132 | HK

Because ne is the ordinary support for predicate nominals, the negative
frame of copular clauses is NP (ka) somo ne . Satō notes that
ne ka somo ki , expected if the copula simply fed the ordinary
somo ki frame, is almost unattested sato2008 22 . The
copular construction keeps its own negation architecture, detailed in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals and negation-somo-standard-clausal .

The evidential example below combines both support verbs in a single sentence.
Ne predicates a zero-place weather verb in the first clause, while
ki hosts the support negation in the second. The layering makes it a
useful diagnostic for the whole support system.

mean cuk ne pe merayke=an ka somo ki no | be.cold.weather autumn COP NMLZ feel.cold=4.S even NEG do ADV | It was a cold autumn, but I did not feel cold. | The first clause uses ne in a predicate-nominal frame over the zero-place weather predicate mean; the second uses the support-verb negation merayke=an ka somo ki closed by no. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 15, after Kayano 1974a

An between existential verb and support

The existential an remains an intransitive verb when it predicates
existence or location, and it enters support constructions from that source. One pathway is
lexical compounding into zero-place ambient predicates. Nakagawa derives
mean 'be cold (weather)' from a non-free root
me- plus an ; such predicates take no person index
because no subject slot remains nakagawa2024 128–129 . Shibatani analyses these
as subject-incorporation, and Ijäs teaches them as avalent weather verbs shibatani1990 33 ; ijas2023 . Where the first element is a free noun, the boundary is unsettled:
Ijäs treats apto as 'it rains' as an avalent unit, while Nakagawa leaves
the analysis open because apto is an autonomous noun ‹contested› nakagawa2024 129–130 .

The v0/vi pairing in this table is directly grounded in Nakagawa's analysis: the
sir- prefix and the me- root are non-autonomous
elements that fuse with an into a zero-place compound, whereas the
felt-temperature verbs take a subject marked by the ordinary S-set nakagawa2024 127 ‹consensus›.

A second pathway keeps an as an overt support verb. Existential and
locational clauses are described in existential-locational-an-oka ; aspectual frames
such as kor an and wa an are treated in
existential-aspect-an-oka . In evidential constructions, the alternation between
copular ne and existential an contributes to the
direct/indirect opposition of formal-noun predicates, with siri an and
related forms routed to the evidential chapters. These support uses preserve the intransitive
origin of an while extending it into TAM and information-source
constructions ‹consensus› nakagawa2024 390–393, 446–449 .

Ijäs and the learner-grammar claims

Ijäs's treatment foregrounds the learner's problem: Ainu negation and auxiliary-like meanings
are built with independent verbs, so the person marker stays with the predicate that supplies the
argument frame. Her rule that somo precedes the verb and cannot split a
person marker from its stem matches Satō's and Nakagawa's description ‹consensus› ijas2023 ; sato2008 124 ; nakagawa2024 407 . Her identification of
ki as a dummy 'do' verb that fills the required verb position because
somo cannot stand as the predicate of a clause also matches the
compositional account in Bugaeva and Nakagawa ‹consensus› ijas2023 ; bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 374 .

niskur an ka somo ki | cloud exist even NEG do | It is not cloudy. | Ijäs negates the existential niskur an 'there are clouds' with the ka somo ki periphrasis; ki supplies the verb position because somo cannot head a clause on its own. Constructed pedagogical example. | ijas2023

Two Ijäs claims carry caveats. Her suggestion that postverbal
ka somo ki reflects Japanese influence on word order is not supported by
the read Hokkaido grammars and should be attributed to Ijäs alone ‹speculative› ijas2023 ; dalcorso2025neg 41–42 . Her presentation of lexical negative verbs as
blocking somo plus an affirmative counterpart also states a tendency too
strongly; the primary sources describe lexicalized negative predicates and preferences, while
coexistence with somo is independently attested in some environments
‹contested› ijas2023 .

Analytical boundaries

The support-verb system is outside the verb word proper, whose person indexes, valency prefixes,
incorporated nouns, and stem-internal suffixes are ordered in verb-word-template .
Valency-changing derivation — applicative, causative, antipassive — changes the argument frame
of the lexical stem; ki , ne , and
an provide a higher predicate host above the already-built stem. The
lexical semantics of the copula and existential are treated in
copula-ne-predicate-nominals and existential-locational-an-oka .

The degree of grammaticalization differs across the three verbs.
Somo ki may be analysed as a compositional support-verb construction or
as a single auxiliary; kor an and wa an behave
aspectually while still showing clause-chain morphology; ne is a
verb-like copula whose object indexing is blocked. These disagreements affect analysis, not the
descriptive ordering: person-marked lexical predicate first, support predicate above or after it,
and clause-level operators outside that support layer ‹contested›.

title: Middle, Anticausative, and the (Non-)Passive Question
summary: Middle/spontaneous and anticausative uses of si-/yay- and detransitives, and whether the a=/an= indefinite-agent construction constitutes a passive.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Middle, Anticausative, and the (Non-)Passive Question
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: Middle/spontaneous and anticausative uses of si-/yay- and detransitives, and whether the a=/an= indefinite-agent construction constitutes a passive.

The ci- middle prefix

Among the arity-decreasing voice prefixes, ci- middle prefix (object-resultative)
presents an event from the affected side and yields an intransitive verb whose sole argument is the
entity undergoing the action nakagawa2024 190–191 . Nakagawa labels the output
中動態 (middle voice) and gives the underlying semantics as "expressing,
from the affected side, an action one thing performs on another" nakagawa2024 190 .
The Handbook glosses the same prefix object-resultative ( RES ), emphasising
the affected argument's endpoint state bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 . Both labels
address the same morphosyntactic fact: the base transitive loses its agent position, arity drops
by one, and the base object surfaces as the intransitive subject. The terminological difference
between "middle" and "object-resultative" is not analytical ‹contested›.

The prefix reduces arity by exactly one. cari scatter something (transitive, +2)
gives ci-cari 'be scattered' (intransitive, +1);
sina tie something (transitive) gives ci-sina 'be tied'.
The prefixed form takes suffixal S-argument indexes (fourth-person =an ,
first-plural exclusive =as ) in place of the A-argument prefixes of the
transitive base — the standard transitivity diagnostic of valency-voice-overview .
The following table gives a selection of attested pairs.

base (vt)
gloss
ci- form (vi)
gloss
source

cari
scatter something
ci-cari
be scattered, lie scattered
nakagawa2024 190

sina
tie something
ci-sina
be tied
nakagawa2024 190

koyki
hunt, catch something
ci-koyki
be caught (→ cikoykip 'game animal')
nakagawa2024 191 ; kayano1996

car
scatter something
ci-car-i
be scattered (possessive-form variant)
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4

aynu kam ka upas ka ta ci-cari kamuyasi kam ka ci-cari kor | human flesh even snow even LOC RES-scatter evil.spirit flesh even RES-scatter while | Human flesh and snow lay scattered on the ground; the evil spirit's flesh too lay scattered, and … | Third-person subjects are morphologically zero; the intransitive form ci-cari takes no object. Both clauses show the affected entity (flesh, snow) as sole argument — the defining property of the ci- middle. | nakagawa2024:190 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 167

kem-ihi ci-car-i uske-he | blood-POSS RES-scatter-POSS place-POSS | the place where blood lay scattered | The possessed-form variant ci-car-i in a nominalised relative clause; ci-cari and ci-car-i are morphological doublets, the latter appearing in nominalised or attributive positions. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.4 | SAR | Handbook ex. 28, after Tamura 1996: 48

Typologically, middle voice marks spontaneous, patient-initiated, or affectee-oriented events
across many of the world's languages — the semantic space where a passive would otherwise be
expected but no agent is expressed shibatani1990 41–48 . In Hokkaido Ainu,
ci- occupies that niche: the source event is agentive (someone scatters,
someone ties), but the prefix profiles its outcome from the affected participant's perspective,
suppressing the agent position entirely.

Person restriction and the ci= / ci- homophony

The middle prefix ci- is restricted to third-person subjects. The form
*ku=ci-montapire ('I was informed') is ungrammatical; a first-person
affected participant instead requires the indefinite-agent construction with objective
cross-referencing: a=en=montapire 'the indefinite agent informed me as
object' nakagawa2024 190 . This restriction distinguishes
ci- sharply from the reflexive
yay- reflexive (direct) and the antipassive
i- antipassive , which are available across persons.

The prefix is homophonous with the first-person plural exclusive A-index
ci= , and both reduce arity by one — the index by filling the subject slot
with a specific 1PL.EXCL argument, the middle by suppressing the agent entirely. Nakagawa treats
them as "probably related but synchronically distinct" and notes a shared phonological behaviour:
before a , e , o the
i drops, yielding surface ca- ,
ce- , co- ( ci-opirasa →
copirasa ) nakagawa2024 190–191 . Disambiguation in context
relies on person morphology and arity: a middle verb takes suffixal S-indexes, while a first-plural
A-prefix takes the O-indexes typical of transitives.

Several ci- forms have lexicalized as uninflected bases. The noun
cikoykip game animal, prey — 'thing that is hunted' — freezes the middle of
koyki hunt, catch something , no longer perceived as a productive derivational
composite by speakers nakagawa2024 191 . Lexicalized middles of this type document
the prefix's historical productivity; the set of active, synchronically transparent pairs is smaller.

Community analysis: ci- as a productive middle

Discussion in the aynu-corpora Discord has proposed that ci- functions as
a systematically productive middle/anticausative across a wider set of lexemes than the published
literature has catalogued (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023–2024)
aynucorporadiscord . The proposal analyses several opaque-looking forms as transparent
middle derivations: copirasa 'spread out, widen (intr)' as
ci=opirasa (from transitive
opirasa spread something out );
ceosmare 'dash forward, plunge in' as
ci=e-osma-re (from an applicative-causative 'cause to thrust into'); and
cikarkarpe as ci=kar-kar-pe 'things being made'
aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›.

The same line of analysis was applied to ci-koykarkar . On this reading the
ci- in hattoho ci-koykarkar is the middle prefix,
yielding a passive-like meaning 'restrictions are imposed on [us]', rather than the first-plural
A-index ci= , which would give 'we impose restrictions together'
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) aynucorporadiscord ‹contested›. The
disambiguation requires pragmatic and discourse context; surface form alone does not decide it.

These community analyses are leads rather than established findings: they extend the attested middle
use productively to forms the peer-reviewed descriptive literature has not formally analysed under
this label. The core claim — that ci- is a productive middle prefix —
is supported by Nakagawa and the Handbook; the community contribution is the extension to a broader
set of lexemes and the identification of middle ci- in forms previously
treated as first-plural-exclusive or as unanalysed compounds. A further diachronic hypothesis that
ci- derives from an earlier passive-like *ti ,
distinct in origin from the person index, was proposed but disputed on phonological grounds —
the affricate [t͡s] realised as
c also appears in non-palatalizing environments, casting doubt on a
straightforward palatalization account (nukopoli and aomidori, aynu-corpora Discord, 2023)
aynucorporadiscord ‹speculative›.

Anticausative semantics and the si- overlap

The reflexive prefix si- reflexive (indirect) covers a semantic domain that
overlaps partially with the middle. Under Satō's analysis, adopted by Nakagawa and the Handbook,
si- marks the subject's indirect involvement — through another agent,
through circumstance, or through a force outside the subject's direct control sato2007 31–39 ; nakagawa2024 188–189 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . The
minimal pair yay-pusu 'float up volitionally (by swimming)' against
si-pusu 'float up of itself' illustrates the anticausative semantics of
si- : the event is profiled as spontaneous, with an affected participant
rising without an expressed agent sato2007 31–32 . Similarly, the transitive
turi lay something down, stretch something yields si-turi
'stretch, lie down (of itself)', which Tamura analyses as a pure intransitivizing derivation
tamura1996 622 . Kobayashi groups such pairs under "anticausativization" — a
transitivity alternation in which the causer is suppressed and the event presented as spontaneous
kobayashi2015 33 .

The morphological and person-marking properties of ci- and
si- remain distinct despite the semantic overlap.
ci- suppresses the agent position entirely and is restricted to
third-person contexts; si- fills the object slot with a self-participant
coreferent with the clause subject and is available across all persons. Spontaneous or
agent-backgrounded events with a first- or second-person participant therefore surface with
si- , not ci- . The full treatment of the
yay- / si- opposition belongs to
reflexive-yay and reflexive-possessive-si ; the present section records
only the semantic intersection with the anticausative domain.

Bugaeva's diachronic study of anticausatives in Ainu traces the historical pathways by which
transitive and causative verbs acquire intransitive partners via ci- ,
si- , and related morphology, setting them within the cross-linguistic
typology of inchoative-causative alternations bugaeva2025 . The inchoative-causative
interface — where a stem such as rewke become soft /
rewe soften something pairs a spontaneous intransitive with a transitive — is
treated in transitivizer-ka-valency-pairs ; the voice interpretation of
ci- and si- in such pairs is among the questions
that diachronic account addresses ‹corpus-suggested›.

Body-region prefixes he- and ho-

Two lexical prefixes of noun origin, he- 'head, tip' and
ho- 'base, bottom', also reduce arity by one by filling the object slot
with the subject's own body-region nakagawa2024 186–187 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 . puni lift something (transitive)
yields he-puni 'raise one's head' (intransitive) and
ho-puni 'get up, fly up' (intransitive);
kiru turn something yields he-kiru 'turn around';
tuye cut something yields ho-tuye 'shout'
(semantically opaque) nakagawa2024 186–187 .

These prefixes are the arity-decreasing (−1) counterparts of the homophonous affiliative
e- 'head of' and o- 'base of', which are
arity-neutral (0) and fill a slot while requiring their own possessor. The minimal pair makes
the arity contrast concrete: he-puni is intransitive and takes S-indexes,
while affiliative e-puni 'raise the head/end of something (belonging to
someone)' remains transitive nakagawa2024 192–193 .

he-puni=an kor somo a=nu wa kusu | head-rise=4.S while NEG 4.A=hear CONJ because | When [I] raised my head, [the sound] could no longer be heard. | The intransitive suffix =an on he-puni confirms that the body-region prefix he- has reduced the arity of puni 'lift' by one; the head-referent fills the object slot. The co-occurring a=nu is the indefinite-agent construction treated below. | nakagawa2024:192 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 171, partial

a=unuhu a=e-puni ruwe ne akusu | 4.A=mother.POSS 4.A=head.of-rise track-POSS COP when | [I] lifted my mother's head, and then — | The affiliative e- 'head of' (arity 0, not the applicative e-) keeps puni transitive; the object argument a=unuhu 'mother' is the entity whose head is being raised. Contrast the intransitive he-puni above, where the subject's own head is raised. | nakagawa2024:193 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 172, partial

Bugaeva and Kobayashi group he- and ho- together
with the ci- middle under the object-resultative heading, noting that they
"function similarly to O-incorporation" — a body-region semantics fills the object slot and makes
the verb intransitive bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.4 . Many surface forms are
semantically opaque: ho-tuye 'shout' transparently contains
tuye 'cut' with ho- 'base', yet the compositional
meaning is not recoverable nakagawa2024 187 .

The indefinite-agent construction: a= as a functional passive

When a transitive event is presented with an unspecified or backgrounded agent, Hokkaido Ainu uses
the fourth/indefinite person prefix a= fourth-person / indefinite agent — or the
dialectal variant an= in Ishikari and some other varieties — as the agent
argument of the transitive verb. The patient stays in object position; no promotion to subject
occurs. The construction is valency-neutral: the verb retains its transitive argument frame, with
the indefinite agent filling the A slot and the patient cross-referenced as O
nakagawa2024 428–429 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §1 .

seta or wa aynu a=kupapa | dog place from person 4.A=bite | A person was bitten by a dog. | The dog appears in the or wa 'from' demoted-agent phrase; the indefinite transitive prefix a= occupies the A slot; the patient aynu 'person' remains the grammatical object. | nakagawa2024:429 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 945″

nikekke wenkur sekor aynu or wa a=reko kor an yakka | wood-break poor.person QUOT people place from 4.A=call while exist.SG even.though | Even though [he] was called 'the wood-breaking pauper' by people — | a=reko 'the indefinite agent calls'; aynu or wa provides the demoted agent. The object (the called person) is not cross-referenced here because it is zero-marked third person. | nakagawa2024:429 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 946, after Tamura 1985: 42

sonno nispa or wa an=omap | very respected.man place from 4.A=love | [He] was greatly loved by a respected man. | The Ishikari variant an= replaces a= as the indefinite transitive prefix; the construction is otherwise identical. The patient is zero-marked (third person, object). | nakagawa2024:429 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 948, after Sunazawa 1983: 73

A demoted agent is expressed with the postpositional phrase or(o) wa
'from', taking the NP that would normally appear as the transitive subject
nakagawa2024 428–429 . The construction is frequent with unintended or inanimate
causers and in polite speech where direct naming of the agent is avoided. The full morphology,
referential range, and dialect distribution of the fourth/indefinite person are treated in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Satō's subjecthood diagnostic makes the non-promotion of the patient explicit. If the patient were
a true intransitive subject, a 'want to be loved' predicate should take bare
rusuy want to, desire with subjective marking. Instead the construction requires
the nominalizer hi together with objective cross-referencing on the verb
sato2008 205–206 :

huci oro wa a=en=omap hi ku=ki rusuy | grandmother place from 4.A=1SG.O=love NMLZ 1SG.A=do DES | I want to be loved by grandmother. | The patient is cross-referenced as object (en= '1SG.O') on the verb, and the nominalized clause hi is required by the desiderative. A construction with a true passive subject would take subjective marking and bare rusuy without hi — that form is ungrammatical. Satō describes the overall structure as 'a very peculiar passive' (受け身文としては極めて特殊な構造, Satō 2008: 205). | sato2008:205 | CHI

The distinction between the ci- middle and the
a= construction also emerges clearly here: in a
ci- clause the affected participant surfaces as the intransitive subject
and no agent may be expressed at all; in the a= clause the patient is
syntactically the object, an agent may appear in or wa , and the verb is
transitive by all formal tests nakagawa2024 429–430 .

Does Ainu have a true passive?

Hokkaido Ainu has no subject-promoting passive nakagawa2024 428 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §1 . Nakagawa states it directly:
それにあたるものはアイヌ語には無い 'there is nothing in Ainu that corresponds
to it' nakagawa2024 428 . The two constructions that carry the functional load a
passive would otherwise bear are structurally distinct from each other and from any classic
subject-promoting passive.

construction
patient's grammatical role
agent expression
arity effect

ci- middle
intransitive subject (S)
impossible
−1 (vi output)

a= / an= indefinite-agent
object (O) — no promotion
optional, in or wa phrase
0 (vt output)

subject-promoting passive (cross-linguistic prototype)
promoted to subject (S)
optional, in oblique phrase
−1 (vi output)

The ci- middle moves the base object into subject position — in that
respect it is the nearest Ainu analogue to a valency-decreasing passive — but it categorically
blocks agent expression nakagawa2024 430 . A classic passive combines
patient-as-subject with an optional by-phrase; no Ainu construction combines these two properties.
Nakagawa adds that the middle has a verse-register periphrastic use with the causative —
ci-ra-na-ran-ke (from ran descend , stretched to five
syllables) — where meaning is unchanged; this verse device is not available for the
a= construction nakagawa2024 430 .

The Handbook labels the a= / an= construction a
"valency-neutral impersonal passive" to capture its agent-defocusing function, while recognizing
that it lacks subject promotion bugaevakobayashi2022 §1 ‹contested›. Satō's
preferred formulation — "a half-active, very peculiar passive structure" — likewise foregrounds
the mismatch with cross-linguistic passive prototypes sato2008 205 ‹contested›.
Neither label implies that the construction is a passive in the structural sense; both describe the
same functional fact from different angles.

The functional distribution that results follows from the structural properties. For third-person
affected participants where no agent is at issue, ci- presents the event
from the affected side, and the patient-as-subject reading is the natural one. For first- or
second-person patients, or wherever the agent may need to be expressed,
a= / an= is used instead, with the patient
retaining object status and the verb its transitive morphology nakagawa2024 429–430 .
A by-phrase that co-occurs with patient-as-subject is unattested in the Hokkaido corpus
‹corpus-confirmed›.

Ijäs's learner grammar does not treat the passive question or the ci-
middle ijas2023 . The nominalizer hi that anchors Satō's
subjecthood diagnostic connects this discussion to the nominalization system of
nominalization-p-pe . The antipassive i- , which defocuses the
object from the subject's side, is the mirror-image operation and is treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i . The productive causative
-re / -te / -e , which
introduces an agent argument rather than suppressing one, is in
causative-morphological-re-e-te .
