title: Negative Predicates of Ability and Cognition
summary: Lexically suppletive negative modal/cognition predicates — eaykap 'cannot', eramiskari 'not know how/never', erampewtek 'not understand' — versus analytic somo negation.
part: Part XVI — Negation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Negative Predicates of Ability and Cognition
Part: Part XVI — Negation
Summary: Lexically suppletive negative modal/cognition predicates — eaykap 'cannot', eramiskari 'not know how/never', erampewtek 'not understand' — versus analytic somo negation.

A class of lexically negative predicates

Most clauses in Hokkaido Ainu are negated analytically, by the preverbal adverb
somo not placed before the predicate (treated in
negation-somo-standard-clausal ). A small set of verbs negates differently: the
negation is part of the verb's lexical meaning, so the negative clause is built on a verb
distinct from its affirmative counterpart rather than on somo plus the
positive verb. The members that express ability and cognition are the most clearly
auxiliary-like part of this set — eaykap cannot ,
koyaykus cannot (the situation prevents it) , niwkes cannot finish ,
eramiskari not know; have never , and
erampewtek not understand . Nakagawa lists them among the
他動詞起源の助動詞 ‘auxiliaries of transitive origin’ (his Table 75); Bugaeva
describes the same forms as verbs that contain negation as part of their meaning
nakagawa2024 314–315 ; bugaeva2012 496 . The existential and
privative members of the wider lexical-negative class, isam ‘not exist’
and sak ‘lack’, are treated in
negation-existential-possessive-isam .

affirmative counterpart
lexical negative
predicate sense
sense as a post-verbal auxiliary

easkay ‘be able; be skilled’
eaykap
be unable; be poor at
cannot V (capacity or circumstance)

— (no morphological pair)
koyaykus
be unable because the situation prevents it
cannot V as things stand

— (no morphological pair)
niwkes
be unable to carry through
cannot V to completion

amkir ‘know, recognize by experience’
eramiskari
not know, not recognize
have never V-ed

eraman / eramuan ‘understand, know’
erampewtek
not understand, not know
not know how to V

The negatives are well represented in the aligned Hokkaido corpus:
eaykap occurs 1,082 times, eramiskari 464,
erampewtek 454, niwkes 177, and
koyaykus 154, against affirmative easkay 1,324
and amkir 143. These are lemma-level token totals, normalized across
dialects with personal clitics stripped, so they measure the salience of each item rather than
the rate of any one construction; on that measure the class belongs to the core grammar of
negation and not to an incidental lexical field ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Morphology and derivation

The ability pair is morphologically transparent. Easkay is
e-askay , the
applicative-e applicative e- on
askay ‘be skilled’, and eaykap is
e-aykap , the same prefix on aykap ‘be
unskilful’ tamura1996 s.v. eaykap ; kayano1996 s.v. easkay ;
ijas2023 . The negation therefore resides in the root: askay
and aykap are themselves a lexical antonym pair ‘skilled’ versus
‘unskilful’, differentiated by s ~ y and not by a
productive negating morpheme. The applicative e- supplies the slot for
the thing or event one is or is not able to do, which is what makes both verbs transitive and
lets them take a clause as their object.

The cognition negatives are lexicalized as wholes. Eramiskari stands as
the negative of amkir , and erampewtek as the
negative of eraman / eramuan ; Tamura classes all
four as transitive verbs and tags eramiskari and
erampewtek explicitly as 否定動詞 ‘negative
verbs’ tamura1996 s.v. eramiskari, erampewtek . The two negatives share the
element eram(u)- ‘mind, understanding’ with their affirmative
counterparts, but neither segments into a recognizable negator, and the accessible grammars do
not analyse them further. Koyaykus and niwkes
are likewise treated as unanalyzed units; koyaykus attaches as a whole
after a verb, as in the dictionary forms mokor koyaykus ‘be unable to
sleep’ and hunara koyaykus ‘be unable to find’
kayano1996 s.v. koyaykus .

The cognition negatives carry minor dialect variants. Saru has
eramiskari , with eramuskari recorded for a single
Nibutani speaker and eramuskare in Ishikari;
erampewtek appears as erampetek elsewhere
tamura1996 s.v. eramiskari ; nakagawa2024 314–315 .

Argument structure and the auxiliary frame

Because they are transitive in origin, these predicates can stand as the main verb of their
clause, indexing an experiencer or agent as subject and taking the event as object, or they can
follow another verb as an auxiliary. In the auxiliary use the lexical verb keeps its personal
marking and the negative auxiliary adds none, exactly as the formal verb
ki do does in the somo ki periphrasis: the negation sits
in the auxiliary, while person stays on the predicate it scopes over
nakagawa2024 314, 360, 372 .

k=apkas eaykap | 1SG.S=walk cannot | I cannot walk. | The simplest frame: apkas ‘walk’ carries the first-person subject, and eaykap follows it person-unmarked. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | NINJAL ed., 4#16

The transitive origin remains active: the auxiliary may instead carry its own personal prefix,
with the preceding clause as its object. In the next example the indefinite person
a= attaches to eaykap , not to the verb
paye ‘go’, so that eaykap predicates ‘one cannot
[go further upstream]’.

na hepera paye a=eaykap ru-we an | more upstream go.PL 4.A=cannot track-POSS exist | Is it that one cannot go any further upstream? | a= indexes the subject of eaykap, and the embedded event paye ‘go (PL)’ is its object; ruwe an is the content-question frame of the evidential. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | NINJAL ed., 61#71

The auxiliary may be preceded by the particle ka even, at all , in the frame V
ka eaykap (also V ka eramiskari ), with no clear
difference in meaning from the plain frame. Nakagawa notes that this
ka slot is available to the transitive-origin auxiliaries but is lacking
for the native auxiliaries, which makes it a diagnostic of the class
nakagawa2024 314 . The strengthening that Ijäs assigns to the slot pedagogically
is the ordinary scalar contribution of ka , not a separate negative
morphology ijas2023 .

a=tekehe ka a=epuni ka eaykap pe ne kusu | 4=hand.POSS even 4.A=raise even cannot NMLZ COP because | Because I could not even raise my hand, | The possessor prefix a= on the possessed noun tekehe is glossed by person alone; the verb epuni keeps its agentive a=, and eaykap follows the predicate after the scalar ka. | nakagawa2024:323 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 532

kira=an ka apkas=an ka eaykap no cis patek a=ki kor | flee=4.S even walk=4.S even cannot ADV cry only 4.A=do while | Unable to flee or to walk, doing nothing but cry, | Two coordinated intransitive verbs each carry the fourth-person subject =an under one eaykap; the negated event links to what follows with no, not wa (see below). | nakagawa2024:315 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 492

The shared post-verbal slot identifies these predicates with the auxiliary zone rather than with
immediately preverbal somo . Satō treats
eaykap as an auxiliary occupying the same position as
somo ki ; the difference is that somo ki negates
through the formal verb ki , whereas eaykap and its
peers are themselves the negative predicate, so somo is not added to them
sato2008 124 ; bugaeva2012 496 .

Ability: eaykap, koyaykus, niwkes

Eaykap is the broad negative of easkay . Nakagawa
defines its range as inability in a wide sense, covering both a lack of capacity and a situation
that does not permit the event, and Tamura adds the further reading ‘be poor at’
nakagawa2024 315 ; tamura1996 s.v. eaykap . The modal semantics of
the easkay / eaykap pair as a category of ability
and possibility are developed in abilitative-easkay-eaykap .

Koyaykus narrows the same space to circumstantial inability: the subject
cannot do the event because the situation or the moment is against it, rather than for want of
skill. Tamura draws the contrast directly — koyaykus for what outside
factors prevent, eaykap for what is beyond one's ability or generally
impossible tamura1996 s.v. koyaykus ; nakagawa2024 314 .

ukuran ku=mokor koyaykus | last.night 1SG.S=sleep cannot | Last night I could not sleep. | The inability is situational — sleep would not come — which is the domain of koyaykus rather than eaykap. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | NINJAL ed., 17#63

Niwkes is more specific again, ‘be unable to carry an action through to
completion’ nakagawa2024 314 . The lexicon of ability pairs it with
eaykap as a doublet, the two glossed together as ‘cannot do’ and ‘cannot
manage’ kanazawa1898 . The articulation of the field has a consequence for glossing:
a negative ability clause is rarely a bare ‘not able’, since the predicate chosen also
classifies where the impossibility comes from ‹consensus›.

Cognition: experience and understanding

The cognition negatives split a domain that English often keeps together. The pair
amkir / eramiskari concerns knowledge by
acquaintance or experience. As a main verb eramiskari is ‘not know, not
recognize’; after a verb phrase it gives an experiential negative, ‘have never V-ed’
tamura1996 s.v. eramiskari ; nakagawa2024 314–315 .

cip a=o ka eramiskari p ne korka | boat 4.A=board even never NMLZ COP although | Although I had never been in a boat, | The event cip a=o ‘board a boat’ is the experiential domain of eramiskari; p ne korka frames it as a concessive nominalized clause. | nakagawa2024:315 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 486

an=cise soyke ta asip=an ka eramuskare | 4=house outside LOC go.out=4.S even never | I had never even gone outside my house. | Eramuskare is the Ishikari variant of eramiskari in the banked source. | nakagawa2024:315 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 488, after Otsuka 1990: 259

The pair eraman / eramuan versus
erampewtek concerns understanding and knowing how. As a main verb
erampewtek is ‘not understand, not know’; after a verb phrase it means
that the subject does not know how to carry the event out, and so cannot
tamura1996 s.v. erampewtek ; bugaeva2012 496 . Tamura keeps the
two cognition negatives apart by source of knowledge: failure to recognize a person or thing is
eramiskari , while failure to understand or to have the knowledge is
erampewtek tamura1996 s.v. eramiskari, erampewtek . Ijäs's
pedagogical contrast between experiential knowledge and understanding follows this same source
split ijas2023 ‹consensus›.

ku=nukar korka k=erampewtek | 1SG.A=see although 1SG.A=not.understand | I looked at it, but I do not understand. | Affirmative perception nukar contrasts directly with the cognition negative erampewtek in one clause. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | NINJAL ed., 40#58

Interaction with somo, and the question of blocking

Two facts show that the lexical negatives do not stand in complementary distribution with
standard negation. First, although somo is not added to the lexical
negative itself, it freely negates a different predicate in the same clause:

e=erampewtek yakka somo ku=iruska | 2SG.A=not.understand even.if NEG 1SG.S=be.angry | Even if you do not understand, I will not be angry. | The cognition negative erampewtek and the standard negator somo co-occur, each over its own verb. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | NINJAL ed., 40#11

Second, the lexical negative may sit inside a clause whose outer frame is itself negative. In
Nakagawa's example the experiential negative a=eramiskari falls within
the proposition, while the rhetorical ru-we somo ne is the outer
conviction frame of the evidential-schema-overview evidential , not a second
analytic negation of eramiskari nakagawa2024 393 . The
layering of negation with the formal-noun evidentials is treated in
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation .

sir-kunne kor soyne hi a=eramiskari aan ru-we somo ne | land-be.dark when go.out NMLZ 4.A=not.know PFV track-POSS NEG COP | I had not known that he went out when night fell. | ruwe somo ne here is the rhetorical/convictional frame ‘was I not unaware’, i.e. emphatic ‘I really did not know’, with the lexical negative eramiskari inside it. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 780

Whether the lexical negative outright blocks standard negation of the corresponding
positive verb is a genuine question. Ijäs states the rule categorically: where a lexical
negative exists, somo plus the positive verb is not normally possible, so
that one says k=erampewtek and not
*somo k=eraman ijas2023 . For at least one pair this is borne
out by a primary source: Tamura's entry for amkir states that negation
with somo is not formed, and the negative verb
eramiskari is used instead tamura1996 s.v. amkir . The
generalization does not hold across the whole class, however. Dal Corso records southern
Hokkaido somo kor ‘not have’ beside privative
sak , with a distinct pragmatic value, and Bugaeva describes these verbs
only as containing negation in their meaning, not as barring
somo dalcorso2025neg 41 ; bugaeva2012 496 .
The blocking is best stated as graded: near-obligatory for the cognition pair
amkir / eramiskari , a strong preference for the
ability verbs, but not a categorical rule, so that Ijäs's blanket statement overstates it
‹contested›.

The lexical negatives license the same polarity-sensitive environment as
somo and isam . Nakagawa groups the
ka -indefinites such as nep ka ‘anything, and under
negation nothing’ with eaykap alongside the standard and existential
negators nakagawa2024 323 ; the licensing and scope of these items are treated in
negation-polarity-items-scope . In clause linkage they pattern with negative
predicates: Takahashi shows that a negated subordinate clause selects the stative linker
no rather than successive wa , and the
lexical-negative auxiliaries take no in the same way, even without
somo takahashi2016 75–77 ; nakagawa2024 340
‹consensus›.

Boundaries and dialect evidence

The class is narrow, and an ordinary antonym is not a member of it. Ijäs lists lexical opposites
such as eese ‘agree’ / kopan ‘refuse’ and
pa ‘find’ / turaynu ‘lose’, which remain ordinary
verbs and take somo in the normal way ijas2023 . The source
check treats this as plausible but not separately verified for every pair, so the grammatical
generalization is confined to the well-described negatives in the table above ‹corpus-suggested›.

The secure baseline for the class is the Saru description of Nakagawa and Tamura, with Ishikari
and Tokachi variants where the examples mark them. Takahashi documents the Tokachi auxiliary use
of the experiential negative:

kuani anakne pu ari an cise ku=nukar eramuskare | 1SG TOP storehouse QUOT exist house 1SG.A=see never | I have never seen a building called a storehouse. | The Tokachi variant eramuskare follows the person-marked verb ku=nukar as the experiential negative ‘have never seen’; pu ari an cise is ‘a building called a pu (storehouse)’. | takahashi2016:74 | TOK | Honbetsu; consultant Sawai Tomeno

Refsing's Shizunai description confirms that eaykap and
somo ki both occur in Shizunai, but the available text for the relevant
pages is too damaged to support Shizunai-specific claims refsing1986 214–215 .
Dal Corso's history of Sakhalin negation bears on the class only diachronically: his proposal
that negative-existential material underlies wider Ainu negation helps explain why the lexical
negatives and standard somo form one connected domain, but the ability
and cognition predicates described here are analysed from Hokkaido sources, and that history
remains a hypothesis rather than a settled Hokkaido-internal fact
dalcorso2025neg 41–42 ‹speculative›.

title: Negative Existential and Possessive: isam
summary: isam as the suppletive negative counterpart of the existentials an/oka, its 'not have' possessive use, and the privative sak.
part: Part XVI — Negation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Negative Existential and Possessive: isam
Part: Part XVI — Negation
Summary: isam as the suppletive negative counterpart of the existentials an/oka, its 'not have' possessive use, and the privative sak.

Place in the negation system

Hokkaido Ainu negates existence and possession with verbs whose meaning already contains the
negation, leaving the preverbal adverb somo absent. The negative
existential is isam not exist, be gone , the lexical counterpart of the positive
existentials an exist, be located (singular) and oka(y)
(plural); the privative sak lack, be without is the lexical counterpart of
kor have bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 450 .
Because the negative resides in the verb, the predicate indexes its arguments in the ordinary way
and no adverbial negator is added. The standard somo strategy is treated
in negation-somo-standard-clausal , the positive existential and locational predicates
in existential-locational-an-oka , and predicative possession with
kor in predicative-possession-clauses .

domain
positive predicate
lexical negative
analytic alternative
basic syntax

existence / location
an SG, oka(y) PL
isam ‘not exist, be gone’
somo an
the absent entity is the sole argument (S)

have-possession
kor ‘have’
sak ‘lack, be without’
somo kor , reported for southern Hokkaido
the possessor is subject, the lacked entity its object/complement

isam and sak belong to a wider class of lexical
negatives that also includes eaykap ‘cannot’,
eramiskari ‘never, not know by experience’, and
erampewtek ‘not understand’, all of which carry negative polarity as part
of their lexical content bugaeva2012 496 ; nakagawa2024 314–315 .
The ability and cognition members of that class are treated in
negation-ability-cognition-predicates ; the present chapter takes the existential and
possessive members. Ijäs groups the same items as ‘lexically negated verbs’, pairing
an / oka with isam and
kor with sak ijas2023 .

The negative existential isam

isam is a one-place intransitive verb. It takes the entity whose absence
is asserted as its sole argument and indexes it with the ordinary intransitive person set —
ku=isam ‘I am gone/absent’, e=isam ‘you are
gone’ — while a third-person subject is zero-marked. Dal Corso notes that
isam is the only intransitive member of the lexical-negative class
dalcorso2025neg 41 . Its single argument slot serves as a constituency diagnostic:
a clause containing two independent noun phrases cannot be a simple
isam predication, since the verb supplies only one position
nakagawa2024 . The Handbook flags one isam clause whose lack of
an overt personal affix it finds unexpected, against the normal pattern in which the verb does
index its subject bugaeva2022 .

i=epetturasi p sinep ka isam | 4.O=rival NMLZ one even not.exist | There is not even one who can rival me. | The negative-polarity phrase sinep ka ‘even one’ is licensed by the negative existential isam; the object prefix i= belongs to epetturasi ‘rival, be a match for’, not to isam. | nakagawa2024:450 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 1025

isam licenses the same ka -indefinite polarity
items that occur under somo : nep ka ‘anything’
reads ‘nothing’ in its scope, and the additive ka may be repeated across
several indefinites for an exhaustive ‘nothing at all’ nakagawa2024 323 . The
indefinite series and the scope of negation over it are set out in
negation-polarity-items-scope .

seta ka nep ka isam pe ne a p | dog even something even not.exist NMLZ COP PFV NMLZ | There was not even a dog or anything, | Repeated ka ‘even’ over seta ‘dog’ and nep ‘anything’ yields the exhaustive reading under isam; the tail pe ne a p nominalizes the clause and carries the adversative ‘but’. | nakagawa2024:323 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 529

k=uni-hi okari anakne nep ka isam | 1SG=home-POSS around TOP something even not.exist | There is nothing around my house. | Satō’s negation example. The locative phrase k=uni-hi okari ‘around my house’ fixes the domain of absence, and the absent entity is the ka-indefinite nep ka, subject of isam. | sato2008 | HK

Two values of isam should be kept apart. The existential value ‘there is
none’ denies presence in a place or discourse domain; the change-of-state value ‘be gone’
presents absence as the result of a disappearance, and extends to a person’s death or absence
bugaeva2012 496 . The second value survives in the auxiliary
wa isam , where a verb phrase linked by wa leads to
a state in which the relevant participant or result has disappeared, the source of renderings
such as ‘V away’ or ‘end up gone’ nakagawa2024 450 . Its aspectual placement among
the wa -auxiliaries is taken up in
phasal-iterative-habitual-aspect .

isam and the somo strategy

The lexical isam does not displace the positive existential
an / oka . An existential or locational predicate may
still be negated analytically by placing somo before
an , as with any verbal predicate nakagawa2024 365–366 ;
sato2008 124 . The read sources agree that isam is the
lexical negative of the existential, but they do not quantify when a speaker selects lexical
isam over analytic somo an .

Ijäs proposes a semantic division: isam denotes complete absence or
disappearance, while somo an is used where an
keeps its broader value ‘live, be located’, and can additionally mark an absurd or impossible
existence ijas2023 . The division is plausible, since an
independently covers existence, location, and residence and positive predicates are negatable by
somo ; the precise split is her pedagogical formulation and is not stated
in those terms by Nakagawa, Satō, or Bugaeva ‹speculative›. The somo an
side of the contrast is treated in negation-somo-standard-clausal preverbal-somo .

The same caution applies to the blocking rule. Ijäs presents isam and
sak as the normal negatives wherever such a paired verb exists, so that
somo plus the positive counterpart is ‘not usually possible’
ijas2023 . Dal Corso reports southern Hokkaido somo kor ‘not
have’ beside sak , with a pragmatic difference supplied by Nakagawa
dalcorso2025neg 41 n.1 , and Bugaeva characterizes these verbs only as containing
negation in their meaning, not as barring somo bugaeva2012 496 .
The evidence supports a lexical preference; a categorical ban overstates the distribution
‹contested›.

Possessive negation: sak

Possessive negation is distributed over two related constructions. Where the missing entity is
profiled as absent from a possessor’s sphere, an existential isam clause
expresses ‘there is no X (for Y)’. Where the possessor is the subject of a privative predicate,
the verb is sak . Bugaeva identifies sak as the
lexical negative of kor ‘have’ bugaeva2012 496 , and Ijäs
gives the same pair ijas2023 . For positive predicative possession and the
nominal affiliative alternatives to kor , see
predicative-possession-clauses and
concept-form-affiliative-form the possessed form .

sak is transitive: the possessor is its subject and the lacked thing its
object, so a nominalized ona sak pe is ‘father-lack NMLZ’, hence ‘one who
has no father’, with the lacked kin term as the complement of sak
bugaeva2012 496 . The privative base also enters lexical compounds, such as the
tabooed snake-name sak-somo-ay-ep recorded by Nakagawa
nakagawa2024 .

ene an nispa maci-hi ene tamasay ka sak ru-we an hi an | such exist rich.man wife-POSS such necklace even lack track-POSS exist NMLZ exist | The wife of such a wealthy man does not even have a necklace. | The possessor is the wife (maci-hi); the lacked entity tamasay ka ‘even a necklace’ is the object of privative sak. The tail ru-we an hi an is the situational-evidential frame, handled in the evidential chapters. | nakagawa2024:323 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 533

ku=kor pukuro sak no ku=hosipi | 1SG.A=have bag lack ADV 1SG.S=return | I went home without my bag. | Ijäs’s constructed example. The lacked entity is the possessive phrase ku=kor pukuro ‘my bag’; sak chains to the main clause with the stative linker no. | ijas2023 | HK

The no of the constructed example reflects a general linkage pattern:
negative and negative-meaning predicates favour the stative adverbial linker
no over successive wa . Takahashi gives Tokachi
evidence that negated subordinate clauses select no , and Nakagawa states
the rule for stative, negative-meaning, or overtly negated verb phrases takahashi2016 75–77 ;
nakagawa2024 340 . Ijäs extends the observation to lexical negatives such as
sak and isam ijas2023 . The
symmetric/asymmetric typology behind the no -only restriction is set out in
negation-somo-standard-clausal typology .

Diachrony: *sam and the negative-existential cycle

Dal Corso’s comparative account of Ainu negation is anchored in a Sakhalin study but makes a
specific Hokkaido-facing proposal. He reconstructs a negative-existential root
*sam and analyses Hokkaido isam as the
antipassive-detransitive-i antipassive prefix i- on that
transitive root, with antipassivization yielding the intransitive ‘not exist’; the standard
negator somo is derived in the same account from
*samno through final stress and an a / o change dalcorso2025neg 41–42 . On this reconstruction a
single source *sam underlies both the surviving negative existential
isam and the general clausal negator somo . The read
Hokkaido reference grammars present no such etymology, so the shared-root analysis is a
diachronic hypothesis rather than a synchronic fact about Hokkaido clauses ‹speculative›.

Ijäs independently reaches essentially the same segmentation, i- plus a
lost transitive sam* ‘something is not located’, and explicitly labels it
speculation ijas2023 . The convergence of her lesson note with Dal Corso’s
philological argument raises the point from an isolated pedagogical guess to a live scholarly
proposal. It remains a proposal: for ordinary clause grammar isam behaves
as a single intransitive negative existential, with no productive alternation involving a free
sam root ‹speculative›.

The reconstruction situates Ainu within two diachronic models that Dal Corso applies to it: the
Givón negative cycle, by which a negator is periodically renewed, and the Negative Existential
Cycle, by which a negative existential predicate develops into a general clausal negator and may
leave existential traces in the result givon1978 ; croft1991 ;
dalcorso2025neg 41–42 . The cycle would explain why a negative existential and a
standard negator are diachronically connected in one account while occupying distinct synchronic
constructions. Its application to Hokkaido is a historical hypothesis, not a synchronic
generalization ‹speculative›: in the present-day grammar isam is a verb,
and somo the preverbal negative adverb of
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

title: Negative-Polarity Indefinites and the Scope of Negation
summary: The ka-indefinite/NPI series under somo, negative concord, constituent vs clausal negation, and the negation of nominal predicates (somo ... ne).
part: Part XVI — Negation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Negative-Polarity Indefinites and the Scope of Negation
Part: Part XVI — Negation
Summary: The ka-indefinite/NPI series under somo, negative concord, constituent vs clausal negation, and the negation of nominal predicates (somo ... ne).

The interrogative-based indefinite series

Hokkaido Ainu builds its principal indefinite series by adding the additive focus particle
ka to an
interrogative-pro-forms interrogative pro-form :
nep ‘what’, nen ‘who’,
hunak / ney ‘where’, and
hempara ‘when’. Outside the scope of negation the resulting forms are
existential — nep ka ‘something, anything’,
nen ka ‘someone, anyone’; within the scope of a negative predicate the
same forms are read ‘nothing’ and ‘no one’ nakagawa2024 323 . The two readings
are values of a single form, fixed by the polarity of the clause that contains it.

interrogative base
with ka
non-negative value
under negation

nep ‘what’
nep ka
something, anything
nothing

nen ‘who’
nen ka
someone, anyone
no one

hunak , ney ‘where’
hunak ka , ney ta … ka
somewhere, anywhere
nowhere

hempara ‘when’
hempara ka
sometime, ever
never

The series is of the cross-linguistically common type whose members derive from interrogatives
and spread across a graded set of functions — specific and non-specific existential, question,
conditional, and negation haspelmath1997 . In Ainu the negation function is
morphologically continuous with the existential one: there is no separate negative pronoun, and
the ‘nothing’ / ‘no one’ reading arises only when the existential indefinite falls under a
negator. The affirmative existential use is attested for all four bases
nakagawa2024 323 and is especially frequent in interrogative and inferential
frames, where nen ka introduces a non-specific ‘someone’:

nen ka ek | who even come | Someone is coming. | Constructed minimal pair to the attested negative in (3): nen ka is the plain existential ‘someone’ in a non-negative clause, the negative-polarity ‘no one’ only under a negator.

The morphology of the bases and the wider set of interrogative-derived forms belong with
interrogative-pro-forms ; the particle ka as additive and
scalar focus belongs with topic-marking-anakne . The question pursued here is how
these forms are licensed under negation, and where the licensing negation takes scope. The
standard negator itself is described in negation-somo-standard-clausal .

The licensing predicates

Any negative predicate in the clause licenses the indefinite series. Three licensers recur:
standard somo negation, whether preverbal somo V
or the periphrasis V ( ka ) somo ki with the formal
verb ki do ; the negative existential isam not exist ; and the
negative ability verb eaykap cannot . In the periphrasis the notional predicate
and its complement stand before somo ki , and the polarity item falls
inside the complement that somo negates bugaeva2012 496 ;
nakagawa2024 374, 407 .

nep a=nukar ka somo ki korka | what 4.A=see even NEG do although | Although I did not see anything, | Nakagawa ex. 833. The base nep takes its indefinite reading because it stands in the complement that somo ki negates; the second ka focuses that complement. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

Preverbal somo licenses the same series. In the topical dictionary
nep ka ‘anything’ falls under somo in a polar
question:

tanpe akkari nep ka somo e=kon rusuy | this.one beyond what even NEG 2SG.A=have want | Do you not want anything more than this? | Kanazawa, 沙流. nep ka ‘anything’ under preverbal somo; the question expects the negative answer ‘nothing’. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | NINJAL topical dictionary, ‘感情’ 010#34

The negative existential isam licenses the series without
somo ; its privative and possessive uses are treated in
negation-existential-possessive-isam . With nen ka the result
is ‘no one’, the negative counterpart of the existential ‘someone’ in (1):

nen ka isam ru-we | who even not.exist track-POSS | Is no one there? | Kanazawa, 沙流. nen ka under isam yields ‘no one’; the reduced clause-final ru-we marks the polar question (see the evidential frames cross-referenced below). | kanazawa1898 | SAR | NINJAL topical dictionary, ‘問答’ 062#48

seta ka nep ka isam pe ne a p | dog even what even not.exist NMLZ COP PFV NMLZ | though there were no dogs or anything, | Nakagawa ex. 529. Two distinct uses of ka in one clause: additive seta ka ‘dogs too’ and the indefinite nep ka ‘anything’, both under isam. | nakagawa2024:323 | SAR

The negative ability verb eaykap — one of the lexical negatives treated
in negation-ability-cognition-predicates — licenses the series in the same way,
here on both the body-part object and the notional predicate:

a=teke-he ka a=epuni ka eaykap pe ne kusu | 4.POSS=hand-POSS even 4.A=raise even cannot NMLZ COP because | because I could not even raise my hand, | Nakagawa ex. 532. The negative-ability predicate eaykap is the licenser; ka marks scalar ‘even’ on each focused constituent. | nakagawa2024:323 | SAR

The dialectal negators that replace somo in the same structural slot
license the same forms. Nakagawa's eastern-Hidaka example uses henne , and
his Shiranuka example the dialectal negator omo with the focus variant
kay for ka nakagawa2024 407–408 .

nep ka menoko payeka siri henne eci=nukar | what even woman pass.by appearance NEG 2PL.A=see | Did you see no sign at all that a woman had passed? | The negator henne licenses the same nep-plus-ka indefinite as standard somo. | nakagawa2024:408 | SHI | Okuda (ed.) 1999; Nakagawa ex. 837

nep kar kay omo an=ki wa okay=an | what do even NEG 4.A=do CONJ exist.PL=4.S | We lived doing no work at all. | kay is the Shiranuka focus variant of ka, omo the Shiranuka negator; the indefinite-licensing pattern is invariant across these dialect forms. | nakagawa2024:408 | HK | Shiranuka; Yotsuya 2007: 140; Nakagawa ex. 835

The generalization across these licensers is a scope condition, not a property of the
indefinite itself: a clause contains one negative predicate, and the
interrogative-plus- ka phrase receives a negative interpretation when it
falls within that predicate's scope ‹consensus›. The system therefore has no negative pronouns
of the English nothing kind, which carry their own negation and can negate a clause
unaided; the Ainu forms are read negatively only in concert with an overt negator
‹corpus-suggested›.

The particle ka: indefinite-formation and focus

The particle ka does two jobs in these clauses. It builds the indefinite
( nep ka ‘anything’), and it focuses the constituent that is negated
( … ka somo ki , … ka somo ne ). The two often
co-occur, so that a single clause carries ka twice, once on the
indefinite and once on the focused predicate:

nep ka pawci a=omare ka somo ki | what even poison 4.A=put.in even NEG do | I did not put in any poison. | Nakagawa ex. 530. The first ka forms the indefinite nep ka ‘anything’; the second ka focuses the notional predicate a=omare before somo ki. | nakagawa2024:323 | SAR

Whether ka is present or absent carries no meaning of its own; negative
clauses simply favour it, because focusing the constituent whose absence is asserted sharpens
the denial nakagawa2024 323, 407 . Three analyses of this
ka compete. Satō isolates a distinct ‘negative particle’
ka , left untranslated in his paradigms, while granting that it is hard to
separate strictly from the ordinary additive particle sato2008 124–125 . Nakagawa
and Bugaeva keep it continuous with additive and scalar focus ‘also, even, at all’, the latter
adding, after Tamura, a nuance of unexpectedness nakagawa2024 323, 407 ; bugaeva2012 496 . Ijäs presents the longer construction as emphatic and broadly exchangeable with plain
somo , glossing ka ‘also, even, at all’
ijas2023 . The disagreement is over how many homophonous particles to recognize for a
single surface form ‹contested›.

The copular negation NP ( ka ) somo ne uses the same
focus particle with the copula ne as its host, and Ijäs treats
ka somo ne as the more emphatic way of saying ‘A is not B’
sato2008 22 ; ijas2023 :

tan pe anak seta ka somo ne wa | this thing TOP dog even NEG COP FIN | This one is not a dog. | Satō ex. 6. The focused predicate nominal seta ka is negated by somo before the copula ne; the same ka that builds nep ka here focuses the complement of the copula. | sato2008:22 | HK

Scalar minimizers and exhaustive negation

A small set of scalar items reinforces the negative reading by naming the low endpoint of the
relevant scale. Numeral-based sinep ka ‘even one’ gives ‘not even one’
under a negator; oar ‘completely’ sharpens absence to ‘absolutely none’;
poka and ponno ka add a minimal-degree ‘even a
little’ nakagawa2024 323, 407 . None of these changes the formal licenser; the
scalar additive poka under preverbal somo is
illustrated in negation-somo-standard-clausal .

i=epetturasi p sinep ka isam | 4.O=be.equal NMLZ one even not.exist | There is not a single one that is my equal. | Nakagawa ex. 1025. The scalar sinep ka ‘even one’ under isam yields ‘not even one’. | nakagawa2024:450 | SAR

nep ka oar isam | what even completely not.exist | There is nothing at all. | The intensifier oar ‘completely’ reinforces nep ka under isam to ‘absolutely nothing’. | biratori1969 | SAR | Kurokawa Teshime, uwepeker ‘Piratorun nupur ekasi’

Exhaustive negation over a list is built by repeating the negated predicate, not by a dedicated
quantifier. Nakagawa's clause denies each member of the series in turn:

tu ipe somo ki re ipe somo ki no | two eat.a.meal NEG do three eat.a.meal NEG do ADV | eating neither two meals nor three meals, | Nakagawa ex. 531. Repetition of somo ki yields exhaustive denial; the stative linker no closes the negated subordinate clause (see the subordinate-asymmetry section of the standard-negation chapter). | nakagawa2024:323 | SAR

The Handbook restates the same pattern with the coordinated
somo ipe somo wakka ku no an ‘neither eating nor drinking’, after
Takahashi's analysis of negated subordinate clauses bugaeva2022 ; takahashi2016 75 . Constructed teaching examples in Ijäs use the same scalar logic, placing
oar under somo for ‘completely not, absolutely
not’; these illustrate an attested pattern but are not corpus evidence ijas2023 . A
dedicated free-choice paradigm — a distinct ‘anything whatever’ or ‘whoever’ series set apart
from the existential one — is not described in the read sources; the scalar minimizers carry the
‘even the least’ load that free-choice items carry elsewhere ‹speculative›.

The scope of negation

The licensing condition is a scope condition: the indefinite is interpreted within the scope of
the negative predicate of its clause, and the negator's scope is the whole verb phrase that
contains the indefinite nakagawa2024 323 ‹corpus-suggested›. The clearest scope
evidence comes from the interaction with the
evidential-schema-overview nominalization-plus-copula evidential . A clause closed
by a formal noun plus ne is negated by negating the verb phrase
inside the nominalization; the negator and the indefinite it licenses sit in the
embedded proposition, and the evidential frame stays outside the negation
nakagawa2024 393 .

sinep ka a=isamka ka somo ki ru-we ne kusu | one even 4.A=lose even NEG do track-POSS COP because | since I did not lose even a single one, | Nakagawa ex. 779. The scalar sinep ka and the negation somo ki sit inside the proposition over which ru-we ne predicates evidential status; the evidential is not in the scope of somo. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR

The mirror-image order locates the boundary. With ruwe somo ne the
negator falls on the copula of the formal-noun predicate, and the clause is no longer plain
propositional negation but a rhetorical or convictional assertion, close to ‘was it not the case
that …?’ nakagawa2024 393 . This high-scope copular negation does not license the
indefinite series; the polarity items nep ka and
sinep ka are licensed only in the lower, negated proposition. The
evidential's own negation belongs with the nonverbal-predicate apparatus of
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation , and the inferential frame itself with
ruwe-ne-inferential .

Within the embedded proposition the placement of somo can in principle
leave the scope underdetermined — whether it negates the notional verb or only the existential
that hosts the evidential — and Dal Corso's evidentiality study reports both readings for the
relevant configurations dalcorso2018 128 . The read Hokkaido grammars describe the
scope facts but settle no constituency analysis that would adjudicate such ambiguities
‹contested›.

Boundaries and open questions

The sources settle the basic pattern: interrogative-plus- ka forms are
existential outside negation and negative within it, and they are licensed alike by standard
somo , by dialectal negators such as henne and
omo , and by lexical negatives such as isam and
eaykap ‹consensus›. They do not provide a quantified corpus study of the
individual indefinite types, or a formal account of the scope ambiguities in evidential and
nominalized clauses; the corpus tools available here count lemmas, not the multi-word
[interrogative] ka … somo configurations, so no per-construction frequency
is asserted ‹corpus-suggested›.

The remaining analytic boundary is with ordinary focus. Because the same
ka marks addition, scalar focus, and the indefinite series, a single
negative clause routinely shows the particle twice — once forming an indefinite, once focusing
the negated predicate — and the grammars agree on the surface distribution while disagreeing on
how many particles to posit ‹contested›. The general account of the particle, including its
positive uses, is the necessary counterpart, given with
topic-marking-anakne the focus and additive particle .

title: The Prohibitive Subsystem: iteki and Negative Directives
summary: The dedicated prohibitive iteki, its asymmetry with declarative negation, apprehensive 'lest' uses, and links to the imperative/hortative system.
part: Part XVI — Negation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Prohibitive Subsystem: iteki and Negative Directives
Part: Part XVI — Negation
Summary: The dedicated prohibitive iteki, its asymmetry with declarative negation, apprehensive 'lest' uses, and links to the imperative/hortative system.

Place in the directive system

The prohibitive is the negative member of the directive system. A command not to act is formed
with a prohibitive adverb in front of the predicate, most often iteki don't in the
Saru and Chitose dialects, answering to an English negative imperative. Nakagawa groups
iteki and its dialect counterparts as 禁止の語句
‘prohibition words’, and Bugaeva glosses the construction as proh before
the verb nakagawa2024 424 ; bugaeva2012 497 . In the aligned
Hokkaido corpus iteki is the frequent, default form, with 1239 tokens
‹corpus-confirmed›.

The propositional negator somo not denies a declarative clause; the prohibitive
adverb withdraws a directive addressed to the hearer. In the typology of standard negation, which
is defined over declarative verbal main clauses, the prohibitive falls outside the core and into
the separate domain of the negative imperative miestamo2005 . The two systems
are formally distinct and can co-occur (below).

function
frame
predicate marking
treated in

standard clausal negation
somo V; V ( ka ) somo ki
finite verb with ordinary person indexing
negation-somo-standard-clausal

bare prohibitive
iteki V
addressee subject unindexed; an object may be indexed
this chapter

polite or plural prohibitive
iteki V yan
yan as in the affirmative imperative
this chapter; imperative-and-prohibitive

softened prohibitive
iteki V hani
final particle softens the command
this chapter; imperative-and-prohibitive

apprehensive warning
ikiya V na
subject person affix on the verb
this chapter

The affirmative imperative and the politeness morphology that the prohibitive shares with it are
set out in imperative-and-prohibitive ; hortative and optative directives in
hortative-and-optative . The bare prohibitive places iteki at
the left edge of the predicate phrase, ahead of the whole verb phrase rather than immediately
before the verb:

iteki ape sam ta ek | PROH fire side LOC come.SG | Do not come near the fire. | nakagawa2024:424 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 924

Forms and dialect distribution

The prohibitive adverb has several Hokkaido shapes. Nakagawa reports
iteki for Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, and Abuta;
itekke for Shizunai, Samani, and Tokachi; etekke
for Shiranuka, Bihoro, and Tokachi; and eciki or
ecikki for Ishikari nakagawa2024 424 . The forms differ
regionally and carry no reported difference in meaning; Tokachi appears for both
itekke and etekke in the source. The distribution
rests on one witness form per locality and is best read as indicative rather than as a closed
conditioning map ‹consensus›.

form
reported Hokkaido distribution

iteki
Saru, Chitose, Horobetsu, Abuta

itekke
Shizunai, Samani, Tokachi

etekke
Shiranuka, Bihoro, Tokachi

eciki , ecikki
Ishikari

tane orowano etekke wenpuri ramu | now thereafter PROH bad.conduct think | From now on, never think bad thoughts. | Shiranuka has no separate build-time dialect label, so the example is tagged HK. The Shiranuka form etekke precedes the object wenpuri and the verb ramu. | nakagawa2024:424 | HK | Shiranuka; Nakagawa ex. 927, after Yotake 2007:149

eciki iruska no hopuni wa e=koucaskuma=an kusu ne na | PROH be.angry CONJ get.up CONJ 2SG.O=explain=4.S PURP COP FIN | Get up without being angry, and I will explain the reason to you. | The Ishikari form eciki heads a no-linked negative-directive clause ‘without being angry’, which is followed by an affirmative directive continuation. | nakagawa2024:424 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 928, after Ōtsuka 1990:182

Position and the imperative verb

Nakagawa states the positional rule more loosely for the prohibitive than for
somo : iteki and its counterparts may stand anywhere
before the verb, whereas somo is in principle directly before the verb form
nakagawa2024 424 ; sato2008 124 . The latitude follows from
function. Standard negation supplies polarity to a finite verb; the prohibitive adverb contributes
directive force to the predicate phrase as a whole, and so occupies its left edge.

In the bare prohibitive the addressee is the understood S or A argument and the verb carries no
subject index. An object may still be indexed on the verb, as in iteki e yan
‘do not eat it’, where the directive subject is unexpressed and the third-person object is borne by
the verb bugaeva2012 497 . The person-marking facts thus run parallel to the
affirmative imperative.

iteki mokor | PROH sleep | Do not sleep. | The bluntest form: prohibitive adverb plus a bare, person-unmarked verb. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | Kanazawa 1898; NINJAL topical dictionary, ‘Sleeping and waking’

The prohibitive shares the politeness scale of the affirmative imperative. A plain verb is the
most direct command; iteki followed by yan with a
plural verb stem addresses a plural or respected hearer; iteki with
hani softens the directive nakagawa2024 424 ; bugaeva2012 497 ; ijas2023 .

iteki neno irara yan | PROH like.that play.tricks POL | Do not play such tricks. | The final yan carries the polite or plural value that also marks affirmative directives. | nakagawa2024:424 | HOR | Nakagawa ex. 925, after Chiri 1937a:102

ponno ka iteki ekimatek yan | a.little even PROH be.startled POL | Please do not be startled in the least. | The polarity item ponno ka ‘even a little’ precedes the prohibitive and is licensed within its scope. | kanazawa1898 | SAR | Kanazawa 1898; NINJAL topical dictionary, ‘Emotions’

Person-marked prohibition and the no-linker

Beyond the bare imperative stem, iteki takes a verb that keeps its person
affix. This is regular when a prohibited predicate is linked by no
‘without V-ing’ to a following directive, so that the prohibitive adverb scopes over a fully
person-marked clause nakagawa2024 425 .

iteki e=esina no e=ye p ne na | PROH 2SG.A=hide CONJ 2SG.A=say NMLZ COP FIN | Say it without hiding it. | Both verbs keep second-person subject indexing. The no-linked first predicate gives the prohibited manner; the p ne na frame delivers the positive directive. | nakagawa2024:425 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 935

iteki i=tura no | PROH 4.O=accompany CONJ | Do not follow me. | The indefinite-person object i= references the narrator; the no-clause links the prohibited action to the following discourse. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, ILCAA Ainu materials, uwepeker 9

The no linkage ties the prohibitive to the asymmetry of negated
subordinate clauses. Takahashi shows for Tokachi that a negated temporal or manner clause selects
no and blocks the successive wa , because a negated
action is construed as the static state of the action not taking place takahashi2016 75–77 . Nakagawa gives the same conditioning from the affirmative side: no
links a preceding phrase that is stative, negative in meaning, or an action verb accompanied by a
negative element nakagawa2024 340 . A prohibitive-headed clause is one such
environment; the symmetry of main-clause negation and the subordinate asymmetry are set out in
negation-somo-standard-clausal .

Ijäs formulates the classroom rule as ‘do Y without doing X’: the prohibited predicate is linked
with no and the following predicate carries the positive directive ijas2023 . Her further suggestion, that a wa linker would let the prohibition
continue across the next predicate, is offered without a supporting Hokkaido example and is left
open ‹speculative›.

Directive frames after iteki

Iteki feeds the same clause-final directive frames as the affirmative
command. Nakagawa lists iteki … yak pirka na ,
iteki … kusu ne na , iteki … p ne na , and
iteki … nankor na nakagawa2024 425 . Negative directive
force is therefore compatible with conditional, intentive, nominalized, and modalized packaging.

frame
value
routing

iteki V
direct negative command
this chapter; imperative-and-prohibitive

iteki V yak pirka na
conditional directive, ‘it is good if you do not V’
hortative-and-optative

iteki V kusu ne na
intentive or requested future non-action
hortative-and-optative

iteki V p ne na
nominalized directive assertion
nominalization-p-pe ; copula-ne-predicate-nominals

iteki V nankor na
modalized negative directive
hortative-and-optative

The conditional element yak underlies two adjacent constructions that must
be kept apart. In the directive frame iteki … yak pirka na the conditional
yak ‘if’ combines with pirka ‘be good’ to deliver a
command. The concessive yakka ‘even if’ instead builds the permission frame
yakka pirka ‘it is all right even if’, and a permission is negated by the
propositional somo , giving ‘you need not V’ rather than a command. The
division follows the boundary between propositional negation and negative directive force; the
illustrating sentence is constructed in Ijäs’s pedagogical text ijas2023 .

somo e=e yakka pirka | NEG 2SG.A=eat even.if good | You need not eat. | Ijäs Lesson 10. The permission frame yakka pirka is negated with somo, not with iteki; the result is a release from necessity, not a command. | ijas2023 | HK

Co-occurrence with somo

Iteki and somo are not in complementary
distribution. Nakagawa’s iteki pasirota somo eci=ki nankor na carries both
the prohibitive adverb and the propositional negator in one clause nakagawa2024 425 .
The prohibitive directs the addressee away from an action; the periphrastic
somo ki adds clausal negation of the embedded proposition. Each layer
contributes its own value, which is why iteki cannot be reduced to an
allomorph of the standard negator.

iteki pasirota somo eci=ki nankor na | PROH scold NEG 2PL.A=do probably FIN | You are certainly not to scold (him). | The notional verb pasirota ‘scold, revile’ is negated through the somo ki periphrasis, and eci=ki hosts the second-person plural index; iteki frames the whole as a directive, with the modal nankor na giving the emphatic ‘certainly not’. | nakagawa2024:425 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 932

Apprehensive warnings

Hokkaido Ainu has a set of warning adverbs adjacent to the prohibitive:
ika , ikiya , ikia(n)kunak ,
inkian , and the elevated ikineypeka nakagawa2024 425 . They express a cautionary ‘lest, be careful not to’ value, and the verb after them takes
ordinary subject person marking, which separates the construction from the bare imperative even
where the discourse force approaches a prohibition.

ikiya e=hacir na | ADM 2SG.S=fall FIN | Be careful not to fall. | Reproduced in Ijäs Lesson 10. The second-person subject is indexed on the verb e=hacir, unlike the unindexed verb of a bare prohibitive. | tamura2000:177 | SAR

The apprehensive warns that an unwanted event may occur; the prohibitive commands the addressee
not to act. Where iteki mokor says ‘do not sleep’, an
ikiya clause with a person-marked predicate cautions against an outcome
outside the addressee’s direct control nakagawa2024 425 .

title: Standard Clausal Negation with somo
summary: The preverbal standard negator somo: its position, scope, periphrastic somo ki construction, and placement in symmetric/asymmetric negation typology.
part: Part XVI — Negation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Standard Clausal Negation with somo
Part: Part XVI — Negation
Summary: The preverbal standard negator somo: its position, scope, periphrastic somo ki construction, and placement in symmetric/asymmetric negation typology.

Place in the negation system

Standard clausal negation in Hokkaido Ainu is carried by the free adverb
somo not , placed before the predicate phrase. The verb itself takes no negative
inflection; the language has no negative conjugation comparable to the Japanese verb-final
suffix ない nakagawa2024 365–366, 407 ; sato2008 124 ; bugaeva2012 496 . Nakagawa is explicit that the preverbal position is not a
negation-specific rule: somo is an adverb, and the general principle that
a modifier precedes the phrase it modifies puts it, like any other preverbal adverb, in front of
the verb nakagawa2024 366 . The negator can also stand alone as a one-word reply,
somo ‘no, that is not so’ nakagawa2024 408 .

strategy
form
main use
treated in

standard verbal negation
somo V
negative declarative verbal clause
this chapter

periphrastic standard negation
V ( ka ) somo ki
ordinary or emphatic verbal negation with the formal verb ki
this chapter

copular negation
NP ( ka ) somo ne
negative predicate-nominal clause
this chapter; copula-ne-predicate-nominals

negative existential and privative
isam , sak
‘there is none’, ‘lack, be without’
negation-existential-possessive-isam

negative ability and cognition
eaykap , eramiskari , and peers
‘cannot’, ‘have never’, ‘do not understand’
negation-ability-cognition-predicates

polarity-sensitive indefinites
nep ka , nen ka
‘nothing’, ‘no one’ under negation
negation-polarity-items-scope

prohibitive
iteki V
negative directive
negation-prohibitive-iteki

The table separates standard negation from the lexical and directive systems that also yield
negative meanings, and the split has syntactic consequences. A clause built on
isam not exist or eaykap cannot contains a negative predicate that
indexes its arguments in the ordinary way; a clause with somo keeps the
positive predicate and places a negative adverb in its scope. Satō formalises the division by
predicate type: a verbal clause is negated as [subject] V (ka) somo ki or
with preverbal somo directly before the verb, while a copular clause is
negated as [subject] [complement] (ka) somo ne , with the negator before
the copula-ne-predicate-nominals copula ne
sato2008 22 . Ijäs presents the same architecture for learners: copular ‘A is
not B’ is A B somo ne , agreeing with Satō’s paradigm and Nakagawa’s
ordering description ijas2023 ; sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 365 .

Preverbal somo

With a verbal predicate, somo stands immediately before the person-marked
verb. Satō states the adjacency as a rule: the negator precedes the verb, and the personal index
is part of the verb form, so the negator cannot intervene between the index and its stem —
somo ku=arpa ‘I do not go’, never *ku=somo=arpa
sato2008 124 ; nakagawa2024 407 . Ijäs gives learners the same
warning against splitting the personal affix from its stem with the negator ijas2023 .

noka-ha poka somo a=nukar | figure-POSS even NEG 4.A=see | One did not even see its figure. | Nakagawa ex. 832. The simple preverbal pattern; the possessed noun noka-ha carries the additive poka ‘even’, a polarity item licensed under somo. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

somo k=arpa wa | NEG 1SG.S=go.SG FIN | I will not go. | bugaeva2012:496 | CHI | Bugaeva ex. 73, siglum NN 28 (Nakagawa Chitose data)

For an ordinary verb the bare frame somo V is grammatical but, by Satō’s
report, used less often than the ka somo ki periphrasis of the next
section sato2008 22 . A modal or other postverbal element follows the verb in
its normal place, with the negator still at the front of the predicate:

tanto anak somo ek nankor wa | today TOP NEG come.SG probably FIN | He probably will not come today. | The negator precedes the verb ek; the dubitative nankor and the final wa stay in their postverbal positions. | sato2008:22 | HK

Standard negation here is symmetric in the sense of the typology of negation: the affirmative
and negative declarative clauses differ only by the added negative morpheme, with no separate
negative verb form or special negative agreement miestamo2005 ; takahashi2016 75 . Takahashi’s Tokachi weather predicate gives the minimal pair, the affirmative
ruyanpe ruy ‘it rained’ differing from its negation by
somo alone:

ruyanpe somo ruy | rain NEG be.intense | It did not rain. | the rain was not intense | Takahashi ex. 11b, against the affirmative ruyanpe ruy ‘it rained’ (ex. 11a); the clauses differ only in somo. | takahashi2016:75 | TOK

Preverbal somo also negates the existential-locational predicate
an exist, be located , where the positive verb stays available alongside the
suppletive negative existential isam . Ijäs draws a semantic line between
the two — isam for outright absence or disappearance,
somo an where the ‘live, be located’ reading of an
is to be preserved — but the exact split is her own formulation and is not stated in those terms
by the read grammars ‹contested› ijas2023 . The privative paradigm and possessive
uses of isam are treated in
negation-existential-possessive-isam .

The somo ki periphrasis

The second verbal frame places the notional predicate before somo ki ,
with optional ka between them: V ( ka )
somo ki . Bugaeva gives the compositional analysis: it is the verb
ki do that somo formally negates, while the notional verb
functions as an object-like complement of ki ; the notional verb keeps its
person marking and ki carries none bugaeva2012 496 .
Nakagawa treats the same string as one use of the formal verb ki , which
supplies a verbal host so that conjunctions and clause-final material can attach nakagawa2024 374, 407 . Ijäs’s pedagogical account agrees, describing ki as a dummy ‘do’ verb
filling the slot that somo cannot occupy on its own ijas2023 .
Against this, Satō’s description of Chitose, as reported by Takahashi, analyses
somo ki as a single auxiliary rather than as ki
negated over a complement; the internal constituency of the periphrasis is therefore genuinely
disputed ‹contested› sato2008 86 ; takahashi2016 74 . Refsing
records the construction for Shizunai as well refsing1986 214–215 .

nep a=nukar ka somo ki korka | something 4.A=see even NEG do although | Although I did not see anything, | Nakagawa ex. 833. The periphrasis with ka; the indefinite nep ka ‘anything’ is licensed under the negation. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

eci=yayeymontasa somo ki yakka | 2PL.S=take.revenge NEG do even.if | Even if you do not take revenge, | Nakagawa ex. 834. The same periphrasis without ka; yayeymontasa is intransitive, so eci= indexes the 2PL subject. | nakagawa2024:407 | SAR

Nakagawa finds no clear semantic contrast between the preverbal frame and the
somo ki frame, and likewise no special meaning in the presence or absence
of ka nakagawa2024 374, 407 ; Ijäs treats the longer
construction as broadly exchangeable with plain somo ijas2023 .
When ka is present it brackets the notional predicate as the focused
complement of ki :

ku=iruska ka somo ki wa | 1SG.S=be.angry even NEG do FIN | I am not angry. | The bracketed [ku=iruska ka] is the object-like complement that ki formally negates. | bugaeva2012:496 | SAR | Bugaeva ex. 74, siglum T2 239 (Tamura 2000: 239)

Ijäs additionally suggests that the postverbal ka somo ki arose as a
calque on Japanese verb-final negation, with preverbal somo as the older
Ainu pattern. No read Hokkaido source supports a contact origin, and Dal Corso’s diachronic
account of Ainu negation derives the periphrastic forms internally, so the contact hypothesis is
best left as her own conjecture ‹speculative› ijas2023 ; dalcorso2025neg 41–42 .

Copular negation and the particle ka

A predicate-nominal clause is negated by placing somo before the copula
ne , optionally with ka : NP
( ka ) somo ne sato2008 22 ; nakagawa2024 365 . Satō notes that the alternative ne ka somo ki , expected if
ne is fed through the ordinary somo ki frame as a
verb, is scarcely attested; the productive negator of the copular clause is
somo ne or ka somo ne sato2008 22 .

tan pe anak seta ka somo ne wa | this thing TOP dog even NEG COP FIN | This one is not a dog. | Satō ex. 6. Copular ka somo ne; somo precedes the copula ne, with ka on the complement seta. | sato2008:22 | HK

The particle ka in ka somo ki and
ka somo ne is the ordinary additive and focus particle ‘also, even, at
all’, here attached to the negated constituent. Nakagawa observes that negative clauses favour
ka — adding focus to the element whose absence is asserted sharpens the
negation — while its presence carries no meaning of its own nakagawa2024 323, 407 .
Satō isolates a distinct ‘negative particle’ ka , left untranslated in his
paradigms, while granting that it is hard to separate strictly from the additive particle; for
Bugaeva, following Tamura, the same ka focuses the complement with a
nuance of unexpectedness sato2008 124–125 ; bugaeva2012 496 . The
divergence is one of analysis and terminology over a single form ‹contested›. Ijäs presents
ka somo ne as the more emphatic equative negation, glossing
ka ‘also, even, at all’ ijas2023 .

The same ka builds the negative-polarity indefinites
nep ka ‘anything, and under negation nothing’ and
nen ka ‘anyone, no one’, whose licensing depends on the scope of the
negator over the whole clause; that interaction is treated in
negation-polarity-items-scope .

Negation under the evidential predicate

A clause closed by a formal noun plus ne — the
evidential-schema-overview evidential frames
ruwe ne , hawe ne ,
siri ne , humi ne — is negated by negating the
verb phrase inside the nominalization, leaving the evidential frame itself in place. Nakagawa
states the rule directly: the negative counterpart of such a clause makes the verb phrase before
the formal noun negative, so the negation scopes below the evidential nakagawa2024 393 .

sinep ka a=isamka ka somo ki ru-we ne kusu | one even 4.A=lose even NEG do track-POSS COP because | Since I did not lose even a single one, | Nakagawa ex. 779. The negated predicate a=isamka ka somo ki sits inside the evidential ru-we ne; the evidential itself is outside the scope of somo. | nakagawa2024:393 | SAR

A surface-similar sequence in which the negator falls on the copula of the evidential,
ruwe somo ne , is not ordinary clausal negation but a rhetorical or
convictional construction, close to ‘was it not the case that …?’ nakagawa2024 393 .
The contrast — propositional negation inside the evidential against the high-scope rhetorical
frame on the copula — belongs with the evidential paradigm in
evidential-schema-overview .

Symmetry and subordinate asymmetry

In main declarative clauses, verbal and copular alike, somo negation is
symmetric: the negative clause retains the predicate architecture of the affirmative and adds
the negative adverb before the predicate miestamo2005 ; takahashi2016 75 . Subordinate clauses behave differently. Hokkaido temporal adverbial clauses link with
several conjunctive particles — wa for a successive event,
no for a stative one — but a negated adverbial clause selects only
no , and the successive wa is blocked takahashi2016 75–77 .

somo ipe no mokor | NEG eat.a.meal ADV sleep | He went to sleep without eating. | The parallel with the successive linker, *somo ipe wa mokor, is ungrammatical: a negated event links only with the stative no. | bugaeva2022; takahashi2016:76 | HK

The restriction is uncontroversial as a fact ‹consensus›; its explanation is the live
contribution. Following Tamura’s analysis that the negation of an action is construed as a
static state rather than as an event, Takahashi and the Handbook treat the
no -only pattern as an asymmetry driven by aspect, with the choice of
linker depending on polarity takahashi2016 76 ; bugaeva2022 .
Nakagawa’s account of no gives the same conditioning from the affirmative
side: no links a preceding phrase that is stative, negative in meaning,
or an action verb accompanied by a negative element nakagawa2024 340 . Whether
the dependency is best modelled as aspect government, as reduced transitivity, or pragmatically
remains open in the literature ‹contested›.

Boundaries and open questions

Several negative meanings adjacent to somo have their own predicate
architecture. The negative existential isam and the privative
sak lack express absence and lack with ordinary verbal syntax; the ability and
cognition negatives eaykap , koyaykus ,
eramiskari , and erampewtek form a lexical-negative
class; and the prohibitive iteki don't belongs to directive mood nakagawa2024 314–315, 424–425, 450 ; bugaeva2012 496–497 . These are routed to
negation-existential-possessive-isam ,
negation-ability-cognition-predicates , and
negation-prohibitive-iteki . Ijäs’s claim that the existence of a lexical negative
normally bars somo plus the corresponding positive verb states the
tendency too strongly: Dal Corso records southern Hokkaido somo kor ‘not
have’ beside privative sak , and the read grammars describe lexicalized
meanings and preferences rather than categorical blocking ‹contested› ijas2023 ; dalcorso2025neg 41 .

Dal Corso’s Sakhalin-focused comparative work links somo and
isam to an older negative-existential root
*sam , deriving somo from a form like
*samno and isam from antipassive
i- on that root; Ijäs reaches the same speculation for
isam , marking it as conjectural dalcorso2025neg 41–42 ;
ijas2023 . The read Hokkaido grammars state no such etymology, so the shared-root
proposal stands as a specific historical hypothesis, not as settled synchronic analysis
‹speculative›.

The standard negator varies dialectally. somo covers most of Hokkaido;
the eastern Pacific coast and parts of the east use other free negators in the same structural
slot, and the eastern dialects also have a rare suffix -ko that derives a
rhetorical negative adverb from a stative intransitive — a genuinely morphological negative in
an otherwise periphrastic system nakagawa2024 407–408 ; takahashi2016 73 ; dalcorso2025neg 41 .

negator
approximate distribution

somo
most of Hokkaido (north, south, west, and central-east)

omo , homo
eastern Pacific coast (Shiranuka, Kushiro)

henne
eastern Hidaka and Shizunai

senne , sen
Horobetsu and Yakumo

sien
Tokachi

The microdistribution is assembled from scattered dialect reports rather than from a full
conditioning study, and no rule is given for which negator a transitional locality selects
‹contested› nakagawa2024 407–408 ; dalcorso2025neg 41 . The wider
dialect classification is treated in hokkaido-dialect-classification-and-dialectometry .
