title: The Nominalizers -p/-pe and -i/-hi (Participant, Event, Place, Fact)
summary: The participant nominalizer -p/-pe ('the one that') and the abstract/event nominalizer -i/-hi ('the V-ing / place / time / fact that'): argument-role readings, the headless-relative continuum, internal clause TAM/person, and the insubordination of nominalized clauses.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Nominalizers -p/-pe and -i/-hi (Participant, Event, Place, Fact)
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The participant nominalizer -p/-pe ('the one that') and the abstract/event nominalizer -i/-hi ('the V-ing / place / time / fact that'): argument-role readings, the headless-relative continuum, internal clause TAM/person, and the insubordination of nominalized clauses.

Form and place among the formal nouns

The nominalizer p ~ pe turns a finite clause into a
noun phrase. It is a member of the closed class of
formal-defective-nouns formal nouns — light, weakly autonomous nouns placed after
another element to build an NP — and it supplies a generic nominal head meaning, in its concrete
uses, ‘the one who’ or ‘the thing that’. Hokkaido Ainu has no participial or otherwise
non-finite verb form for this work: the verb inside the nominalized phrase keeps its ordinary
person marking, polarity, number, and clause-internal morphology, and subordination is signalled
by the following formal noun rather than by any change in the verb
bugaeva2016 100, 226–228 ; nakagawa2024 503, 512–513 ; sato2008 174–175 .

The allomorphy is phonologically conditioned: the short form -p follows a
stem-final vowel, and -pe follows a stem-final consonant. The same split
holds whether the host is a single stative stem ( poro-p ‘the big one’,
pon-pe ‘the small one’) or a full clause ( a=e p
‘what one eats’, okay pe ‘what one carries’)
sato2008 175 ; refsing1986 260 ; bugaeva2022 ch. 14 .
Satō records a sandhi variant pa at the junction of
p and a following conjunctive wa
sato2008 175 .

stem-final segment
allomorph
representative forms

vowel
-p / clausal p

ami-p ‘clothes’, poro-p ‘the big one’,
a=e p ‘what one eats’

consonant
-pe / clausal pe

an-pe ‘one that is’, mokor-pe ‘the sleeping
one’, okay pe ‘what one carries’

The form spans lexical derivation and syntax without a break. Shibatani describes
-p(e) as deriving a noun for a person or thing characterized by the verb,
as in pirka-p ‘good thing’ and ray-pe ‘the dead’,
and as a productive phrasal and clausal nominalizer besides
shibatani1990 33–34 . In Bugaeva's areal account of nominalization, Hokkaido
expresses clausal nominalization through a nominalizing word placed after the clause, the type
[(np…) V p/pe] , a stage from which lexical items such as
aep food have crystallized; the Sakhalin varieties extended the same system to
possessed verbal forms bugaeva2016 243, 385–397 . Among the formal nouns,
p ~ pe keeps a ‘thing/person’ profile beside the
maximally abstract hi and the sensory-evidential heads
ruwe , siri , hawe , and
humi , whose inventory and possessed morphology are set out in
formal-defective-nouns and whose clause-final evidential use is treated in
evidential-schema-overview .

Participant nominalization: the one who, the thing that

In its participant reading, p ~ pe is a generic
head whose referent is fixed by the argument structure of the clause before it. The clause
contains a gap, and the role of that gap — agent, patient, or an oblique participant — is
recovered from the verb's valence and from any postposition left in place
nakagawa2024 512 ; bugaeva2015 47 . This is the headless edge of
the relative-clause system: where an ordinary relative clause precedes a lexical head noun,
p ~ pe supplies the head itself. The gap strategy,
the accessibility hierarchy, and the postposition-retention facts are developed in
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses , and the place of the headless type within the wider
typology of noun-modifying clauses in
headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses .

tapan kina a=e p ne toan kina somo a=e p ne | this plant 4.A=eat NMLZ COP that plant NEG 4.A=eat NMLZ COP | This plant is one that people eat; that plant is one that people do not eat. | The fourth person a= gives a generic human eater; p is the object head of each clause — the thing eaten. The vowel-final e selects the short allomorph -p. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 14 (first half) [aa-irc/020#27]

a=se wa okay pe | 4.A=carry.on.back CONJ exist.PL NMLZ | the things one is carrying along | pe is the object of se and the subject of okay; the consonant-final okay selects the allomorph -pe. | nakagawa2024:512 | HK

Lexicalized items preserve the syntax at word scale. aep food is
a=e-p , ‘thing one eats’, with the fourth-person transitive prefix and the
nominalizer bugaeva2016 245–251 . Nakagawa's contrast between the synthesized
a=e-suke-p ‘kitchen utensil’ and the compound
sukemakiri ‘kitchen knife’ shows the diagnostic: the relative-clause
nominalization keeps the applicative e- that licenses the instrument it
names, whereas a compound leaves the relation between its two nouns grammatically unspecified
nakagawa2024 506–507 . The lexicalized inventory is treated in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

a=e-suke-p | 4.A=APPL-cook-NMLZ | kitchen utensil | thing one cooks with | The applicative e- licenses the instrumental participant that p nominalizes; the plain compound sukemakiri has no such prefix. | nakagawa2024:507; kayano1996:281 | SAR

An oblique participant is nominalized the same way, with the governing postposition retained
inside the clause so that the relation between head and predicate stays overt. With an
instrument the postposition ani ‘with’ remains, and
p names the instrument nakagawa2024 504 ; bugaeva2015 47–49 ; keenancomrie1977 .

ani usey a=ku p | INS hot.water 4.A=drink NMLZ | a thing to drink hot water with | The instrumental ani stays inside the clause; p is the generic instrument head, the object of ku 'drink'. | nakagawa2024:504 | HK

Ijäs teaches the same rule for relative clauses generally: the modifying verb phrase precedes
the head, there is no relative pronoun, and an oblique head keeps the postposition fronted within
the clause ijas2023 . Her examples are constructed teaching material and align with
Nakagawa, Satō, and Bugaeva.

ani cep ku=tuypa makiri | INS fish 1SG.A=cut knife | the knife I cut fish with | Ijäs Lesson 11. The instrumental ani is fronted and retained; the head here is the common noun makiri, the same gap strategy that p and pe carry without a lexical head. | ijas2023 | HK

Event and fact nominalization

When the clause before it is already saturated — every argument the verbs require is present —
p ~ pe nominalizes the event or fact as a whole and
serves as a complementizer rather than a participant head. Nakagawa treats some formal nouns,
p ~ pe among them, as filling this complementizing
role; Satō draws the line explicitly between a nominalizer ( 名詞化辞 ) that
turns a whole content clause into a noun phrase and a formal noun that heads a relative clause,
the first allowing no reconstruction of an underlying sentence nakagawa2024 503–509, 512–513 ; sato2008 174–180 .

ne kusu haweoka wa cis pa p ka a=erampewtek | COP because speak.PL CONJ cry PL NMLZ even 4.A=not.understand | I did not even understand why they were crying. | p is not an argument of speak or cry; the clause haweoka wa cis pa is complete (arity 0). p nominalizes the whole reason clause, which is then the object of a=erampewtek. | nakagawa2024:512–513 | HK

ratcitara rok=an pe ne na | quietly sit=4.S NMLZ COP FIN | One should sit quietly. | pe nominalizes a saturated intransitive; the predication pe ne na carries the deontic 'should' reading of Satō's content-clause use. | sato2008:177 | CHI

In this use p ~ pe stands beside the other
complementizing formal nouns — abstract hi and the sensory heads — which
differ in the construal they impose. The complement-taking predicates and the control and
raising diagnostics are treated in nominalized-complements-control-raising ; the
future-oriented sequence kuni p in
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements ; the quotative complementizer
sekor and the indirect-speech markers in
quotative-complementation-sekor ; and the clause-final evidential reflexes of the
sensory heads in ruwe-ne-inferential the inferential ruwe ne .

a=e=túskote kuni p ne ru-we ne na | 4.A=2SG.O=tie.up PURP NMLZ COP track-POSS COP FIN | You will surely be tied up. | The fourth-person agent a= with the second-person object e= yields a passive-like reading; kuni p forms a future/deontic nominal, and the framing predicate ru-we ne presents it as established fact. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak monologue 2 [aa-irc/004#130]

Arity and the boundary between the two uses

Nakagawa's valence arithmetic keeps the participant and complementizer uses from collapsing into
one reading. A formal noun has arity −1, on a par with hi and
uske ; a clause that still requires a participant has positive arity, and
the formal noun fills the open slot, giving a relative-clause NP; a clause of arity 0 is complete,
and the formal noun nominalizes the whole of it, giving an event or fact nominal nakagawa2024 261–262, 504, 507 . The surface string [clause] p/pe is identical in both; the
grammatical relation differs.

clause before p ~ pe
role of p ~ pe
resulting NP
treated in

arity ≥ +1 (a participant slot is open)
generic head, ‘the one who / the thing that’
headless relative-clause NP
prenominal-gap-relative-clauses

arity 0 (the clause is saturated)
complementizer over the whole clause
event or fact nominal
nominalized-complements-control-raising

single stem or lexicalized predicate
derivational nominalizer
lexical noun
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization

Analyses and open questions

The descriptions agree on the substance — p ~ pe
builds lexical, phrasal, and clausal nominals, heads a generic participant in a relative clause,
and nominalizes a saturated clause as an event or fact — and differ on the labels. Shibatani and
Refsing use ‘nominalizer’ broadly; Satō separates the whole-clause nominalizer from the
relative-clause-heading formal noun; Nakagawa makes the formal noun a word class and the
complementizer a syntactic role available to some of its members; Bugaeva treats these light
bound nouns as the generic heads of gapped relative clauses shibatani1990 33–34 ;
refsing1986 259–264 ; sato2008 174–175 ; nakagawa2024 508 ; bugaeva2015 47 ‹contested›.

The constituency of the nominalized clause plus a following copula — the frame
[clause] p/pe ne — is the deeper disagreement, and it is the same one that
runs through the evidential system. One line analyses the structure as biclausal, a formal or
possessed noun heading the preceding clause and closed by the copula; another holds that the
erstwhile noun is grammaticalizing toward a monoclausal complex predicate. No source read here
offers a constituency proof that decides between them tamura2000 227 ; sato2008 175 ; bugaeva2016 306–315 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 ‹contested›.

Ijäs's observations on relative clauses fit this system, with two cautions. Her report that the
subject of a ditransitive resists being the head matches Bugaeva's finding that no example of an
A-of-three-place-verb head is attested; the construction is an attested gap rather than a settled
ungrammaticality ‹corpus-confirmed›. Her difficulty in finding ablative
wa(no) ‘from which’ or mutative ne ‘as which’ as
relative-clause heads is shared by the sources read here, so the forms she suggests for them are
left aside rather than presented as established ‹corpus-suggested› ijas2023 ; bugaeva2015 48 .

The same nominalizer feeds clause linkage at the far end of the cline: a bleached
p ~ pe works as an adversative conjunction
‘though, and yet’, no longer an argument of the following clause, and the Horobetsu nominalizers
grammaticalize a step further into adverbial-clause markers nakagawa2024 515–516 ;
takahashi2018 107–112 .

Token frequency confirms that pe is common but cannot measure this
construction directly. The corpus word-frequency table returns 13,249 tokens of
pe (rank 15), a figure that folds together the formal noun, the numeral
counter -pe , and lexical pe ‘water’, while the
short allomorph p is obscured by segmentation. A per-function count would
require construction-level annotation ‹corpus-suggested›.

title: Nominalized Complements and Control/Raising
summary: Complement clauses formed by nominalization (hi, -i, ruwe) under perception, cognition, and evaluative predicates, and the control/raising diagnostics of same-subject complex predicates.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Nominalized Complements and Control/Raising
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: Complement clauses formed by nominalization (hi, -i, ruwe) under perception, cognition, and evaluative predicates, and the control/raising diagnostics of same-subject complex predicates.

Overview

Clausal complements in Hokkaido Ainu are formed through the same nominalization strategy that
builds relative-clause NPs: a finite, person-marked clause is followed by a formal noun or
complementizer that turns the whole into a nominal argument of a higher predicate. The verb inside
the embedded clause keeps its ordinary person affixes, polarity, and aspect morphology; no
non-finite or participial form exists
bugaeva2016 100, 226–228 ; nakagawa2024 503 . What separates a
complement clause from a relative clause is a single structural condition, the
arity of the embedded clause, treated in the section that follows. The inventory of
complementizers includes the abstract formal noun hi thing, fact, time, place ,
the sensory possessive forms siri appearance , hawe voice , and
humi sound, feeling , the concrete nominalizer p (thing) ~ pe (thing/person) , and the irrealis complementizer kuni (purposive/deontic) , together with a
set of indirect-speech markers treated in quotative-complementation-sekor . Their
grammaticalization from lexical nouns and their role in evidential clause-final constructions are
set out in formal-defective-nouns and evidential-schema-overview ;
p ~ pe as a headless relative-clause nominalizer is treated in
nominalization-p-pe , which cross-references the present chapter for the
complementizer use.

The arity-0 diagnostic

Nakagawa's arity (アリティ) accounting provides the structural boundary between a
relative clause and a complement clause. Every NP in the clause contributes −1 to a running
count; every argument slot required by the verb contributes +1. A formal noun itself has arity
−1. When the clause before a formal noun has total arity ≥ +1, a participant slot remains open
and the formal noun fills it as the head of a relative-clause NP. When the clause already has
arity 0 — every argument slot is satisfied — the formal noun nominalizes the whole clause as an
event or fact; this is the complement reading
nakagawa2024 261–262, 504, 507 .

arity of the embedded clause
role of the following formal noun
construction type

≥ +1 (a participant slot is unfilled)
head of the relative clause, fills the open slot
relative-clause NP → prenominal-gap-relative-clauses

0 (the clause is fully saturated)
complementizer, nominalizes the whole clause
complement clause (this chapter)

Satō's formulation of the same distinction is categorical: a nominalizer (名詞化辞)
nominalizes a whole content clause and allows no reconstruction of an underlying sentence,
whereas a formal noun (形式名詞) heading a relative clause can be so reconstructed
sato2008 174–175 . Nakagawa prefers to treat the formal nouns as a word class and
the complementizer function as a syntactic role available to some members of that class
nakagawa2024 508 . The same surface string [clause] hi
or [clause] p is parsed differently depending on which reading the arity
yields; examples below illustrate both.

The following illustrates the complement reading of hi . The embedded
clause hekattar tura sinot=an has arity 0:
sinot 'play' is an intransitive verb (+1) whose S slot is satisfied by
the fourth-person suffix =an (−1), leaving no gap.
hi therefore nominalizes the whole clause as a fact:

hekattar tura sinot=an hi ka a=onaha kopan | children COMIT play=4.S NMLZ even 4.A=father refuse | My father refused even that I play with the children. | The embedded clause hekattar tura sinot=an has arity 0 and hi nominalizes it as a fact-complement. Nakagawa's arity count: sinot (+1) with =an (−1) = 0. hi cannot be a relative-clause head here because there is no open slot to fill. | nakagawa2024:507 | HK

Complement-taking predicates

The matrix predicates that select a nominalized complement fall into three semantic groups:
cognition, perception, and speech/directive. A verb's argument structure places an upper bound
on the number of person affixes it can carry — typically one for the agent (A) and one for the
object (O); the complement clause then occupies the single object slot
nakagawa2024 503–509 . This is directly related to the
person-marking-architecture two-affix cap on the verb template .

predicate
gloss
preferred complementizer(s)
semantic class

nukar
see
sir-i , hi
visual perception

nu
hear, ask about
haw-e , hum-i , hi
auditory/non-visual perception

ramu
think
kuni , kunak , yak , sir-i , hum-i
cognition

eramiskari / eramuskari
not know, not understand
hi , p ~ pe
cognition (negative)

eraman / eramuan
understand, know
hi , p ~ pe
cognition

kopan
refuse, not want
hi
volition

ye
say, tell
kuni , kunak , yak , kusu
speech

eyam
worry
yak
emotive

takar
dream
yak
emotive/cognitive

The list above is drawn from Nakagawa and Satō and is not exhaustive
nakagawa2024 475–480, 503–513 ; sato2008 175–178 . The
complementizer preferences are typical but not always categorical: ramu ,
for instance, co-occurs with kuni for future and wished content, with
yak for past or ongoing content, and with the sensory heads
sir-i and hum-i when thought involves a
quasi-perceptual quality ‹corpus-suggested›.

hi as complementizer

The formal noun hi thing, fact, time, place is the maximally abstract
complementizer. Satō describes it as carrying "almost no concrete meaning" and functioning to
nominalize the entire clause before it
sato2008 169, 174 ; Nakagawa calls it a 補文標識 (complementizer) when its
preceding clause has arity 0
nakagawa2024 507 . In this role it appears most frequently as the object of
cognition predicates such as eramiskari 'not understand' and
kopan 'refuse':

a=ye hi ka a=kar hi ka a=eramiskari | 4.A=say NMLZ even 4.A=do NMLZ even 4.A=not.understand | not knowing what to say or what to do | Two parallel hi complements coordinated with ka 'even/also': a=ye hi 'the saying-thing' and a=kar hi 'the doing-thing' both serve as objects of a=eramiskari. Each embedded clause has arity 0 (the 4th-person agent a= fills the A slot of a transitive verb). | ninjal2003 | CHI | Oda Ito, アイヌ語口承文芸コーパス (NINJAL) [ninjal-folklore/026#61]

A nominalized clause headed by hi can also serve as the grammatical
subject of a stative predication. Bugaeva cites (after Tamura) a clausal-subject construction
in which a coordinated VP is nominalized by hi and predicated as 'best':

ku=kemeyki kor ku=itak hi iyotta pirka | 1SG.A=sew while 1SG.A=speak NMLZ most be.good | Speaking while sewing is best. | After Tamura (2000: 126). hi nominalizes the saturated coordinated clause ku=kemeyki kor ku=itak (arity 0: each transitive verb has its A slot filled by ku=). The nominalized NP is the clausal subject of the predication iyotta pirka 'is best'. | bugaeva2016:261 | SAR

The Okuda–Nakagawa analysis of hi distinguishes a 'place where' reading
(arity ≥ +1, RC head, requires a case postposition such as oro ta ) from
a 'time/thing/fact' reading (arity 0, complementizer, no postposition required)
nakagawa2024 510–511 . This distinction is a tendency, not absolute: the
Horobetsu example seta utar osoma hi 'the dogs' privy' (literally 'the
place dogs defecate') lacks oro ta yet takes the 'place' reading
nakagawa2024 511 ‹contested›. The 'place' analysis rests on Okuda (1989), read
only second-hand via Nakagawa here.

The sensory nominalizers as perception complements

The four possessed-form nominalizers ru-we , sir-i ,
haw-e , and hum-i function primarily as
clause-final evidential heads (treated in evidential-schema-overview and
ruwe-ne-inferential ), but Satō shows that three of them — sir-i ,
haw-e , and hum-i — also nominalize embedded
clauses as perception-object NPs under nukar 'see' and
nu 'hear/ask', with a semantic specialization:
sir-i marks an event in progress at reference time (visual perception),
haw-e a speech event heard, and
hum-i a non-visual sensation
sato2008 175–178 ; nakagawa2024 508 .

In this use the possessed-form nominalizer takes a saturated clause as its implicit possessor:
the 'appearance/voice/feeling of [the embedded event]'. The whole
[clause] sir-i or
[clause] haw-e then fills the O slot of the perception verb:

hekaci apkas sir-i ku=nukar | child walk appearance-POSS 1SG.A=see | I saw the child walking. | sir-i ( ne or existential an ) is the same
construction extended to main-clause predication
nakagawa2024 508 . The Dal Corso debate about whether these structures are
biclausal or partially grammaticalized monoclausal predicates applies equally here and is taken
up in the constituency section below.

kuni as complement marker

The complementizer kuni (purposive/deontic) nominalizes the preceding clause and
expresses content that is future-oriented, inferred, desired, or directed: what the matrix
subject says must happen, thinks should happen, or intends to happen
nakagawa2024 476 . Nakagawa classifies kuni as a
conjunction with formal-noun-like use (接続助詞の形式名詞的な用法); Bugaeva analyses the complex
kuni-p as deontic 'what should be'
bugaeva2016 294 . The purpose and adverbial uses of kuni
are treated in kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements ; the focus here is on the
complement-of-speech and complement-of-cognition uses.

With ye 'say', kuni marks a directive or an
anticipated future event reported in speech:

hosipi=an kuni a=ye | return=4.S PURP 4.A=say | I said I would return. | kuni nominalizes the saturated clause hosipi=an (intransitive 'return', +1 with =an −1 = 0) as the object of a=ye. The kuni complement presents the speaker's stated intention — a future event in the speaker's assertion. | nakagawa2024:476 | HK

ahun kuni a=ye akusu toykooripak | enter PURP 4.A=say when be.very.abashed | When I told them to enter, they were very abashed. | The directive use: the matrix subject (4th-person narrator) tells the addressees to enter. The complement's agent (the addressees) differs from the matrix S. akusu 'thereupon/when' links to the consequence clause. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 8 (part 2) [aa-irc/007#213]

With ramu 'think', kuni expresses a wished or
expected state of affairs:

pirka kuni ku=ramu | be.good PURP 1SG.A=think | I think it would be good. | Constructed to illustrate the kuni + ramu pattern. pirka 'be.good' is a stative intransitive (arity +1); without an overt S it has arity +1 — but in this context the subject is pragmatically recoverable. For the complementizer reading, the argument is that the speaker's kuni-complement expresses wished or inferred content, not a specific participant gap. Compare the analysis of arity in Nakagawa (2024: 476). | HK

The combination kuni p forms a deontic or purposive nominal
bugaeva2016 294 . The embedded clause is nominalized by kuni
and then a second formal noun p further nominalizes the resulting
complex as 'a thing one should / will do':

míre kuni p ére kuni p nepki ne ki wa | clothe PURP NMLZ feed PURP NMLZ be.work COP do CONJ | making things to clothe (the grandchild) and things to feed (her) her work, | Two parallel kuni p nominals ('thing to clothe', 'thing to feed') coordinate as the content of nepki ne ki wa 'making (it) one's work'. Each kuni p packages the action as a purposive-deontic event-nominal. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 7 [aa-irc/005#355]

The corpus count of kuni is 6,113 tokens (rank 36), a figure that
conflates the complementizer, purposive, and adverbial uses; no per-function count is derivable
from word-frequency data alone ‹corpus-suggested›.

Other indirect-speech complementizers

Beyond kuni , four elements function as complementizers in indirect
speech and thought contexts, each encoding a different epistemic or temporal profile
nakagawa2024 475–480 ; sato2008 176–177 :

complementizer
content-type
typical matrix predicate

kuni
future, wished, directed
ye , ramu

kunak
future, inferred (in speech)
ye , ramu

yak
past, ongoing, general truth
ye , ramu , takar

kusu
causative-directive 'that X do'
ye

ya
polar or content question embedded
eramiskari , eraman

A shared diagnostic: all of these elements nominalize the preceding VP and make it the object of
the matrix transitive. The form yakne is not an indirect-speech marker —
it is a conditional conjunction, distinct from the complementizer yak
nakagawa2024 477 . Final particles of the type used in main clauses cannot
appear inside these embedded complements, and person marking shifts from the original
speaker's viewpoint to the narrator's
nakagawa2024 475 . The kunak and yak complementizers, and the quotative
sekor , are treated in detail in
quotative-complementation-sekor .

ku=matnepo tanto ek kunak ye kor an | 1SG.A=daughter today come COMP say while exist | My daughter is saying she will come today. | kunak nominalizes the prospective clause ek 'come' as the content of ye 'say'. The periphrastic ye kor an ('is saying') marks ongoing speech. Person marking in the embedded clause (3rd person zero-marked ek) reflects the narrator's viewpoint, not the daughter's. | sato2008:177 | CHI

The embedded question complementizer ya introduces content questions and
polar questions as the object of cognition predicates such as
eramiskari 'not know'. Nakagawa distinguishes the polar-question use
('whether') from the content-question use ('what/where/when'), both employing
ya
nakagawa2024 510 .

Bare complementation

A small residue of complement constructions in the corpus involves no overt nominalizer: the
embedded clause appears without a following formal noun, directly as the argument of the matrix
predicate. Bugaeva identifies this as Stage I of her diachronic typology of Ainu nominalization
— the oldest stratum, in which a bare finite clause functions nominally without a nominalizing
word
bugaeva2016 196–200, 229 . Refsing similarly notes cases in Shizunai Ainu that
she tentatively treats as "ellipsis of the nominalizer"
refsing1986 . The construction is residual in the Hokkaido attested record
and not systematically documented for modern Saru or Chitose ‹corpus-suggested›. Its
relationship to compounding and to the lexicalized nominalization cline (where
p ~ pe has contracted into lexical items such as
aep food ) is treated in nominalization-p-pe and
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

Participant identity across the clause boundary

Because complement clauses in Hokkaido Ainu are finite and carry their own person morphology,
the embedded subject is always expressible: the verb inside the complement bears its own person
affixes independently of the matrix clause. No PRO or empty-category mechanism is posited by the
read sources, and no formal control or raising analysis of HA complements appears in the
literature surveyed here.

Nevertheless, patterns of participant identity are observable. With cognition predicates
( ramu , eramiskari ), the person marking of the
embedded clause in the corpus tends to match the matrix subject: the one who thinks or fails to
understand is also the agent of the thought or the one about whom the proposition holds. The
examples above show this — ku=ramu 'I think' with an embedded clause
about a state of affairs the speaker cares about, a=eramiskari 'one
does not understand' with an embedded clause about what that same person ought to say or do.
The regularity, however, follows from the semantics of cognition verbs, not from a grammatical
control mechanism, and divergent persons are possible where the content demands it ‹corpus-suggested›.

With the directive use of ye + kuni , the pattern
runs in the opposite direction: the matrix subject (the one who speaks) addresses the matrix
object (the addressee), and the embedded clause's agent is typically that addressee, not the
speaker. The corpus example from Kawakami Matsuko above — 'when I told them to enter' — shows
the fourth-person speaker ( a=ye ) directing a different group (the
addressees) toward an action ( ahun 'enter') whose agent goes unmarked in
the embedded clause because it is recoverable from discourse
ilcaa1976 ‹corpus-suggested›. The indirect-speech analysis that best captures this
is given in Nakagawa's treatment of indirect speech perspective shifts
nakagawa2024 475–476 .

The term raising (movement of an embedded subject to a matrix position) has no
attestation in the analytical literature on Hokkaido Ainu clausal complementation, and the
available data supply no diagnostic that would motivate such an analysis. The embedded clause
remains a nominalized finite clause whose internal arguments are expressed by the embedded verb's
own person affixes ‹speculative›.

The constituency of the nominalized clause

Whether the structure [clause] NMLZ ne is biclausal or a grammaticalizing
monoclausal complex predicate is a live debate that cuts across complement clauses, evidential
constructions, and the formal noun system generally. The two positions are:

The biclausal external-RC analysis treats the nominalized clause as a full NP whose
head is a possessed formal noun. The copula ne then closes the outer
predication. Tamura, Okuda, and Satō take this position
tamura2000 227 ; sato2008 175 .

The grammaticalization-to-monoclausal analysis holds that the erstwhile noun is
considerably bleached and the construction is moving toward a complex predicate. The diagnostic
is that a fully lexical noun can take demonstratives and attributive modifiers
( pirka menoko 'a beautiful woman'), whereas the evidential formal noun
cannot: *pirka ruwe ne kewtum is ungrammatical
bugaeva2016 306–315 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 . No read source
provides a constituency proof — a bracket test or movement diagnostic — that decides between
these ‹contested›. The same unresolved question is noted in the dedicated treatments of
ruwe ne in ruwe-ne-inferential and of
p ~ pe in nominalization-p-pe .

For the complement constructions treated in this chapter, the practical consequence is that the
formal nouns function synchronically as clause-final markers that package a finite clause as a
nominal argument. The diachronic source in lexical nouns remains transparent (Bugaeva's
nominalization-cline stages, bugaeva2016 229–304 ), and partial
grammaticalization explains why the same forms build RC heads, complement clauses, evidential
assertions, and — at the furthest end of the cline — conjunctions and adverbial-clause markers
nakagawa2024 515–516 ; takahashi2018 107–112 .

Ijäs's pedagogical grammar introduces relative clauses and notes that formal nouns such as
uske 'place/time' are best understood as nouns that have historically
originated from relative-clause constructions, without committing to a formal analysis of the
biclausal-vs-monoclausal debate
ijas2023 . This aligns with the historical-cline view and is graded as
consistent with the consensus on diachrony ‹consensus›.

title: Object Indexing: Monotransitive and Ditransitive Clauses

part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Object Indexing: Monotransitive and Ditransitive Clauses
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: How transitive and three-place verbs index their object(s) via the objective affix set, the subject/object indexing asymmetries, and the secundative-vs-indirective alignment of ditransitive indexing.

The O set in the person paradigm

A monotransitive verb indexes its object with the O column of the personal-index paradigm. In
the standard Saru–Chitose baseline, that column contains en= for 1SG.O,
un= for 1PL.EXCL.O, e= for 2SG.O,
eci= for 2PL.O, a zero exponent for third person, and
i= for the fourth/indefinite person nakagawa2024 169 ;
bugaeva2012 471 ; sato2008 31–32 . The three-set S/A/O paradigm
and its cell-by-cell alignment are set out in person-marking-architecture ; this
chapter examines each O form in its monotransitive function, its morphological and positional
properties, and the allomorphic contrasts that arise in specific combinations.

formal person
free pronoun (Saru)
O: object index
A and S forms (contrast)
role pattern

1SG
kani
en=
A = S = ku=
nominative–accusative (S=A≠O)

1PL.EXCL
coka
un=
A = ci= ; S = =as
tripartite (S≠A≠O)

2SG
eani
e=
A = S = e=
neutral (S=A=O)

2PL
ecioka
eci=
A = S = eci=
neutral (S=A=O); also 1A→2O portmanteau

3
sinuma / oka
Ø
A = S = Ø

4 / INDEF

A = a= (dialectally an= ); S =
=an
tripartite (S≠A≠O)

A finite verb carries at most one subject index and one object index; a third argument — such
as the theme in a ditransitive clause — is never additionally person-indexed and surfaces as a
bare noun phrase sato2023b 39, 47 ; sato2025a 369–371 . The
object index occupies the prefixal argument-marking layer immediately before the lexical stem,
alongside the transitive subject index; derivational material — applicatives, the causative, the
antipassive, the reflexive, and the root-attaching plural -pa — sits
closer to the stem. The full template is treated in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Ijäs's Lesson 5 introduces the O set through an "object markers" frame: the English object
pronoun corresponds to a verbal prefix on the Ainu finite verb, so that "me" and "you" map onto
en= and e= respectively ijas2023 . Her
pedagogical notation uses hyphens throughout; the convention in this grammar is that
= marks the personal-index boundary and - is
reserved for derivational and stem-number morphology.

First-person singular object: en=

The prefix en= indexes a first-person singular object. Its S/A
counterpart ku= covers both intransitive subject and transitive subject
functions; the opposition ku= (S=A) versus en=
(O) is the one nominative–accusative cell in the Hokkaido Ainu paradigm bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 ; nakagawa2024 166–169 ; sato2008 31–32 . The full 1SG row and the morphological properties of ku= are
developed in first-second-person-singular-affixes .

en=epunkine wa en=kore yan | 1SG.O=protect CONJ 1SG.O=give IMP | Please protect me. | An imperative clause with an implicit second-person subject; both instances of en= index the narrator as the object of 'protect' and 'give' respectively. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, menokoyukar 'Kamuy yukar 4' [aa-irc/011#5]

nep kameaske ene ne yakkay en=kasuy kun pe an cik pirka hike | what monster thus COP even 1SG.O=help should NMLZ exist if good although | It would be good if any monster were to help me. | en= indexes the speaker as the object of kasuy 'help'; the transitive subject is not specified by an overt prefix. | nakagawa2024:169 | TOK | Nakagawa ex. 122, after Takahashi 2007: 63

The combination 2A→1SG.O takes a dialect-specific shape. Most Hokkaido dialects form it with a
second-person A prefix followed by en= : the expected sequences are
e=en= (2SG.A→1SG.O) and eci=en=
(2PL.A→1SG.O). Saru and Chitose drop the second-person A prefix and use bare
en= for 2SG→1SG; Chitose extends the bare form to 2PL→1SG as well,
so that the same surface prefix covers both 3→1SG and, in those dialects, 2→1SG — subject
identification falls to discourse context or overt noun phrases nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 ‹consensus›.

An oral-literary parallel uses i= where the colloquial register uses
en= : in the elegant register the 2SG subject is again unexpressed, and
i= appears as the first-person object prefix in the 2SG→1SG
configuration sato2008 261 . The literary referential range of
i= is part of the fourth-person narrative system, developed in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

First-person plural exclusive object: un=

The prefix un= indexes a first-person plural exclusive object. It
belongs to the tripartite 1PL.EXCL row alongside ci= (A) and
=as (S), all three formally distinct nakagawa2024 166–169 ; bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 . The row is exclusive
throughout: un= marks a speaker-group object that excludes the
addressee, while addressee-inclusive 'us' is indexed in the object position by the fourth-person
prefix i= . Tokachi confirms the same exclusive–inclusive split, with
un= systematically opposed to the fourth-person object takahashi2015 206–209 ‹consensus›.

sanbyakugojuen a=un=kore hike ka | 350.yen 4.A=1PL.EXCL.O=give although even | Although they gave us 350 yen, … | The prefix a= marks an indefinite fourth-person transitive subject; un= indexes the exclusive first-plural group as the recipient-object of kore 'give'. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, isoytak 'Monologue 2' [aa-irc/004#109]

The full exclusive paradigm and the clusivity opposition are developed in
first-person-plural-and-clusivity .

Second-person objects: e= and eci=

Second-person indexes are role-neutral: e= marks 2SG as A, S, or O,
and eci= does the same for 2PL, with no dedicated object form for
either tamura1972 27 ; sato2008 32 ; bugaeva2012 472 . The role of e= in a transitive clause follows from discourse: a
form such as e=kore may be read as 2SG.A acting on a zero-indexed
third-person O, or as a zero-indexed third-person A acting on a 2SG.O, with overt noun phrases
or context resolving the ambiguity. Ijäs notes this as one of the practically challenging
features of the O set for learners of Saru ijas2023 .

kanpisos e=kore | book 2SG.O=give | She gave you the book. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 5. With e= read as 2SG.O, the transitive subject is a zero-indexed third person; the same e=kore is equally grammatical with e= as 2SG.A ('you gave the book (to her)'). | ijas2023 | SAR

The form eci= additionally serves as the portmanteau exponent for a
first-person A acting on a second-person O: the four combinations 1SG.A→2SG.O, 1SG.A→2PL.O,
1PL.EXCL.A→2SG.O, and 1PL.EXCL.A→2PL.O all surface as bare eci= ,
without a separate first-person subject prefix tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 . The portmanteau
and its consequences for the combination paradigm are developed in
eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

Fourth-person object: i=

The prefix i= is the O member of the fourth-person tripartite row,
alongside a= (or dialectally an= ) as A and
=an as S. Nakagawa lists five referential uses for the form-set —
inclusive first-plural, second-person honorific, impersonal or agent-defocusing, oral-narrative
protagonist, and logophoric reference in reported speech; Bugaeva covers the same range under
"indefinite", reanalysing the quotation use as logophoric; Tamura distributes the functions
across analytically separate persons nakagawa2024 169, 174–179 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; tamura1972 18–20, 28, 34, 39 ‹contested›. The full referential paradigm of
the form-set is treated in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm ; the concern here is
i= as the object prefix common to all of those contexts.

sunku tono mat i=kaopiwki wa i=kore | Sunku lord wife 4.O=help CONJ 4.O=give | Help me and take care of me, wife of the lord of Sunku. | Both i= prefixes index the oral-narrative protagonist as the object; kaopiwki 'help' and kore 'give' are linked by the conjunctive wa. This clause is an address within the narrative. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 8 (part 1)' [aa-irc/006#243]

a=rehe Ikuresuye sekor a=i=réko | 4.POSS=name Ikuresuye QUOT 4.A=4.O=call | I was named Ikuresuye. | The possessive a= on the noun rehe 'name' and the transitive-subject a= on the verb réko 'call/name' are independent instances of the fourth-person prefix; i= indexes the oral-narrative protagonist as the object of the naming action. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 'Folktale 7' [aa-irc/005#1]

The prefix i= is homophonous with the detransitivising prefix
i- , which derives intransitive predicates from transitives — as in
i-ruska be angry at something unspecified beside transitive
ruska be angry at someone . The two items belong to different grammatical layers:
the object marker i= is a personal index in the paradigm, while the
detransitivising i- belongs to valency-changing morphology and patterns
with the reflexive yay- and the reciprocal u- tamura1972 35 ; nam2021 62–74 . Their historical relationship is debated: the older
analysis derives the antipassive from the fourth-person object marker; Bugaeva reverses the
direction; Nam proposes a common source in the generic noun
*hi/*ihi 'thing, place, time' ‹contested›. The detransitivising prefix
is treated in antipassive-detransitive-i .

Zero third-person object

Third-person objects carry no overt index. With a first- or second-person subject, the visible
personal prefix is the A form alone — ku= , ci= ,
e= , eci= , or a= ; with
third-person subject and object alike, the finite verb carries no overt person prefix nakagawa2024 169 ; bugaeva2012 471 .

moyuk ku=nukar | raccoon.dog 1SG.A=see | I saw a raccoon dog. | Constructed following Ijäs Lesson 5. The third-person object has no overt index; only the 1SG.A prefix ku= appears on the verb. | ijas2023 | SAR

Whether the absence of a third-person index reflects a zero affix or the simple absence of any
third-person exponent is contested. Nakagawa treats third person as lacking an affix; Satō
argues that a zero third-person affix must be posited to sustain the two-index cap — the
ungrammaticality of sequences such as *Ø=en=e=kore 'one makes me give
you' follows if both subject and object slots are already occupied by zero, blocking a third
index nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2023b 39 ; sato2025a 369 ‹contested›. The alignment implications of the zero versus absent-exponent analysis are
pursued in alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

title: Architecture of the Personal-Affix System: The Four Persons and the S/A/O Paradigms
summary: The master chapter laying out the whole personal-affix system — the four 'persons', the subjective (A/S) vs objective (O) sub-paradigms, prefix vs suffix exponents, and the terminological wars.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Architecture of the Personal-Affix System: The Four Persons and the S/A/O Paradigms
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The master chapter laying out the whole personal-affix system — the four 'persons', the subjective (A/S) vs objective (O) sub-paradigms, prefix vs suffix exponents, and the terminological wars.

The four formal persons

Hokkaido Ainu marks person on the finite verb. An intransitive predicate indexes its single
argument; a transitive predicate indexes the combination of its subject and its object.
Independent pronouns — kani ‘I’, eani ‘you’,
coka ‘we (excluding you)’, asinuma ‘the
fourth-person self’ — are available for emphasis or contrast and are used more sparingly than
their Japanese counterparts, while the verbal index carries the ordinary work of tracking
arguments nakagawa2024 163–165, 169 ; tamura1972 17 . Satō
describes the pattern as head-marking in the sense of nichols1986 : the verb,
the head of its clause, bears the central information about which participants fill its valence
frame, and a transitive verb may host both a subject index and an object index sato2025a 361 .

orwano ku=cise or ta ku=an kor | then 1SG.POSS=house place LOC 1SG.S=exist while | Then, while I was in my house, … | No pronoun appears; the intransitive existential ku=an indexes its 1SG.S subject. The possessor ku=cise carries the homophonous first-person prefix in the nominal domain. | nakagawa2024:164 | ISH | Nakagawa ex. 104, after Sunazawa Kura 1983: 75

Nakagawa recognises four formal persons, numbered 1 to 4, each with a singular and a plural, and
treats the numerals as form-classes detached from any fixed speaker-or-addressee value; the
mapping between form and reference is many-to-one nakagawa2024 163 . The fourth
person is the limiting case: it is a single form-set into which everything that persons 1 to 3
cannot express is gathered, so that the referential range of those three is narrowed in turn
nakagawa2024 164, 174 .

Terminology for this set is unsettled. Nakagawa calls it the fourth person; Tamura divides the
same Saru forms into separate persons — the inclusive, the quotative, the second-person
honorific, and the indefinite — counting eleven persons in all; Bugaeva uses “indefinite” and
reanalyses the quotation use as logophoric; Satō treats the forms as polysemous; and Takahashi,
working on Tokachi, adopts “inclusive person” as the label that unifies the otherwise disparate
uses nakagawa2024 174–179 ; tamura1972 18–20, 39 ; bugaeva2012 474–476 ; bugaeva2008 40–43 ; sato2008 203–208 ; takahashi2015 205–210 ‹contested›. The form-label “fourth” or “indefinite” is used here; its referential functions
are set out in indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm the indefinite/fourth-person chapter
and fourth-person-honorific the honorific and logophoric chapter .

The S/A/O paradigm

The person indexes divide into three role sets. S is the sole argument of an
intransitive verb, A the subject of a transitive verb, and O its object.
Nakagawa presents these as separate intransitive-subject, transitive-subject, and object tables,
and Bugaeva’s southern Hokkaido table gives the same Saru–Chitose paradigm in S/A/O terms nakagawa2024 165–169 ; bugaeva2012 471 . The three-way distinction is load-bearing: two persons keep
distinct forms for all three roles, one opposes an S=A pair to O, and two collapse the roles
entirely. The rows below give the architectural cell values for the standard Saru–Chitose
baseline; allomorphy, dialect microvariation, and the person-combination rules belong to the
chapters that follow.

formal person
free pronoun (Saru)
A: transitive subject
S: intransitive subject
O: object
alignment of the cell

1SG

ku=
en=
S=A≠O; nominative–accusative

1PL.EXCL

S≠A≠O; tripartite

2SG

e=

2PL

4 / INDEF

The first singular is the one nominative–accusative cell: ku= serves both
S and A, opposed to the object en= bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 . Its forms, together with the second singular, are treated in first-second-person-singular-affixes . The second-person cells are neutral, e= and
eci= spanning A, S, and O alike. The third person is unmarked. Whether the
blank reflects a zero affix or the absence of any third-person exponent is disputed: Satō argues
that a zero third-person affix is required by the cap of two indexes per verb and by the
ungrammaticality of three-index combinations, against an analysis that recognises no third-person
affix at all nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2023b 39, 47 ; sato2025a 369 ‹contested›.

aynu itak k=eramuan wa kusu icen k=eunkeray wa pirka pirka | Ainu language 1SG.A=understand CONJ because money 1SG.A=receive CONJ good good | Because I had learnt Ainu, I was able to receive money, which was very good. | The reduced 1SG.A prefix k= is the form ku= takes before a vowel. | nakagawa2024:167 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 111

Side-encoding: prefixes for A, suffixes for S

The architectural asymmetry of the paradigm is the side of the verb on which the subject index
appears. First singular and second person are prefixal, or zero, in both subject roles. First
plural exclusive and the fourth person switch sides by transitivity: ci=
and a= stand before a transitive stem as A, while
=as and =an follow an intransitive stem as S. This
side-encoding is the morphological basis on which Bugaeva calls these two rows tripartite nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

subject set
S on an intransitive verb
A on a transitive verb
object counterpart

1PL.EXCL
stem =as
ci= stem
un= stem

4 / INDEF
stem =an
a= stem
i= stem

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | One clause chain indexes the same exclusive group as A by the prefix ci= on the transitive o and as S by the suffix =as on the intransitive plural arki. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

wen kamuy a=maketa wa a=rayke ru-we ne | bad god 4.A=defeat CONJ 4.A=kill track-POSS COP | The evil kamuy was defeated and killed. | The prefix a= indexes an indefinite transitive subject and defocuses the agent; the patient stays the object of the transitive verbs. | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 145

The object counterparts of these two sets, un= and
i= , are prefixal, so each set keeps its three roles formally distinct in
both shape and position. The fourth-person object i= indexes, among other
referents, the narrator’s own in oral narrative nakagawa2024 169 :

i=erampokiwen i=rewsire wa i=kore | 4.O=pity 4.O=lodge CONJ 4.O=give | Take pity on me and give me lodging. | The fourth-person object i= recurs three times; with a= for A and =an for S, one person distinguishes all three roles, the tripartite pattern of the table above. | nakagawa2024:169 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 127

The choice of a= over an= for the fourth-person A
is itself dialectal: Saru and the western dialects use a= , while Tokachi,
Ishikari, Shiranuka, and Samani favour an= under conditions that vary by
locality, and Nakagawa takes an= to be the older form nakagawa2024 167–168 .

The boundary symbols used throughout the grammar follow a working convention: = writes the personal-index boundary and - the boundary of derivational and
number morphology. The convention is deliberately neutral, because the sources disagree about the
morphological status of the individual indexes. Bugaeva, on prosodic and separability grounds,
distinguishes word-like =an and =as , clitic-like
a= and eci= , and fuller prefixes such as
ku= , en= , and i= that shift
the accent kernel; Nakagawa writes the whole set with = for convenience
yet flags the unusual autonomy of =an , which can be separated from its verb;
Sakaguchi’s explicit clitic/affix criteria, developed for Sakhalin material, likewise rank
fourth-person =an as the most autonomous subject marker bugaeva2012 472–473 ; nakagawa2024 49, 183 ; sakaguchi2024 ix, 77–85, 163
‹contested›. The ordering and boundary diagnostics are pursued in personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

Syncretism and portmanteau cells

Second person collapses the role distinction: e= marks 2SG as A, S, and
O, and eci= does the same for 2PL. The same eci=
additionally serves as the portmanteau exponent for a first-person A acting on a second-person O,
covering 1SG→2SG, 1SG→2PL, 1PL.EXCL→2SG, and 1PL.EXCL→2PL with a single prefix in place of a
sequence of first- and second-person indexes tamura1972 27–28 ; bugaeva2008 38 ; bugaeva2012 474 ; nakagawa2024 170, 173 . The surrounding
clause resolves the resulting ambiguity, and the cell is developed in eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural .

nep ka eci=kasuy pe an ya
2SG.O=help NMLZ exist Q"
tr="Is there anything I can help you with?"
cite="nakagawa2024:173"
dial="SAR"
place="Nakagawa ex. 128, after Honda 2001: 44"
note="The single exponent eci= encodes the 1SG.A→2SG.O combination; no separate ku= or e= appears."
/>

The mirror configuration is less regular. Most dialectal paradigms express 2→1 with a
second-person A index followed by the first-person object form, but Saru and Chitose drop the
expected second-person subject and use bare en= for 2SG→1SG, Chitose
extending the bare form to 2PL→1SG as well nakagawa2024 170, 173 ; sato2008 146–147 .

A verb admits at most one subject index and one object index, and no third argument is ever
indexed. The cap shapes ditransitive marking. With verbs such as kore
‘give’ the object index falls on the human recipient while the theme stays a bare noun phrase,
a secundative pattern that Satō takes as further evidence for a zero third-person affix; Nakagawa
describes the same three-place verbs through valence, with two bare object phrases and neither
obligatorily indexed when both are third person sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 39, 47 ; sato2025a 368–371 ; nakagawa2024 363–365 ‹contested›. Object
indexing and the three-place verbs are treated in object-indexing-monotransitive .

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | Both non-subject participants are third person and surface as bare noun phrases; only the 1SG.A subject is indexed. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

Number and the fourth person

The person paradigm intersects with number at two layers. The first plural row is exclusive:
ci= , =as , and un= exclude
the addressee, while addressee-inclusive ‘we’ belongs to the fourth person. Tokachi confirms the
same division, an exclusive first-plural series beside an inclusive fourth-person series whose
forms take a plural verb even when the referent is single nakagawa2024 163–164, 174 ;
tamura1972 18–19 ; bugaeva2012 472, 475 ; takahashi2015 206–209 . The exclusive paradigm is developed in first-person-plural-and-clusivity .

Verb number is its own system. Hokkaido uses suppletive singular/plural stems —
ek / arki ‘come’,
arpa / paye ‘go’ — alongside a root-attaching
plural suffix -pa and, in Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari, an outer plural
pa that sits outside the person index. The minimal pair
iki=an pa and iki-pa=an places the outer
pa after the fourth-person suffix and the stem-forming suffix
-pa before it nakagawa2024 141–159 ; bugaeva2012 471, 481 ; sakaguchi2024 12 . The Sakhalin verbal plural =hci and
its relatives attach outside the person-inflected verb; their classification as number rather than
third-person marking is a point on which Nakagawa and Sakaguchi converge against earlier
descriptions nakagawa2024 151–155, 178 ; sakaguchi2024 77–83, 163 ;
baek2021 73 . Person–number agreement is taken up in person-number-interactions .

Several fourth-person uses select plural morphology for a single referent. Honorific address to
one esteemed person and the inclusive ‘you and I’ take the fourth-person form with a plural stem,
as does much narrative first-person use outside Saru and Chitose nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–179 ; tamura1972 28, 34 .

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | The honorific addressee is a single person, yet the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural stem arki. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140, woman to an adult man

The whole semantic range of the fourth person — inclusive first plural, honorific second person,
impersonal agent-defocusing, narrative first person, and logophoric reference inside a quotation
— rests on this one form-set. The old account of an “elevated” literary person distinct from an
everyday one is contested: Nakagawa assigns the narrative use to viewpoint and text type, Satō
derives it from a first-person-plural strategy, and Bugaeva analyses the quotation use as
logophoric and finds it outside folklore as well nakagawa2024 174–179, 600–604 ;
sato2004 179–184 ; sato2008 259–262 ; bugaeva2008 40–44 ‹contested›. The fourth-person object i= must be kept apart from the
homophonous detransitivising prefix i- ‘something’, whose origin is itself
debated nam2021 62–74 ; the detransitiviser belongs to the valency system and is
treated under antipassive-detransitive-i the antipassive .

Mixed alignment and the verb template

Read person by person, the system is mixed in morphological alignment: 1SG is
nominative–accusative with S=A≠O, the second and third persons are neutral with S=A=O, and the
first plural exclusive and the fourth person are tripartite with S≠A≠O. Bugaeva’s analysis of
Hokkaido Ainu rests directly on the S/A/O paradigm and on the side-encoding of
ci= / =as and
a= / =an , and she characterises the language as mixed
but basically tripartite bugaeva2012 472, 498 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

The person indexes do not split the intransitive subject by agentivity: agentive and patientive
intransitives take the same S set once valence is fixed, so the agreement paradigm is not
split-intransitive in the morphological sense nakagawa2024 126–127 . The split
that the literature pursues under that name surfaces in verb classes, noun incorporation, and
valency-changing morphology, and is treated in alignment-split-intransitive . The
competing system-wide analyses — tripartite, hierarchical, inverse — are sharpened by Sakhalin
work: Dal Corso rejects a tripartite component for Sakhalin in favour of a mixed
nominative–neutral–inverse account, while warning that the diachrony may not carry over to
Hokkaido dalcorso2025align 187–188, 200, 213 ‹contested›. The Hokkaido paradigm
above supplies the empirical base for alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

The personal indexes occupy the outer argument-marking layer of the verb. Subject and object
indexes precede the stem in transitives, while the intransitive S forms
=as and =an follow it; derivational material —
applicatives, causatives, the reflexive, the reciprocal, the antipassive
i- , and the stem-forming plural -pa — sits closer
to the root, and a reflexive or reciprocal fills the object slot rather than co-occurring with an
object index for the same participant tamura1972 35 ; nakagawa2024 183, 196–199 ; sato2023b 40 . The full ordering of these positions is the subject of personal-affix-template-and-ordering .

title: Person–Number Interactions and Plural Agreement
summary: How the personal affixes interact with number marking — the -pa plural, the =as suffix, and agreement resolution across A, S, and O.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Person–Number Interactions and Plural Agreement
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: How the personal affixes interact with number marking — the -pa plural, the =as suffix, and agreement resolution across A, S, and O.

Two layers of number

Person and number meet in Hokkaido Ainu at two formally distinct layers. Within the
personal-affix paradigm, number is built into specific person rows: the first plural exclusive
series ( ci= / =as / un= ),
second plural eci= , and the fourth-person series, whose number behavior
is reference-dependent rather than form-fixed. Verbal number forms a second layer, realized
through suppletive singular/plural stem alternations and through two homophonous formatives: a
root-attaching plural suffix -pa and an outer plural auxiliary or clitic
pa that follows the person-inflected verb nakagawa2024 140–162 ; bugaeva2012 471 ; nakagawa2022 . The person inventory is set out
in person-marking-architecture ; the stem alternations in
suppletive-verbal-number ; the exclusive row and clusivity in
first-person-plural-and-clusivity ; and the full range of fourth-person functions in
indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

exponent
layer
position
controller
Hokkaido distribution

=as
personal affix
intransitive stem-final (suffix)
1PL.EXCL subject
pan-Hokkaido

eci=
personal affix / portmanteau
pre-stem (prefix)
2PL A, S, O; also 1A→2O
pan-Hokkaido

suppletive plural stem
verbal number
root alternation
S (intransitives); O or event (transitives)
lexically governed

-pa
verbal number
inside person marking (root-attaching suffix)
S (intransitives) or O/event (transitives)
pan-Hokkaido V/ -pa stem class

outer pa
outer number
outside the person-inflected form
participant or event plurality
Saru, Chitose, Ishikari; clitic/auxiliary status ‹contested›

Shizunai ci / Horobetsu -ci
outer or derived number
varies by dialect (see below)
participant or event plurality
Shizunai (outer); Horobetsu (fossilized inner suffix)

Corbett's distinction between the indexing of participant number and the marking of event or
action number corbett2000 maps onto these two layers. Intransitive plural
marking in Hokkaido is agreement-like: the number of S determines both the stem choice and the
S-affix form. Transitive plural marking is oriented primarily to the object or to the action,
bringing it closer to event-number marking in Corbett's terms nakagawa2024 142–145 .

What controls the plural verb

With intransitive verbs the plural stem — whether suppletive ( ek / arki 'come', arpa / paye
'go', an / oka(y) 'exist') or suffixal (V/ -pa pairs such as rewe / rew-pa ) — agrees with S. The 1PL.EXCL suffix =as and a plural
fourth-person suffix =an both obligatorily co-occur with a plural
intransitive stem; a singular S affix selects the singular stem nakagawa2024 142–146 ; bugaeva2012 471 .

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | The intransitive arki is the plural suppletive of ek 'come'; it co-occurs with the 1PL.EXCL S suffix =as. The same group is indexed as A on the transitive o 'board' by the prefix ci=. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

With transitive verbs the picture diverges. Plural marking is tied primarily to the plurality of
O or of the action; a first- or fourth-person plural A prefix ( ci= , a= ) does not by itself reliably trigger the plural stem or outer pa . Nakagawa characterises intransitive plural as agreement-like and
transitive plural as action- or object-oriented and often optional nakagawa2024 142–146 ; bugaeva2012 471 . Ijäs's learner grammar states the same split: intransitive
plural follows S, transitive plural ordinarily follows O or the action ijas2023 . The
corpus shows verb-specific and dialect-variable departures from any simple threshold, and
Nakagawa's discussion of numerals and quantifiers treats the co-occurrence with plural morphology
as complex and speaker-variable nakagawa2024 160–162 .

apkas=as pa | walk=1PL.EXCL.S PL | We walk. | Constructed teaching form from Ijäs L9; illustrates outer pa following the 1PL.EXCL S affix on an intransitive verb in Saru-type dialects. | ijas2023

Stem-forming -pa and outer pa

Nakagawa distinguishes the two pa elements by their position relative to
the person-inflected form. Stem-forming -pa builds the plural member of a
V/ -pa pair and attaches to the root, inside any person marker or
causative suffix. Outer pa follows the fully person-inflected verb,
outside any person affix, and marks participant or event plurality nakagawa2024 151–159 . Sakaguchi applies a formal positional criterion — a clitic (接語) attaches to the
person-inflected form rather than to the root — and concludes that the Saru, Chitose, and
Ishikari outer element is a clitic, distinct from the root-attaching suffix sakaguchi2024 12, 77–83 . The hypothesis that outer pa is a later western Hokkaido development
that displaced an earlier pan-Ainu outer (h)ci is argued in nakagawa2022 ‹contested›.

The minimal pair demonstrating the position contrast uses the fourth-person intransitive suffix
=an . When outer pa is used, it follows =an ; when stem-forming -pa is used, it precedes =an .

nenpoka iki=an pa wa siknu=an wa | somehow do=4.S PL CONJ live=4.S CONJ | Somehow we managed and survived, and… | Outer pa follows the fourth-person S suffix =an. | nakagawa2024:158 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 87

irwak sinne sorekusu iki-pa=an | sibling like exactly do-PL=4.S | We were like siblings. | Stem-forming -pa precedes the fourth-person S suffix =an, confirming root-level attachment. | nakagawa2024:158 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 88

rus patek a=mi wa hotke=an pa | fur only 4.A=wear CONJ sleep=4.S PL | We wore only furs and slept. | Fourth-person transitive a=mi (wear) followed by intransitive hotke=an (sleep); outer pa follows the person-inflected form of the second predicate. | ilcaa1976:aa-irc/005#157 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, ILCAA Ainu materials, uwepeker 7

Outer pa can repeat across conjoined predicates in a clause chain because
its scope is over the person-inflected predicate, not over the root. The inner suffix -pa belongs to the stem and contributes its number reading at the lexical
level. The two may co-occur on the same verb — a V/ -pa stem combined with
outer pa — and both combine with causative suffixes while maintaining
their respective attachment sites nakagawa2024 156–159 .

Fourth-person plurals and number mismatches

The fourth-person series triggers plural verbal morphology in four of its five uses, regardless of
the actual referent count. Nakagawa enumerates five functions at 2024: 174–179; the number
behavior follows from the function nakagawa2024 160–161, 174–179 ; tamura1972 28, 34 ; bugaeva2012 475–476 .

Inclusive first plural (use ①, 'you and I') always takes the plural verb stem, since the
referent is inherently multiple nakagawa2024 174 ; takahashi2015 209 . In Tokachi, the inclusive series consistently co-occurs with plural verb forms even when
referring to a pair, and this co-selection is obligatory takahashi2015 209 .

eani hem paye=an ro | 2SG also go.PL=4.S HORT | Let's go together, you and I. | The plural suppletive paye co-occurs with =an; the reference is inherently multiple (speaker + addressee). | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

Honorific second-person address (use ②) requires the plural verb stem for a single addressee, as
a form of social amplification nakagawa2024 175–176 ; tamura1972 28 . The mismatch between grammatically plural morphology and a singular referent is systematic
and socially conditioned.

hunak wa arki=an ru-we an | where from come.PL=4.S track-POSS exist | Where have you come from? | One person is addressed; the fourth-person S suffix =an appears on the plural suppletive stem arki. | nakagawa2024:175 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 140, woman addressing an adult man

Indefinite/impersonal use (use ③) — the equivalent of French on or German man — also takes the plural verb stem, with an unspecified agent defocused from
the event nakagawa2024 176–177 ; tamura1972 34 ; sato2008 203–205 .

Narrative first-person use (use ④) is where dialectal variation is sharpest. In Saru and
Chitose, a single protagonist may appear with the singular verb stem. Horobetsu also permits
singular stems for a singular narrator, though the only available free pronoun is the formally
plural aokay . Shizunai shows the opposite configuration: the verb is
consistently plural regardless of narrator number, while the pronouns distinguish anani (singular) from anoka (plural) nakagawa2024 174–179, 254–256 . The crossed mismatches — singular verb with plural pronoun in Horobetsu, plural verb with
singular pronoun in Shizunai — confirm that the fourth-person form-set is not simply a plural
of the first person, and they supply evidence for the historically composite character of the
series ‹consensus›.

Shizunai ci and Sakhalin =hci: position as the diagnostic

Shizunai uses an outer ci where Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari use outer pa . The position is the same: Shizunai ci follows
the person-inflected form, outside =an , in exactly the outer-plural slot
nakagawa2024 151–155 .

hoski no hotke=an ci akus | first ADV sleep=4.S PL then | When we slept first, … | Shizunai ci follows the fourth-person S suffix =an in the outer-plural slot, parallel to Saru outer pa. | nakagawa2024:154 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 74, after Okuda 1999

Horobetsu presents a formally different element. Its -ci suffix appears
fossilized in items such as iki-ci and kar-ici ,
and the diagnostic ordering places it inside the person clitic ( iki-ci=as ),
with the derivational layer rather than the outer plural slot. The Horobetsu form therefore
behaves like a root-level suffix, not like the outer elements of Shizunai or Saru nakagawa2024 154–155 .

The Sakhalin form =(a)hci (Aniwa Bay =(a)hsi ) was
described by Murasaki as an optional third-person plural marker murasaki1979 49–52 . Nakagawa disputes the classification: the element is non-obligatory and appears with subjects
of all persons, placing it with the outer-auxiliary set rather than with person-agreement affixes
nakagawa2024 151–155, 178 ‹contested›. Sakaguchi applies formal positional
diagnostics and arrives at the same result: =hci attaches to the
person-inflected verb, can follow an auxiliary, and precedes a nominalizer — the signature of a
clitic over the person-inflected form, not a root-level agreement suffix sakaguchi2024 77–83, 163 . Baek adds that Sakhalin -(a)hci extends to the nominal domain, a
trait shared with Uilta and Nivkh in the Sakhalin area and absent from Hokkaido baek2021 73 .

eciokaya-hcin naa eci=paye-hci-hi nee nanko | 2PL-PL still 2PL.S=go.PL-PL-NMLZ COP probably | You people must have gone too. | The Sakhalin plural element -hci/-hcin appears with a second-person plural subject, not a third-person one, undermining its analysis as third-person plural agreement. Hokkaido dialects lack productive =hci; the corresponding function is served by outer pa (Saru/Chitose/Ishikari) or outer ci (Shizunai). | murasaki1979:52; nakagawa2024:155 | SA | Murasaki ex. 70 (Sakhalin contrast)

The result for Hokkaido is that no dialect has a productive third-person plural agreement suffix.
Plural participant reference is encoded at the verbal-number layer — suppletive stems, inner -pa , outer pa or ci — and
through the number mismatches of the fourth-person row, not through an additional person-affix
position nakagawa2024 151–155 .

Multiple plural marking

The plural exponents occupy different layers of the verb and can therefore accumulate within a
single predicate. A V/ -pa stem encodes plural at the root level; outer pa can follow the person-inflected form of that same stem to mark
additional participant or event plurality at the outer layer. Similarly, a V/ -pa stem combines with causative morphology before the person affix while outer pa appears outside. In clause chains, outer pa may repeat on successive
predicates because its scope is over the person-inflected predicate rather than the clause
sequence as a unit nakagawa2024 156–159 .

Plural accumulation does not affect the cap on person affixes. A finite verb carries at most one
subject index and one object index, and no third argument is indexed by person. In ditransitives
such as kore 'give', plural morphology can co-occur with the ordinary
object-indexing pattern in which the object affix tracks the human recipient while the theme
remains an unindexed bare noun phrase sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 39, 47 . The two-affix cap and the ditransitive pattern are treated in
object-indexing-monotransitive and personal-affix-template-and-ordering .
