title: The Personal-Affix Template: Position Classes and Affix Ordering
summary: The ordering of subject and object personal affixes relative to each other, to valency prefixes, and to the verb stem — the personal-affix portion of the verb template.
part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Personal-Affix Template: Position Classes and Affix Ordering
Part: Part X — The Personal-Affix System and Alignment
Summary: The ordering of subject and object personal affixes relative to each other, to valency prefixes, and to the verb stem — the personal-affix portion of the verb template.

The position-class template

The cell values of the personal-index paradigm — which forms exist and what morphological
alignment each person row exhibits — are established in person-marking-architecture .
This chapter examines how those indexes are ordered on the verb word: which positions they
occupy relative to the stem, how that ordering relates to valency-changing morphology, and what
the positional difference between subject prefixes and subject suffixes entails for the verb's
internal structure. The chapter covers the personal-affix layer of the verb; the full verb word
template, including TAM, negation, and nominalization layers, is treated in
verb-word-template .

position class
material
linear position

left personal layer

A prefixes: ku= , ci= ,
a= / an= ,
e= , eci= ; O prefixes:
en= , un= ,
i=

before the derived stem

valency-prefix layer

applicatives e- , ko- ,
o- ; antipassive/detransitive
i- ; reflexive yay- ,
si- ; reciprocal u-

inside personal prefixes, before the root

stem
lexical root; also suppletive and incorporated stems
template centre

stem-internal suffix

stem plural -pa ; causatives
-re , -e ,
-ka

after the root, before the right-edge person suffix

right personal layer

=as (1PL.EXCL.S); =an (4.S)

after the intransitive stem or derived intransitive

outer number layer

auxiliary pa (Saru, Chitose, Ishikari); auxiliary
ci (Shizunai)

outside the person-inflected verb

The table is a positional map, not a template in which every slot must be filled. Most verb
forms contain only the stem and one index; a verb with a third-person subject and third-person
object shows no overt personal material. The outer number layer is relevant here because it
supplies an ordering diagnostic for the right edge: material that follows a fully
person-inflected form belongs outside the personal-affix template, even where earlier
descriptions treated comparable segments as person markers nakagawa2024 151–159 ;
bugaeva2012 471, 481 ; sato2023b 39–40 .

The left personal layer: A and O prefixes

When a transitive verb is inflected for a non-third subject, the A index occupies the leftmost
position in the word: 1SG ku= , 1PL.EXCL
ci= , 4th/indefinite a= or
an= , 2SG e= , and 2PL
eci= all stand before the stem. Object indexes are prefixal across all
persons: 1SG en= , 1PL.EXCL un= , 4th
i= , and 2SG/2PL e= / eci=
stand before the stem when overt nakagawa2024 166–170 ; bugaeva2012 471 .

aynu itak k=eramuan wa kusu icen k=eunkeray wa pirka pirka | Ainu language 1SG.A=understand CONJ because money 1SG.A=receive CONJ good good | Because I had learnt Ainu, I was able to receive money, which was very good. | ku= reduces to k= before a vowel-initial stem. Both transitive predicates place the A index in the left personal layer. | nakagawa2024:167 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 111

The left personal layer is outside the valency-prefix layer. Applicatives
ko- , e- , and o- , the
reflexive and reciprocal prefixes yay- , si- ,
and u- , and the detransitivising prefix i- all
attach closer to the root than any personal index. The detransitivising
i- must be kept apart from the homophonous 4th-person object prefix
i= : the two occupy different positions, carry different boundary
symbols, and have distinct etymological histories; the detransitiviser is treated in
antipassive-detransitive-i . Valency morphology derives a transitive or intransitive
base from the root, and the personal indexes appear outside that derived base, indexing its
arguments tamura1972 35 ; nakagawa2024 196–199 ; sato2023b 40 .

The 4th-person A prefix shows a dialectal split within the left personal layer. Saru and the
western dialects use a= exclusively; Tokachi, Ishikari, Shiranuka, and
Samani favour an= under locally varying conditions, and Nakagawa takes
an= to be the older form nakagawa2024 167–168 . Both
shapes occupy the same left-edge position ‹consensus›.

wen kamuy a=maketa wa a=rayke ru-we ne | bad god 4.A=defeat CONJ 4.A=kill track-POSS COP | The evil kamuy was defeated and killed. | The prefix a= indexes an indefinite transitive subject; the patient is not subject-promoted. Both predicates place the 4th-person A prefix in the left personal layer. | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 145

The right personal layer: =as and =an

Two subject indexes appear at the right edge of the inflected verb: 1PL.EXCL
=as and 4th/indefinite =an . Both mark S on
intransitive verbs and follow any stem-internal suffix. Their transitive-subject counterparts,
ci= and a= / an= , stand in
the left personal layer. This side-switch — suffix on an intransitive S, prefix on a transitive
A — is the positional basis on which Bugaeva calls these two rows tripartite: each keeps A, S,
and O formally and positionally distinct nakagawa2024 166–167 ; bugaeva2012 472 ; bugaeva2008 32 .

person row
S: intransitive (right-edge suffix)
A: transitive (left-edge prefix)
O (left-edge prefix)

1PL.EXCL
stem =as
ci= stem
un= stem

4 / INDEF
stem =an

a= stem (or an= stem, dialectal)

i= stem

kuruma an wa ci=o tek arki=as | car exist CONJ 1PL.EXCL.A=board then come.PL=1PL.EXCL.S | There was a car, and we boarded it and came. | The same exclusive group is A on the transitive o, marked by the prefix ci=, and S on the intransitive plural arki, marked by the suffix =as — both positions filled within a single clause chain. | takahashi2015:206 | TOK | Takahashi ex. 2

eani hem paye=an ro | 2SG even go.PL=4.S HORT | You too, let us go! | The 4th-person S suffix =an attaches to the intransitive plural stem paye. In this inclusive/hortative use the addressee is part of the referent; the hortative particle ro follows the fully inflected verb. | nakagawa2024:174 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 138

All other persons keep their subject index on the left. First singular
ku= marks both S and A as a prefix, opposed to the object
en= . Second-person e= and
eci= are neutral — the same form serves A, S, and O — and always
prefixal. Third person is zero in all roles. The portmanteau function of
eci= for a first-person A acting on a second-person O is a separate
rule and is developed in eci-portmanteau-and-second-plural . The right personal
layer is the exclusive domain of the two side-switching rows ‹consensus›.

Ordering with valency morphology and number suffixes

Valency-changing material and the stem-forming plural occupy positions between the root and any
right-edge person suffix. The causatives -re ,
-e , and -ka , and the stem-forming plural
-pa , all follow the root and precede =as or
=an . A causativised intransitive, for example, yields a derived stem to
which the right-edge person suffix then attaches: the personal layer is outside the valency
layer in both directions nakagawa2024 196–199 ; sato2023b 40 .

The clearest ordering diagnostic for the right edge comes from Nakagawa's minimal pair with
iki 'do'. Hokkaido has a stem-forming plural suffix
-pa that attaches to the root, and in Saru, Chitose, and Ishikari an
outer plural auxiliary pa that follows the person-inflected form. The
pair contrasts their positions directly:

nenpoka iki=an pa wa siknu=an wa | somehow do=4.S PL CONJ live=4.S CONJ | Somehow we managed to do it, and went on living. | The outer auxiliary pa follows the fully person-inflected form iki=an. It is outside the right personal layer. | nakagawa2024:158 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 87

irwak sinne sorekusu iki-pa=an | sibling one.like in.that.way do-PL=4.S | We lived like siblings. | The stem-forming plural suffix -pa precedes the person suffix =an, inside the right personal layer. The contrast with the preceding example places stem -pa inside and auxiliary pa outside. | nakagawa2024:158 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 88

Sakaguchi formalises the inside/outside distinction as a clitic diagnostic: a marker is a
clitic when it attaches to the fully person-inflected form, an affix when it attaches to the
root. He applies this to Sakhalin Ainu, where the verbal plural
=hci appears outside the inflected verb and the root-attaching plural
suffix -pa inside it sakaguchi2024 12, 77–85, 163 . His
criterion bears on the Hokkaido outer pa by extension from a
Sakhalin-focused analysis ‹contested›. The verbal number system, the
pa / ci auxiliary alternation across dialects, and
the interaction with suppletive stems are treated in person-number-interactions and
suppletive-verbal-number .

The two-index ceiling

A finite verb hosts at most one subject index and one object index. No additional personal
prefix or suffix is possible regardless of how many semantic participants the predicate
involves. The restriction is most visible in three-place predicates: with
kore 'give', recipient and theme cannot each receive an overt index.
When both are third person, neither is indexed and both surface as bare noun phrases nakagawa2024 363–365 ; sato2023b 39, 47 .

topenpe hekattar ku=kore | sweets children 1SG.A=give | I gave the children sweets. | Both non-subject participants are third-person bare noun phrases; only the 1SG.A subject is indexed. The ceiling prevents a separate object index for either recipient or theme. | nakagawa2024:127 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 11a

When a non-third participant is present with a three-place predicate, the object index is
assigned to the human recipient. Satō analyses this as a secundative pattern, treating the
recipient as the selected undergoer of the indexing slot; Nakagawa frames the same verbs as
three-place predicates with two bare object phrases that may occur in varying word order, and
neither obligatorily indexed when both are third person sato2008 147 ; sato2023b 40, 47 ; sato2025a 368–371 ; nakagawa2024 363–365 ‹contested›.
Object-indexing rules and the recipient-vs-theme selection are developed in
object-indexing-monotransitive .

The ceiling also determines how reflexive and reciprocal derivations interact with personal
marking. Once yay- , si- , or
u- occupies the internal argument position as part of a derived stem,
the verb is indexed according to its derived valence; a separate object index for the same
participant would exceed the cap and is ungrammatical tamura1972 35 ; nakagawa2024 183, 196–199 . Satō uses this two-index restriction to argue that a zero third-person index must be posited
in the paradigm, so that the cap is stateable uniformly, in disagreement with Nakagawa's earlier
position that third person has no affix sato2023b 39 ; sato2025a 369 ; nakagawa2024 169 ‹contested›.

Clitic and affix status of the personal indexes

The boundary symbol = used throughout this grammar at personal-index
junctions is a notational convenience, not a settled morphological claim. Nakagawa employs
= for all person markers as a convenience mark explicitly decoupled
from phonology and syllable structure nakagawa2024 49 . Tamura and Satō write
the same markers with hyphens. Bugaeva grades the indexes into three classes on prosodic and
separability grounds bugaeva2012 472–473 :

grade
forms
diagnostic

word-like
=an , =as

may be separated from the stem by intervening particles; form autonomous
prosodic units

clitic-like
a= , eci=
inseparable; do not shift the verb's accent kernel

full prefix

ku= , ci= ,
e= , en= ,
un= , i=

inseparable and shift the accent kernel

Nakagawa arrives at a comparable assessment of =an through a different
route. His 分離人称 (separated-person) data show =an detached from its
verb by the additive particle ka or the topic marker
anak — a separability property he characterises as clitic or 倚辞 status,
possibly tracing the form to the existential verb an 'exist' nakagawa2024 183 :

tane iperusuy ka =an sinki ka =an kor | now hungry even 4.S tired even 4.S while | Now, being hungry and also tired, … | The particle ka intervenes between each intransitive stem and its =an, demonstrating the separability that distinguishes =an from the full prefixes and that Nakagawa cites for its clitic characterisation. | nakagawa2024:183 | CHI | Nakagawa ex. 161

Sakaguchi's 2024 thesis on Sakhalin Ainu draws a formal distinction between affix boundaries
( - , root-attaching) and clitic boundaries ( = ,
attaching to the fully person-inflected form). Under his analysis the 4th-person
=an / an= is classified as highly autonomous and
clitic-like, while 1PL.EXCL -as is treated as a root-attaching affix —
a verdict that parts from Bugaeva, who places =as in the same word-like
class as =an sakaguchi2024 ix, 62–64, 77–85, 101, 163 ;
bugaeva2012 472–473 ‹contested›. Sakaguchi's systematic diagnostics were
developed for Sakhalin material; the only explicitly Hokkaido application in that work is the
clitic status of the outer verbal plural pa in Saru, Chitose, and
Ishikari, derived from Nakagawa's inside/outside ordering criterion. A dedicated clitic/affix
study of Hokkaido person-marking at comparable rigour has not been produced; the Hokkaido
boundary gradation rests on Bugaeva's prosodic classification and Nakagawa's separability data
‹contested›. The boundary analysis is taken further in
alignment-hierarchical-and-the-debate .

The Ijäs learner template

Ijäs's online pedagogical grammar presents the same left-right ordering for L2 learners,
grouping person indexes as the verb's obligatory argument positions and treating noun phrases
as optional outer constituents ijas2023 . The framing correctly reflects the
head-marking character of the system: the verb carries the obligatory participant indexes while
the independent pronouns are optional sato2025a 361 ; nichols1986 . Her examples are constructed teaching forms, not corpus attestations, and she writes
personal-index boundaries with hyphens as a pedagogical convention.

ku=hosipi | 1SG.S=return | I return. | Constructed following Ijäs lesson 2. She writes ku-hosipi; this grammar uses = for the personal-index boundary. The form shows 1SG.S on an intransitive stem in the left personal layer. | ijas2023 | SAR

kunneywa wakka e=ku | morning water 2SG.A=drink | You drink water in the morning. | Constructed following Ijäs lesson 3. The object is third person with no overt index; e= occupies the left personal layer as 2SG.A. She writes e-ku. | ijas2023 | SAR

Two features of the Ijäs presentation are pedagogical conventions rather than grammatical
generalisations. Her word-order guidance for ditransitives treats the indirect object as
occupying a fixed position between subject and direct object — a useful classroom rule that
does not correspond to a syntactic constraint; Nakagawa's data on three-place predicates allow
the two bare object phrases to appear in varying orders nakagawa2024 363–365 .
The zero-marking of third-person transitive arguments in her examples is factually accurate but
theoretically neutral: it is compatible with Nakagawa's position that there is no third-person
affix and with Satō's posit of a zero index required by the two-index ceiling nakagawa2024 169 ; sato2023b 39 ; sato2025a 369 ‹contested›.

title: Predicative Possession: kor, Existential Possession, and isam
summary: Clause-level 'have/belong' predication via transitive kor, existential-based possession, and negative isam, plus the noun-level affiliative-vs-kor choice for alienable nouns; placed on Stassen's predicative-possession typology and interfaced with the nominal affiliative system (Part VII).

title: Relative Clauses: The Gap Strategy and the Accessibility Hierarchy
summary: The head-final, relativizer-less gap relative clause and the Keenan–Comrie accessibility hierarchy — subject/object relativize directly, obliques only after e-/ko-/o- applicative promotion — plus possessor, locative/temporal, and standard-of-comparison relativization.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Relative Clauses: The Gap Strategy and the Accessibility Hierarchy
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The head-final, relativizer-less gap relative clause and the Keenan–Comrie accessibility hierarchy — subject/object relativize directly, obliques only after e-/ko-/o- applicative promotion — plus possessor, locative/temporal, and standard-of-comparison relativization.

Overview

The Hokkaido Ainu relative clause is prenominal and head-final: the modifying clause precedes
its head noun, there is no relative pronoun, and the relativized participant is simply absent
from the clause — a zero gap nakagawa2024 503 ; sato2008 168 ;
shibatani1990 39 ; bugaeva2015 47 . Stative property verbs
modify a following noun directly and without any special marking:
hure suwop 'red box'. Any verb, stative or dynamic, can head the
modifying clause; the verb inside the RC carries the same person prefixes, number-sensitive
suppletion (singular ek come vs plural arki come (PL) ),
polarity, and clause-internal morphology as it would in a main clause
shibatani1990 39–40 ; sato2008 168 . Subordination is
signalled by the structural position of the clause before its head, not by any morphological
change to the verb bugaeva2016 100 ; nakagawa2024 503 .

This architecture — a full finite clause immediately before its head — is shared by the
headless relative (where a formal noun such as
p thing, person ~ pe thing, person supplies the head) and the
complement clause (where a formal noun nominalizes a saturated clause). The boundary between
these constructions is governed by Nakagawa's valency (arity) principle, discussed in the
following section and with worked examples in
nominalization-p-pe the chapter on the nominalizer
p ~ pe . The headless variant is treated
in headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses ; lexicalized RC-headed
nominalizations such as aep food in
lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization .

The arity ≥ +1 diagnostic

Nakagawa's arity accounting provides the formal test that separates the relative clause from
the complement clause. Each verb contributes a positive arity value equal to the number of NP
arguments it requires; each NP present inside the clause subtracts one; a formal noun has
arity −1. When the summed arity of the embedded clause plus its formal-noun head is still
positive (≥ +1), one argument slot remains unfilled — that is the gap — and the whole unit
functions as a relative clause. When the arity reaches zero, the clause is saturated and the
formal noun nominalizes the whole clause as a complement
nakagawa2024 261–262, 504, 507 .

clause arity before the formal noun
formal noun arity
total
result

+2 (transitive verb, both arguments missing)
−1
+1
relative clause (one participant gapped)

+1 (intransitive verb, subject present)

0 (clause fully saturated)
−1
−1

The surface string [clause] p/pe is identical in the relative and the
complementizer readings; only the arity count distinguishes them. Satō's rule of thumb
for the same distinction is whether the original sentence from which the head noun was
extracted can be reconstructed: for a relative clause it can, for a nominalizer it cannot
sato2008 174–175 .

The gap strategy and postposition retention

The gap is a zero element: no pronoun, resumptive or otherwise, marks the relativized
position inside the RC bugaeva2015 47 ; dalcorso2020 51 .
When the head noun fills a core argument position (S or O) in the embedded clause, this gap
is sufficient, because the verb's argument structure and the remaining NPs identify the role
of the missing participant. When the head fills an oblique position — instrumental, comitative,
locative, dative — the postposition that governs that oblique remains inside the RC, moved to
a position at or near the left edge of the embedded clause. This retained postposition is what
marks the semantic role of the gapped head when that role is not recoverable from the verb's
argument structure alone
bugaeva2015 47–49 ; nakagawa2024 504 .

The practical consequence is that a postposition's presence inside an RC signals an oblique
head. Conversely, removing the postposition collapses the arity to zero and makes the
relative-clause reading unavailable. Nakagawa's minimal pair makes this visible:
nea menoko ani turepta ni is grammatical as an instrumental RC
('the stick with which that woman dug uba-lilies'), but the postposition-free string
*nea menoko turepta ni has no gap and cannot relativize the instrument
nakagawa2024 505–506 .

nea menoko ani turepta ni | that woman INS dig.uba-lily stick | the stick with which that woman dug uba-lilies | The instrumental ani must stay in the clause; its removal collapses the arity. The lexicalized compound tureptani 'uba-lily-digging stick' contains the same morphological material but grammatically specifies no instrumental relation. | nakagawa2024:506 | HK

Only postpositions that are capable of appearing independently with a zero-anaphoric NP in a
main clause — ani with (instrumental) ,
turano together with (comitative) ,
eun to it (dative) , pakno up to, as far as — undergo this
retention directly. For locative and allative postpositions that require a case-marking
particle ( ta , un ), the relational noun
oro place, there must be inserted before the particle; it serves as the NP
host for the postposition and as the covert correlate of the gapped head
bugaeva2015 49 ; sato2008 172 ;
nakagawa2024 510–511 .

oro ta ku=yamuk cikuni | there LOC 1SG.A=gather.chestnuts tree | the tree at which I gathered chestnuts | The relational noun oro supplies the NP argument of the locative ta; cikuni 'tree' is the external head. Without oro the particle ta has no host. | sato2008:172 | CHI

The Keenan–Comrie accessibility hierarchy

Bugaeva maps Hokkaido Ainu relativization against the noun phrase accessibility hierarchy of
keenancomrie1977 and finds that HA relativizes all core and most oblique
positions; the only positions that resist are the alienable possessor and, directly, the
standard of comparison bugaeva2015 45, 47–49 . These findings are confirmed
for southern Hokkaido data by dalcorso2020 51 .

Core argument positions

Subject (S) relativization leaves a gap in the single argument position of an intransitive
verb:

cise soy pakno arki utar | house outside up.to come.PL people | the men who came up to the doorway | arki is the suppletive plural of ek 'come'. The S (the men) is gapped; the directional adjunct cise soy pakno 'up to outside the house' stays in the clause. utar 'people' is the external head. | bugaeva2015:47 | HK

Agent (A) relativization gaps the subject of a transitive verb while leaving the object
inside the RC. A minimal pair from Bugaeva illustrates two parallel A-relativizations
under the same verb:

kem sawot pe tumi sawot pe | famine flee NMLZ war flee NMLZ | people fleeing famine, people fleeing war | sawot 'flee, escape' is transitive; kem 'famine' and tumi 'war' are the retained O arguments. The A (the fleeing people) is gapped and the formal noun pe supplies the generic head 'person'. In the corpus, O-relativization is more frequent than A-relativization (Bugaeva 2015: 48). | bugaeva2015:48 | HK

Object (O) relativization is the most frequent type in the corpus
bugaeva2015 48 . The head noun fills the O slot:

ku=nukar kanpisos | 1SG.A=see book | the book I read | Ijäs Lesson 11. The O (the book) is gapped; ku= is the 1SG agent prefix on the transitive verb nukar 'see, read'. The gap strategy is identical when the head is a bound noun rather than a common noun. | ijas2023 | HK

Three-place verbs

For three-place (ditransitive) predicates, either the Recipient or the Theme may be in O
position and relativized. Which argument occupies the O position is determined by the
object-indexing conventions treated in object-indexing-monotransitive ;
relativization follows that indexing. Bugaeva attests the Recipient as head with the
applicative e- applicative prefix (on the applicative system,
applicative-e ):

kampi a=e-pakasnu hekattar | paper 4.A=APPL-teach children | children who have been taught to read | hekattar 'children' is the Recipient-O head; kampi 'paper/literacy' is the retained Theme. The applicative e- licenses the Recipient in the O position. The A of the three-place verb (the teacher) is not the gapped position; it is simply unrealized (generic). | bugaeva2015:48 | HK

No attested example of the A of a three-place verb serving as RC head has been reported.
Bugaeva states the absence explicitly — "no examples attested so far" — without deciding
whether the position is grammatically excluded or merely rare
bugaeva2015 48 ‹corpus-confirmed (as gap)›.

Oblique positions

The instrument, comitative, and dative are all relativizable with their postpositions
retained.

ani usey a=ku p | INS hot.water 4.A=drink NMLZ | a thing to drink hot water with | The instrumental ani is retained at the clause edge. The formal noun p is the generic instrument head. The indefinite fourth-person a= gives a generic reading. | nakagawa2024:504 | HK

turano ku=yupo kamuy cotca acapo | COMIT 1SG=older.brother bear shoot uncle | the uncle my brother shot the bear with | turano 'together with' is the retained comitative postposition. ku=yupo 'my older brother' is the A of cotca 'shoot'; kamuy 'bear' is the O. acapo 'uncle' is the external head — the gapped comitative co-participant. | bugaeva2015:48 | HK

eun a=nu usi ka isam | to.it 4.A=ask place even not.exist | there was no place to ask about it | eun is the retained dative postposition. usi 'place' is the formal-noun head; it fills the dative position. nu has the meaning 'ask, inquire about' here (Bugaeva 2015: 49 fn.9). | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Locative and allative RC heads require the relational noun oro place, there
before the case particle; the oro hosts the particle and serves as
the implicit correlate of the gap:

oro un ku=arpa ipeusi | there ALLAT 1SG.S=go restaurant | the restaurant I went to | Ijäs Lesson 11. oro un 'to there' is the retained allative phrase. Without oro the allative un has no NP host and the sentence is ill-formed. ipeusi 'restaurant' is the external head. | ijas2023 | HK

Inalienable possessor

An inalienable possessor can be relativized. The possessed noun inside the RC retains its
possessive suffix, which serves as the morphological trace of the possessor-possessee
relation; the head noun fills the gapped possessor position
bugaeva2015 49 ; shibatani1990 41 .

cikir-ihi tanne kikir | leg-POSS long insect | an insect whose legs are long | cikir-ihi is the possessed form of cikir 'leg'; the POSS suffix -ihi remains inside the RC and identifies the possessor as the gapped position. kikir 'insect' is the external head — the gapped possessor. tanne 'long' is the stative predicate. | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Unattested and blocked positions

Alienable possessor

Alienable possession in Hokkaido Ainu is itself expressed by a gapped prenominal relative
clause: ku=kor cep 'my fish' is literally '[the fish that I have]',
with kor have, possess as the transitive verb and ku=
as the 1SG agent bugaeva2022 ch. 1 ; bugaeva2015 49 .
Extracting a possessor from this structure would require relativization from inside a
relative clause — an island constraint — and the construction is accordingly unavailable
bugaeva2015 49 ‹consensus›.

Standard of comparison

Direct relativization of the standard of comparison is not attested. What Bugaeva
documents is a degree-adjunct workaround: a clause of the type 'a rich man [to whose
degree of wealth no rich man exists]' places the degree comparison inside the RC as a
modifier on the head, rather than gapping the standard participant from a transitive clause
bugaeva2015 45, 49 :

a=ona pakno nispa isam nispa ne | 4.A=father equal rich.man not.exist rich.man COP | my father was a rich man with no equal | Bugaeva's discovery example. The prenominal RC [pakno nispa isam] 'to whose degree of wealth no rich man exists' is a degree-adjunct modifier on the second nispa (the head). The standard of comparison is expressed as the negated content of the RC, not as a gapped participant. | bugaeva2015:49 | HK

Whether this is a grammaticalized equivalent to comparison-standard relativization or a
pragmatic repair is not settled ‹corpus-suggested›.

Ablative and mutative heads

Heads corresponding to ablative wa(no) 'from which' and mutative
ne 'as which' are absent from Bugaeva's inventory of retainable
postpositions and are not attested in any source read here
bugaeva2015 48–49 . Ijäs reports the same absence and explicitly withholds
constructed examples for these positions ijas2023 . They are not presented as
established ‹corpus-suggested›.

RC heads: common nouns and bound nouns

The external head of an HA relative clause may be a common noun or a
formal-defective-nouns formal (bound) noun . Among the formal nouns,
kur person (honorific) , utar people ,
p thing, person ~ pe thing, person ,
uske place, time , and hi thing, time, place (abstract) are,
in Bugaeva's account, "even more common" as RC heads than common nouns
bugaeva2015 47 . The bound noun supplies a generic semantic category —
person, people, thing, place — while the RC clause provides all the identifying content.

When the formal noun is p ~ pe , the
construction shades into the headless relative. That continuum is the subject of
nominalization-p-pe and headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses .
The lexicalized end — former RC-plus-formal-noun combinations frozen into compound nouns
such as aep food ( a=e-p , 'thing one eats') — is
treated in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization . The interaction of
relativization with noun incorporation, where a patient argument merges into the verb stem
and changes the arity count, is treated in noun-incorporation-object .

Ijäs Lesson 11 — graded claims

Ijäs's English-language grammar guide covers relative clauses in Lesson 11
ijas2023 . Her examples are constructed teaching material; none are corpus-attested.
Her core grammatical claims — prenominal head-final order, absence of a relative pronoun,
gap strategy for core arguments, postposition retention for obliques (including the
oro -insertion rule for locatives/allatives) — align precisely with the
primary sources ‹consensus›. Her demonstration that
ku=kor cep 'my fish' is itself a relative clause ('the fish that I
have') follows the Handbook's alienable-possession analysis
bugaeva2022 ch. 1 ‹consensus›. And her identification of the ditransitive
subject as a problematic head position matches Bugaeva's "no examples attested so far"
bugaeva2015 48 ; she is consistent in flagging it as an open question rather
than asserting ungrammaticality ‹corpus-confirmed (as gap)›.

Two of her claims need a hedge. Her report that the ordering of multiple stative modifiers
is free ( poro retar seta ~ retar poro seta
'big red dog, red big dog') is her own inference from the absence of counter-examples; she
acknowledges this explicitly. Modifier stacking is attested — Shibatani gives
ampene pirka sukup aynu from yukar data
shibatani1990 39 — but a free-order generalization across dialects exceeds
what the sources establish ‹corpus-suggested›. Her inability to locate attested examples of
ablative wa(no) or mutative ne as RC heads is
shared by all sources read here; the forms she cautiously suggests for these positions should
not be treated as established ijas2023 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Analyses and open questions

All major descriptive accounts converge on the externally-headed, prenominal gap strategy:
shibatani1990 39–42 , refsing1986 259–264 ,
sato2008 168–173 , nakagawa2024 503–511 , and
bugaeva2015 47–49 all describe the construction in these terms. The
differences among them are terminological — Refsing calls the process "adnominalization",
Nakagawa uses "関係文", Bugaeva "gapped prenominal relative clause" — rather than empirical.

Dal Corso 2020 proposes that certain southern Hokkaido indirect-evidential constructions
( siri ki , hawe as ,
humi as ) show a layout reminiscent of internally-headed relative
clauses, instantiated through what he calls classificatory noun incorporation
dalcorso2020 51–52 . This proposal covers the indirect-evidential
constructions specifically; it has not been adopted by Bugaeva, Satō, or Nakagawa; and its
claim of classificatory NI remains unconfirmed in the wider literature ‹contested›. The
relativization system described in this chapter is the externally-headed construction; the
IHRC/CNI question bears on the evidential construction and is addressed in
headless-internally-headed-noun-modifying-clauses .

Three gaps in the accessible evidence remain open. First, whether the ditransitive subject
position is grammatically excluded or merely unattested is not decided: the absence is
systematic — reported independently by Bugaeva 2015: 48 and by Ijäs — but no source
provides a grammaticality judgment supported by speaker elicitation ‹contested›. Second,
ablative and mutative RC heads are unconfirmed in any source read here ‹corpus-suggested›.
Third, the accessibility-hierarchy findings rest primarily on Saru and Chitose data
bugaeva2015 ; whether all Hokkaido dialects relativize the same positions has not
been systematically tested ‹speculative›.

title: Quotative Complementation and Reported Speech (sekor)
summary: The dominant complementation strategy: framing a (direct-form) clause with the quotative sekor under verbs of speech, cognition, and intention.
part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Quotative Complementation and Reported Speech (sekor)
Part: Part XIII — Nominalization, Relativization, and Complementation
Summary: The dominant complementation strategy: framing a (direct-form) clause with the quotative sekor under verbs of speech, cognition, and intention.

sekor in the complementizer system

Hokkaido Ainu reports speech and thought by placing the invariable particle
sekor at the right edge of the quoted string, before the matrix
verb of saying or thinking. This mechanism occupies a distinct slot in Refsing's
four-way clause-embedding typology: conjunctionalization, nominalization,
quotationalization , and adnominalization — with quotationalization, realized by
sekor (and its variant ari ), forming a
separate category from the nominalized-complementizer system
refsing1986 §14.14 . The nominalized complementizers —
kuni , kunak , yak ,
kusu — that Nakagawa treats in his indirect-speech section
operate through the formal-noun nominalizing machinery described in
nominalization-p-pe and
nominalized-complements-control-raising ;
sekor does not nominalize — it quotes directly
nakagawa2024 475–480 .

The matrix verbs that take sekor overlap substantially with
those that select the indirect-speech markers: ye say ,
itak speak, say , haweok speak up , and
ramu think . The choice between the quotative and the nominalizing
complementizers encodes the direct-vs-indirect distinction and controls the
person-marking pattern in the embedded clause
nakagawa2024 475 ; bugaeva2008 31–35 . How the
formal-noun class that enables nominalized complementation is constituted is set out
in formal-defective-nouns .

Form and position

sekor is morphologically invariant: it carries no person
affixes, no TAM morphology, and no allomorphic alternation conditioned by the shape
of the preceding clause. Its canonical position is immediately after the last element
of the quoted string and before the matrix verb:
[quoted clause] sekor [matrix verb]. Refsing identifies a
variant ari with the same quotative function in the Shizunai
dialect; both are treated as "quotationalizers" forming a class distinct from the
nominalized-complementizer inventory refsing1986 §14.14 .

The quoted clause preserves all features of a main clause: person affixes, polarity,
TAM markers, and clause-internal particles are intact inside the quotation.
Sentence-final particles ( na , wa , etc.)
may appear within the quoted string but not between sekor and
the matrix verb — a distributional restriction shared with the indirect-speech markers,
whose embedded clauses also exclude final particles
nakagawa2024 475–476 .

pirka sekor a=ye | be.good QUOT 4.A=say | He/she said, 'It is good.' | Minimal sekor frame: an unmodified stative predicate quoted before the fourth-person matrix verb a=ye 'say'. | HK

ek=an sekor ku=ramu | come=4.S QUOT 1SG.A=think | I thought, 'He/she will come.' | sekor with the thought verb ramu: the fourth-person intransitive suffix =an on ek 'come' marks the referent of the thought inside the quote. The matrix ku=ramu carries the first-person speaker-agent. | HK

Direct and indirect quotation

The clearest evidence for the sekor vs indirect-complementizer
distinction is person marking. Under sekor , the original
speaker's person forms are preserved unchanged inside the quote. Under
kuni , kunak , and
yak , the embedded clause's person is adjusted to the reporting
narrator's viewpoint: an original first person shifts to the fourth, the
indefinite/logophoric person
nakagawa2024 475 . The indirect-speech markers also differ among
themselves in the temporal or semantic value they impose on the embedded proposition
nakagawa2024 475–480 ; sato2008 176–177 .

The person contrast between sekor and
kuni is illustrated by the following pair. The proposition
'I will return' appears first as a direct quotation (constructed), then under the
indirect-speech complementizer kuni (attested in Nakagawa's
grammar):

ku=hosipi sekor a=ye | 1SG.S=return QUOT 4.A=say | He/she said, 'I will return.' | Direct quotation under sekor: the first-person intransitive prefix ku= on hosipi 'return' is the original speaker's own form, intact inside the quote. The matrix a=ye carries the fourth-person narrator-agent. | HK

hosipi=an kuni a=ye | return=4.S COMP 4.A=say | I said I would return. | Indirect quotation under kuni: the original first-person subject is shifted to the fourth-person intransitive suffix =an, adopting the narrator's viewpoint. kuni nominalizes the embedded clause as the nominal object of a=ye 'say'. | nakagawa2024:476 | HK

The temporal contrast between kunak (future proposition,
reported as ongoing) and yak (past or general proposition) runs
in parallel within the indirect system. Satō's kunak example
combines the indirect-speech marker with the ongoing-aspect phrase
kor an :

ku=matnepo tanto ek kunak ye kor an | 1SG.POSS=daughter today come COMP say CONJ exist | My daughter says she will come today. | kunak frames the future proposition ek 'come.SG'; kor an 'while/is' marks the report as ongoing. The embedded subject of ek is the shifted fourth person (the daughter). The possessive ku=matnepo 'my daughter' is the topic of the whole utterance. | sato2008:177 | CHI

The directive use of kuni — 'tell X to do Y' — appears
frequently in folktale dialogue and marks the complement as a directed action rather
than a proposition:

a ahun kuni a=ye akusu toykooripak | PFV enter COMP 4.A=say CONJ be.abashed | When I told them to enter, they were very abashed. | kuni follows the directed action ahun 'enter'; a=ye 'say/tell' is the fourth-person matrix verb. akusu marks the sequential relation to the consequent clause. The perfective a closes the speech event before the consequence. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話8 (その2) [aa-irc/007#213]

The purposive and deontic extensions of kuni beyond the
reported-speech domain are treated in
kuni-complementizer-purpose-complements .

hawe and itak as framing elements

The formal noun hawe — possessed form of
haw voice , 'the voice/report of' — extends the quotative architecture
in two directions. As a bare complementizer it nominalizes a speech event and supplies
it as the perceptual object of verbs like nu hear, learn of : the
complement clause becomes "the voice/talk of" the event
sato2008 175–178 . Combined with the copula, hawe ne
forms the reportative evidential frame; when sekor precedes it,
the compound sekor hawe ne attributes a quoted proposition to
an unspecified external source ‹corpus-suggested›:

ek sekor haw-e ne | come QUOT voice-POSS COP | It is said that he/she came. | sekor hawe ne: the quotative particle sekor intervenes between the embedded clause ek 'come' and the reportative frame haw-e ne. The internal morphology haw-e shows the source noun haw 'voice' plus the possessed ending -e. | HK

The clause-final reportative evidential hawe ne standing alone —
where it marks information acquired through speech without framing an embedded quotation —
is treated in hawe-ne-reportative , and the four-way evidential paradigm
to which it belongs in evidential-schema-overview .

itak speak, say, talk is the most frequent speech verb in narrative
dialogue — narrators routinely use it to introduce character utterances — while
ye say appears predominantly in indirect-speech and complementizer
frames. Both appear after sekor and both select
kuni / kunak for indirect complements
nakagawa2024 476 ; sato2008 176 . A stable semantic or
register distinction between the two verbs in quotative syntax is not established in
the read sources ‹corpus-suggested›.

The logophoric fourth person in speech reports

Person adjustment in the indirect-speech complementizer frames reflects what Bugaeva
identifies as logophoricity in Southern Hokkaido Ainu. The fourth-person markers
a= / ci= on transitives and
=an on intransitives replace an original first person in the
embedded clause, reanchoring it to the reporting narrator's viewpoint. Nakagawa
formulates this as a categorical rule: under the indirect-speech markers, "final
particles cannot be embedded; the speaker's-viewpoint person shifts 1→3/4"
nakagawa2024 475 .

Bugaeva's study of reported discourse and logophoricity in Southern Hokkaido Ainu
provides the analytical framework: the fourth person here is not a simple third-person
referential shift but a logophoric device, indexing the embedded protagonist — the
original speaker or thinker — whose perspective the narrator temporarily adopts
bugaeva2008 . The surface form is identical to the generic/indefinite fourth
person (also a= / =an ); the logophoric
reading is determined by the presence of a complementizer frame and by contextual
co-reference with the matrix subject ‹contested›. The full fourth-person paradigm and
its logophoric distribution across narrative genres are treated in
fourth-person-honorific and indefinite-fourth-person-paradigm .

Nikitina and Bugaeva develop a stronger claim: the construction is better characterized
as logophoric speech rather than indirect speech in the conventional theoretical
sense, because the person system encodes the narrator's perspective adoption, not a
syntactic binding of the embedded subject to a matrix controller
nikitina2021 . The discourse contexts — first-person narration in uwepeker
and yukar — where this perspective adoption is most systematic are examined in
logophoric-narration-and-reported-discourse ‹contested›.

Under sekor direct quotation, no person shift applies: the
original speaker's person forms are preserved inside the quote, and the logophoric
reanchoring does not arise in the embedded clause. This is the morphological correlate
of the direct/indirect boundary:
sekor preserves the perspective of the original utterance;
the indirect-speech markers reanchor it to the narrator's viewpoint
bugaeva2008 31–35 ; nikitina2021 .

Analyses and open questions

The descriptions converge on the categorical point. Refsing separates quotationalization
from nominalization as coordinate clause-embedding types, placing
sekor / ari outside the nominalizer class
refsing1986 §14.14 . Nakagawa's §18.2, which treats the indirect-speech
markers extensively as nominalization-based complementizers that take a nominal-object
slot, is consistent with keeping sekor as a non-nominalizing
quotative; his account of the person shift and the final-particle ban applies to
kuni / kunak / yak
and not to sekor
nakagawa2024 475–480 . Shibatani, whose treatment foregrounds the
formal-noun complementizer system, does not single out a quotative particle in the
dialect data he covers shibatani1990 83–84 .

Three questions remain open in the read sources. First, the geographical and register
distribution of sekor vs ari across
Hokkaido dialects is documented only by Refsing's Shizunai attestation of both
refsing1986 §14.14 ; whether ari carries any
semantic or pragmatic distinction from sekor or is a
register/lexical variant is not established ‹speculative›. Second, the
grammaticalization status of the compound sekor hawe ne frame
is not settled: whether the sequence is transparently compositional (quotative + reportative
evidential) or a lexicalized reportative formula cannot be decided from the sources
read here ‹speculative›. Third, the logophoricity analysis of Nikitina and Bugaeva
awaits corpus-level testing at construction level; whether the person-shift pattern
under kunak / yak distributes differently
from the generic fourth-person use is not settled by evidence in the read sources
‹contested›.

Ijäs's pedagogical grammar covers relative clauses and the formal-noun nominalizer
system in Lesson 11 — the material banked for this grammar's nominalization domain —
but her treatment of direct and indirect speech falls in a separate lesson not included
in the factbase read for this domain ijas2023 . No Ijäs claims on
sekor or quotative framing are available for grading here.

title: The Reciprocal u-
summary: The reciprocal prefix u- 'each other/mutually', its collective/associative readings, and its interaction with plurality.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Reciprocal u-
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The reciprocal prefix u- 'each other/mutually', its collective/associative readings, and its interaction with plurality.

The prefix u- and its arity effect

The prefix u- reciprocal is a valency-decreasing derivational morpheme with
arity −1: it fills the object slot of a transitive verb with a mutual or collective
participant, detransitivizing the verb nakagawa2024 187–188 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 . The derived verb always takes a plural or
collective subject — only two or more participants can stand in a mutual relation. The
person-marking diagnostic (the operative test of derived transitivity set out in
valency-voice-overview ) confirms the intransitivization: a verb that takes the
prefixal A-indexes a= or ci= shifts to the
suffixal S-indexes =an or =as after
u- bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 ;
sato2008 232–233 .

The slot u- occupies is the same nominal/applicative position as the
reflexive-yay reflexive prefixes yay- reflexive (direct) and
si- reflexive (indirect) and the
antipassive-detransitive-i antipassive i- antipassive ; all
three subtract one arity unit by filling the object slot, but their semantic domains differ —
self-directed action, object defocusing, and mutual participation, respectively. The prefix
is highly productive: it attaches to base transitive verbs, to applicative-prefixed stems,
and to causativized forms, with the arity calculus predicting the transitivity of each output
(see valency-voice-overview inventory ). A further complication is that
u- covers not only canonical bilateral reciprocal meaning ('each
other') but also joint or associative participation ('together'), and the two readings are not
always distinguishable without context.

The canonical reciprocal

In its canonical use, u- expresses mutual action: each participant in
the plural subject simultaneously acts on and is acted on by the others. The paradigm pair
koyki fight, attack sb (transitive) →
u-koyki fight each other (intransitive), and
siru rub sth → u-siru rub each other , illustrates the
productive base pattern nakagawa2024 187–188 .

nea nispa utar u-koyki kor oka sir-i a=nukar | DEM chief ASS RECP-fight CONJ exist.PL appearance-POSS 4.A=see | I saw the chiefs fighting each other. | Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 ex. 11a. u-koyki is intransitive and takes no A-prefix; the clause sir-i a=nukar ('the appearance of which was seen') frames the event as a witnessed scene. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.1 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K8010281UP.168

u- also operates on ditransitive (three-argument) bases. When the
Addressee or Recipient of a ditransitive is the argument filled by the mutual relation, the
construction is termed indirect reciprocal bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 . The
epenthetic glide w surfaces between u- and a
following vowel-initial prefix:

u-w-e-pakasnu | RECP-EP-APPL-teach | teach each other | Indirect reciprocal: the Addressee of e-pakasnu 'teach sb sth' is reciprocalized; the Theme content remains as the surviving object. The epenthetic glide w surfaces between u- and the vowel-initial applicative e-. | tamura1996:808 | SAR

The learner grammar of ijas2023 (Lesson 9-10) also lists
unukar meet each other (from nukar see sb ) as a standard
reciprocal, and gives the pair
u-hekote be a married couple (singular) /
u-hekotpa be a married couple (plural suppletive) ; both are confirmed by the
primary sources ‹consensus› nakagawa2024 187–188 .

Joint-participation and collective readings

Several forms with u- do not yield a strictly bilateral 'each other'
reading. Here u- signals joint involvement or a two-party social
relation without requiring symmetric action between every pair of participants
nakagawa2024 187–188 .

The clearest instance is u-tures-kor be siblings , which incorporates the noun
tures sibling of the same sex and the verb kor have . The
surface subject may be syntactically singular — a young man described in terms of having a
sister — yet the verb carries u- because the sibling relation
inherently involves two parties:

Iskar emko ta u-tures-kor okkaypo okay pe | Ishikari upper LOC RECP-sibling-have young.man exist.PL NMLZ | A young man with a sister, up the Ishikari River. | Nakagawa ex. 164. The singular okkaypo 'young man' is the surface subject, yet u-tures-kor carries u- because having a sibling is an inherently two-party relation. The plural existential okay reflects the broader story context. | nakagawa2024:187 | SAR

Similarly, u-mina-re laugh together does not mean 'make each other laugh' but
'all laugh together' — a sociative reading arising from the arity calculus:
u- (−1) prefixed to the already-causativized intransitive
mina-re (arity 2) yields net arity 1 (intransitive), encoding
collective action rather than imposing a new causer slot nakagawa2024 188 .
This u- + causative-on-intransitive path to a sociative reading is
treated further in reciprocal-u sociative below.

Semantic types

Bugaeva and Kobayashi identify five constructional types for u-
reciprocals, organized by the nature of the mutual relation and the morphosyntactic base
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 :

type
base
example
translation

canonical (subject-oriented)
2-arg transitive
u-tumam-an
'we held each other'

indirect (Recipient reciprocalized)
3-arg ditransitive
u-w-e-pakasnu
'teach each other'

object-oriented (causativized)
intransitive reciprocal + -re
u-koyki-re
'make them fight each other'

joining-type
2-arg transitive with applicative
u-ko-sina
'tie (bow and arrow) together'

quasi-possessive
u- + [possessed noun + verb]
u-oruspe-nu
"hear each other's stories"

The quasi-possessive type distributes each participant's possessed noun across the group:
u-tek-kisma 'shake hands' (lit. 'shake each other's hand') and
u-oruspe-nu are further instances
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 . For the parallel reflexive construction in
which a possessed noun is incorporated with yay- or
si- , see reflexive-possessive-si .

u-oruspe-nu=an kor oka=an ayne | RECP-story-hear=4.S CONJ exist.PL=4.S CONJ | listening to each other's stories, living on | Excerpt from Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 ex. 13. u-oruspe-nu: oruspe 'story' is the possessed noun distributed across the participants; nu 'hear' is detransitivized by u-, taking the 4th-person S suffix =an. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.2.1 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K8010291UP.517

The causativized reciprocal

When the productive causative suffix -re is added to an already-reciprocalized
intransitive, arity rises by one, and the construction means 'cause sb to do X to each other'.
The chain is: base transitive (arity 2) → add u- (−1) → intransitive
(1) → add -re (+1) → transitive (2), with a new causer as subject:

u-koyki-re | RECP-fight-CAUS | make them fight each other | Object-oriented causativized reciprocal. Arity: koyki (2) + u- (−1) = 1 (vi); then u-koyki (1) + -re (+1) = 2 (vt). After Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 §4.2.1.

The contrasting sociative reading — 'all do X together' — arises when u-
is prefixed to a form already causativized from an intransitive base, so that the net arity is
1 − 1 = 0:
u-horip-pa-re all dance/exorcise together ,
u-kira-re all escape together
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.2 . The arity of the base verb and the order of
operations determine which reading obtains; surface form alone is insufficient
nakagawa2024 188 ‹corpus-suggested›.

Lexicalized forms and extension to non-verbal bases

A range of forms with u- have undergone semantic specialization to
the point where the mutual motivation is conventional. Kin- and social-relation verbs such as
u-tures-kor be siblings and u-hekote be a married couple
encode relational membership. unukar meet each other is the standard word for
'meet' in Saru nakagawa2024 187–188 .

The prefix extends beyond the verb paradigm to locative nouns, postpositions, and adverbs:
u-sam near each other , u-tura together ,
u-neno similar to each other
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.1 .

Two further forms show opacity. uwanpare gaze intently decomposes as
u-anpa-re (RECP-carry-CAUS), but the semantic path from 'cause each
other to carry' to 'observe intently' is unresolved in the sources ‹contested›
aynucorporadiscord (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024).
uwatte multiply, breed (of animals) has been analysed as
u-w-at-te (RECP-EP-attach-CAUS); the semantic path 'cause each other
to cling/pair' → 'breed' is plausible for a verb restricted to animal populations, and its
complement uwaste increase (of humans, plants) is semantically differentiated
aynucorporadiscord (gengojiro, aynu-corpora Discord, 2024) ‹corpus-suggested›.

The sociative uko- and uwe-

The prefixes uko- together, mutually and uwe- together, mutually
are synchronically unitary markers but decompose as
u- + applicative-ko ko- and
u- + applicative-e e-
respectively nakagawa2024 208, 211–212 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.2 . Because the applicative contributes arity +1 and
u- contributes −1, the net arity is zero: the sociative is
valency-neutral. The minimal pair u-kor marry (lit. have each other) (arity −1,
intransitive) versus u-ko-kor have together (arity 0, transitive) makes this
decisive nakagawa2024 208 :

matkaci a=maci turano a=u-ko-kor hine oka=an wa | girl 1SG.POSS=wife together 1SG.A=RECP-APPL-have CONJ exist.PL=4.S CONJ | Together with my wife, I had a daughter, and we went on living. | Nakagawa ex. 218. a=u-ko-kor is transitive (arity 0 preserves the base kor's transitivity): the 1SG.A prefix a= on the verb confirms this. The plain reciprocal u-kor 'marry each other' is intransitive and takes =an. | nakagawa2024:208 | SAR

kamuy-utar opitta uko-yay-kopuntek | spirit-ASS all RECP.APPL-REFL-rejoice | All the spirits rejoiced together. | Bugaeva & Kobayashi 2022 ex. 34. uko- (= u- + ko-) combines with the reflexive yay-; the whole form is intransitive (arity: uko- = 0, then yay- = −1). | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.3.2 | HK | Nakagawa corpus OI990423KY.066

uko- is commoner than uwe- in Saru texts,
reflecting the higher productivity of ko- as an applicative
bugaeva2006 188 . The two are partially interchangeable in derivationally
transparent forms ( uko-yaykopuntek ~
uwe-yaykopuntek 'rejoice together'), but
ko- 's comitative and goal-directed semantics give it the advantage
where joint orientation toward shared participants is foregrounded
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.3.2 . For the full applicative semantics of
ko- and e- , see
applicative-system-overview ; for their stacking with other prefixes,
applicative-stacking-relativization-feeding .
