title: The Reflexive-Possessive si- and the yay-/si- Division
summary: The prefix si- 'oneself / one's own / by itself' and its division of labor with yay- in argument identification.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Reflexive-Possessive si- and the yay-/si- Division
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The prefix si- 'oneself / one's own / by itself' and its division of labor with yay- in argument identification.

Overview

The prefix si- reflexive; one's own reduces the argument frame of a
transitive verb by one, identifying the object position with the subject and yielding an
intransitive verb. Like the sister prefix yay- reflexive (direct)
(treated in reflexive-yay ), it is glossed 自分 'self'
and carries arity −1 in Nakagawa's calculus, so the derived verb shifts from the prefixal
A-set ( a= , ci= ) to the suffixal S-set
( =an , =as ), as diagnosed in
person-marking-architecture nakagawa2024 188–189 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

si- is more restricted in distribution than yay- : the latter attaches
to transitives, intransitives, and nouns alike (with arity 0 on nouns, as in
yay-kotan one's own village ), whereas si- is productive only on
transitive bases nakagawa2024 188 . Beyond the basic reflexive reading it
also functions as a reflexive-possessive , marking the subject-coreferent possessor
of an incorporated nominal, and it enters a 'pretend to' construction when combined with
the causative suffixes -re and -ka
nakagawa2024 189 ; sato2007 . The meaning that underlies all
three uses is the same: the subject's involvement passes through another participant or
external circumstance rather than arising from direct self-initiated action.

The yay-/si- division of labour

Four analyses of the yay-/si- contrast are on record, all working from the same minimal
pairs. The classic pair is pusu float something up →
yay-pusu pop up volitionally 'pop up by swimming (volitionally)' versus
si-pusu float up spontaneously 'float up of itself', attributed to Kindaichi
(1931: 149, reported by sato2007 ) and tamura1996 622 .
Nakagawa adds rayke kill → yay-rayke commit suicide
'commit suicide' versus si-rayke be ruined by ill fortune
'be ruined by ill fortune' nakagawa2024 188–189 .

Competing analyses of the yay- / si- contrast

analytic parameter
yay-
si-
source

intentionality
intentional ( 意志的 )
unintentional, spontaneous
Kindaichi 1931: 149 (via sato2007 ); tamura1996 622

self-role
agentive self
patientive self
Kirikae 1994: 316 (reported by sato2007 )

argument position
any thematic role
Theme or Patient only — never causee
Kobayashi (2008: 140, cited in bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 );
kobayashi2010

mode of involvement
direct self-action
indirect — via another participant or circumstance
sato2007 31–38 ; adopted by nakagawa2024 189
and bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

The direct/indirect analysis (line four) is the framework Nakagawa (2024: 189) and the
Handbook adopt as the current working account ‹contested›. Satō (2007: ≈31–38) builds it
from three paradigm cases: si-nuye have oneself tattooed 'have oneself
tattooed' (traditional Ainu tattooing required another person to do the work);
si-kasuy-re have others help oneself 'have others help oneself' (the help
comes from outside); and si-ranpewtek-ka pretend not to know 'pretend not
to know' (the pretence succeeds only if another observer believes it). In each case the
subject's reflexive involvement is mediated by another party's action or perception. Satō
himself qualifies the account as not yet established
( 定説ではない ) sato2007 .

None of the earlier analyses is fully discredited. The intentionality framing (Kindaichi,
Tamura) captures the spontaneous reading of si-pusu but not the pretend-to
construction, where the subject acts with full intent. The agentive/patientive framing
(Kirikae 1994: 316, reported by sato2007 ) separates the forms at the
thematic role level, but feigning sleep — si-mokor-e pretend to sleep —
is agentive and falls outside the patientive characterisation. The argument-structural
restriction that Kobayashi (2008: 140) derives — si- occupies Theme or Patient
but never causee — correctly excludes causee arguments but does not explain the opposition
between si-pusu and yay-pusu , where both occupy a Theme role
kobayashi2010 .

The Handbook extends the empirical base with two additional minimal pairs:
yay-tuy-tuy-e brush dust off oneself versus
si-tuy-tuy-e shake off (while becoming a bird) — where the latter signals a
shedding of human clothes driven by the subject's transformation into bird form, an outward
change not self-initiated — and yay-etaye draw oneself outward 'draw
oneself out' versus si-etaye withdraw, pull back 'withdraw, pull back in
response to another's approach' bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

A discussion on the aynu-corpora Discord (nukopoli, aynucorporadiscord , 2024)
labels the contrast as anticausative ( si- ) versus reflexive proper
( yay- ), on the grounds that si- marks 'something that happens to the
subject through another force' and yay- marks 'the subject acting on itself
directly'. The labelling is consistent with the Satō analysis and invokes an explicit
anticausative category ‹contested›. A separate thread (aomidori_cs_,
aynucorporadiscord , 2023) proposed that one prefix may originally have encoded
a voice distinct from reflexive proper, without adducing corroborating evidence
‹speculative›.

sunku anakne peka ta si-pusu wa mom pe ne | Ezo.pine TOP surface LOC REFL-float.up CONJ flow NMLZ COP | The Ezo pine floats up to the water surface and flows along by itself. | si-pusu 'float up spontaneously' has no agentive subject; yay-pusu would require one. The absence of an A-set prefix confirms the intransitive output. | sato2007 | CHI | Shirasawa Nabe; Satō ex. 4 (approx. p. 36)

i=nukar rok pe si-etaye wa isam | 4.O=see PRF NMLZ REFL-withdraw CONJ not.exist | The thing that had caught sight of me withdrew and was gone. | si-etaye 'withdraw, pull back' contrasts with yay-etaye 'draw oneself outward'; the withdrawal here is triggered by the narrator's presence, the canonical indirect-reflexive context. The fourth-person object prefix i= on nukar references the narrator logophorically. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | Oda Steno, 'reyep kamuy'; ILCAA aa-irc/027#108

The reflexive-possessive function

When a transitive verb incorporates a body-part or closely possessed nominal,
si- can mark that nominal as belonging to the subject — the reflexive-possessive
function. The example cited by the Handbook is
si-etu-uyna hold one's own nose 'hold one's nose', where si-
supplies the possessor 'one's own' for the incorporated noun etu nose and
uyna hold something is thereby intransitivized
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . The parallel
yay- -possessive yay-par-oyki feed oneself; do to one's own mouth
— 'feed oneself', literally 'do to one's own mouth' — shows that both prefixes can supply
the possessor of an incorporated nominal, with the yay-/si- distinction projecting onto
the possessive domain in the same way it does onto the reflexive bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

The reflexive-possessive reading also surfaces obligatorily in locative contexts: when a
locative nominal's possessor is coreferential with the clause subject,
si- (or the applicative construction built around it) is required, and
substituting a non-coreferential possessor yields a distinct, non-reflexive reading
sato2008 251–253 .

In arity terms the function remains −1: the incorporated nominal fills one argument slot,
si- fills that nominal's possessor internally, and one external argument is
removed. The parallel in noun incorporation is the possessor-requiring subject type, where
the stranded possessor receives a reflexive interpretation by recovery from the clause
subject — the 'reflexive interpretation rescue' in Satō's markedness calculus, treated in
noun-incorporation-subject-possessor-stranding satoni2022 §3.3, §5 .

The 'pretend to' construction

si- combined with a causative suffix yields a 'pretend to V' reading.
Nakagawa (2024: 189) gives three examples with the productive suffix
-re : si-mokor-e feign sleep 'feign sleep',
si-kamuyne-re pretend to be a kamuy 'pretend to be a kamuy', and
si-ponpene-re act childishly 'act childishly'. With the direct-causative
-ka , Satō (2007: ≈36) gives
si-ranpewtek-ka pretend not to know 'pretend not to know'. In each
instance the state or appearance is externally attributed: sleep must be witnessed to be
believed, kamuy status requires another to be deceived, ignorance requires an observer to
accept it. The 'pretend to' reading is therefore a direct realisation of the
indirect-reflexive semantics of si- in a context of social inference.

ku=ko-si-ranpewtek-ka wa k=an | 1SG.A=APPL-REFL-know.nothing-CAUS CONJ IPFV | I was pretending not to know about it. | ko- is the applicative referencing what the subject ostensibly does not know; si- contributes the indirect-reflexive reading; -ka is the direct-causative suffix. Together si-ranpewtek-ka yields the pretend-to reading. The final k=an is a contracted progressive existential (kor an). | sato2007 | CHI | Satō ex. 6 (approx. p. 36)

The construction is limited to verbs of state or appearance assignable by an external
observer; verbs of direct physical action or creation do not enter it
nakagawa2024 189 .

Position in the verb template

In the slot ordering summarised in valency-voice-overview ,
si- precedes the root and any incorporated nominal but follows the applicative
prefixes. The ordering constraint with yay- is decisive:
yay-si- is attested — Nakagawa (2024: 189) cites
yay-si-turi-re lay oneself down while stretching 'lay oneself down while
stretching' — while the reverse *si-yay- is not
nakagawa2024 189 . This places si- closer to the root, in a
position Nakagawa compares to forming affixes.

yay-si-turi-re=an | REFL-REFL-stretch-CAUS=4.S | I lay down, stretching myself out. | The verb form yay-si-turi-re is attested in Nakagawa (2024: 189) to demonstrate the yay-outer / si-inner ordering; the surrounding clause is constructed. The fourth-person S suffix =an confirms that si-turi-re is intransitive.

The derivation chain illustrates the ordering across the arity calculus: the ditransitive
ko-i-ruska yields the intransitive
si-ko-i-ruska when si- is prefixed — it absorbs the
applicative complex's object slot while remaining to its left
nakagawa2024 210–211 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 . In
the polysynthetic example discussed in valency-voice-overview —
a=e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa — a second si- sits
immediately before the incorporated noun ram heart in the
reflexive-possessive reading ('one's own heart'), while yay- occupies the outer
layer at the applicative boundary. The yay-outer / si-inner hierarchy is visible in a
single stacked verb form shibatani1990 73 ;
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.1.1 .

title: The Reflexive yay-
summary: The reflexive prefix yay- 'self', its A/O coreference and detransitivization, and its autobenefactive/emotive extensions.
part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: The Reflexive yay-
Part: Part XI — Valency, Voice, and Noun Incorporation
Summary: The reflexive prefix yay- 'self', its A/O coreference and detransitivization, and its autobenefactive/emotive extensions.

The reflexive yay- in the valency system

The prefix yay- reflexive fills the object position of a transitive verb with
a participant coreferential with the subject, reducing arity by one and converting the base
transitive to an intransitive nakagawa2024 188 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . Placed in the first-person plural, a verb bearing yay- takes the
intransitive S-suffixes =as (exclusive) or =an
(fourth-person) rather than the transitive A-prefixes — the person-affix test of the
derivation described in valency-voice-overview
sato2008 96 ; nakagawa2024 137 . A sister prefix
si- indirect reflexive occupies the same template slot and carries the same
arity value; its own distribution and the two prefixes' contrast are treated below
and in reflexive-possessive-si .

Nakagawa lists yay- among the 名詞的接頭辞 (nominal prefixes) of the
verb-derivation chapter alongside si- , the reciprocal
u- reciprocal , the antipassive i- antipassive , and the
lexical body-region prefixes he- head of / ho- bottom of , all
of them arity −1 and all filling the object slot from the inside of the verb word
nakagawa2024 185–192 . antipassive-detransitive-i and
reciprocal-u develop the parallel operators. Ijäs's learner lessons (L3 and L9)
do not cover the reflexive prefixes, as she addresses them in later material
ijas2023 .

Morphological range and the possessive yay-

yay- is nearly unrestricted in base selection: it attaches to
transitives and ditransitives of any semantic class, and to derived transitives
(causativized or applicativized stems) nakagawa2024 188 . The prefix also
attaches to nominals, where arity is unchanged because a noun carries no object slot to
absorb — the reading is then possessive, 'one's own':
yay-kotan one's own village ( yay- +
kotan village ) is a representative form
nakagawa2024 188 .

A productive subtype is the quasi-possessive reflexive
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 : where an incorporated body-part noun is interpreted as belonging to the subject,
yay- supplies the possessor rather than filling the object slot
directly. In yay-par-oyki feed oneself (literally 'do about one's own
mouth'), par mouth is incorporated as the object of
oyki do about, handle , while yay- identifies the
subject as the possessor of that mouth. The arity is still reduced by one because the
incorporated noun absorbs the object slot; the reflexive element determines whose body
part is implicated. This intersects with the object-incorporation system treated in
noun-incorporation-object .

The accent of yay- is stable: the prefix carries primary stress
as yáy- regardless of the following segment, unless lexicalization
has displaced it nakagawa2024 188 . Forms such as
yáyesiru confirm that the accent falls on
yáy even before a vowel-initial stem; this accent pattern is
corroborated by community corpus observation (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024,
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-confirmed›.

si- is more restricted than yay- in base
selection, and when both co-occur the ordering is fixed:
yay-si-turi-re 'lay oneself down stretching' places
yay- outside si- , while the reverse
*si-yay- is unattested nakagawa2024 189 .
Nakagawa infers that si- sits closer to the root, behaving more
like a forming element than an independent prefix.

Self-affecting verb classes: grooming, emotion, and self-transformation

Three semantic fields attract yay- most productively. Body-treatment
and grooming verbs form the first: yay-kara treat oneself, attend to oneself
builds on kara treat, handle , the same verb that underlies
cise-kar build a house and related compounds
(see denominal-deverbal-verb-derivation ). Community corpus work independently
documents -kar as productive for 'handle/treat' operations —
nimawe-kar (treat with a bark compress),
osoro-kar — making yay-kara 'treat
oneself' the expected reflexive of that class (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024,
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹corpus-suggested›. A rolling-about verb in the Saru
narrative corpus shows causative stacking on a grooming-motion base:

asinuma ka nusa sam ta yay-karkarse-re=an kor paraparak | 1SG.EMPH even altar beside LOC REFL-roll-CAUS=4.S CONJ wail | asinuma ka nusa sam ta yaykarkarsere=an kor paraparak | I too rolled about by the altar, wailing. | yay-karkarse-re 'roll oneself / roll about': yay- fills the object slot of karkarse-re 'cause to roll (something)'; the fourth-person S suffix =an on the derived intransitive confirms the detransitivization. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話11; ILCAA materials aa-irc/013#377

A community decomposition of yayhonokka 'educate, discipline' as
yay-ho-nok-ka (REFL-bottom.body.region-CAUS) would show
yay- stacking over the body-region prefix
ho- and a causative suffix, with cross-dialectal support from Saru
and Shizunai forms
( ku=yayhonokka ; ci=honokka ;
taan hanokka )
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2026, aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›.
The decomposition has not been verified against the analytic literature.

Emotion and mental-state verbs make up a second field. Ainu transitive verbs of feeling
take a stimulus as their object; yay- reflexivizes that position,
yielding an intransitive of self-directed affect. The minimal pair
yay-rayke 'commit suicide' vs
si-rayke 'be destroyed by misfortune', both from
rayke kill , shows how the same base divides between the two prefixes; the
opposition is examined in the yay-/si- section below
nakagawa2024 188–189 .

Self-transformation verbs form the third class. With kar make, do , the
prefix yields yay-kar 'transform oneself, take on a different form':

aynu ne yay-kar wa kamuy ko-sunke wa an ru-we ne | human COP REFL-make CONJ deity APPL-lie CONJ exist track-POSS COP | He had taken on human form and was deceiving the deity. | yay-kar 'transform oneself': yay- fills the object slot of kar 'make', turning the transitive 'make X into Y' into an intransitive of self-directed change. an in an ruwe ne is the existential/copular verb 'be (in that state)', not a person suffix. | bugaevakobayashi2022:§4.1.1 | SAR | Nakagawa corpus K8007292UP; Bugaeva & Kobayashi ex. 4b

The yay-/si- distinction: competing analyses

The functional boundary between yay- and
si- has produced several accounts that remain in tension
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 ; nakagawa2024 187–189 ;
sato2007 . The four most developed positions are set out in the table below;
the sources for the first two and third are cited second-hand through the works that
survey them.

analysis
yay-
si-
source(s)

intentional vs unintentional
intentional, volitional action on self
unintentional; self undergoes without volition
Kindaichi (1931), Tamura (1988a, 1996: 622) — via
nakagawa2024 188 ; sato2007

agentive vs patientive self
agentive self: subject acts on itself
patientive self: subject undergoes the action
Kirikae (1994: 316) — via bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

Theme/Patient restriction
no restriction on the role of the reflexive participant
reflexive participant must be Theme or Patient; causee impossible
Kobayashi (2008: 140) — via bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

direct vs indirect reflexive
subject acts directly on itself, without mediation
subject is involved only indirectly: through others, through circumstance, or through social perception
sato2007 ; adopted by nakagawa2024 189 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2

Satō's direct/indirect account, developed for Chitose, is adopted by Nakagawa and the
Handbook as the current best analysis ‹contested›
sato2007 ; nakagawa2024 189 ; bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . It holds that yay- marks an action the subject performs
directly on itself, without requiring any mediating agent or social condition.
si- marks a situation where the subject's involvement is indirect:
the outcome is achieved through another participant's action, through circumstances
beyond the subject's control, or through a state that holds only because others
perceive it as holding.

The canonical Chitose minimal pair is yay-pusu 'pop up by
swimming (volitionally)' vs si-pusu 'float of itself
(spontaneously)', both from pusu float something up
sato2007 . The si- form applies to a physical property
of spruce:

sunku anakne peka ta si-pusu wa mom pe ne | spruce TOP surface LOC REFL-float CONJ drift thing COP | Spruce floats on the water and drifts — that is its nature. | si-pusu 'float of itself': buoyancy is a property of the wood, not a directed self-act. The spruce has no volition, showing that the si-/yay- distinction is not intentional vs unintentional but direct vs indirect involvement. The contrast with yay-pusu 'pop up by swimming' is Satō's primary minimal pair. | sato2007 | CHI | Satō ex. 4; narrator 白沢ナベ, Chitose

The si- class also covers a productive 'pretend' construction:
si-mokor-e 'feign sleep',
si-kamuy-ne-re 'pretend to be a deity',
si-ranpewtek-ka 'pretend to know nothing'
nakagawa2024 189 ; sato2007 . In each case the subject's
performed state depends on how others perceive her — a condition that cannot hold
without an audience, hence the indirect marking:

ku=ko-si-ranpewtek-ka wa ki=an | 1SG.A=APPL-REFL-know-CAUS CONJ do=4.S | I was pretending to know nothing about it. | ko- promotes the object-of-pretence to applied object; si- marks that the unknowing-state depends on others' belief; -ka causativizes the resulting stative. ki=an (do=4.S) marks the ongoing activity as intransitive, confirming the final vi status of the complex. | sato2007 | CHI | Satō ex. 6; Chitose

From Nakagawa, a parallel emotional pair: yay-rayke 'commit
suicide' (direct, deliberate self-directed action) vs si-rayke
'be destroyed, come to ruin (by misfortune)' (the outcome is brought about by external
forces) nakagawa2024 188–189 . Bugaeva and Kobayashi add
yay-etaye 'draw oneself out (actively)' vs
si-etaye 'withdraw, pull oneself back in (in response to
something)' bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 . The Shizunai corpus
supplies an attested instance of si-etaye where the withdrawal
is triggered by being seen:

i=nukar rok pe si-etaye wa isam | 4.O=see PRF thing REFL-withdraw CONJ be.absent | On seeing me, it withdrew and was gone. | si-etaye: withdrawal triggered by the act of being seen — an externally caused, indirect involvement of the subject in its own motion. Its yay- counterpart yay-etaye 'draw oneself out' involves no external trigger. | ilcaa1976 | SHI | Oda Suteno, reyep kamuy; ILCAA materials aa-irc/027#108

Satō himself notes that the direct/indirect analysis has not reached consensus
( 定説ではない ) sato2007 ; nakagawa2024 189 . The earlier intentional/unintentional framing (Kindaichi, Tamura) remains in
pedagogical use ‹contested›. A community reformulation characterizes
yay- as 'by one's own power' (reflexive) and
si- as 'happens to / gets through others' (anticausative), with
yaykaopiwki 'save oneself by one's own action' cited as a
diagnostic (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024, aynucorporadiscord )
‹contested›. This formulation is consistent with Satō's account while aligning the
si- end with anticausative typology. The hypothesis that the
two prefixes represent historically distinct voice categories rather than two subtypes
of reflexive (aomidori_cs_, aynu-corpora Discord 2023, aynucorporadiscord )
‹speculative› — no diachronic evidence bearing on a pre-reflexive function of either
prefix has been identified in the sources consulted for this grammar. Bugaeva and
Kobayashi note that no other reported language encodes the distinction between
dependence-on-others vs absence-of-such-dependence through a pair of reflexive
prefixes, making the Ainu opposition typologically unusual
bugaevakobayashi2022 §4.2.2 .

The full treatment of si- — including its unaccusative uses,
its possessive-reflexive function with incorporated body-part nouns
( si-etu-uyna 'hold one's own nose'), and the pretend-to
constructions — is in reflexive-possessive-si .

Stacking with applicatives and the verb-word template

yay- combines freely with the applicative prefixes
e- , ko- , and
o- (see applicative-system-overview ). Since an
applicative raises arity by one (+1) and yay- lowers it by
one (−1), their combination on a transitive base preserves the original transitivity
while shifting which object position holds the reflexive participant. On a base that
already has an outer applicative-object, yay- fills the inner
original-object slot with 'self'; the outer applicative slot remains available for an
external NP.

The combination ko-yay-irayke 'be grateful to someone for
something one has experienced oneself' is attested in the Saru narrative corpus — here
ko- promotes the deity as the addressee-goal, and
yay- fills the inner object of
irayke be grateful with the subject:

petkorkamuy kamuy katkemat a=ko-yay-irayke hi a=ye a a=ye a kor | river.deity deity woman 4.A=APPL-REFL-be.grateful NMLZ 4.A=say PFV 4.A=say PFV CONJ | petkorkamuy kamuy katkemat a=koyayrayke hi a=ye a a=ye a kor | Again and again giving thanks to the river-deity woman, as she was telling it … | Surface koyayrayke: the phonological sequence yay-irayke surfaces as yayrayke at the morpheme boundary (dialectal and orthographic variants yayirayke ~ yairayke reflect the same junction). ko- = addressee applicative; yay- = reflexive on the inner object slot. | ilcaa1976 | SAR | Kawakami Matsuko, uwepeker 民話9; ILCAA materials aa-irc/009#289

The derivational chain through ruska be angry at demonstrates the
calculus across further steps: ruska (vt) →
ko-ruska (vd, 'be angry toward') →
yay-ko-ruska (vt, 'be angry at oneself') →
i-ruska (vi, 'be angry', object defocused) — each step
changes arity by exactly one unit nakagawa2024 212 ; sato2023b 40 ‹consensus›. The antipassive i- in the same chain
is treated in antipassive-detransitive-i .

The verb template places reflexive and applicative prefixes inside the person-index
layer and outside the root: person-index — reflexive/applicative — root —
forming-suffix — causative — person-index
nakagawa2024 210–212 . Two reflexive prefixes and two applicatives can
co-occur on a single root, as in the showcase word
a=e-yay-ko-tuyma-si-ram-suy-pa discussed in
valency-voice-overview template
shibatani1990 73 ; bugaeva2014 .
A community-proposed decomposition of
yaykipniwkes as yay-ki-p-niwkes —
with ki 'do' as a light verb and
niwkes as the state predicate (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord
2024, aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested› — illustrates the opaque prefix
stacking that occurs in this zone; the analysis awaits verification against primary
morphological descriptions. The reciprocal u- , which parallels
yay- in its arity value while filling both S and O slots
simultaneously, is in reciprocal-u .

title: Relational and Spatial (Locative) Nouns and Their Possessed Forms
summary: The obligatorily-possessed relational/spatial noun system (or, ka, corpok, sam, tum...), the first-/second-class locative split, the affiliative forms of these nouns, and or's drift toward a general locative; a bridge to the postpositional case system.
part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Relational and Spatial (Locative) Nouns and Their Possessed Forms
Part: Part VIII — Pronouns, Demonstratives, Postpositions, and Numerals
Summary: The obligatorily-possessed relational/spatial noun system (or, ka, corpok, sam, tum...), the first-/second-class locative split, the affiliative forms of these nouns, and or's drift toward a general locative; a bridge to the postpositional case system.

Summary: The single rhotic phoneme (flap [ɾ]) with positional allophones, and coda /r/ as the most morphophonologically active segment of the language.

— chapter in preparation —

title: ruwe ne — the Inferential / Visual-Trace Evidential
summary: The default assertive-evidential ruwe ne ('it is the trace/fact that'), marking inference from evidence or established fact, the most frequent and most grammaticalized term of the paradigm.
part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: ruwe ne — the Inferential / Visual-Trace Evidential
Part: Part XV — Evidentiality and Mirativity
Summary: The default assertive-evidential ruwe ne ('it is the trace/fact that'), marking inference from evidence or established fact, the most frequent and most grammaticalized term of the paradigm.

Place in the evidential paradigm

Hokkaido Ainu marks the speaker's source of information periphrastically: a finite clause is
nominalized by a bound formal noun and the result is predicated with the copula
ne . The language has no dedicated evidential verbal affix. Four formal
nouns build the core paradigm, each
the possessed form of a sensory noun: ruwe (< ru track, path ), siri (< sir visible appearance, land, weather ),
hawe (< haw voice ), and
humi (< hum sound, feeling )
nakagawa2024 258, 392 ; hirosawa2026 64 . Mapped onto the
typology of aikhenvald2004 63–65 , the system is of the
C1 type — visual, non-visual sensory, inferred, and reported — realized analytically,
through a native nominalization-plus-copula construction
hirosawa2026 64 ; takahashi2013 129 . The architecture common to
all four terms is set out in evidential-schema-overview ; the present chapter
treats the central member ruwe ne , and its siblings in
siri-ne-situational the situational evidential ,
hawe-ne-reportative the reportative , and
humi-ne-sensory the sensory evidential . The closed class of formal nouns that
makes the construction possible is defined in formal-defective-nouns .

formal noun
possessed morphology
source noun
information source

ruwe
ru-we ‘the trace of —’
ru track, path
fact recognized through knowledge, reasoning, or an assimilated trace

siri
sir-i
sir appearance, land
directly visible situation

hawe
haw-e
haw voice
verbal report or hearsay

humi
hum-i
hum sound, feeling
non-visual sensation

Dal Corso resolves these four direct terms into an eight-form system whose indirect
counterparts replace the copula with the existential an , the verb
ki ‘do’, or as ‘stand’, organized by two
parameters, source reliability and event accessibility ; within it
ruwe ne is the highest-reliability direct form, and the trace-noun
ru forms direct evidentiality alone — the southern dialects have no
indirect *ru -counterpart to siri an
dalcorso2018 304, 309, 337 .

ruwe ne also dominates the paradigm quantitatively. In Dal Corso's
Hokkaido reference texts it supplies 1508 of 2232 evidential tokens — roughly two thirds of the
eight-form system, and far ahead of hawe ne ,
siri ne , and humi ne dalcorso2018 72 . The present Hokkaido corpus of 167,890 sentences makes the skew sharper. A string search
returns 16,350 tokens of ruwe ne beside 796 hawe ne ,
607 siri ne , and 200 humi ne among the direct
forms, and 304 siri an , 395 hawe as ,
210 humi as , and 218 siri ki among the indirect:
some 86% of the eight forms together, and about 91% of the four direct ones (the two corpora are
compared below). The string count folds in the homophonous lexical noun
ruwe and the reduced final particle, so 86% is an upper bound on the
evidential proper. The asymmetry is far too large to follow from that conflation, and it confirms
the descriptive consensus that ruwe ne is the unmarked, most
grammaticalized term of the four ‹corpus-confirmed›.

Morphology: ruwe is ru-we, ‘the trace of —’

The head ruwe is the
concept-form-affiliative-form possessed form of the noun
ru track, path . In Tamura's Saru dictionary the ordinary possessed form is
ruwe(he) ‘the track that — passed along, the path made by —’s passing’,
and the evidential formal noun is analysed as ru-e , the noun plus a
possessive ending tamura1996 s.v. ru, ruwe . For glossing this is decisive: the
morpheme is the possessed ru-we , ‘the trace of —’, whose implicit
possessor is the event described by the nominalized clause. The same
possessed-noun pattern underlies sir-i ,
haw-e , and hum-i nakagawa2024 258, 390 . A subtle point distinguishes the evidential from the full noun: the lexical possessed forms
may carry an extra ending ( ruwehe , humihi ),
whereas the evidential formal nouns block it ( *ruwehe is ungrammatical
as the evidential) tamura1996 s.v. ruwe .

The construction is therefore a copular predication over a nominalized clause —
[clause] ruwe ‘the trace/fact that [clause]’, closed by the copula
ne treated in copula-ne-predicate-nominals . It is not,
however, an ordinary relative-clause head plus a copula. The head has lost the syntax of a free
noun: it admits no demonstrative or attributive modifier of its own (contrast a true lexical
head such as pirka menoko ‘a beautiful woman’), it cannot head a common
noun ( *pirka ruwe ne kewtum ), and it is fixed in linear position —
kusu ne ruwe ne is well formed but *ruwe ne kusu ne
is not nakagawa2024 393 ; dalcorso2018 24–25 . The accurate
synchronic label is thus a partially grammaticalized formal-noun evidential,
intermediate between a fused verbal suffix and a fully lexical noun, and it is best analysed
alongside the
formal-noun nominalization treated in lexical-head-formal-noun-nominalization and
the general clausal nominalizers of nominalization-p-pe ‹consensus›.

eci=ekanok kusu ek=an ru-we ne | 2PL.O=meet PURP come=4.S track-POSS COP | I came to meet you. | Dal Corso ex. 21. The indefinite/fourth person =an serves as the narrative first person; ek=an ruwe ne presents the speaker's own arrival as an established fact, the reasoning-based direct value (DIR.RSN) in Dal Corso's terms. | dalcorso2018:24 | CHI | Ito Oda, Chitose; Dal Corso siglum BUG:126

Meaning: fact from knowledge, not raw perception

Four framings of ruwe ne compete, and they are best read as describing
one form from different angles. The oldest, the mood tradition, classed
ruwe as the 確説法 / 事実法
‘assertive / fact mood’, with Chiri deriving it diachronically from ‘there is a trace of X’ to
‘there is the fact of X’ kindaichi1936 ; hirosawa2026 65–68 . The
evidential framing, now standard, retains a source semantics: Nakagawa defines
ruwe ne as marking that the speaker recognizes the clause content
as fact nakagawa2024 392 , and Dal Corso treats it as a direct
evidential of reasoning or knowledge (glossed dir.rsn /
dir.knw ), the highest-reliability direct source. This reliability is a
matter of source, not of epistemic modality: it names the psychophysical or cognitive channel
of acquisition and says nothing about the speaker's degree of certainty, and the original
stimulus is typically backgrounded once the information has been assimilated into personal
knowledge dalcorso2018 305, 327, 335, 339 .

Satō's question data refine what that channel is. Among the four nominalizers,
ruwe applies where the relevant content is an abstract state of affairs,
recognized only indirectly through the exercise of understanding — the domain left once
hawe takes an utterance, siri a visible ongoing
scene, and humi a sensation sato2008 134–135 . He further
notes that the very fact that these nominalizers stand in the possessed form
presupposes an antecedent — a possessed noun needs something to belong to — so the form
grammatically encodes that the speaker already has the relevant situation in hand
sato2008 135 . The diagnostic case is a predicate whose truth no sense can
deliver:

tan cise e=uni ne ru-we | this house 2SG=home COP track-POSS | Is this house yours? | Whether a house ‘is one's own’ is an abstract relation, not a perceptible state; hence ruwe, not the visual siri. The bare clause-final ruwe is the polar-interrogative reduction discussed in the section on frames. | sato2008:131, 135 | CHI

The fourth framing is Hirosawa's, who relocates ruwe ne into
information structure. He argues it marks discourse- new information much as Japanese
のだ does, and more generally declares the content to lie inside the
speaker's territory of information (情報のなわ張り); since ruwe
references processed knowledge (知識), this reconciles its two
apparently opposite uses — introducing brand-new information and ‘summing up’ (前方統括)
information already developed in the discourse hirosawa2026 75–81, 89 . The
self-identification below involves no inference and no perceptual source at all; the sea-god
simply supplies his identity to the protagonists as new information:

atuy kor kamuy a=ne hine an=an ru-we ne | sea have god 4.A=COP CONJ exist=4.S track-POSS COP | I am the god who holds the sea. | Hirosawa ex. 16. The fourth-person transitive prefix a= marks the copula and the suffix =an the existential; a self-identification packaging discourse-new information for the addressee. | hirosawa2026:77 | SAR | Wateke, Tamura audio materials 05-02 ‘Folktale 1’: 6

The same mechanism lets a narrator assert events of the story world that she could not have
witnessed, presenting them under ruwe ne as traditional, shared
knowledge — the use that makes it the default predicate of the narrative frame-text
dalcorso2018 331 ; hirosawa2026 75 :

pananpe an penanpe an hine sir-an pe ne ru-we ne hike | Pananpe exist Penanpe exist CONJ appearance-exist thing COP track-POSS COP although | There were Panampe and Penampe, and … | Hirosawa ex. 13; the storyteller's frame-text presents a story-world state of affairs under ruwe ne as established narrative fact. | hirosawa2026:75 | SAR | Wateke, Tamura audio materials 05-02 ‘Folktale 1’: 4

These accounts are not interchangeable, and at one point they genuinely conflict: Hirosawa
builds his analysis on territory-of-information, whereas Dal Corso explicitly argues that the
territory-of-information theory is inapplicable to Hokkaido direct evidentiality
dalcorso2018 335–337 ; hirosawa2026 89 ‹contested›. The
synthesis adopted here is that ruwe ne grammatically encodes
epistemic incorporation : the clause is predicated as a possessed trace
( ru-we , ‘the trace of —’) whose possessor is the described event, so
that the proposition is offered as content already taken into the speaker's knowledge and
thereby made assertable. This single device derives both Dal Corso's
observation that the stimulus is backgrounded once information enters personal knowledge and
Hirosawa's that the form marks new information and claims it for the speaker's territory:
incorporation is exactly what lets a freshly perceived, inferred, remembered, or just-narrated
state of affairs be presented as a fact the speaker now owns. The possessed morphology — which,
as Satō notes, presupposes an antecedent — is the formal anchor of this account, and it
explains why the older ‘fact mood’ label, the modern source label, and the discourse-new label
each capture a true facet of the same form ‹original-needs-review›.

The ruwe ne / siri ne puzzle

The hardest boundary in the system is between ruwe ne and
siri ne , because both can rest on what the speaker sees. Dal Corso states
plainly that a sense-channel hierarchy does not predict the split: if both are ‘visual’, it
fails to say why Ainu so often chooses the trace-form where sight is available, and he leaves
the question open, suspecting a driver beyond elicitation dalcorso2018 311–312, 335–336 . Hirosawa's response is to separate raw sight from processed knowledge —
siri profiles the visible situation, ruwe the
knowledge the situation yields — but he offers this as an explicit hypothesis (仮説), not a
settled result hirosawa2026 81, 89 ‹contested›.

The double-evidential distribution gives a positive argument that resolves part of the puzzle.
When two evidentials stack, the indirect visual siri an is reinforced
specifically by ruwe ne , and not by siri ne
dalcorso2018 326–327, 331–333 . Were ruwe and
siri two co-equal visual channels this would be unmotivated; it follows
naturally if ruwe is not a fourth sense parallel to sight, sound, and
speech, but a higher knowledge-from-trace operator that ratifies the output of another
evidential as established fact. On this reading Dal Corso's unresolved ‘why
ruwe and not siri when sight is available’
dalcorso2018 311–312 and Takahashi's Tokachi finding that individual-level —
cognitively stable — properties take ru while transient scenes take
sir takahashi2013 131–133 become two faces of one
contrast: siri profiles the accessible appearance, and
ruwe the cognized fact extracted from it ‹original-needs-review›. The
boundary with hawe is similarly non-rigid — Hirosawa cites near-identical
sentences alternating hawe ne and ruwe ne , which
fits a form that can absorb information of any original channel once it has become knowledge
hirosawa2026 81 .

Clause-final frames and illocution

ruwe heads a small family of sentence-final frames that vary the
following predicate and particle. Nakagawa arranges them into a paradigm shared by all four
formal nouns: NMLZ + ne declarative,
NMLZ + an content question (with a question word),
bare NMLZ polar question and exclamation, and
NMLZ + un affirmative answer nakagawa2024 392–396 . The
bare polar reduction is an instance of insubordination — a once-dependent nominalized clause
used as a main clause — analysed for the Tokachi cognate by takahashi2013 132 . These interrogative uses belong with the wider interrogative system in
interrogative-strategies-question-particles , and the existential frame with
existential-locational-an-oka the existential an .

frame
value
note

ruwe ne
declarative, recognized fact
formal noun + copula; may take a , nankor , and final particles

ruwe an?
content question
existential frame with a question word; also expanded ruwe an hi an?

ruwe?
polar question / exclamation
reduced, insubordinated frame; Saru reinforced ruwe he an?

ruwe un
affirmative answer
un is a final particle, not the evidential itself

ruwe tapan
formal / ceremonial assertion
register-marked frame in Tamura's dictionary, not a separate category

ruwe somo ne
rhetorical / convictional
outer frame, distinct from inner-scope somo negation

The content question replaces the copula with the existential and is opened by a question word,
the formal noun staying in place:

mak an pe kusu e=moyre a ru-we an | how exist NMLZ because 2SG.S=be.late PFV track-POSS exist | Why were you late? | Dal Corso ex. 124. The wh-expression mak an pe kusu ‘for what reason’ opens the clause and the existential an replaces the copula; the perfective a closes the embedded predicate before ruwe. | dalcorso2018:143 | SAR | Wateke, Saru; Dal Corso siglum TMA: 12

ku=nu ru-we un | 1SG.A=hear track-POSS FIN | Yes, I heard it. | The final particle un forms the affirmative-answer frame while the formal noun is retained. | nakagawa2024:396 | HK | Nakagawa ex. 794

Negation belongs inside the evidential: the proposition is denied by negating the
verb before the formal noun (the pattern … somo ki ruwe ne ‘it is a fact
that [one does not …]’), which leaves ruwe ne itself outside the scope of
negation. The rhetorical ruwe somo ne is a separate outer frame, a
high-scope convictional construction distinct from this inner propositional negation
nakagawa2024 393 . The interaction of the evidential with the
sentence-final negation and TAM apparatus on a copular predicate is treated in
nonverbal-predicate-tam-negation .

nep eci=e-sirkirap ka somo ki ru-we ne na | something 2PL.A=APPL-worry even NEG do track-POSS COP FIN | You will not be troubled by anything. | Dal Corso ex. 128. Negation sits inside the evidential: somo ki denies the verb sirkirap, while ruwe ne stays outside the scope of negation and predicates the whole as recognized fact. | dalcorso2018:145 | SAR | Kaizawa Tsurushino, Biratori; Dal Corso siglum KAY: 2-2,18

Scope over TAM and the finiteness of the embedded clause

The constituent before ruwe ne is a finite, clause-sized predication. It
carries its own person marking, number-sensitive verb choice
(singular ek vs. suppletive plural arki ‘come’,
so íne nispa ek ruwe ne ‘four men came’), aspect and perfective
auxiliaries, and negation; the evidential then scopes over that whole TAM-marked clause
nakagawa2024 393 . This is also why ruwe ne can close a
non-verbal predicate, as in the copular kamuy a=ne … ruwe ne example
above. Ordering confirms the layering: the evidential may be followed by aspectual and clausal
material ( a , nankor , na ,
wa , hine , kusu ), and it
sits above clause-internal aspect but below the clause-linkers, so
kusu ne ruwe ne is grammatical and *ruwe ne kusu ne
is not nakagawa2024 393 .

mat-kor hine oka=an ru-we ne a p | wife-have CONJ exist.PL=4.S track-POSS COP PFV NMLZ | he had taken a wife, and we went on living — and yet, | Dal Corso ex. 170 (excerpt). The perfective a follows ruwe ne in the post-evidential slot, and the nominalizer p carries the adversative ‘but’ that turns the narrative — confirming that aspect and clause-linking material attach above the evidential. | dalcorso2018:196 | SAR | Wateke, Saru; Dal Corso siglum TMA: 2

Dal Corso ties the choice of post-nominal predicate to perspective: the copula
ne expresses an internal (speaker-anchored) viewpoint and so hosts the
direct forms, while the existential and posture/action verbs an ,
as , and ki express an external viewpoint and host
the indirect forms dalcorso2018 313–315 . For ruwe the
paradigm is asymmetrical — in the southern dialects there is no indirect
*ru form — so ruwe ne functions as the direct,
personal-knowledge anchor of the whole system dalcorso2018 309, 326 . What the
accessible sources do not settle is the formal size of the nominalized clause: that it
is finite is clear from its TAM and person marking, but no read source provides a constituency
account that would decide between a finite nominalization, a nominalized predicate phrase, and a
construction-specific evidential projection ‹speculative›. The complementation side of the same
formal-noun strategy — ruwe and hi clauses under
perception and cognition verbs — is taken up in nominalized-complements-control-raising .

Predication type: the Tokachi constraint

Takahashi's Tokachi study adds a factor outside the reach of both the mood tradition and a pure
source typology. In Tokachi the visual nominalizer sir is restricted to
imperfective, stage-level (transient) predication: it presents a passing scene. For an
individual-level (permanent) property — even one known by sight — Tokachi instead uses
ru , which is unmarked for information type. Of the four, only
sir carries this restriction; hum ,
haw , and ru work at both levels
takahashi2013 131–133 . The constraint surfaces on the nominalizer itself, which
Takahashi parallels with the ban on individual-level complements of English perception verbs.

taan imo siwnin ru an | this potato be.green track exist | This potato is green. | In Tokachi the bare concept form ru (not the possessed ruwe) closes the clause, and the existential an replaces the copula. An individual-level property takes ru even though it is visually accessible, because sir is barred from permanent properties. | takahashi2013:131–133 | TOK | Honbetsu; consultant Sawai Tomeno

For the present form this matters in two ways. First, the ru domain is
broader than ‘inference’ narrowly understood: it is the unmarked choice where
sir is blocked by predication type, which is consistent with the
knowledge-from-trace reading developed above. Second, it shows the Hokkaido system to be
organized partly by the ontology of the predicate — eventive, stage-level, individual-level —
alongside source of information; this does not overturn Dal Corso's reliability/accessibility
account but bounds its domain, marking where the grammar of predication independently
constrains the available evidential ‹consensus›.

Mirativity and double evidentiality

ruwe ne is often rendered ‘it turned out that …’ in surprising contexts,
but the surprise is not contributed by ruwe . Mirativity in Hokkaido Ainu
is carried mainly by the perfective auxiliary aan and by
anan ; ruwe ne then packages the resulting
proposition as fact hirosawa2026 90 . Dal Corso is explicit that Hokkaido shows
no evidence of the evidential paradigm itself systematically generating mirative meanings — so
mirativity may co-occur with ruwe ne but is not its value
dalcorso2018 336 ‹contested›. The system-level treatment of the
evidential-to-mirative interface, evidential stacking, and the cline to sentence-final particles
is given in mirativity-scope-grammaticalization .

supuya at kor sir-an ru-we ne anan | smoke rise while appearance-exist track-POSS COP MIR | Smoke was rising, it turned out! | Dal Corso ex. 268: the indirect visual siri an is layered under ruwe ne, with the admirative tail anan glossed mirative here. The supporting form is systematically ruwe ne, never siri ne. | dalcorso2018:327 | HK | Nakagawa text, Dal Corso siglum NGF:176

Double evidentiality, more frequent in Hokkaido than Sakhalin, confirms the special standing of
ruwe ne argued for above. Two patterns recur: a character's indirect
source layered under the narrator's ruwe ne as shared knowledge
( … hawe as ruwe ne ), and ruwe ne reinforcing a
low-reliability direct form ( … hawe ne nankor ruwe ne )
dalcorso2018 331–333 . In both, ruwe ne is the member
that can ratify an already evidentially marked proposition as established fact, behaving as a
knowledge operator that scopes over the other evidentials.

Diachrony, dialect microvariation, and a Sakhalin contrast

The diachronic path is transparent and is stated already in the earliest grammars: the noun
ru ‘track, path’ > the possessed formal noun
ru-we ‘the trace of —’ > the clause-final evidential
[clause] ruwe ne , part of the larger cline by which
sir , hum , haw , and
ru all move from sensory nouns to nominalizers to evidentials
tamura1996 s.v. ruwe ; hirosawa2026 65 . Dal Corso stresses that
the result is only partially grammaticalized — the lexical source stays visible and the
choice of post-nominal predicate still matters — so grammaticalization here is a matter of
degree dalcorso2018 24–25 ; takahashi2018 111 .

The endpoint of that cline is reached unevenly across Hokkaido, and the differences are
morphosyntactic: a single semantic field realized by varied forms, which cautions against
writing one morphology for all of Hokkaido ‹contested›:

Saru, Chitose, Biratori — the southern core of Dal Corso's data — keep the
possessed forms ruwe / siri / hawe / humi and the eight-form
direct/indirect system dalcorso2018 304–337 .

Tokachi (and frequently Ishikari) close the clause with the bare concept
forms ru / sir / haw / hum instead of the possessed forms
nakagawa2024 110, 390 ; takahashi2013 129–131 .

Horobetsu grammaticalizes the possessed-form nominalizers a step further,
into adverbial-clause and conjunction-like markers — a ‘versatile nominalization’ use not
reported for well-described Saru takahashi2018 107–112 .

Asahikawa and Shiranuka have an inferential ru an ,
apparently in complementary distribution with the siri an of the
south — a genuine exception to the generalization that ru forms direct
evidentiality alone dalcorso2018 326 .

The Sakhalin reflex. The Sakhalin varieties split the trace-form where the
southern Hokkaido system has a single member. Beside the copular
ruwehe ne(e) they carry an existential ruwehe ʔan ,
and the two divide one inferential field by reliability: the existential encodes inference from a
less-processed visual stimulus, the copular form inference from a stimulus already worked through,
the more reliable of the pair dalcorso2018 281–284 . Southern Hokkaido has only
ruwe ne and admits no existential *ruwe an ; the
ru an of Asahikawa and Shiranuka is the one Hokkaido locus where the
trace-noun enters the existential frame, and Dal Corso reads it as the Hokkaido counterpart of
Sakhalin ruwehe ʔan dalcorso2018 326, 336 . The split sits
within a wider reorganization of nominalization in the Sakhalin variety
bugaeva2016 .

pon nay ohta i-huraye ruwe-he ne | be.small river LOC 4.O-wash track-POSS COP | She washed me in a small river. | Dal Corso ex. 224. The copular ruwehe ne(e) presents a fully processed, personally witnessed event — the more reliable counterpart of the existential ruwehe ʔan; the indefinite-person object i- here references the narrator. East Sakhalin keeps the possessed -he that the southern Hokkaido evidential drops (ruwe-he, not ru-we). | pilsudski1912; dalcorso2018:281 | SA | Piłsudski corpus; Dal Corso siglum PLA: 227

tan husko karauto an ruwe-he an manuy | this be.old box exist track-POSS exist REP | There was this old box. | there is a trace that this old box is there | Dal Corso ex. 226. The inferential is built on the existential an over a clause that itself ends in existential an; the literal ‘there is a trace of —’ stays transparent. Southern Hokkaido lacks this existential ruwehe ʔan and uses only the copular ruwe ne. | pilsudski1912; dalcorso2018:284 | SA | Piłsudski corpus; Dal Corso siglum PLA: 200

title: Sequential wa and Clause Chaining
summary: The default connective wa 'and (then)', multi-clause event chains, and the culminative linker aine, disambiguated from the homophonous resultative/perfect wa.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Sequential wa and Clause Chaining
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The default connective wa 'and (then)', multi-clause event chains, and the culminative linker aine, disambiguated from the homophonous resultative/perfect wa.

Overview

The particle wa and (then), so (connective) is the most versatile and most
frequent of Hokkaido Ainu's conjunctive particles. Its core function, in Tamura's formulation
as transmitted by Nakagawa, is to gather two events E1 and E2 into one coherent action and
present them as a single sentence-unit
nakagawa2024 342 . Where hine merely lists events in
temporal order, wa asserts a closer bond: the first event supplies the
result-state, the manner, or the cause that licenses the second. The three semantic readings
share the surface form and overlap in natural speech; the context, the verb's aktionsart, and
the presence of reinforcing adverbs (such as the sequence-marker
orowa(no) ) determine which reading is primary in any given clause pair
nakagawa2024 339–342 ; sato2008 45 . The system as a whole is
laid out in clause-linkage-overview ; the present chapter treats
wa and its culminative companion ayne
specifically.

Three semantic readings

Satō identifies three uses of wa : sequential continuation (often
reinforced by orowa 'and then'), cause-to-effect (reinforced by
kusu 'therefore'), and attendant circumstance or manner
sato2008 45 . Nakagawa adds a unifying temporal criterion: the first VP must be
at its result-state stage — the action has concluded and that result-state frames E2 — rather
than ongoing (simultaneity, kor ) or stative-negated (absence of change,
no ) nakagawa2024 341 .

particle
phase of first VP
core reading
minimal example

wa
result-state after an action
sequence; manner; cause–effect

ipe wa soyne

'ate, then went out'

kor
during the action
simultaneity

ipe kor soyne

'went out while eating'

no
stative or negated action
'in the state of'; 'without doing'

somo ipe no soyne

'went out without eating'

The minimal triad comes from Nakagawa's analysis of the Saru particle system
nakagawa2024 341 ; for kor and no
see simultaneous-kor-clauses . Nakagawa also notes that the result-state account
alone does not predict every wa collocation: the fixed phrase
apkas wa arpa 'walk-and-go' shows wa marking
walking as the route or method of going, even though the walking partly overlaps the going.
Tamura's integration criterion (via Nakagawa) accommodates this under a "state/method/reason"
cluster that subsumes all three readings nakagawa2024 342 . The manner reading
appears clearly in the following Saru corpus example:

corpokke a=eapkas wa arpa=an ayne | beneath.it 4.A=walk.along CONJ go=4.S CONJ | Walking beneath it, I went along, and then… | wa marks the walking as manner and route of the going; ayne is the culminative particle treated in §5 below. | nakagawa2024:339 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 605, after Kayano 1974a: 68

The cause–effect reading is clearest when E1 directly occasions E2:

hokure pirkano e=ipere wa mimus yakne | INTERJ properly 2SG.A=feed CONJ get.fat COND | Now, feed her well so that, once she has fattened up… | wa links the feeding (E1, cause) to the fattening (E2, effect); the subsequent conditional yakne opens a premise on the result-state. | nakagawa2024:339 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 604

Subject continuity: statistical preference without a constraint

In Refsing's Shizunai material, approximately ninety percent of the clause pairs joined by
wa share a subject refsing1986 240 . The preference
reflects the particle's integration semantics: if E1 is the manner or prior stage of E2, the
same actor typically performs both. Yet the preference is not a syntactic rule. Satō shows
that the two clauses joined by wa may have different subjects — unlike
Japanese 〜て , which requires coreference — and that fact bears on its
analysis as a conjunctive particle rather than a same-subject converb
sato2008 45–46 ; bugaeva2022 .

hekaci suma osura wa ontaro hokus | child stone throw CONJ barrel fall.over | The child threw a stone, and the barrel fell over. | The thrower (child) is the A of the first clause; the barrel is the S of the second. Satō uses this example to show that wa carries no same-subject requirement. | sato2008:45 | SAR

Reference tracking across a wa chain rests on the obligatory person
indices that each finite verb carries independently. The statistical same-subject preference,
combined with the absence of a formal constraint, places wa in the
configuration that the cosubordination typology of
foleyvanvalin1984 describes — finite clause-chaining without converb
morphology and without a grammaticalized same/different-subject opposition. The comparison with
hine and akusu , and the broader question of
whether any Hokkaido particle encodes subject continuity in a switch-reference-like way, belong
to hine-akusu-switch-reference .

Multi-clause chains in narrative

Hokkaido Ainu prose narrative strings several wa -linked clauses into
a single chain. Each medial predicate closes on its own person indexing followed by
wa , before the chain continues to the next step. The result presents a
process as an integrated sequence of stages:

sikerpeni kapu a=sospa wa siwnin pe a=uyna wa kusuri ne a=kar | Phellodendron bark 4.A=peel CONJ yellow.bark NMLZ 4.A=take CONJ medicine COP 4.A=make | She peeled the bark of the Phellodendron tree, took the yellow inner bark, and made it into medicine. | A three-step wa-chain; each step is a completed result-state enabling the next. pe after siwnin nominalizes the NP; the fourth-person a= carries the narrating subject. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 横山裕之, 'sikerpeni', Ainu Times no. 18/3, text 12; corpus ainu-times/018/3#12

The free connective adverb orowa(no) 'and then, from there after' can
follow a wa -marked clause to make the sequential boundary more prominent
nakagawa2024 516 ; ijas2023 . This stacking — connective particle
before, connective adverb after — is one of the clearest diagnostics separating the two word
classes. The treatment of orowa(no) as a discourse boundary marker
belongs to tail-head-linkage-and-narrative-cohesion .

The culminative ayne

The particle ayne and finally, at last (after a protracted action) closes a
stretch of sustained effort and points to the eventual outcome. Where
wa integrates events step by step, ayne marks
the endpoint of a duration — a process that went on and finally yielded a result
nakagawa2024 346 ; sato2008 44 . Refsing notes a nuance of
impatient delay ('and then at last') absent from the neutral chainer
hine refsing1986 247 .

The most characteristic environment is the intensifier pattern
V a V a ayne , in which the perfective auxiliary
a reduplicates the verb to signal repeated iteration before the
particle closes the stretch:

ku=cis a ku=cis a ayne | 1SG.S=cry PFV 1SG.S=cry PFV CONJ | Crying and crying, and finally… | The V a V a ayne intensifier pattern: the perfective a marks each iteration; ayne closes the protracted stretch and points to the outcome. The full sentence continues with the result clause. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 丸野和子, '"Kuma" turano k=an', Ainu Times no. 15/1, text 24 (excerpt)

The particle easir 'only then, at that point' frequently follows
ayne to underscore the delay before the outcome
sato2008 44 ; ijas2023 . The pattern
wa…wa…ayne is common in narrative: a run of
wa -chained steps leads up to a final ayne
that marks the point of resolution, as in the manner example above
( corpokke a=eapkas wa arpa=an ayne ).

The etymology is transparent: durative auxiliary a plus the neutral
chainer hine , grammaticalized into a separate particle (Chiri 1936;
Tamura, via refsing1986 247–248 ). Tokachi uses
ahinne (occasionally ainne ), and some Tokachi
varieties such as Honbetsu lack the form altogether, distributing its function across
hine and wa
nakagawa2024 346–347 . With a negated preceding clause,
ayne shifts to a 'before having V-ed' reading (negated-protraction),
attested in Chitose nakagawa2024 347 .

wa as a gateway to the auxiliary system

Wa is the conjunctive particle most recruited as the pivot of Ainu's
periphrastic aspect constructions refsing1986 240 . The result-state reading
extends naturally into a family of analytic forms in which the result-state of a closed event
is overlaid by an existential or auxiliary verb, yielding a resultative, a terminative, a
prospective, an attemptive, or a benefactive nuance. Nakagawa's §15 (pp. 452–455) lists the
principal forms; the full aspectual analysis belongs to
existential-aspect-an-oka .

construction
glossed structure
core value

V wa an / V wa oka
V CONJ exist.SG / exist.PL
resultative state: 'be in the state resulting from V-ing'

V wa isam / V wa okere
V CONJ be.no.more / finish
terminative/completive: 'have V-ed (to completion)'

V wa anu
V CONJ put.aside
prospective: 'do V in preparation, keep V-ed for future use'

V wa inkar / V wa inu
V CONJ try.seeing / try.hearing
attemptive: 'try V-ing'

V wa kore
V CONJ give
benefactive: 'do V for someone'

The resultative wa an — where the preceding verb closes on its
result-state and the existential an predicates that state as ongoing —
produces a modifier-head construction interpretable as a resultative relative clause:

racitke wa an tuki | hang CONJ exist cup | a hanging cup | racitke wa an is the attributive resultative: 'the cup that hangs / is in the state of being hung'. The construction modifies tuki as a head noun. | nakagawa2024:339 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 607

The terminative wa isam foregrounds completion rather than persistence:

arpa wa isam | go CONJ be.no.more | S/he has gone.

In all these constructions wa is the conjunctive particle, the same
item that links finite clauses in the sequential reading; the boundary with the connective use
is syntactic (an auxiliary follows immediately and predicates only aspect) rather than formal.
The perfective auxiliary a that often precedes
wa in scene-switch positions adds a further layer, completing the
situation before the chain continues; that interaction is treated in
perfective-anterior-a .

The dialectal allomorph ma

When wa immediately follows a stem ending in
-n or -m , the initial
w- assimilates to the preceding nasal and the particle surfaces as
[ma] . Refsing documents the alternation for Shizunai
refsing1986 240 , and Nakagawa writes the allomorph phonetically in his
transcriptions: the resultative an wa an appears as
an ma an nakagawa2024 339 . The pattern is phonological
and pan-dialectal at the segment level ‹corpus-confirmed›: the standard romanized orthography
retains wa throughout to represent the underlying form, while katakana
transcription writes マ in the relevant environment, reflecting the surface pronunciation
(nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024; aynucorporadiscord ).

The same assimilation applies before any stem ending in a nasal, including
an 'be/exist', oman 'go (honorific)', and
verbs whose final syllable carries -n . The allomorph is predictably
conditioned; no separate particle ma exists alongside
wa .

Disambiguation

The form wa covers at least three distinct grammatical entries, kept
apart by position and syntactic host rather than by form
nakagawa2024 340 ; ijas2023 .

entry
grammatical class
key diagnostic
example

wa sequential
conjunctive particle
follows a finite VP; always opens a further clause; swappable for
hine

ipe wa soyne

'ate and went out'

wa(no) ablative
case particle / postposition
follows a NP or locative nominal; glossed ABL; extends to
wano

mosir wa

'from the land'

wa sentence-final
final particle
clause-final; emphatic or exclamatory; no following clause

pirka wa

'it's fine! (emph.)'

The connective wa always follows a finite verbal predicate and always
opens a following clause; it never occurs clause-initially. The ablative
wa(no) attaches to a nominal and typically co-occurs with a spatial or
temporal noun (cf. orowa = or +
wa 'from that place'). The sentence-final emphatic is isolable at the
end of a complete utterance. In natural text the three are rarely ambiguous; glossing discipline
requires keeping them distinct, since the particle counts for the sequential
wa are inflated by ablative and final-particle tokens in any raw
word-form search ‹corpus-suggested›.

title: Simultaneous kor 'while' and Overlapping-Event Clauses
summary: The conjunctive particle kor marking temporal overlap, distinguished from the homophonous possessive verb kor and the progressive auxiliary kor an.
part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
text:
A Grammar of Hokkaido Ainu
Variant: Hokkaido Ainu
Chapter: Simultaneous kor 'while' and Overlapping-Event Clauses
Part: Part XVII — Clause Linkage and Adverbial Subordination
Summary: The conjunctive particle kor marking temporal overlap, distinguished from the homophonous possessive verb kor and the progressive auxiliary kor an.

The form and its environments

The conjunctive particle kor while, as (simultaneous) joins two finite
clauses in Hokkaido Ainu and carries two distinct linking readings: a
simultaneous reading ('while, as, at the same time as') and a
habitual/factual conditional reading ('whenever, when habitually'). The same
segment is also the transitive verb kor have, possess 'have, possess',
creating a three-way collision of forms that both Nakagawa and Ijäs flag explicitly
nakagawa2024 340, 347 ; ijas2023 . Word-class and linear
position resolve the collision: the conjunctive particle follows a finite predicate and
precedes the next clause; the possession verb carries its own person-index prefixes
directly ( ku=kor 'I have it',
a=kor huci 'one's grandmother'). As a conjunctive particle,
kor belongs to the bound post-predicate class
( 接続助詞 ) inventoried in clause-linkage-overview .

Whole-corpus token counts place kor at approximately 29,300
occurrences (rank 5 in the aligned Hokkaido corpus), but that figure conflates the
possession verb, the simultaneous particle, and the conditional reading, so the raw total
is an upper bound on any single function ‹corpus-suggested›
nakagawa2024 340 .

Overlapping events: the simultaneous reading

The simultaneous reading of kor signals that two events or states
co-occur: the first predicate is ongoing while the second takes place, or both proceed
together. Nakagawa's primary gloss is Japanese 〜しながら
nakagawa2024 340 ; Satō gives the same value for Chitose
sato2008 49 .

Among the temporal-succession connectives of Saru, kor occupies a
phase-specific slot. Nakagawa draws a three-way contrast according to the temporal phase of
the first predicate nakagawa2024 341 :

particle
temporal phase of the first predicate
primary reading
illustrative pair

wa
result-state following completion of the action
〜して、〜したあとで 'and then; after doing'

ipe wa soyne
'ate, then went out'

kor
action or state ongoing at the time of the second event
〜しながら 'while doing; as'

ipe kor soyne
'went out while eating'

no
stative or negated predicate (no temporal change)

〜の状態で、〜しないで
'being X; without doing'

somo ipe no soyne
'went out without eating'

Nakagawa notes that the phase account alone does not explain every distribution: the
fixed collocation apkas wa arpa 'walk and go' resists
*apkas kor arpa , suggesting that additional manner or cause semantics
govern some wa collocations (Tamura 1972b: 152, via
nakagawa2024 342 ). The no particle and its
negation distribution are treated in manner-and-degree-adverbial-clauses ;
sequential wa belongs to
sequential-wa-and-clause-chaining .

The following example from Nakagawa's Saru materials shows the simultaneity construction
in a narrative sequence. The silver knife descends
( san ) while gleaming
( mikemike ):

sirokane otcike or sirokane makiri saya eusi wa kasi mikemike kor san hine | silver tray place silver small.knife sheath be.with CONJ surface sparkle CONJ descend CONJ | The silver knife on the silver tray, with its sheath, came descending while gleaming on the surface. | The mikemike kor san sequence is the simultaneity construction: gleaming (ongoing) kor descending. The preceding saya eusi wa closes an attendant-state clause ('sheathed, and'); hine continues the chain. | nakagawa2024:340 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 610

The simultaneous kor carries no same-subject requirement. Unlike
Japanese 〜ながら , which requires coreferential subjects in both
clauses, Ainu kor permits the two linked predicates to have
different subjects. Satō demonstrates this with an example where the speaking subjects
(mother and father) are distinct from the entity that acts in the matrix clause
sato2008 49–50 ; Ijäs makes the same observation, citing a Tamura example
where the dancer and the looker are different persons ijas2023 :

ku=horipi kor en=nukar | 1SG.S=dance CONJ 1SG.O=look.at | While I was dancing, s/he looked at me. | Different-subject kor clause: the dancer (first person, ku=) and the observer (third person, unexpressed) are distinct. After Tamura 2000: 155, adapted by Ijäs as a teaching example. | ijas2023

Reference tracking across the chain relies on the obligatory person-index prefixes on
each finite predicate. Ainu has no morphological switch-reference — no dedicated
same-subject or different-subject converb morphology — as established in
clause-linkage-overview and examined in hine-akusu-switch-reference .
For the Shizunai cognate kane , Refsing observes a statistical
tendency toward same-subject co-occurrence in her materials, with documented exceptions
refsing1986 250 ; no equivalent quantitative statement has been made for
Saru kor ‹corpus-suggested›.

The progressive: kor followed by an existential

When the simultaneous kor is followed immediately by the
existential verb an (singular) or oka
(plural), the combination functions as the Ainu progressive: the event of the first
predicate is in progress at the time of reference. Nakagawa gives the paradigmatic Saru
form and glosses it 歩いている 'is walking'
nakagawa2024 340 :

apkas kor an | walk CONJ exist | (s/he) is walking | Paradigmatic progressive: simultaneous kor followed by the existential an. The subject is third/fourth person, zero-marked on both predicates in this canonical form. | nakagawa2024:340 | SAR

The progressive contrasts directly with the resultative-perfect
wa an : where wa presents a result-state
following completion of an action, kor an presents the action as
still unfolding. This contrast follows directly from the temporal-phase distinction in the
table above. The example below shows the progressive integrated into a clause where the
attendant no marks a concurrent negated state:

a=onaha somo mokor no kamuyoritak kor an | 4.POSS=father NEG sleep CONJ pray.to.god CONJ exist | Father was praying to the gods without sleep. | no closes the negated somo mokor clause ('without sleeping'); kor an on kamuyoritak forms the progressive 'was praying'. Both no and kor are simultaneous-type connectives, assigned by the temporal phase of their respective first predicates. | nakagawa2024:340 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 614

The full distributional and frequency evidence for the kor an
progressive, its contrast with wa an , and the eight-form
aspectual paradigm as a whole are developed in existential-aspect-an-oka ,
which draws on Satō's dedicated Chitose aspect study sato2006 . The
TAM system is surveyed in tam-overview . The cognate
kane an continuative of Shizunai and the related
siran forms are treated in
continuative-siri-siran-kane-an .

Habitual and factual conditional: the whenever reading

The second conjunctive reading of kor expresses general causation
or habitual recurrence: whenever E1 obtains, E2 follows as a general truth or matter of
routine. Satō characterizes this as 一般的因果関係
sato2009 51 ; Nakagawa glosses it
〜すると（たびに） and gives as a stock example
sironuman kor cikap utar iwak
'when it gets dark the birds go home' nakagawa2024 347 . The relationship
is a general truth or a habitual pattern across occasions.

Satō's study of Ainu conditionals identifies a mood constraint on this reading: with a
declarative matrix clause, the habitual kor expresses a general
truth; for a one-off individual event it is restricted to wh-interrogative matrix clauses
sato2009 51 . The contrast with the hypothetical conditional
yakun is stark: yakun applies to one-time
premises and takes any matrix mood, making it the broadest member of the system. The full
conditional paradigm, mood constraints, and Satō's cross-linguistic comparison are set out
in conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses .

The following Saru example from Nakagawa illustrates the habitual conditional in a
narrative context. The sentence additionally presents all three homonymous readings of
kor within three words: the first two instances are the
possession verb ( a=kor 'one's grandmother/grandfather'), and the
third is the conditional particle:

a=kor huci a=kor ekasi earkinne i=omap wa sirkunne kor i=ukotumam wa hotke=an | 4.A=have grandmother 4.A=have grandfather both 4.O=love CONJ become.night CONJ 4.O=hold.together CONJ sleep=4.S | My grandmother and grandfather both loved me, and whenever night came they would hold me close and sleep. | The first two a=kor are the transitive possession verb 'have' (4th-person A prefix); sirkunne kor is the habitual conditional 'whenever it became night'. The recurrent pattern — night after night — is the core habitual reading. | nakagawa2024:347 | SAR | Nakagawa ex. 643

A corpus narrative by 丸野和子 in the アイヌタイムズ shows both the habitual conditional and
the progressive in a single clause, integrated with the
V a V a ayne durative-reduplication pattern
nakagawa2024 346–347 :

ku=cis a ku=cis a ayne k=esikarun kor ku=cis patek ku=ki kor k=an | 1SG.S=weep PFV 1SG.S=weep PFV CONJ 1SG.S=recall CONJ 1SG.S=weep only 1SG.A=do CONJ 1SG.S=exist | I cried and cried, and finally, whenever I recalled it, I was doing nothing but weep. | k=esikarun kor: habitual conditional 'whenever I recalled it'. ku=ki kor k=an: progressive 'was continuously doing'. Two distinct kor readings appear within the same clause chain. | ainukoraci1991 | SAR | 丸野和子, 'Kuma turano k=an', Ainu Times 015/1

Dialect distribution and cognate forms

The two linking functions that Saru assigns to a single kor are
distributed across separate particles in other Hokkaido varieties. Shizunai presents the
clearest formal split:

function
Saru / Chitose
Shizunai
Horobetsu / Kushiro
source

simultaneous 'while, as'
kor
kane
ko

nakagawa2024 342, 347–348 ;
refsing1986 248–250

habitual / general-causation conditional 'whenever'
kor
konno / koonno
ko

progressive ( V kor an )
kor an
kane an
ko an

In Shizunai, Refsing describes kane as the 'contemporal'
conjunctionalizer — 'at the same time as' — and assigns konno to
a separate 'sequential' slot covering inevitable consequence, closer to what Saru expresses
with the habitual conditional kor
refsing1986 248–250 . Nakagawa traces the Saru/Shizunai particle split
to Tamura 1972b: 148 nakagawa2024 342 . The following Shizunai example —
also cited in clause-linkage-overview — places the two Shizunai particles
side by side:

a=kor katkemat utar an=oskur kane kesto an konno oka=an | 4.A=have lady PL 4.A=cherish CONJ every.day exist CONJ exist.PL=4.S | Cherishing our ladies, we lived on day after day. | kane marks the simultaneity that Saru kor covers; konno marks the recurrent-time relation that Saru also assigns to kor in its conditional reading. The Shizunai system distributes these functions across two distinct particles. | nakagawa2024:342 | SHI | Nakagawa ex. 617, after Okuda; Shizunai

The reduced Horobetsu/Kushiro form ko reflects the regular
reduction of word-final r attested in other Hokkaido environments
(e.g. kur 'person' → ku ) and noted from
切替 2003: 313 by Nakagawa nakagawa2024 348 . The Shizunai
kane an continuative and its relationship to the
siran family of forms are taken up in
continuative-siri-siran-kane-an .

kor in derived particles

Two high-frequency conjunctive particles are built on kor and
inherit its temporal semantics transparently.

korka but, although (adversative) , the adversative conjunctive particle,
is formed as kor 'when' + ka 'even'
= 'even when (X, nevertheless Y)' nakagawa2024 351 ; ijas2023 .
The etymology is stated consistently across Nakagawa, Satō, and Ijäs and is uncontested.
Adversative korka is treated in
conditional-temporal-concessive-clauses .

sekor (quotative complementizer) ~と , the quotative complementizer,
is analysed as se 'say, make a sound' +
kor 'while' = 'while saying / while making the sound of …',
with the simultaneous value of kor still transparent in the
etymology nakagawa2024 470 ; ijas2023 . Community analysis
corroborates this parsing as an asserted, well-grounded etymology (nukopoli, aynu-corpora
Discord, 2024 aynucorporadiscord ). A further community-proposed analysis
holds that sekor is a specifically Saru innovation and that the
pan-dialectal ari / ani represents the
older form — a hypothesis put forward by nukopoli (aynu-corpora Discord, 2024
aynucorporadiscord ) ‹contested›; the primary grammars read for this chapter
do not settle the dialectal priority question. The framing function, person-switch
mechanism, and dialect distribution of sekor are developed in
quotative-complementation-sekor .
